                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - close interactive script
                - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
                - ### Network Bridge Functionality 00:00-00:23
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [A bridge connects network segments together, and in](undefined)[this case isolates electrical signals between hubs.](undefined)[This prevents unnecessary messages](undefined)[from propagating between hubs.](undefined)[Let's install a bridge between our two hubs.](undefined)[This bridge will now make forwarding decisions.](undefined)[It will decide which messages should be forwarded to](undefined)[this hub and which should be contained to just this hub.](undefined)
                - ### Message Handling by the Bridge 00:23-00:50
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [Suppose the device connected to this hub sends](undefined)[a message intended for a device on the same hub.](undefined)[When the message is sent, it's repeated to](undefined)[all other ports, including to the bridge.](undefined)[The bridge inspects the message, and because it's](undefined)[intended for a device connected to this hub, the](undefined)[bridge discards the message so it won't be forwarded.](undefined)[However, if the message were addressed to a](undefined)[device connected to this hub, the bridge will](undefined)[forward the message on to the second hub.](undefined)
                - ### Building the Forwarding Database 00:50-01:17
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [A bridge makes these decisions by building a forwarding](undefined)[database which contains the Mac addresses of all devices on](undefined)[a network and the port that is used to reach each device.](undefined)[Bridges start with empty forwarding databases,](undefined)[so they need to build their databases overtime.](undefined)[This is done by analyzing network traffic.](undefined)[These two hubs here are connected together](undefined)[by a bridge in the middle, and since this is](undefined)[a new bridge, it's forwarding database is.](undefined)
                - ### Message Processing and Database Update 01:17-01:59
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [Now suppose computer A sends a message to computer](undefined)[B and both computers are connected to the same hub.](undefined)[Computer A sends out A-frame which](undefined)[contains the source Mac address.](undefined)[The destination Mac address, and](undefined)[the message data to the hub here.](undefined)[The hub then sends the frame to all](undefined)[connected devices, including the bridge the](undefined)[bridge receives and processes the frame.](undefined)[The bridge looks at the source Mac address and](undefined)[sees if the address is in its forwarding database.](undefined)[Since it isn't, the bridge adds an entry to the](undefined)[database for the Mac address of Computer A and](undefined)[identifies which port the computer is connected to.](undefined)[The bridge now has a Mac address along](undefined)[with the Port Association for Computer A.](undefined)
                - ### Forwarding Decisions and Traffic Isolation 01:59-03:08
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [Next, the bridge looks at the destination](undefined)[Mac address and checks to see if it has this](undefined)[Mac address in the forwarding database.](undefined)[If the destination Mac address isn't found in](undefined)[the forwarding database, the bridge will forward](undefined)[the frame through to the other hub over here.](undefined)[The bridge has a Mac address and](undefined)[port associated with computer A.](undefined)[Suppose computer B wants to send a message to computer A.](undefined)[The message is sent to the hub and then](undefined)[forwarded to all devices, including the bridge.](undefined)[The bridge looks at the source Mac address from Computer B.](undefined)[Since computer B isn't in the forwarding database, a table](undefined)[entry is created with its Mac address and Associated Port.](undefined)[The bridge then looks at the destination Mac](undefined)[address and compares it to the forwarding database.](undefined)[Because a table entry exists for computer A's](undefined)[Mac address, and because the port associated](undefined)[with computer A is on this side of the bridge,](undefined)[the message is discarded and not forwarded.](undefined)[This process happens on both sides of](undefined)[the bridge and continues until the bridge](undefined)[has a comprehensive forwarding data.](undefined)[At this point, the bridge is able to](undefined)[distinguish when to forward messages through](undefined)[to the other hub and when to discard messages.](undefined)
                - ### Network Traffic Management 03:08-03:58
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [With a bridge, network, traffic is isolated to a](undefined)[specific group and unnecessary traffic between segments](undefined)[is reduced because bridges examine the Mac address in](undefined)[the frame, they're classified as layer two devices.](undefined)[They make decisions based on the Mac address, another](undefined)[type of a bridge connects 2 network segments that use](undefined)[different transmission mediums or different architectures.](undefined)[Suppose you have a wired Ethernet network and a](undefined)[wireless network that need to connect together.](undefined)[If both the wired network and the wireless network have the](undefined)[same subnet, a bridge is used to connect the two networks.](undefined)[The bridge does this by taking frames sent](undefined)[from 1 architecture and converting them](undefined)[into frames used by the other architecture.](undefined)[Because the bridge is only dealing with frames.](undefined)[And not subnets.](undefined)[It still operates at layer two of the OSI mode.](undefined)
                - Resume Auto-Scroll
                - An Ethernet bridge works at the Data Link layer (layer 2) to establish       separate physical network segments while keeping all nodes in the same       logical network. This reduces the number of collisions caused by having       too many nodes contending for access.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/qfOmT7sd--7lJ6iJWj_lrmPtaMjcz80Cvqp1Bq6xpVCjayfoCGDrhZoJyfe1d5FWIAyOLh6sdu8HPoTrMkan8rGMwN0BUNAuPtqFEy3B41pkNCtfUTRzb8OYvdvS3tyk.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The broadcast domain includes collision domain A and collision domain           B. Collision domain A shows a 100 M b p s hub connected to four end           systems. Collision domain B shows a 10 M b p s hub connected to two           end systems. 100 M b p s hub and 10 M b p s hub are connected outside           the domains via a bridge.
                    -  *Collision and broadcast domains. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - The previous figure shows how a bridge creates separate collision       domains. Each hub is a shared access media area. The nodes connected to       the hubs share the available bandwidth—a 100 Mbps Ethernet for domain A       and a 10 Mbps Ethernet for domain B—because only one node within each       collision domain can communicate at any one time. The bridge isolates       these segments from each other, so nodes in domain B do not slow down or       contend with nodes in domain A. The bridge does allow nodes to communicate       with the other collision domain. It does this by forwarding only the       appropriate traffic. This creates a single logical network, referred to as       a layer 2 broadcast domain.
                - An Ethernet bridge builds a forwarding database to track which addresses       are associated with which of its ports. When the bridge is initialized,       the database's **MAC           address table** is empty, but information is constantly       added as the bridge listens to the connected segments. Entries are flushed       out of the table after a period to ensure the information remains current.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Ji2k-sxub8fKSb45XUIA3Vf7sRvOVVG1vR55lrs1icQorOf0lgeE6CPWbliI6He50BxR9nVxPqmfGsiFI5A-grpI3l9hHwQBMwnEIOFk0K6Vgis-JGo8F-7ykJhIvQhZ.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The steps are as follows.
                    - 1. Host A A sends a frame to A C and the hub repeats the transmission           on all other ports.
                    - 2. The bridge sees the frame on port F 0, but ignores it as it does           not need to be forwarded.
                    - 3. Host B A is in a separate collision domain to A A so it sees that           the network is free and sends a frame to A E; hosts B B and B C see           the frame and ignore it, but detect that the network is busy.
                    - 4. The bridge receives the frame from B A on port F 1 and looks up           destination A E.
                    - 5. The bridge finds A E is associated with port F 2 so forwards the           frame.
                    - 6. A E receives the frame; to host A D the network is busy.
                    -  *Collision and broadcast domains. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - If no record of the MAC address exists or the frame is a broadcast or       multicast, then the bridge floods the frame to all segments except for the       source segment (acting like a hub).
            - ## 3.2.3 Switches
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - 
                - 
                - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_switch_layer2_n09/v_switch_layer2_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)
                    - Video Previous Segment
                    - Video Control Resume Play
                    - Video Control Pause
                    - Video Next Segment
                - 
                - 00:001. Evolution from Bridges to Switches

                - ### Evolution from Bridges to Switches 00:00-00:24
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [Originally, bridges were simple 2 port](undefined)[devices that connected network segments, but](undefined)[as networks got bigger and more sophisticated,](undefined)[bridge technology developed into switches.](undefined)[A switch is a multi-port bridge.](undefined)[Instead of a single device connecting 2 segments together,](undefined)[each switch port is its own segment on a four-port switch.](undefined)[There are four network segments connected.](undefined)
                - ### Switch Forwarding Database 00:24-00:43
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [As with a bridge, a switch creates a forwarding](undefined)[database, while the database is empty.](undefined)[A switch will forward frames to all connected devices.](undefined)[Overtime a switch creates a comprehensive](undefined)[forwarding database of all connected devices.](undefined)[When built, it will forward frames to](undefined)[only the intended device or segment.](undefined)
                - ### Optimal Switch Connections 00:43-01:12
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [Switches are designed to have only](undefined)[devices connected to each port.](undefined)[While it's possible to connect a hub to a](undefined)[switch port, it's not the best way to do things.](undefined)[It's best if a switch has only one device per port.](undefined)[By doing this, it establishes a sort of virtual](undefined)[circuit between devices, increasing network efficiency.](undefined)[If a hub which connects multiple devices is connected to](undefined)[the switch, then all the devices on the hub would share](undefined)[the virtual circuit, which impacts network communications.](undefined)
                - ### Unicast Communications in Switches 01:12-01:39
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [Suppose you have four devices connected to a switch.](undefined)[For normal communications.](undefined)[A switch uses unicast communications.](undefined)[Unicast frames have the destination Mac address](undefined)[followed by the source Mac address, and both of](undefined)[these addresses each identify a connected.](undefined)[When the switch receives a unicast frame that](undefined)[uniquely identifies a destination Mac address, and](undefined)[if that information is in its forwarding table, it](undefined)[forwards that information to the correct port.](undefined)
                - ### Broadcast Frames and Switch Behavior 01:39-02:07
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
                - [Sometimes a switch will receive a frame](undefined)[addressed to the broadcast address.](undefined)[The broadcast address is a special](undefined)[Mac address set to all FFS.](undefined)[Instead of being sent to a single device, the broadcast](undefined)[address shows that the frame is to be sent to all devices.](undefined)[When a switch receives a broadcast frame, it knows](undefined)[the frame needs to be sent to all connected devices.](undefined)[In this situation, the switch acts as a hub by sending](undefined)[the frame to all devices connected to the switch.](undefined)
                - Resume Auto-Scroll
                - The problems created by contention can be more completely resolved by       moving from a shared Ethernet system to a switched Ethernet. Hubs and       bridges are replaced with switches. Gigabit Ethernet and faster can  *only         * be deployed using switches.
                - An Ethernet switch performs the same sort of function as a bridge, but in       a more granular way and for many more ports than are supported by bridges.       Each switch port is a separate collision domain. In effect, the switch       establishes a point to point **full-duplex** link between any two       network nodes. This is referred to as **microsegmentation**.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/AlAu6cI63UTcI2Ai9hubjI1kBPjcTKVhHmcu-bcHJfnDhcqhLq3QsqGDTH-w19Y3kq7Vby0XcyJqrf-Xr-h6vtViC8TlC3znHfM9A_-0ADmflKAqKMraOHer-spF2Vv8.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The steps are as follows.
                    - 1. Host A A sends a frame to B C.
                    - 2. The switch reserves the frame into a port buffer and reads the           designation M A C address from the Ethernet frame.
                    - 3. The switch uses its M A C address table to look up the port           connected to the destination M A C address.
                    - 4. The switch uses its high-speed backplane to send the frame out of           port G 5 for host B C to receive (creating a temporary virtual           circuit).
                    - 5. None of the other connected devices see any activity on the           network while this process takes place. Those other devices are able           to transmit and receive at the same time.
                    -  *Switch operation. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - Because each port is in a separate collision domain, collisions can occur       only if the port is operating in half-duplex mode. This would only be the       case if a legacy network card or a hub is attached to it. Even then,       collisions affect only the microsegment between the switch port and the       connected interface; they do not slow down the whole network. As with a       bridge, traffic on all switch ports is in the same broadcast domain unless       the switch is configured to implement **virtual LANs (VLANs)**.
            - ## 3.2.4 Ethernet Switch Types
                - Ethernet switches from different vendors come in a variety of ranges to support various sizes of networks. While a basic model might feature 12 to 48 ports and little scope for expansion, advanced switches support interconnections via high-speed backplanes and expandable capacity through plug-in modules plus power supply redundancy, management consoles, and transceivers for fiber optic connectivity.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/0lfnatAxXpENTZMpvPyqBCYasEQcvD1FcO_gxDWtqrWZi-1nh4EG18MDVPJ9hOK7y_pxahsnxGNjEAuAG7UPttst-R-fo8iYaUDeRg7rXA3meKaZaZTFT4pG7ei1T_hN.png)
                -  *An example of a workgroup switch. (Image © 123RF.com.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - The market is dominated by Cisco's Catalyst and Nexus platforms (over 55% of sales), but other notable vendors include HP Enterprise, Huawei, Juniper, Arista, Linksys, D-Link, NETGEAR, and NEC.
                - Ethernet switches can be distinguished using the following general categories:
                    - **Unmanaged versus managed**—On a SOHO network, switches are more likely to be unmanaged, standalone units that can be added to the network and run without any configuration. The switch functionality might also be built into an Internet router/modem. On a corporate network, switches are most likely to be managed. This means the switch settings can be configured. If a managed switch is left unconfigured, it functions the same as an unmanaged switch does.
                    - **Stackable**—Switches that can be connected together and operate as a group. The switch stack can be managed as a single unit.
                    - **Modular versus fixed**—A fixed switch comes with a set number of ports that cannot be changed or upgraded. A modular switch has slots for plug-in cards, meaning it can be configured with different numbers and types of ports.
                    - **Desktop versus rack-mounted**—Simple unmanaged switches with five or eight ports might be supplied as small freestanding units that can be placed on a desktop. Most larger switches are designed to be fitted to the standard-size racks that are used to hold networking equipment.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/EvjhO2PbO2FAwvQGdlHJONjdVvhOA1wzhWxy3gQjbv7g_mL88TVz2Z7phY78qBZ4bc95TLR6dcJCzSPGJzJS8u7qt6cy23jEgDUGZq1Ms8TdReNvuJZPiN3vnuYdg_lR.png)
                -  *An example of a modular chassis that allows provisioning multiple access switches. (Image © 123RF.com.)* 
                - 
                -

            - ### Data Structure in Networking 00:00-00:12
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Data is broken up into datagrams that are placed](undefined)[in a packet, which is then placed in a frame.](undefined)[The frame contains the sender's](undefined)[Mac address and the destination.](undefined)[The packet contains the IP address.](undefined)
            - ### Analogy of Data Transmission 00:12-00:34
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Think of a time that you got a letter in the mail.](undefined)[The letter was inside an envelope.](undefined)[The envelope showed the address of](undefined)[the person that sent you the letter.](undefined)[It also showed your street address and](undefined)[your ZIP code data traveling over your](undefined)[network is like the letter in the envelope.](undefined)[The street address is like the Mac address](undefined)[and the ZIP code is like the IP address.](undefined)[While the letter itself is the data.](undefined)
            - ### Switch Forwarding in LAN 00:34-00:49
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A switch forwarding messages in a local area](undefined)[network or LAN is similar to mailing a letter](undefined)[to your friend across the neighborhood.](undefined)[The local post office can keep the letter in the](undefined)[local area and deliver it for you, according to the](undefined)[street address, just like a Switch uses a Mac address.](undefined)
            - ### Routing Across Distances 00:49-01:04
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [If you want to send a letter to someone across the](undefined)[country, the local post office looks at the ZIP code.](undefined)[And sends it to a distribution center, which](undefined)[then forwards it to the correct ZIP code.](undefined)[The distribution center is like a router](undefined)[sending packets through the Internet.](undefined)
            - ### Unique Device Identifiers 01:04-01:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Just like every house has its own unique address.](undefined)[Every device that connects to a network, whether](undefined)[wired or wireless, has a unique identifier called](undefined)[a media access control address or Mac address.](undefined)
            - ### Building the MAC Address Table 01:15-01:36
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Inside every switch is a table of Mac addresses](undefined)[and the port the device is plugged into.](undefined)[This is called the Mac address table.](undefined)[As data traverses the switch, it builds this](undefined)[table based on the frames passing through it.](undefined)[This process continues until all devices](undefined)[connected to the switch are accounted for](undefined)[and the Mac address table is complete.](undefined)
            - ### Efficient Data Delivery 01:36-01:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [When the Mac address table is complete, the switch](undefined)[can send data directly to the appropriate end device.](undefined)[Since the table defines which](undefined)[port each device is connected to.](undefined)
            - ### Full Duplex Switches 01:45-01:54
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [It's also important to know that](undefined)[switches are full duplex devices.](undefined)[This means they can send and receive data simultaneously.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 3.2.5 Switch Interface Configuration
                - Configuration of a managed switch can be performed at a command-line interface (CLI). Once you have established a connection to the switch's management interface, you can configure settings for each of the switch port interfaces. These settings control the network link configured for each client device attaching to the switch. Most switch operating systems work in multiple command modes or hierarchies. For example, Cisco IOS has three principal modes:
                    - **User EXEC mode**—This is a read-only mode where commands can be used to run basic troubleshooting tools. This mode is indicated by the **>** prompt.
                    - **Privileged EXEC mode**—This allows the user to report the configuration, show system status, reboot or shut down the appliance, and backup and restore the system configuration. This mode is activated using the `enable` command from user EXEC mode. It is denoted by a **#** prompt.
                    - **Global configuration mode**—This allows the user to write configuration updates. It is activated by using the `configure terminal` command from privileged mode and indicated by a **(config)#** prompt.
                - Most switch CLIs also support `TAB` and/or use of `?` to list different ways to complete a partial instruction.
                - Interfaces are identified by type, slot, and port number. For example, GigabitEthernet 0/2 (or G0/2) is port #2 on the first 10/100/1000 slot (or only slot).
                - Stackable switches precede interface identifiers with a module ID. For example, GigabitEthernet 3/0/2 is the second port on the first slot in the third module in the stack. Note that this numbering does vary between manufacturers. Also, some start from zero and some from one.
                - Switches normally support a range of Ethernet standards so that older and newer network adapters can all be connected to the same network. In most cases, the port on the switch is set to auto-negotiate **speed** (10/100/1000) and full- or half-**duplex** operation. A static configuration can be applied manually if necessary.
                - If you don't use autonegotiation, you need to manually configure the speed and duplex to match both devices. For best performance, if one end of the connection is hard coded, it's advised to hard code the other end and not rely on autonegotiation.
                - To configure the first interface, from global config mode, run `interface GigabitEthernet0/1`. This changes the prompt to **(config-if)#**. Some of the main subcommands are the following:
                    - `shutdown` disables the interface; `no shutdown` enables the interface.
                    - `speed`and `duplex`are both normally set to `auto`(the default). Using `speed 100` and `duplex half` would apply a static configuration.
                    - `switchport`configures switching mode characteristics. Interfaces connected to computer devices are usually set to `switchport mode access`. `switchport port-security` allows configuration of various security mechanisms.
                - Once done, run `exit`. To make changes persistent, run `do copy running-config startup-config`.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/tyzc7A-wzhmAQiICW43nMFLlX-cSvD_A1bx168vjOJzaFnjnKEqGvJnQBtk_IUbuqLVOs_yWg_-aQwuJw6ChuQPJC_5k7ffJP3Ksux0JK-4sNqJH2a7dGDquNwI0E93V.png)
                -  *Cisco IOS switch interface configuration commands. (Image © and Courtesy of Cisco Systems, Inc. Unauthorized use not permitted.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - copy is a privileged mode command. do copy allows you to run the command from within config mode.
            - 
            - You can use the range command to configure a number of interfaces simultaneously. For example, interface range GigabitEthernet0/1-24 enters configuration mode for all 24 interfaces in module 0.
            - 
            -

            - ### Intro 00:00-00:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To easily remote-manage your switch with the command](undefined)[line interface, or CLI, SSH is the preferred method.](undefined)[In this case, our switch doesn't have SSH access](undefined)[enabled by default, so we'll have to configure it](undefined)[with the web graphical user interface, or web GUI.](undefined)[Another method might be to enable it by a console port.](undefined)[However, we won't be covering that in this demo.](undefined)
            - ### Enable SSH 00:28-00:48
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [First, we must enable the SSH service](undefined)[since it's not currently running.](undefined)[To do so, we go to 'Security' and then 'TCP/ UDP Services'.](undefined)[Here, you can see the active running services on the switch.](undefined)[Our SSH service is not checked, so](undefined)[we click 'Enable' and then 'Apply'.](undefined)
            - ### Create User 00:48-01:36
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's create a user for our CLI access.](undefined)[We're going to go to 'Administration'](undefined)[and then 'User Accounts'.](undefined)[Next, we're going to select 'Add'.](undefined)[For this case, we're going to keep our username](undefined)[convention similar, so I'll name the username 'ciscoCLI'.](undefined)[Then we type in a password and confirm the same password.](undefined)[The user level is what determines](undefined)[which kind of access the user has.](undefined)[Since the purpose of this account is only to access the CLI,](undefined)[we're going to select 'Read/Limited Write CLI Access (7)'.](undefined)[Click 'Apply' and close when done.](undefined)[Since we haven't saved our config change to the](undefined)[startup configuration, we're going to do that now.](undefined)[We can easily click the red flashing notice and](undefined)[click 'Apply' and then 'OK' to the pop-up window.](undefined)
            - ### Test Login 01:36-02:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In order to verify our changes worked, we're](undefined)[going to use PuTTY to SSH into this switch.](undefined)[I already have the IP address of the](undefined)[switched saved, so I'll just click on it.](undefined)[Enter the username and then the password.](undefined)[Let's try a few commands.](undefined)[The 'sh arp' command shows known stored IP](undefined)[addresses and MAC addresses connected to the switch.](undefined)[Let's try 'config t' to enter configuration mode.](undefined)[Using both commands tell us our access is](undefined)[working as these wouldn't work in read-only mode.](undefined)[Let's minimize this and go back to the web GUI.](undefined)[If we go back to 'Security', 'SSH Server', and 'SSH User](undefined)[Authentication', the table here shows all active logins](undefined)[to the switch and which IP address they're coming from.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:25-02:44
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we discovered how](undefined)[to configure SSH on a switch.](undefined)[From there, we created a user strictly for CLI access and we](undefined)[tested out the account by using PuTTY to SSH into the switch.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll

        - **3.3 Switch Port Configuration** 
            - ## 3.3.1 Link Aggregation and NIC Teaming
                - **Link         aggregation** means combining two or more separate cabled links       into a single logical channel. For example, a single network adapter and       cable segment might support 1 Gbps; bonding this with another adapter and       cable segment gives a link of 2 Gbps. Link aggregation can also be used in       an uplink between two switches or between a switch and a router or between       two routers.
                - From the host end, this can also be called **NIC           teaming**; at the switch end, it can be called port       aggregation and is referred to by Cisco as an EtherChannel. The term       "bonding" is also widely substituted for "aggregation."
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/0vMXjMDkEtlFDdxQVC-gaOyxJm9DEGjOhprz2KuHQh1RUGD5fkWtmlEUA6eUiX0f947VQh04hUl7OywCRya6RLkFdjKgZMx6VszkW6EV7xhrW8Tg6Z0-giDLFjh5jqdY.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - 2-port 1 G b p s N I C S connected to workgroup or access switch via           4 G b p s channel. It is labeled link aggression group of four 1 G b p           s ports. Router or layer 3 switch is connected to workgroup or access           switch via 20 G b p s channel. It is labeled link aggregation group of           two 10 G b p s ports. Text beside workgroup or access switch reads, 10           G b p s transceivers.
                    -  *A server node uses NIC teaming to create a 4 Gbps channel link from           four 1 Gbps ports to a workgroup switch, while the workgroup switch           bonds its uplink transceivers to create a 20 Gbps channel to a router.* 
                - Link aggregation can also provide redundancy; if one link is broken, the       connection is still maintained by the other. It is also often       cost-effective; a four-port Gigabit Ethernet card might not match the       bandwidth of a 10 GbE port but will cost less.
                - This configuration is fully redundant only if the       business function does not depend on the full speed of the bonded link. If       one port fails, and the link drops to 1 Gbps, but that bandwidth is       insufficient, there is not full redundancy. Full redundancy is achieved when the total bandwidth equals the sum of the bandwidths of all individual links. This ensures that even if one link fails,the remaining links can still provide the required bandwidth to maintain network operations without degradation in performance.
                - Link aggregation is typically implemented using the IEEE 802.3ad/802.1ax       standard. 802.3ad bonded interfaces are described as a link aggregation       group (LAG). 802.3ad also defines the **Link         Aggregation Control Protocol (LACP)**, which can be used to detect       configuration errors and recover from the failure of one of the physical       links.
                - On a Cisco switch, the following commands configure LACP to group the       first four Gigabit interfaces into a single channel with the ID 2:
                - `interface range GigabitEthernet0/1-4`
                - `channel-group 2 mode active`
                - The following commands configure the 10G interfaces into a channel group       with ID 1. In this example, the 10G interfaces are on a different module       than the Gigabit interfaces:
                - `interface range 10GigabitEthernet1/1-2`
                - `channel-group 1 mode passive`
                - On the router/layer 3 switch, the channel should be set to active:
                - `interface range 10GigabitEthernet0/1-2`
                - `channel-group 1 mode active`
                - Optionally, both sides can be configured as active.       However, if both sides are set to passive, no bonded channel will be       created. The channel ID on each side does not have to match, but it is       easier to manage the connection if it is the same on both switches.
                - 
                -

            - ### Configure Port Aggregation 00:00-00:20
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Link aggregation is the process of combining](undefined)[multiple network ports together to increase](undefined)[throughput and provide a means of redundancy.](undefined)[Instead of just having an active passive bond, we're](undefined)[able to utilize both network ports at the same time.](undefined)
            - ### Configure LAG 00:20-01:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In order to set up LACP, which stands for](undefined)[Link Aggregation Control Protocol, we have to](undefined)[add ports to a LAG, or Link Aggregation Group.](undefined)[To do this, we go to 'Port Management', 'Link](undefined)[Aggregation', and then 'LAG Management'.](undefined)[We're going to select the first](undefined)['LAG 1' and click the 'Edit...'](undefined)[button.](undefined)[Our name is going to be 'windows_server'.](undefined)[However, if you have a host name, that may](undefined)[be a better way to document the connection.](undefined)[Make sure the LACP checkbox is set to 'Enabled'.](undefined)[Otherwise, it'll be grayed out once the LAG group is saved.](undefined)[Next, we're going to add Port 1 and Port 2, as](undefined)[these are the two ports I have my host plugged into.](undefined)[Now, click 'Apply' and 'Close'.](undefined)[It may take a short amount of time for the](undefined)[ports to reset themselves and for the host](undefined)[to pick up the new connection settings.](undefined)[The host has to be properly set up for LACP](undefined)[in order for this configuration to work.](undefined)
            - ### Configure LAG VLAN 01:15-02:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Normally, network engineers don't use the default](undefined)[VLAN on the switch for general network use.](undefined)[That being said, we're going to configure](undefined)[our new LAG group to another VLAN.](undefined)[First, we go to 'VLAN Management'](undefined)[and then 'Interface Settings'.](undefined)[Change the Filter from Port to 'LAG' and click 'Go'.](undefined)[Our LAG 1 doesn't need to be set up as a Trunk](undefined)[since a single VLAN will be going to our host.](undefined)[Now select 'Edit' and switch the VLAN mode to 'Access'.](undefined)[Click 'Apply' and 'Close' to exit the config window.](undefined)
            - [Now that this is set, we're going to go to 'Port VLAN](undefined)[Membership' and change the Filter from Port to 'LAG'.](undefined)[Select LAG 1 and click 'Join VLAN'.](undefined)['VLAN 1 Untagged' should be removed since](undefined)[we know our general network VLAN is 13.](undefined)[We're going to use that one.](undefined)[Click 'Apply' and close the window.](undefined)[Keep in mind that VLANs other than the](undefined)[default will need to be created on the switch.](undefined)
            - [Let's go back and check on the status of our LAG group.](undefined)[Go to 'Port Management', 'Link](undefined)[Aggregation', and back to 'LAG Management'.](undefined)[We can see our link state is Up](undefined)[and we have two Active Members.](undefined)[This connection is now ready to use LACP.](undefined)[To save our changes, we're going to select the flashing](undefined)[red 'Save' button, apply the running config to the](undefined)[startup config, and click 'OK' on the pop-up window.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:39-02:51
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we configured link aggregation, created](undefined)[a LAG group, and assigned a LAG group to a VLAN.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 3.3.2 Maximum Transmission Unit
                - A standard Ethernet frame has a maximum length of 1,518 bytes, excluding the preamble. Each frame has an 18-byte header:
                    - 6-byte destination and source MAC address fields.
                    - 4-byte error checking field.
                    - 2-byte EtherType field.
                - The maximum size of the data payload is 1,500 bytes. This upper limit of the payload is also referred to as the **maximum transmission unit (MTU)**.
                - In circumstances where data payloads can be very high, a 1500-byte MTU means using a lot of frames. A **jumbo frame** is one that supports a data payload of up to 9,216 bytes. This reduces the number of frames that need to be transmitted, which can reduce the amount of processing that switches and routers need to do. It also reduces the bandwidth requirement somewhat, as fewer frame headers are being transmitted. The benefits of jumbo frames are somewhat disputed, however.
                - When implementing jumbo frames, it is critical that all hosts and appliances (switches and routers) along the communications path be able and configured to support them. It is also vital to ensure that each device supports the same MTU. Also, it can be complex to calculate the MTU if any additional headers are used (for IPSec, for instance).
                - Jumbo frame support can be configured using the command `mtu 9018`, where 9,018 is the required size. On some appliances, this must be configured for the whole system; on others, it can be configured on a per-interface basis.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - ### Enable Jumbo Frame Support 00:00-00:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Jumbo frames are Ethernet frames larger than 1,500 bytes.](undefined)[Normally, the Jumbo Frames option isn't enabled](undefined)[by default and can only be enabled to reduce](undefined)[network bandwidth or a system's CPU cycles.](undefined)[Some examples of when you might want to](undefined)[enable this could be for a single VLAN or](undefined)[SAN, which stands for storage area network.](undefined)[Adjusting to accommodate jumbo frames could be](undefined)[labeled as MTU, or maximum transmission unit.](undefined)
            - ### Enable Jumbo Frames 00:32-01:12
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To enable Jumbo Frames on this switch, you must](undefined)[go to 'Port Management' and then 'Port Settings'.](undefined)[Keep in mind that Jumbo Frames will have](undefined)[to be enabled on the device side, too.](undefined)[There's one check box at the](undefined)[top here to enable Jumbo Frames.](undefined)[We're going to click 'Apply' after the box is checked.](undefined)[To make this change effective, let's click on the flashing](undefined)[red 'Save' button and then click 'Apply' so the running](undefined)[configuration applies to the startup configuration.](undefined)[Next, we're going to click on 'Reboot' under](undefined)['Administration' and 'OK' on the pop-up window.](undefined)[It's going to take a while for the switch](undefined)[to reboot, so we're going to fast forward.](undefined)
            - ### Statistics and Troubleshooting 01:12-02:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Great, our switch is rebooted, so let's log in and continue.](undefined)[One good way to make sure no issues are occurring with](undefined)[Jumbo Frames is to check out the statistics on the switch.](undefined)[Depending on the brand and model,](undefined)[some switch data could look different.](undefined)[On this switch, we're going to go to 'Status](undefined)[and Statistics', 'RMON', and 'Statistics'.](undefined)[The first menu you're seeing is only](undefined)[statistics from the first switch port.](undefined)[We don't have anything plugged into this port, so we'll](undefined)[have to switch this to port 28 to check out some data.](undefined)[Briefly looking at the numbers, you](undefined)[can see this port has received data.](undefined)[The most concerning things if there were Jumbo](undefined)[Frames issues would be Undersize Packets,](undefined)[Oversize Packets, Jabbers, and Collisions.](undefined)[If you're unsure what a jabber is, it's](undefined)[a device that has malformed data frames.](undefined)[Since all four of these numbers are 0, that's a good thing.](undefined)[Ethernet frames should be checked on network](undefined)[devices to make sure jumbo frames are working](undefined)[and have a bigger frame size then 1,500 bytes.](undefined)
            - [The most important thing to take away from this](undefined)[is not mixing jumbo frames with non-jumbo frames.](undefined)[Some examples would be having several devices with](undefined)[Jumbo Frames enabled and not enabled simultaneously.](undefined)[VLANS and separate networks can section off jumbo](undefined)[frame devices so that network issues don't occur.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:25-02:38
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we looked into jumbo frames.](undefined)[We also showed you how Jumbo Frames can be](undefined)[enabled and troubleshooted it on a switch.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 3.3.3 Spanning Tree Protocol
                - Large networks make use of multiple switches configured in a mesh or partial mesh topology to implement redundant links. Multiple paths are part of good network design as they increase resilience; if one link fails, then the network can remain operational by forwarding frames over a different path. However, Ethernet has no concept of a "time to live" value for frames, so layer 2 broadcast and flooded traffic could continue to loop through a network with multiple paths indefinitely.
                - The **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)** is a means for the bridges or switches to organize themselves into a hierarchy and block loops. The switch at the top of the hierarchy is the root. The switch with the lowest ID, comprising a priority value and the MAC address, will be selected as the root.
                - Each switch then determines the shortest path to the root bridge by exchanging information with other switches. This STP information is packaged as bridge protocol data unit (BPDU) multicast frames. Different **port roles** are assigned to the interfaces participating in the spanning tree. A port that forwards "up" to the root, possibly via intermediate switches, is identified as a root port. Ports that can forward traffic "down" through the network with the least cost are identified as designated ports. A port that would create a loop is identified as a blocking or non-designated port. Subsequently, bridges exchange Topology Change Notifications if devices are added or removed, enabling them to change the status of forwarding/blocked ports appropriately.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/HkrKR6AmFuMrwtdcbX1R3rlURX5LUuiXiBVqhvbFec7HpAsenN2JMC2qBIvqiN-E7rNsmw3uKrPby-gyra_TVI94M2Tz0VT86JiC6tnaOSuJiAn5ntyyuKoltPPcq2i4.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The diagram shows three bridges: root bridge, bridge A, and bridge B. The designated port of bridge A is connected to blocked port of bridge B. The root port of bridge A is connected to designated port of root bridge. The other designated port of root bridge is connected to root port of bridge B.
            -  *Spanning tree configuration. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - This image shows the minimum configuration necessary to prevent loops in a network with three bridges or switches. The root bridge has two designated ports (DP) connected to Bridge A and Bridge B. Bridges A and B both have root ports (RP) connected back to the interfaces on the root bridge. Bridges A and B also have a connection directly to one another. On Bridge A, this interface is active and traffic for Bridge B can be forwarded directly over it. On Bridge B, the interface is blocked (BP) to prevent a loop, and traffic for Bridge A must be forwarded via the root bridge.
            - ## 3.3.4 Spanning Tree Protocol Configuration
                - If a switch supports spanning tree, it should operate by default without       configuration. An administrator can (and should) set the priority value to       predetermine **root           bridge selection**. The root will usually be part of a       high-bandwidth backbone or core switch group; performance will suffer if a       switch on a low-bandwidth segment becomes root. You can use the `show         spanning-tree` command to report the current configuration. Using       `spanning-tree id root primary` and `spanning-tree id         root secondary` assign main and backup priority values to the       chosen switches.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/y9w5RRyfYvDjkpuyEPyMGPhb_kRorJoFUSJ3dMAlIXKouHzibk8FxoFpQ3uJktWapmfI07fUiMwEW6E-U9Ol_B-sLDMfaeARHu74vDnBNhIw-5DNqW14XcemTDIt4VKj.png)
                -  *Viewing spanning tree configuration on a Cisco switch. This switch           has been designated the root. (Image © and Courtesy of Cisco Systems,           Inc. Unauthorized use not permitted.)* 
                    - The following table shows the different **port           states** associated with spanning tree operation.
                    - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/IfkhPFp4Ebf33xLwJJR7s7xO2Y6lFNxt2s8zf6ZFvlG40ZtpCvl_z9SaVu-Z9af0PJ9LgwyNZh2UNVX8BT8zv5ybs09w57j9LPI55tWGNJZ8dpB5FibDtJXLOb1Qq96e.png)
                        - Description
                        - 
                        - It provides data on the priority, address, cost, port, and hello time           of root I D and priority, address, hello time, and aging time of           bridge I D.
                        -  *One of this switch's interfaces would make a loop, so it has been           put in the blocking state. (Image © and Courtesy of Cisco Systems,           Inc. Unauthorized use not permitted.)* 
                    - When all ports on all switches are in forwarding or blocking states, the       network is converged. When the network is not converged, no communications       can take place. Under the original 802.1D standard, this made the network       unavailable for extended periods—tens of seconds—during configuration       changes. STP is now more likely to be implemented as 802.1D-2004/802.1w or       Rapid STP (RSTP). The rapid version creates outages of a few seconds or       less. In RSTP, the blocking, listening, and disabled states are aggregated       into a discarding state.
                    -

            - ### Configure Spanning Tree 00:00-00:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [STP, or Spanning Tree Protocol, prevents loops and](undefined)[broadcast storms when multiple switches are connected.](undefined)[Things like these can cripple your](undefined)[network or even make it unusable.](undefined)[It's common to have this on by default, anyway,](undefined)[but there might be tweaks you want to make that](undefined)[fit your network topology and setup better.](undefined)[It's a good idea to do your research before you](undefined)[enable any global- or interface-level settings, as](undefined)[this could affect your network's functionality.](undefined)
            - ### STP Global Settings 00:32-01:06
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [First, we're going to look at STP Global Settings.](undefined)[These are located under 'Spanning Tree'](undefined)[and 'STP Status & Global Settings'.](undefined)[We can see that STP is Enabled,](undefined)[and Operation Mode is Rapid STP.](undefined)[This allows faster STP that doesn't create forwarding loops.](undefined)[The Classic STP option provides a single path](undefined)[between any two end stations, while Multiple](undefined)[STP detects Layer 2 loops and mitigates them.](undefined)[Multiple STP instances can be tied to individual VLANs.](undefined)[This is a good approach if you're looking](undefined)[to limit your impact on loops per VLAN.](undefined)
            - ### Configure BPDU Guard 01:06-02:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, we're going to look at individual STP interface](undefined)[settings by selecting 'STP Interface Settings'.](undefined)[We have one device connected to port 1.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and select that port and click the 'Edit...'](undefined)[button.](undefined)[We know this port is set up on an access VLAN, which](undefined)[means it's most likely meant for an end user device.](undefined)[We do want STP set to 'Enabled' on this port.](undefined)[However, it's a best practice to enable](undefined)[BPDU Guard on an access port as well.](undefined)[BPDU, which stands for Bridge Protocol Data Unit, is a](undefined)[collection of frames that contain information about STP.](undefined)[In this case, access ports don't need to](undefined)[have an influence on the STP topology.](undefined)[This job should only be reserved for](undefined)[switches and bridges not end user devices.](undefined)[We're going to select 'BPDU Guard',](undefined)[click 'Apply', and close the window.](undefined)[To copy these settings on multiple access ports,](undefined)[click 'Copy Settings' down here and then copy the](undefined)[config from port 1 to other known access ports.](undefined)[Be mindful where your trunk or uplink ports are, as those](undefined)[shouldn't have BPDU Guard turned on like here on port 28.](undefined)
            - [To save our changes, we're going to](undefined)[select the flashing red 'Save' button.](undefined)[Then we click 'Apply' to apply the running config to](undefined)[the startup config and click 'OK' on the pop-up window.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:26-02:38
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we showed you some](undefined)[basics of Spanning Tree Protocol.](undefined)[We also showed you how to configure](undefined)[BPDU Guard on switch access ports.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 3.3.5 Power Over Ethernet
                - **Power over Ethernet (PoE)** is a means of supplying electrical power from a switch port over ordinary data cabling to a connected powered device (PD), such as a VoIP handset, IP camera, or wireless access point. Powering these devices through a switch is more efficient than using a wall-socket AC adapter for each appliance. It also allows network management software to control the devices and apply schemes, such as making unused devices go into sleep states and power capping.
                - PoE is defined in two IEEE standards (now both rolled into 802.3-2018):
                    - **802.3af**—Power is supplied as 350mA@48V and limited to 15.4 W output. Given that some of this dissipates over the length of cable, it supports PDs that require up to about 13 W.
                    - **802.3at (PoE+)**—Supplies at 30 W, with a maximum current of 600 mA. This can support PD requirements of up to about 25 W.
                    - **802.3bt (PoE++)**—Supplies at 60 W (Type 3) or 90 W (Type 4), with up to 51 W and 71 W usable power, respectively.
                - PoE switches are referred to as endspan (or endpoint) power sourcing equipment (PSE). On a Cisco switch, the command `power inline auto max 15000` enables a port for PoE and sets a maximum output of 15,000 mW (or 15 W).
                - When a device is connected to a port on a PoE switch, the switch goes through a detection phase to determine whether the device is PoE enabled. If not, it does not supply power over the port and, therefore, does not damage non-PoE devices. If so, it determines the device's power consumption and sets the supply voltage level appropriately.
                - If a switch does not support PoE, a device called a power injector (or midspan) can be used.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - ### Intro  00:00-00:30
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [PoE, or Power over Ethernet, is the process of passing](undefined)[both power and data through a twisted-pair Ethernet cable.](undefined)[This is highly preferred for access](undefined)[points, IP phones, cameras, and more.](undefined)[The ease of one network cable prevents you from having to](undefined)[plug in separate power supplies and networks into the device.](undefined)[Although this is a PoE switch, you can still](undefined)[plug regular network devices into a PoE port.](undefined)
            - ### PoE Settings 00:30-01:22
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Our Cisco SG300 28 Port Switch here is PoE-enabled.](undefined)[Not all switches have this technology, so](undefined)[it's best to do your research and make sure](undefined)[the one you want is compatible with PoE.](undefined)[The settings for PoE are under 'Port Management' and 'PoE'.](undefined)[First, we're going to go to 'Properties'.](undefined)[There are two types of Power Modes.](undefined)[Port Limit sets the maximum power per port, while Class](undefined)[Limit detects the power class and power limit automatically.](undefined)[You many run into trouble if your port limit power is](undefined)[too low for the device you're attempting to power, so be](undefined)[mindful of the power consumption of your PoE devices.](undefined)[We're going to leave the setting as 'Class Limit' so that](undefined)[Automatic Mode will take care of the power requirements.](undefined)[Here we have some statistics that show Nominal](undefined)[Power, Consumed Power, and Available Power.](undefined)[The current devices are only taking up 9 watts, so there's](undefined)[a lot more headroom if we were to plug in more devices.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Priority  01:22-02:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, we're going to check out 'Settings'.](undefined)[This shows the PoE table of all the ports.](undefined)[Note that in this switch, only twenty-four of](undefined)[the twenty-eight ports can be used for PoE.](undefined)[Here at the bottom, we have two devices](undefined)[that are currently using PoE power.](undefined)[Let's click 'Edit' on 'Port 23'.](undefined)[Some settings would be changeable in here](undefined)[if we were to have Port Limit enabled vs.](undefined)[Class Limit enabled.](undefined)[We're interested, though, in 'Power Priority Level'.](undefined)[The device plugged into this port happens](undefined)[to be a security camera, so I want to make](undefined)[sure this is the last device to lose power.](undefined)[Setting this to 'Critical' gives it a higher](undefined)[priority than the default setting of Low.](undefined)[You would only run into an issue if the Max](undefined)[Power Allowed for the PoE switch was near 100%.](undefined)[Any low-priority devices would](undefined)[potentially lose power in this case.](undefined)[We're going to click 'Apply' and 'Close' on this window.](undefined)
            - [Don't forget to save your changes.](undefined)[Click on the flashing red 'Save' button over here, and click](undefined)['Apply' and then 'OK' on the second window that pops up.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:24-02:34
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we discussed PoE and how setting priority on](undefined)[devices could prevent a critical device from losing power.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll

        - **3.4 Switch Troubleshooting** 
            - ## 3.4.1 Hardware Failure Issues
                - When you are using the CompTIA Network+ troubleshooting model, it is wise to rule out physical hardware failure and Data Link layer issues before diagnosing a Network layer or application issue.
                - ## Power Issues
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Like any computer system, networks require stable power to operate properly. Power anomalies, such as surges and spikes, can damage devices, under-voltage events (very brief power loss) can cause systems to lock up or reboot, while power failures  will down everything, including the lights. Enterprise sites have systems to protect against these issues. Uninterruptible power supplies (UPSs) can keep servers, switches, and routers running for a few minutes. This provides time to either switch in a secondary power source (a generator) or shut down the system gracefully, hopefully avoiding data loss. Most power problems will have to be escalated to an electrician or the power company, depending on where the fault lies.
                - ## Hardware Failure Issues
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - If power is not the issue, consider other components that might have experienced hardware failure, including host network adapters, switch/router/modem appliances, and the cabling between them. Complete hardware failure is relatively uncommon, so if you can rule out power and cabling problems, then for a network adapter, verify that the driver is working correctly. The easiest thing to do is to replace the driver (in Windows, use Device Manager to do this). For a network appliance, use status LEDs to confirm operation and check that things such as plug-in cards and modules are seated correctly. You should also consider overheating as a potential cause of hardware issues. Make sure there is good airflow around the intake and outlet vents. Check that fans and internal components are not clogged with dust and that systems are not exposed to direct sunlight.
                - At the Data Link layer, most wired hosts connect to the network via a switch. If you suspect a device such as a switch, analyze the topology of your network. You should be able to view those users who are suffering from the problem, identify which part of the network is affected, and identify the problem bridging or switching device.
                - When you have narrowed the problem to a device, you must determine what the nature of the problem is. It is always worth resetting the switch to see if that resolves the problem. Often, restarting network devices can clear transitory errors.
                - Do be aware that restarting a switch, router, or server can be very disruptive to the rest of the network. Identify how to mitigate potential impacts and seek authorization for your plan before proceeding. Also, remember that a restart will apply the startup configuration. Any unsaved changes in the running configuration will be discarded.
                - 
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

                - Video Previous Segment
