            - ### Use arp and netstat  00:07-00:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we'll look at how to use](undefined)[two useful network management utilities, the](undefined)[address resolution protocol (ARP) and netstat.](undefined)
            - ### Use arp 00:15-01:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's start this demo with the ARP utility.](undefined)[You can use the ARP utility to view](undefined)[and manage the ARP table on an IP host.](undefined)[Remember, whenever you send a packet to a destination host](undefined)[on an IP network, you have to know that host's MAC address.](undefined)
            - [Every network interface has a MAC address.](undefined)[The MAC address is the hardware address that's](undefined)[burned into the ROM of each network interface.](undefined)[The issue is that the MAC address does not change;](undefined)[it's associated with a specific network interface.](undefined)[There are some rare cases where this isn't true,](undefined)[but typically, a MAC address is globally unique.](undefined)[This means that no two hosts in the](undefined)[world should have the same MAC address.](undefined)
            - [IP addresses, on the other hand, are logical addresses.](undefined)[This means that a host could have one IP address one](undefined)[day and a completely different IP address the next day.](undefined)[Therefore, we have to map IP addresses to MAC](undefined)[addresses so we know the physical interface that](undefined)[a particular packet needs to be delivered to.](undefined)
            - [That's the job of ARP.](undefined)[The ARP utility is used to manage the ARP protocol.](undefined)[You can use the ARP utility to view mappings of IP](undefined)[addresses to MAC addresses on a particular host.](undefined)[You can also use it to add or remove mappings of](undefined)[IP addresses to MAC addresses on a particular host.](undefined)
            - ### View IP-to-MAC Address Mappings 01:28-02:20
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's take a look at how it works.](undefined)[If you want to view a current list of IP](undefined)[address to MAC address mappings, you enter 'arp](undefined)[-a' at the command prompt of the Windows system.](undefined)[You can see here that we have several mappings.](undefined)[This is the ARP table on the host.](undefined)
            - [Notice that the mapping for 192.168.1.10](undefined)[maps to this physical address, this MAC](undefined)[address, and it was dynamically discovered.](undefined)
            - [That means that when a packet was sent out to the](undefined)[192.168.1.10 host, the ARP protocol sent out a](undefined)[broadcast saying, "Okay, which one of you has the IP](undefined)[address 192.168.1.10" The host with that IP address](undefined)[responded back saying, "Hey here's my MAC address."](undefined)
            - [The ARP protocol recorded that](undefined)[mapping in the ARP table on this host.](undefined)[That's why it's dynamic in nature over here, under type.](undefined)
            - ### Delete and Add arp Mappings 02:20-04:40
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [If necessary, you can also use the ARP utility to either](undefined)[add or delete a mapping in the ARP table on a Windows host.](undefined)[For example, let's suppose that](undefined)[this device has been swapped out.](undefined)
            - [Of course, the network interface of the](undefined)[new device has a different MAC address.](undefined)[The new device has the same IP as the old](undefined)[device, but we need to update the MAC address.](undefined)[Let's look at how this is done.](undefined)
            - [In order to remove an entry from the ARP table, you have](undefined)[to have administrator privileges on in the command window.](undefined)[Let's get administrator privileges.](undefined)[We'll right-click the Start button, go up](undefined)[here, and select Command Prompt (Admin).](undefined)[Say yes to this.](undefined)[Move this up a little bit.](undefined)
            - [We want to delete the mapping 192.168.1.101,](undefined)[which is a Linux server on the network.](undefined)[We enter 'arp -d 192.168.1.101' and enter 'arp -a' again.](undefined)[You can see that the mapping was deleted](undefined)[from the ARP table on this workstation.](undefined)
            - [We can add it back in a couple of different ways.](undefined)[One way is to send a ping packet to the destination host.](undefined)[We enter 'ping 192.168.1.101'.](undefined)[When we do this, the ARP protocol looks in the ARP table](undefined)[and says, "I don't have a mapping for that IP address.](undefined)[I need to get it."](undefined)[It will send out a broadcast saying, "Hey, which](undefined)[of you has an IP address of 192.168.1.101?"](undefined)
            - [The host responds back with its MAC address.](undefined)[The ping command sends the ping packet.](undefined)[If we do an arp -a, you can see that 192.168.1.101](undefined)[has been added back into the ARP table of the system.](undefined)
            - [You could do it manually if you wanted to.](undefined)[If you knew the MAC address of the host and you wanted](undefined)[to add it in manually, you could use the arp -s command](undefined)[followed by the IP address of the host, 192.168.1.101,](undefined)[followed by the MAC address of the host.](undefined)
            - [When we press Enter, ARP manually adds](undefined)[this IP address mapping into the ARP table.](undefined)[We won't do that now because it's already](undefined)[there, from when we pinged that address.](undefined)
            - [That's how ARP works.](undefined)[Let's type 'cls' to clear the screen.](undefined)
            - ### Use netstat 04:40-05:18
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The next utility we'll use is the netstat utility.](undefined)[netstat stands for network statistics.](undefined)[It's a command line utility you can use to display](undefined)[information about the network interfaces in a system.](undefined)
            - [You can view information about network connections, view the](undefined)[routing table, look at protocol statistics for each network](undefined)[interface you have installed in your system, and so on.](undefined)[Let's begin with the basic netstat command.](undefined)
            - [The netstat command without any parameters](undefined)[displays a list of active connections with](undefined)[this host, both incoming and outgoing.](undefined)[Let's type 'netstat' and press Enter.](undefined)[You can see that we have several established connections.](undefined)
            - ### Use netstat and Parameters 05:18-08:21
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now let's enter 'netstat -a'.](undefined)[Let's scroll up.](undefined)[The -a option displays not only a list of all connections,](undefined)[but also all the open ports that are listening on the system.](undefined)
            - [This is useful because it's a](undefined)[great way to find rogue processes.](undefined)[If you have some type of malware running on your system that](undefined)[has opened an IP port and is either sending or receiving](undefined)[information, using netstat is one way to identify it.](undefined)[netstat -a gives a list of all open](undefined)[connections and open ports on this system.](undefined)[These are ports that are listening for connections.](undefined)[This connection right here, 192.168.1.100](undefined)[that uses the TCP protocol on port 139, is](undefined)[a mapped drive to a share on the server.](undefined)
            - [Notice we also have several other open](undefined)[ports that are in a listening state.](undefined)[Here, you can see what the port number is.](undefined)[If you see something that you don't](undefined)[recognize, you can research it and find out if](undefined)[it's a legitimate open port, or if it is not.](undefined)
            - [We also have the active listening](undefined)[UDP ports down here, as well.](undefined)
            - [In addition to detecting malware, the netstat -a](undefined)[command is also a useful tool for verifying that a](undefined)[network service is running the way it's supposed to.](undefined)
            - [For example, you start a particular service and you're not](undefined)[able to establish connections to it from a remote host.](undefined)[You check the firewall, and the firewall has the port open.](undefined)[You can use the netstat -a command to see if](undefined)[the port is open and listening on the host.](undefined)
            - [That will help you troubleshoot what](undefined)[might be wrong with the service.](undefined)[Let's do a 'cls' to clear the screen.](undefined)
            - [In addition to the -a option, you can also enter](undefined)['netstat -es' to display Ethernet statistics for](undefined)[each protocol configured on your network interface.](undefined)[If we scroll up a little bit here, you can see information](undefined)[about the various protocols for the network interfaces.](undefined)
            - [For example, up here, you have a summary of interface](undefined)[statistics: the number of bytes sent and the number of](undefined)[bytes received; the number of unicast packets sent;](undefined)[the number of non-unicast packets; packets that were](undefined)[discarded; errors and unknown protocols; and so on.](undefined)
            - [There is a lot of useful information](undefined)[here for troubleshooting.](undefined)[Once again, let's clear the screen.](undefined)
            - [You can also use the netstat](undefined)[command to view the routing table.](undefined)[Let's enter 'netstat -r'.](undefined)[Let's scroll up a little.](undefined)[This will show you both IPv4 and IPv6.](undefined)[We're not going to talk about IPv6 right now, but it](undefined)[will display the routing table for both protocols.](undefined)
            - [Here you can see the IPv4 route table.](undefined)[This is the same information you can](undefined)[get by entering the route print command.](undefined)[We have a route for the local network segment, 192.168.1.0.](undefined)[We have the default route here, 0.0.0.0.](undefined)[Any packets not addressed to a host on the 192.168.0.0](undefined)[network are immediately sent to the default route up here.](undefined)[The packets are then forwarded to](undefined)[the 192.168.1.1 default gateway.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 08:21-08:30
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we analyzed and](undefined)[configured the arp and netstat tools.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 4.6.3 IP Forwarding Issues
                - If the address configuration on the local host seems to be correct, you can complete a series of connectivity tests using ping to determine the likely location and scope of a fault.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/IXkenOntit9vmG0k6zb6bhCA0N3jGr6HrZf3vfhLUEYAvKLxoWRZ37ymbdfCVeC2x39047zkc-xMNbxw9sn7kwWXQpAlToxrJZbXJJIAMp0rGpJdJJ_4CdSW_hDN1C8U.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The sequence is as follows: ping loopback, ping host, ping gateway, ping remote host, ping host name, and establish session.
            -  *A general ping sequence for identifying connectivity issues.* 
                1. Ping the loopback address (`ping 127.0.0.1`) to verify TCP/IP is installed and loaded correctly. If this fails, reinstall the network protocol stack.
                2. Ping the IP address of the local host to verify it was added correctly and to verify that the network adapter is functioning properly. If you cannot ping the host's own address, there might have been a configuration error, or the network adapter or adapter driver could be faulty.
                3. Ping the IP address of the default gateway to verify it is up and running and that you can communicate with another host on the local network.
                4. Ping the IP address of other hosts on the same subnet to test for local configuration or link problems.
            - If a local host cannot be pinged and the error is destination unreachable, then verify the IP configuration does not contain an incorrect IP address or netmask. If these are correct but pings still time out, suspect either a security issue (such as a switch port security issue) or a problem at the Data Link or Physical layer.
                1. Ping the IP address of a remote host to verify you can communicate through the router. If a remote IP address cannot be contacted, check the default gateway parameter on the local host to rule out an incorrect default gateway issue. If the gateway is configured correctly and you can ping the router, you need to start investigating the routing infrastructure.
            - When performing tests using ping, always be aware that ICMP could be blocked by a firewall or other security software, especially when pinging remote hosts.
            - 
            - This methodical approach is suitable when you cannot diagnose the cause of a problem, or when you are verifying a new or changed IP configuration. In practice, you might start with a simple ping test to a remote host to identify or reproduce the problem.
            - 
            - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_ping_tracert_n09/d_ping_tracert_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_ping_tracert_n09/d_ping_tracert_n09.jpg)

            - ### Use ping and tracert 00:07-00:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we'll work with](undefined)[two network troubleshooting utilities.](undefined)[First, we'll look at ping and then, we'll look at tracert.](undefined)[We'll practice using those commands](undefined)[on a workstation on subnet A.](undefined)[We'll use these utilities to work with other hosts](undefined)[on the same subnet, hosts on different subnets within](undefined)[the same organization, and hosts on the internet.](undefined)
            - [As you can see, there are two routers](undefined)[between this workstation and the internet.](undefined)[One router here and one router here.](undefined)[Any traffic that goes to the internet from the](undefined)[workstation must go through those two routers.](undefined)
            - ### Verify IP Information 00:39-01:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [First, we'll verify the IP](undefined)[configuration on this workstation.](undefined)[Let's open a command prompt.](undefined)[We'll use the ipconfig command to do that.](undefined)[We use the /all parameter so we can](undefined)[see all the available information.](undefined)
            - [When we do, we see that the IP address](undefined)[assigned to this workstation is 192.168.1.100.](undefined)[Its subnet mask is 255.255.255.0.](undefined)[That means we are on the 192.168.1.0 subnet.](undefined)[The default gateway is 192.168.1.1.](undefined)
            - [If you look here, you can see that the](undefined)[DHCP server has the address 192.168.1.10.](undefined)[We also have a DNS server with an IP address](undefined)[of 192.168.1.10, as well as a backup DNS server](undefined)[on the internet with an IP address of 8.8.8.8.](undefined)
            - ### Examine a ping Response 01:39-03:36
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's type 'cls' to clear the screen.](undefined)[The first utility we'll practice](undefined)[working with is the ping utility.](undefined)[The ping utility sends an ICMP echo request](undefined)[packet to a remote host that you specify.](undefined)[When that host receives the packet, it](undefined)[responds back with an ICMP echo response.](undefined)
            - [If your system receives an ICMP echo](undefined)[response, that tells you two important things.](undefined)[It tells you that there is physical connectivity between](undefined)[the workstation and the remote host that you're pinging.](undefined)[It also tells you that the protocols are properly](undefined)[configured on that physical infrastructure.](undefined)[The packets are making it through.](undefined)
            - [Let's try using it.](undefined)[Let's ping a host that's on this same subnet.](undefined)[We'll ping the DNS server.](undefined)[We'll ping 192.168.1.10.](undefined)[When we do, four ICMP echo request packets](undefined)[are sent to 192.168.1.10 and four ICMP echo](undefined)[response packets are received in return.](undefined)
            - [There's a lot of good information](undefined)[we can pull from the output of ping.](undefined)[This parameter, here, tells you how big](undefined)[the ICMP echo request packet was in bytes.](undefined)[The time parameter, over here, tells you how long it](undefined)[took from the time that you sent the ICMP echo request](undefined)[to the time that you received the ICMP echo response.](undefined)[As you can see, each one took around one millisecond.](undefined)
            - [Some summary statistics are displayed down here.](undefined)[We sent four requests.](undefined)[We received four responses.](undefined)[Zero were lost.](undefined)[You will, on occasion, have instances where you](undefined)[send four requests and get three responses back.](undefined)[One is lost.](undefined)[That could be caused by network congestion,](undefined)[bad network cards, and all kinds of things.](undefined)[If that happens, you may need to troubleshoot.](undefined)[We also have some averages for the round-trip time.](undefined)[The response was basically almost instantaneous](undefined)[(one millisecond) because we are on the same subnet.](undefined)
            - ### ping Using Domain Names 03:36-04:27
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Notice up here that we pinged using](undefined)[the IP address of the remote host.](undefined)[You don't have to do that.](undefined)[You can also ping by domain name.](undefined)[Let's try that.](undefined)[Enter 'ping demo.local'.](undefined)[Notice that ping first resolves this](undefined)[host name into the associated IP address.](undefined)[Then it sends the same four ICMP echo](undefined)[request packets to the remote host.](undefined)
            - [Pinging by IP address first and then](undefined)[by DNS name can be very valuable.](undefined)[If you can ping the remote host by IP address but](undefined)[not by its DNS name, you have basic connectivity.](undefined)[The protocols are working, but you may have](undefined)[a problem with the name resolution system.](undefined)[It could be the wrong DNS server address is](undefined)[configured, the DNS server is down, or there isn't](undefined)[a record for the host that you're trying to ping.](undefined)
            - ### ping Another Subnet 04:27-05:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's clear the screen.](undefined)[We know everything is working well so far.](undefined)[So, let's send a ping through](undefined)[this router, onto the internet.](undefined)[We'll type 'ping'.](undefined)[Then we'll type the DNS name of a](undefined)[server on the internet, 'example.com'.](undefined)
            - [DNS will resolve example.com](undefined)[into the appropriate IP address.](undefined)[We'll send the four ICMP echo request packets.](undefined)[Again, we got all the responses](undefined)[for each of those packets back.](undefined)[This means that we have good connectivity to the internet.](undefined)
            - [Notice that there is something different here.](undefined)[The round-trip time is much slower.](undefined)[That's because these packets had to go](undefined)[through two of the organizational routers.](undefined)[Then, the packets went to the internet and](undefined)[through many different routers (probably eight or](undefined)[more) before they reached the destination host.](undefined)
            - [The round-trip time was considerably longer because](undefined)[the ping packets had to travel such a long distance.](undefined)[We can see that down here, in the summary statistics.](undefined)
            - ### Use tracert 05:24-06:04
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [At this point, let's shift gears](undefined)[and look at the tracert utility.](undefined)[Ping is awesome for testing connectivity between two hosts.](undefined)[However, it doesn't reveal much about the path](undefined)[a packet takes through various networks between](undefined)[the sending host and the destination host.](undefined)
            - [If you need to troubleshoot routing](undefined)[problems, use the tracert command.](undefined)[Be aware that on Windows, this](undefined)[command is tracert (trace rt).](undefined)[On Linux systems, it's trace route.](undefined)[Also, be aware that the way the command](undefined)[works is different on Linux and Windows.](undefined)[On Windows, it uses the ICMP protocol, just like ping.](undefined)[On Linux, it uses the UDP protocol instead of ICMP.](undefined)
            - ### How tracert Works 06:04-07:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Tracert uses the same ICMP echo request packets as ping.](undefined)[However, it manipulates the ICMP echo request packet a](undefined)[little bit to reveal the IP address of each router between](undefined)[the source-sending system and the destination system.](undefined)
            - [It does that by manipulating the](undefined)[time to live, TTL, parameter.](undefined)[You don't want packets circling the internet endlessly](undefined)[because they couldn't reach their destination host.](undefined)[The ICMP protocol is designed so that every time a](undefined)[packet crosses a router, the router automatically](undefined)[subtracts the value of the TTL by one.](undefined)[If this number gets down to zero, you can](undefined)[assume that the packet is going nowhere.](undefined)[Instead of letting it circle the](undefined)[network endlessly, it gets dropped.](undefined)
            - [Let's test this out.](undefined)[We'll do a tracert between this system,](undefined)[here, and the default gateway, here.](undefined)[First, we'll do this with the IP address.](undefined)[They're on the same subnet, so](undefined)[we shouldn't see a lot happening.](undefined)
            - [Now let's do a tracert using the domain name.](undefined)[Let's do 'demo.local'.](undefined)[As you can see, we got one hop, right here.](undefined)[Each router it goes through is called a hop.](undefined)[We see the DNS name and IP address of this first hop router.](undefined)[Let's do something a little more complex.](undefined)[First, we'll type 'cls' to clear the screen.](undefined)
            - ### Use tracert to Remote Destinations 07:29-08:55
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's do a tracert to www.example.com.](undefined)[This requires some time to complete because](undefined)[we're sending packets to a host on the internet.](undefined)[It'll have to hop through several routers](undefined)[before it reaches the destination system.](undefined)[Let's see what happens.](undefined)
            - [We're seeing many hops delivered by the tracert command.](undefined)[You'll notice, here, we have the same](undefined)[first hop that we looked at before.](undefined)[Now it continues going through routers,](undefined)[or hops, to the destination device.](undefined)
            - [Sometimes you will see a message from one of](undefined)[the routers that says, "Request timed out."](undefined)[This usually means the router is configured](undefined)[not to respond to ICMP echo requests in](undefined)[order to prevent denial of service attacks.](undefined)[Traffic goes through the router, but it](undefined)[ignores the ICMP traffic that's sent to it.](undefined)
            - [You can see that in order to get to example.com,](undefined)[a packet moves through 10 different routers.](undefined)[There are 10 hops between this system and www.example.com,](undefined)[with the last IP address being the end device.](undefined)[Normally, you don't count the last device as](undefined)[a hop, since it is not a router in most cases.](undefined)
            - [If you're not able to reach hosts on the internet,](undefined)[you can use this information to troubleshoot problems.](undefined)[For example, if the second router was down, you](undefined)[would see no output for the rest of the routes](undefined)[because none of the packets made it past this router.](undefined)[That can help you know where to start troubleshooting](undefined)[because you know that this router is working.](undefined)[The problem is with this router, here.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 08:55-09:08
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demo, we practiced two useful troubleshooting tools.](undefined)[First, we looked at ping, and then we looked at tracert.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll

        - [Size]();-[H3]()
    - ### 5.0 Configuring Routing and Advanced Switching
        - **5.1 Routing Technologies** 
            - ## 5.1.1 Routing Tables and Path Selection
                - Most end system workstation and server computers are configured with a single network adapter connected to only one network. Although potentially capable of routing, they are not equipped with the necessary interfaces and knowledge of the location of other networks. A router is a multihomed intermediate system with links and network topology information to facilitate forwarding packets between subnets or around an internetwork.
                - Information about the location of other IP networks and hosts is stored in the **routing table**. Each entry in the routing table represents an available path to a destination network or host. The following main parameters define a routing entry:
                    - **Protocol**—The source of the route. Paths can be configured statically or learned by exchanging information with other routers via a dynamic routing protocol.
                    - **Destination**—Routes can be defined to specific hosts but are more generally directed to network IDs. The most specific (longest) destination prefix will be selected as the forwarding path if there is more than one match.
                    - **Interface**—The local interface to use to forward a packet along the chosen route. This might be represented as the IP address of the interface or as a layer 2 interface ID.
                    - **Gateway/next hop**—The IP address of the next router along the path to the destination.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/og91dF2jFmeldqckh4ZoZAbCXLbex4kbWmdFlzT3V45HxcRrRE9bB6-OOmIqvBzwYZJVDYIM71JINGb1ppwv0Mrrkz5Fhi65gsBhn8hIbgUEGpyM2M5neokVOMrDm-5j.png)
                -  *Routing table for a Cisco router showing directly connected subnets (C) and routes learned from the EIGRP dynamic protocol (D). There is also a static route identifying the gateway of last resort/default gateway. (Image © and Courtesy of Cisco Systems, Inc. Unauthorized use not permitted.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - An IPv4 host route has a /32 prefix, while an IPv6 host route has a /128 prefix. Directly connected hosts are often defined by host routes. Anycast addressing also requires that the anycast address be entered in routing tables as a host route.
            - ## 5.1.2 Static and Default Routes
                - Routing table entries fall into four general categories:
                    - **Directly connected routes**—For subnets for which the router has a local interface.
                    - **Remote routes**—For subnets and IP networks that are not directly attached.
                    - **Host routes**—To a specific IP address. A host route has a /32 (IPv4) or /128 (IPv6) prefix.
                    - **Default route**—To use when an exact match for a network or host route is not found.
                - ## Directly Connected Routes
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The IP network or subnet for each active router interface is automatically added to the routing table. These are known as **directly connected routes**.
                - ## Static Routes
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A **static route** is manually added to the routing table and only changes if edited by the administrator. Configuring static routing entries can be useful in some circumstances, but it can be problematic if the routing topology changes often, as each route on each affected router needs to be updated manually.
                - Static routes can be configured either as non-persistent or persistent/permanent. A non-persistent route is removed from the routing table if the router is rebooted. A non-persistent route might be added as a troubleshooting action, for instance. If a static route is not reachable, it will be disabled.
                - ## Default Route
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A **default route** is a special type of static route that identifies the next hop router for a destination that cannot be matched by another routing table entry. The destination address 0.0.0.0/0 (IPv4) or:-/0 (IPv6) is used to represent the default route. The default route is also described as the gateway of last resort. Most end systems are configured with a default route (pointing to the default gateway). This may also be the simplest way for an edge router to forward traffic to an ISP's routers.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.1.3 Routing Table Example
                - As examples of directly connected, static, and default route entries that might appear in a routing table, consider the following example of three routers connected in a series:
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/_KufCGB7eQGYubJRURm0LyC-zl5lojx6KWcMZWC77Kf28eqBHDW1aT1ShwuBX4yqPGQJR1dHQM38-xQ-Ft1na2vfZYiotyhEt2FLFAVFFQbog2uOWFT98pRfuutvgCoF.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The G 0 port of router A is connected to the network, 10.0.1.0 slash 24. The G 1 port router A is connected to the G 0 port of router B via the network, 10.0.2.0 slash 24. The G 1 port of router B is connected to the G 0 port of router C via 10.0.3.0 slash 24. The G 1 port of router C is connected to the network, 10.0.4.0 slash 24. Three tables are provided. Each table has three columns labeled network, interface, and source. Router A routing table infers the following data. 10.0.1.0 slash 24: G 0 and connected. 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 1 and connected. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 1 and static. 10.0.4.0 slash 24: G 1 and static. Router B routing table infers the following data. 10.0.1.0 slash 24: G 0 and static. 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 0 and connected. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 1 and connected. 10.0.4.0 slash 24: G 1 and static. Router C routing table infers the following data. 0.0.0.0 slash 0: G 0 and static. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 0 and connected. 10.0.4.0 slash 24: G 1 and connected.
            -  *Routing tables for three routers connected in a series. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - First, consider the routing table of router A:
                - The router is directly connected to networks 10.0.1.0/24 (via interface G0) and 10.0.2.0/24 (via interface G1).
                - The router has been configured with static routes to 10.0.3.0/24 and 10.0.4.0/24, both of which are reachable via interface G1.
                - Router B has been configured in the same way, but here the networks 10.0.2.0/24 and 10.0.3.0/24 are directly connected, and the paths to 10.0.1.0/24 and 10.0.4.0/24 are configured as static entries.
                - Router C has been configured differently. It is directly connected to 10.0.3.0/24 and 10.0.4.0/24, but the only static route configured is for 0.0.0.0/0. This is a default route. While the router has no specific knowledge of networks 10.0.1.0/24 and 10.0.2.0/24, it will forward packets for these destinations over its G0 interface.
            - ## 5.1.4 Packet Forwarding
                - When a router receives a packet, it reads the destination address in the packet and looks up a matching destination network IP address and prefix in its routing table. If there is a match, the router will forward the packet out of one of its interfaces by encapsulating the packet in a new frame:
                    - If the packet can be delivered to a directly connected network via an Ethernet interface, the router uses ARP (IPv4) or Neighbor Discovery (ND in IPv6) to determine the Data Link layer address of the destination interface.
                    - If the packet can be forwarded via a gateway over an Ethernet interface, it inserts the next hop router's MAC address as the destination address in a new frame and uses the MAC address of the outgoing interface as the source addresss.
                    - If the packet can be forwarded via a gateway over another type of interface (leased line or DSL, for instance), the router encapsulates the packet in an appropriate frame type.
                    - If the destination address cannot be matched to a route entry, the packet is either forwarded via the default route or dropped (and the source host is notified that it was undeliverable).
                - ## Hop Count
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - If the packet is forwarded via a gateway, this process is repeated at each router to deliver the packet through the internetwork. Each router along the path counts as one **hop**. For example, in the network shown in the figure, Host A takes one hop to communicate with LOCAL_SRV via a directly connected interface on the LAN router. Note that the switches do not count as hops. Host B takes multiple hops (nine) to communicate with REMOTE_SRV, with traffic routed via two ISP networks. Also, observe the alternative routes that could be taken. Do any have a lower hop count?
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/oCm8oMpEEYB6SHqW9Tb5PzHHpwMEy04cylacE-G9me5slIuRjJOH3kFo6-Va_YSVUFFkfI_WB7UJJqAgC5dUZdNoORteLFv7JzuFLl6TTw7Drp1CaNvKJvPaoqKPA5Wy.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The diagram shows four blocks: local L A N, I S P 1, I S P 2, and remote L A N. Local L A N: a local server is connected to a switch. Two users, A and B are connected to a switch. These two switches are connected to a router. This router is connected to another router and the hop count is labeled 1. The second router is connected to a router in I S P 1 and the hop count is labeled 2. This router is connected to two other routers. The connection to the third router is labeled with the hop count, 3. Each router is connected to the same pair of two routers. The fifth router is connected to the sixth router. The third router is connected to the seventh router and labeled 4. I S P 2 shows a router connected to three other routers. The seventh router of I S P 1 is connected to the second router of I S P 2 and labeled 5. The second and third routers are connected and labeled 6. The third and fourth routers are connected and labeled 7. The sixth router of I S P 1 is connected to the fourth router of I S P 2. The fourth router of I S P 2 is connected to the first router in remote L A N and labeled 8. This router is connected to a switch and labeled 9. The switch is connected to a remote server.
            -  *Measuring hop count through an internetwork. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - ## Time to Live
                - At each router, the **Time to Live (TTL)** header field is decreased by at least 1. This could be greater if the router is congested. The TTL is nominally the number of seconds a packet can stay on the network before being discarded. While TTL is defined as a unit of time (seconds), in practice, it is interpreted as a maximum hop count. When the TTL is 0, the packet is discarded. This prevents badly addressed packets from permanently circulating the network.
                - In IPv6, the field is named Hop Limit to formalize the fact that it is a counter not a timer.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.1.5 Fragmentation
                - IP provides best-effort delivery of an unreliable and connectionless nature. Delivery is not guaranteed, and a packet might be lost, delivered out of sequence, duplicated, or delayed. It is possible that due to limitations in the underlying network, IP may fragment the packet into more manageable pieces to fit within the maximum transmission unit (MTU) of the Data Link protocol frame.
                - In IPv4, the ID, Flags, and Fragment Offset IP header fields are used to record the sequence in which the packets were sent and to indicate whether the IP datagram has been split between multiple frames for transport over the underlying Data Link protocol. For example, the MTU of an Ethernet frame is usually 1,500 bytes. An IP datagram larger than 1,500 bytes would have to be fragmented across more than one Ethernet frame. A datagram passing over an internetwork might have to be encapsulated in different Data Link frame types, each with different MTUs.
                - Most systems try to avoid IP **fragmentation**. IPv6 does not allow routers to perform fragmentation. Instead, the host performs path MTU discovery to work out the MTU supported by each hop and crafts IP datagrams that will fit the smallest MTU.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.1.6 Router Configuration
                - Routers serve both to link physically remote networks and subdivide autonomous IP networks into multiple subnets. Router placement is primarily driven by the IP networks and subnets that have been created:
                    - Hosts with addresses in the same subnet or IP network must not be separated by a router.
                    - Conversely, hosts with addresses in different subnets or IP networks must be separated by a router.
                - The figure shows a simplified example of a typical network configuration. An edge router/firewall provides access to the Internet. Traffic between the local subnets is controlled by a separate internal router.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/rAdXbOe955G7UeuC1OX5kPPYabbjRzu-u8yq-dLw6D36x2psLolUHseUBiM4jVb2yrgnVEcrPB7aqayfCmLentg-98BUQruc3g9cSR5AAXzuZ7svwKarnSs0t2S3zdWx.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The diagram shows two regions: exterior and interior. The exterior region shows the second port of the edge router connected to I S P. The first port of the edge router is connected to the first port of an internal router. The second port of the internal router is connected to three devices in the first subnet whose ports are labeled .17, .18, and .19 via 198.51.100.16 slash 28. The third port of the internal router is connected to three devices in the second subnet whose ports are labeled .33, .34, and .35 via 198.51.100.32 slash 28. The third port of the internal router is connected to three devices in the third subnet whose ports are labeled .49, .50, and .51 via 198.51.100.48 slash 28.
            -  *Network placement of edge and internal routers. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - As a router appliance does not have a screen or keyboard, it is configured locally either via a serial connection known as a console port or (more usually) remotely over the network by using a protocol such as Secure Shell (SSH). SSH can be used to communicate with the router via the IP address of any configured interface. However, as any given physical interface could suffer a hardware fault or be temporarily unavailable for various reasons, it is considered best practice to create a virtual interface, known as a loopback interface, in the router's operating system and assign it an IP address for use in remotely managing the router. This is a way of giving the router an internal IP address, not connected to any physical network, that is therefore not reliant on a specific network link being available.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/LPzK8mXVXjWY62cXE3TwlTXulycXUMk3ZOlDwYgiDzAw5v7ibKeIr3brLq0r8Hw6jItSlEGiaxkwX4T5__fxKVYThLbylh3oa9LRUEunSokhTNORC9MnScSvugA8wTN2.png)
                -  *Configuring RIP on a VyOS-based software router. The host can be configured at a local terminal or from a remote computer over Secure Shell (SSH).* 
            - Having placed the router at an appropriate point in the network, connected its cabling, and established a management session, the principal configuration tasks are as follows:
                - Apply an IP configuration to each interface.
                - Configure one or more routing protocols and/or static routes so that the router can serve its function.
            - ## 5.1.7 Routing Table Tools
                - Various command line tools are available to report the routing       configuration on routers and end systems.
                - ## show route
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The `show route`, `show ip route`, `show         ipv6 route`, or similar**           show route command** will output the active routing       table. As well as destination, gateway, AD/metric, and interface, the       output will show the source of the route, identified as a letter code (C =       connected, S = static, R = RIP, B = BGP, D = EIGRP, O = OSPF, and so on).
                - ## show arp
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - As with any IP host, a router keeps a cache of IP addresses that have       been resolved to MAC addresses via the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP).       Inspecting the ARP cache table is useful for discovering duplicate IP       addresses, IP misconfigurations, and routing protocol misconfigurations.       To view the cache, use `show arp` or `show ip arp`.
                - Each of the router's interfaces has a separate ARP cache. If an entry is       listed as incomplete, the router has sent an ARP request but has not       received a reply. This indicates that it expects a host with that IP to be       present on that network but that the host is not available.
                - ## route and ip route
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The **route         command** is used to view and modify the routing table of end       system Windows and Linux hosts.
                - Apart from loopback addresses and the local subnet, the routing table for       an end system generally contains a single entry for the default route. The       default route is represented as the destination 0.0.0.0/0. Any traffic       that is not addressed to the local subnet is sent over this default route.       In Windows, to show the routing table, run `route print`.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/H4Cd17vGb9PNkTxjaJyuS-QDAnYOC0lhFjs56sABW-J4-qaXUGZOIyMrbh9hNQwrd-RJ63RkvxQkN-LyZ_baFOqujgmUCMUr1ENyEToTGjjAni1Je9b9DYrOjm4HHQA6.png)
                -  *IPv4 and IPv6 routing tables for a Windows host. For IPv4, the host           uses 10.11.2.5 as its default gateway. IPv6 default gateway uses the           router's link local interface address. (Screenshot used with           permission from Microsoft.)* 
                    - In Linux, the `route`command is part of the older,       deprecated package of tools. You can use `ip route show` and `ip         route add` to achieve the same ends.
                    - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/35cRfPGp2ieURtX6cmPTIRYDVjycYVHWohrMk0ydOgxAQxI7uu-fUS-czOCykRdA8SlQ0M4mwK9Ye0wnXYncvq6rHfb1-S-4wjBC5yInQk8vmXzREqDGn4g_YI4FngSd.png)
                -  *Output of Linux ip route show command.* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.1.8 tracert and traceroute
                - The **traceroute** tool allows you to test the whole path between two nodes with a view to isolating the node or link that is causing the problem.
                - ## traceroute
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - `traceroute` is supported on Linux and router operating systems, such as Cisco IOS. `traceroute` uses UDP probe messages by default. The command issues a UDP probe for port 33434 with a TTL of 1. The first hop should reduce the TTL to zero and respond with an ICMP Time Exceeded message. The command then increments the port number and TTL by one and sends a second probe, which should reach the second hop router. This process is repeated until the end node is reached, which should reply with an ICMP Port Unreachable response.
                - The output shows the number of hops, the IP address of the ingress interface of the router or host (that is, the interface from which the router receives the probe), and the time taken to respond to each probe in milliseconds (ms). If no acknowledgment is received within the timeout period, an asterisk is shown against the probe. Note that while this could indicate that the router interface is not responding, it could also be that the router is configured to drop packets with expired TTLs silently.
                - `traceroute` can be configured to send ICMP Echo Request probes rather than UDP by using `traceroute -I`. The `traceroute -6` or `traceroute6` commands are used for IPv6 networks.
                - ## tracert
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - On a Windows system, the same function is performed using the `tracert` command. `tracert` uses ICMP Echo Request probes by default. The command issues an Echo Request probe with a TTL of 1. The first hop should reduce this to zero and respond with a Time Exceeded response. `tracert` then increments the TTL by one each time to discover the full path.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/ytbAwRD1ETCzbkNty4rnreqMcJqKg1A1oZ14rp7cxYXk39iGXuSoxoHU3t_BzEuB7t3vB-K4gwq9i1D7MKc_HOksxDv1BlYJ__1bjBjNueWQ91kAvyMgqMxxXtKasBKS.png)
                -  *Using tracert in Windows to plot the path from a host in the UK to CompTIA's web server. (Screenshot used with permission from Microsoft.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - `tracert` can be used with several switches, which must precede the target IP address or host.
                - You can use the `-d` switch to suppress name resolution, `-h` to specify the maximum number of hops (the default is 30), and `-``w` to specify a timeout in ms (the default is 4,000). If, after increasing the value, destinations are then reachable, you probably have a bandwidth issue to resolve. When used with host names (rather than IP addresses), `tracert` can be forced to use IPv6 instead of IPv4 by adding the `-6` switch.
                - `tracert -6 www.microsoft.com`
                - 
                - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_cli_router_tools_n09/d_cli_router_tools_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment

            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Troubleshooting Routers with Common Commands 00:00-00:59
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's look at troubleshooting our routers.](undefined)[There are common commands we can run in order to](undefined)[troubleshoot our routers to find any misconfigured devices.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and log into our router.](undefined)[The first command we're going to look at is traceroute.](undefined)[Traceroute is used to track the path that](undefined)[your data is going to take to its destination.](undefined)[We'll run the command traceroute](undefined)[and an IP address of a server.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and look at the route our](undefined)[data will take to the Google DNS server.](undefined)[We'll see a list of all the routers our](undefined)[data will pass through along the way to](undefined)[its destination of Google's DNS server.](undefined)[We'll get to see the route that it's taking, how long it](undefined)[takes to reach each hop, and the IP address of each hop.](undefined)[This can help identify when the connection](undefined)[might be slowing down or failing.](undefined)
            - ### Viewing and Modifying the IP Routing Table 00:59-02:11
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, let's look at the route command.](undefined)[This command will allow you to view](undefined)[and modify the IP routing table.](undefined)[First, let's enter global configuration mode,](undefined)[and then we'll start with the command IP route.](undefined)[This is helpful in providing a default route](undefined)[when routers are connected to multiple subnets.](undefined)[So, for example, let's say I need to create a default](undefined)[route for my local subnet of 192.168.1.0 with a subnet](undefined)[mask of 255.255.255.0, and I'm trying to connect to](undefined)[the next network over, which would be 10.0.0.1.](undefined)[I can run this IP route command in order to](undefined)[statically add this route into the router.](undefined)[Now, whenever the router receives data](undefined)[destined for the 10.0.0.1 network, it will](undefined)[make that next hop over from our local subnet.](undefined)[Occasionally, these static routes can become outdated,](undefined)[and we need to either remove them or update them.](undefined)[We can check this by first exiting global configuration](undefined)[mode and running the show IP route command.](undefined)[This will show us all configured IP routes on this router.](undefined)[We'll see destination networks, next](undefined)[hop, and metrics for each route.](undefined)[We can then review this table and](undefined)[make any changes to it as needed.](undefined)
            - ### Identifying Devices with the ARP Command 02:11-02:47
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The last command we're going to look at is the ARP command.](undefined)[An ARP table is a table that has a list of all](undefined)[devices this router has communicated with, the IP](undefined)[address of those devices, and the MAC address.](undefined)[Sometimes we might get rogue devices on the](undefined)[network, or devices may be misconfigured.](undefined)[Again, this is a useful tool in seeing which](undefined)[devices we are connecting to and finding them](undefined)[in order to identify misconfigured devices.](undefined)[We'll run the show ARP command.](undefined)[And here, we'll see a list of all the devices this](undefined)[router has communicated with, and their MAC address.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - 
        - **5.2 Dynamic Routing Technologies** 
            - ## 5.2.1 Dynamic Routing Protocols
                - A **dynamic         routing** protocol uses an algorithm and metrics to build and       maintain a routing information base. This database stores information       about the networks to which the router is connected. Where there are       multiple paths, the algorithm and metrics prioritize one over the others.       This information can be shared with the router's neighbors. A learned       route is one that was communicated to a router by another router. A router       can add learned routes from one or more routing protocols to its IP       routing table.
                - ## Topology and Metrics
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The algorithms used for path selection can be categorized according to       the topology and metrics that they use to build and update a routing       information base and prioritize optimal (or least-cost) paths. Most       algorithms are classed as either **distance         vector** or as **link         state**. Some protocols use a hybrid of different methods to       perform path selection more efficiently.
                - For each protocol that it runs, the router maintains a routing       information base of routes discovered by that protocol. These databases       are separate to the IP routing table used to determine the forwarding       path. The routing protocol's database might contain more than one route to       the same destination prefix. In this case, a metric is calculated to       determine which path will be selected for use in the IP routing table. The       path with the lowest cost metric is preferred.
                - The type of algorithm determines which factors are used to calculate the       metric. For example, distance vector protocols use the number of hops to       the destination as the metric. The route with the fewest hops is the       least-cost path and will be selected for use.
                - ## Convergence
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - **Convergence**       is the process whereby routers running dynamic routing algorithms agree on       the network topology. Routers must be capable of adapting to changes such       as newly added networks, router or router interface failures, link       failures, and so on. Routers must be able to communicate changes to other       routers quickly to avoid sinkholes and loops. A sinkhole means that a       packet is discarded without notification back to the source; a loop causes       a packet to be forwarded around the network until its TTL expires.
                - A network where all the routers share the same topology is described as       steady state. The time taken to reach steady state is a measure of a       routing protocol's convergence performance.
                - A flapping interface is one that frequently changes       from online to offline and offline to online. Similarly, route flapping       refers to a router changing the properties of a route it is advertising       quickly and often. Flapping can cause serious convergence problems.
                - ## Autonomous Systems
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - As well as the algorithm used to determine the network topology, routing       protocols can be classified according to the way they deal with       administrative boundaries. A network under the administrative control of a       single owner is referred to as an **autonomous         system (AS)**. An Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP) is one that       identifies routes within an AS. An Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP) is one       that can advertise routes between autonomous systems. An EGP includes a       field to communicate the network's autonomous system ID and allows network       owners to determine whether they can use paths through another       organization's network.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.2.2 Routing Information Protocol
                - The **Routing Information Protocol (RIP)** is a distance vector routing protocol. RIP only considers a single piece of information about the network topology—the next hop router to reach a given network or subnet (vector). It considers only one metric to select the optimal path to a given destination network—the one with the lowest hop count (distance). While RIP is no longer widely deployed, it is useful to review how it works to help to understand the function of more advanced and widely used protocols.
                - RIP sends regular updates (typically every 30 seconds) of its entire routing database to neighboring routers. It can also send triggered updates whenever changes occur. When a router receives an update from a neighbor, it adds unknown routes to its own routing table, increases the hop count by one, and identifies the originator of the update as the next hop to the specified networks.
                - In the following figure, RIP has been used to propagate route information between three routers connected in a chain. Router A learns about networks 10.0.3.0/24 and 10.0.4.0/24 from Router B. It adds one to the hop count metric of these routes. Router B learns about 10.0.1.0/24 from Router A and about 10.0.4.0/24 from Router C. Router A and Router C do not exchange any information directly. The distance vector process by which Router A learns about Router C's networks is often referred to as "routing by rumor."
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/lXvpthCixm_UGa_2njZv1xmeLrietAI0djRgBvd3wC0kUK3YLb89MGVnjFnR8Du80emS5HtHKIjFLR7HmA4nSuDRyVLTwQKQZ0bQYC856HTIa6nhhleY7d7lpGiUOhdh.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The G 0 port of router A is connected to the network, 10.0.1.0 slash 24. The G 1 port router A is connected to the G 0 port of router B via the network, 10.0.2.0 slash 24. The G 1 port of router B is connected to the G 0 port of router C via 10.0.3.0 slash 24. The G 1 port of router C is connected to the network, 10.0.4.0 slash 24. Three tables are provided. Each table has four columns labeled network, interface, metric, and source. Router A routing table infers the following data. 10.0.1.0 slash 24: G 0, 0, and connected. 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 1, 0, and connected. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 1, 1, and R I P. 10.0.4.0 slash 24: G 1, 2, and R I P. Router B routing table infers the following data. 10.0.1.0 slash 24: G 0, 1, and R I P. 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 0, 0, and connected. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 1, 0, and connected. 10.0.4.0 slash 24: G 1, 1, and R I P. Router C routing table infers the following data. 10.0.1.0 slash 24: G 0, 2, and R I P. 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 0, 1, and R I P. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 0, 0, and connected. 10.0.4.0 slash 24: G 1, 0, and connected.
            -  *Routers connected in a series exchanging distance vector path updates.* 
            - The following example illustrates a mesh topology where there are multiple paths between networks. Router A has two possible paths to network 10.0.3.0/24, which it learns from Router B and Router C. It can forward a packet out of its G1 interface over network 10.0.2.0/24, which will take one hop to reach the destination. It could also forward the packet out of G2 and reach the destination via Router C and then Router B. This takes two hops and so is not used as the preferred route.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/V1b3HV4W8azVBXc_4yAfss9R_TdPIi3oPVxhVEf_LF9WXXIH5fsJKQjLaRVwihwRSmkXdH4eaf88xC8N-MVdC43EBkgnFQOhXXcgX-pO3NQ-Ukk11lRRaBDHO0a6MNjs.png)
            - Description
            - The G 0 port of router A is connected to the network, 10.0.1.0 slash 24. The G 1 port router A is connected to the G 0 port of router B via the network, 10.0.2.0 slash 24. The G 1 port of router B is connected to the network 10.0.3.0 slash 24. The G 2 port of router A is connected to the G 0 port of router C via 10.1.4.0 slash 24. The G 1 port of router C is connected to the G 2 port of router B via 10.0.5.0 slash 24. Router A routing table has five columns labeled: network, interface, metric, via, and source. Data are as follows. 10.0.1.0 slash 24: G 0, 0, blank, and connected. 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 1, 0, blank, and connected. 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 2, 2, 10.0.4.0, and R I P. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 1, 1, 10.0.2.0, and R I P. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 2, 2, 10.0.4.0, and R I P. 10.0.4.0 slash 24: G 2, 0, blank, and connected. 10.0.5.0 slash 24: G 1, 2, 10.0.2.0, and R I P. 10.0.5.0 slash 24: G 2, 1, 10.0.4.0, and R I P.
            -  *Routers connected in a mesh topology exchanging distance vector path updates.* 
            - If Router A's G1 link goes down, those entries will be removed from the routing table, and the alternative routes via 10.0.4.0/24 will be selected:
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/SYZbPmxDtUUy0BoiWy7Xv3ZJKlFCKLhsaDsi2LpEFmaWEwOqiudVKvVFo4qXD4NQZCj_8aRB9XMKRk_8f2H-0CPMbEMkarRAw78SPmkw-dA2qUPDSwHWr-ugHGcaJJSa.png)
            - Description
            - The G 0 port of router A is connected to the network, 10.0.1.0 slash 24. The G 1 port router A is connected to the G 0 port of router B via the network, 10.0.2.0 slash 24. G 1 port has a cross mark. The G 1 port of router B is connected to the network 10.0.3.0 slash 24. The G 2 port of router A is connected to the G 0 port of router C via 10.1.4.0 slash 24. The G 1 port of router C is connected to the G 2 port of router B via 10.0.5.0 slash 24. Router A routing table has five columns labeled: network, interface, metric, via, and source. Data are as follows. 10.0.1.0 slash 24: G 0, 0, blank, and connected. (Cross mark) 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 1, 0, blank, and connected. 10.0.2.0 slash 24: G 2, 2, 10.0.4.0, and R I P. (Cross mark) 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 1, 1, 10.0.2.0, and R I P. 10.0.3.0 slash 24: G 2, 2, 10.0.4.0, and R I P. 10.0.4.0 slash 24: G 2, 0, blank, and connected. (Cross mark) 10.0.5.0 slash 24: G 1, 2, 10.0.2.0, and R I P. 10.0.5.0 slash 24: G 2, 1, 10.0.4.0, and R I P.
            -  *Routers connected in a mesh topology exchanging distance vector path updates.* 
            - To help prevent looping, the maximum hop count allowed is 15. Consequently, this limits the maximum size of a RIP network, since networks that have a hop count of 16 or higher are unreachable.
            - ## 5.2.3 Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol
                - The Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (IGRP) was developed by Cisco to       provide a routing protocol for routing within a domain or autonomous       system. Limitations in IGRP, such as lack of support for classless       addressing, led to the development of **Enhanced         IGRP (EIGRP)**. There are versions for IPv4 and IPv6.
                - Like RIP, EIGRP is a distance vector protocol because it relies on       neighboring routers to report paths to remote networks. Unlike RIP, which       is based on a simple hop count metric, EIGRP uses a metric composed of       administrator weighted elements. The two default elements are bandwidth       and delay:
                    - **Bandwidth**—Applies a cost based on the lowest         bandwidth link in the path.
                    - **Delay**—Applies a cost based on the time it takes for         a packet to traverse the link. This metric is most important if the         route is used to carry time-sensitive data, such as voice or video.         Delay is calculated as the cumulative value for all outgoing interfaces         in the path.
                - Where RIP sends periodic updates of its entire routing information base,       EIGRP sends a full update when it first establishes contact with a       neighbor and thereafter only sends updates when there is a topology       change. This is more efficient and less disruptive to large networks,       giving it the best convergence performance in many scenarios. EIGRP does       use regular hello messaging to confirm connectivity with its neighbors.       Unlike RIP, EIGRP maintains a topology table alongside its routing       information base. The topology table is used to prevent loops while also       supporting a greater number of maximum hops than RIP (nominally up to       255).
                - EIGRP is a default IP protocol, which means that it is encapsulated       directly in IP datagrams, rather than using TCP or UDP. EIGRP also uses hello messaging, which confirms connectivity with its neighbors. It is tagged with       the protocol number 88 in the Protocol field of the IP header. Updates are       transmitted using multicast addressing.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.2.4 Open Shortest Path First
                - A distance vector algorithm relies on directly connected neighbors for information about remote networks. By contrast, a link state algorithm allows a router to store the complete network topology and assess the least-cost paths from this topology database.
                - **Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)** is suited to large organizations with multiple redundant paths between networks. Where EIGRP is a flat routing system, OSPF is hierarchical. Networks and their connected hosts and routers within an autonomous system are grouped into OSPF areas. Routers within a given area share the same topological database of the networks they serve. Routers that can connect to multiple areas are known as area border routers. A backbone (always called Area 0) is created by the collection of border routers. This backbone is only visible to the border routers and invisible to the routers within a specific area.
                - In a given area, routers exchange OSPF hello messages, both as a form of a keep-alive packet and in order to acquire neighbors with which to exchange routing information. Neighbors share Link State Advertisement (LSA) updates to build a consistent link state database (LSDB) that represents the network topology of the area. The router applies an algorithm called shortest path first (SPF) to analyze the LSDB and add least-cost, loop free routes to its routing table. This use of a topology table of the whole network to select routes is the key difference between link state and distance vector algorithms.
                - The small, frequent updates used by OSPF lead to more rapid convergence and more efficiently support larger networks. The use of areas to subdivide the network minimizes the amount of routing traffic that must be passed around the network as a whole, further improving convergence performance. However, link state algorithms can be more expensive to implement because they require more CPU and memory resource.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/i0W3mxwuf6eWL1RtSWVE9ovF0Ob0v6H2p6b2axxcjEgzD56HG1j3t2OMcYbuKzhnlVwb4QhTu1mQY2T6BtEGAhY3nm_Gn5zFhcIAusgLXGEH8bXBT4YoNRD5gG2EZcyc.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The O S P F topology shows A S 0 0 2 connected to the A S border router of area 10 in A S 0 0 1. A S 0 0 1 has four areas: area 0, area 10, area 30, and area 20. The backbone router of area 0 is connected to the area border routers of area 0 and area 10, area 0 and area 20, and area 0 and area 30. In area 30, the area border router is connected to three other routers. In area 20, the area border router is connected to three other routers. In area 10, the area border router is connected to the A S border router.
            -  *Typical OSPF topology. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - Messages are sent as multicasts using OSPF's own datagram format. This is tagged as protocol number 89 in the IP datagram's Protocol field. There are various packet types and mechanisms to ensure sequencing and reliable delivery and to check for errors. OSPF also supports plaintext or cryptographic authentication.
            - ## 5.2.5 Border Gateway Protocol
                - The **Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)** is designed to be used between routing domains in a mesh internetwork and as such is used as the routing protocol on the Internet, primarily between ISPs.
                - EIGRP and OSPF are used for communications between routers within a single routing domain, referred to an autonomous system (AS). BGP is primarily used for routing between autonomous systems. An AS is designed to hide the complexity of private networks from the public Internet. If all Internet locations had to be propagated to all Internet routers, the routing tables would become too large to process. Edge routers for each AS exchange only as much network-reachability information as is required to access other autonomous systems (the AS path), rather than networks and hosts within each AS. BGP prioritizes stability and can be slow to converge. Autonomous system numbers (ASN) are allocated to ISPs by IANA via the various regional registries.
                - BGP works with classless network prefixes called Network Layer Reachability Information (NLRI). Path selection is based on multiple metrics, including hop count, weight, local preference, origin, and community.
                - BGP works over TCP on port 179.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.2.6 Route Selection
                - If a router has multiple entries to similar networks in its routing table, it must determine which route to prefer. The first determining factor is that longer prefixes are preferred over shorter ones. This is referred to as longest prefix match. For example, a routing table contains the following two entries:
                - `198.51.100.0/24 g0`
                - `198.51.100.0/28 g1`
                - If the router receives a packet for `198.51.100.1`, the packet will be routed via `g1`, as that has the longer and more specific prefix.
                - Each routing protocol supported by the router can add a single route for any given destination prefix to the routing table. This means that there might be more than one route with an identical length prefix in the routing table. Each routing protocol uses its metric to determine the least-cost path for routes with identical prefix lengths. However, as routing protocols use different methods to calculate the metric, it cannot be used to compare routes from different protocols in the overall IP routing table. Instead, an **administrative distance (AD)** value is used to express the relative trustworthiness of the protocol supplying the route. Default AD values are coded into the router but can be adjusted by the administrator if necessary.
                - This means, for example, that given identical prefix lengths, a static route will be preferred to anything other than directly connected networks and that a route discovered by EIGRP would be preferred to one reported by OSPF. The value of 255 for unknown routes means that they will not be used.
                - Conversely, a static route with a high AD could be defined to function as a backup if a learned route update fails. In normal circumstances, the router will prefer the learned route because it has a lower AD.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
        - **5.3 Network Address Translation** 
            - ## 5.3.1 Edge Routers
                - Edge routers, placed at the network perimeter, are typified by distinguishing external (Internet-facing) and internal interfaces. These routers can perform framing to repackage data from the private LAN frame format to the WAN Internet access frame format. The customer's router is referred to as the customer edge (CE), while the service provider's router is referred to as the provider edge (PE).
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/aoq5IUUyZQb6jI1gYMU1ilAmgAG7Ke9zXK6Cmm0OuRAw-9w0wxHk4a-yMHg7wZPUdULBtH1Z4l2seUy-3-03MIixZtYpXj4sAcxji9Sny8FfhnNEVvYbo-qJaEg3X7XP.png)
                -  *An integrated services router. This type of device combines DSL Internet access with Ethernet switch, Wi-Fi, and VoIP for a "one box" solution for remote sites and branch offices. (Image © 123RF.com.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Routers designed to service medium to large networks are complex and expensive appliances. They feature specialized processors to handle the routing and forwarding processes, and memory to buffer data. Most routers of this class will also support plug-in cards for WAN interfaces. Another important feature is support for different methods of configuring site-to-site virtual private networks (VPNs).
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Cw4rPWcdkUOvLA1XqyS7JswBWar17DnevZSKjjKlm0IVI45VsyQyDORfa4QEdrLZme-a5LwgcRkxiMdNK9MmzKMETTdt9sdwgsVIV7e8wMwy4fK3ypH_dPScmyp5DX2x.png)
                -  *An advanced services router. This type of device provides network edge connectivity over Carrier Ethernet networks. (Image © 123RF.com.)* 
            - ## 5.3.2 Network Address Translation Types
                - An edge router must facilitate communications between the private and public network. To communicate on the Internet, a host must use an address from a public IP range. As these are in short supply for IPv4, various mechanisms have been devised to reduce the need for public addresses. **Network Address Translation (NAT)** is an example of one of these mechanisms.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_nat_n09/v_nat_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_nat_n09/v_nat_n09.jpg)

                - Video Control Resume Play
