            - ### NAT Routing Process 00:00-01:37
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Suppose we have a host on a private network](undefined)[that has an IP address of 192.168.1.10](undefined)[and the private side of the NAT router has an IP address](undefined)[of 192.168.1.1 assigned.](undefined)[This is the default gateway router for the network segment.](undefined)[When this host, 192.168.1.10,](undefined)[tries to request resources from an IP address](undefined)[that's not on this network segment,](undefined)[it automatically knows that it needs to forward](undefined)[that request to the default gateway, which is a NAT router.](undefined)
            - [When the NAT router receives that request,](undefined)[it strips off the source address of the transmission,](undefined)[192.168.1.10, and replaces the source address](undefined)[with its public IP address (137.65.7.2).](undefined)[It then sends it to the public network](undefined)[where the request is routed to the appropriate host](undefined)[on the internet.](undefined)[The host on the public network that receives this request,](undefined)[the recipient of the transmission,](undefined)[doesn't see that request came from 192.168.1.10.](undefined)[Instead, it sees it coming](undefined)[from the NAT router itself, 137.65.7.2.](undefined)
            - [The receiving host responds](undefined)[and sends the requested information back.](undefined)[It sends the response back to 137.65.7.2,](undefined)[so the request returns to the NAT router.](undefined)[The NAT router changes the recipient address](undefined)[of the transmission to the original requesting host](undefined)[on the private network (192.168.1.10)](undefined)[and forwards it through its 192.168.1.1 interface](undefined)[to the host that originally requested the information.](undefined)
            - ### NAT and Port Address Translation (PAT) 01:37-03:10
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The NAT router may have to process thousands](undefined)[of translation requests at any given time.](undefined)[When information is returned from the public network,](undefined)[it keeps track of which request goes to which host](undefined)[on the internal network using Port Address Translation](undefined)[(PAT).](undefined)
            - [When a request is sent, the NAT router sends it](undefined)[from a randomly selected dynamic port](undefined)[on its public IP address.](undefined)[The Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA)](undefined)[has assigned the port range 49,152 to 65,535](undefined)[to be used as dynamic ports.](undefined)[The NAT router picks one of these dynamic ports](undefined)[and forwards the request out on the public network](undefined)[from the selected port.](undefined)
            - [Each request that comes through for address translation](undefined)[is assigned to a different dynamic port.](undefined)[The NAT router keeps a table in memory](undefined)[called the translation table](undefined)[that temporarily maps a particular port](undefined)[to a particular request from a specific host source IP](undefined)[on the private network.](undefined)[That way, when the data returns from the host](undefined)[on the internet,](undefined)[it returns to the port from which it was sent.](undefined)[The NAT router then looks at its translation table](undefined)[and identifies which private host address](undefined)[is currently associated with that port number.](undefined)[It knows that information coming back](undefined)[from the public network](undefined)[needs to be forwarded to the private network](undefined)[to this particular host with this IP address.](undefined)
            - [The NAT router maps the IP address](undefined)[of the originating host to a particular dynamic port](undefined)[and stores it in the translation table.](undefined)[By doing this, it can keep track](undefined)[of which returning information goes to which host](undefined)[on the private network.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - NAT is primarily deployed as a service translating between a private (or local) addressing scheme used by hosts on the LAN and a public (or global) addressing scheme used by an Internet-facing device. To perform this role, NAT is configured on a border device, such as a router, proxy server, or firewall. NAT is not a security mechanism; security is provided by the router/firewall's access control list (ACL).
            - In a static NAT configuration, a 1:1 mapping is made between the private (inside local) network address and the public (inside global) address. If the destination network is using NAT, it is described as having outside global and outside local addressing schemes.
            - Static NAT is useful in scenarios where an inbound connection to a host must be supported. For example, you might position a web server behind a firewall running NAT. The firewall performs 1:1 address translation on the web server's IP address. This means that external hosts do not know the true IP address of the web server, but they can communicate with it successfully.
            - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/m7m42c_FaWfIBf3Fp3Y2ZnQuw6S-Om9841n-3EpQcQzmcKMcf4MthQ67YFQoTUPPE0Fde18UG9E8bscF__JPvmx-cg9A5L6FjwRVS_y2Z1_W_yo8MpCkXL4MbCk20tcD.png)
            - Description
            - The diagram shows three P Cs with network addresses, 10.0.0.101, 10.0.0.102, and 10.0.0.103 connected to the N A T firewall via the inside interface: 10.0.0.101 and 10.0.0.199. The N A T firewall is connected to the Internet via outside interface: 85.234.150.160 and 85.234.150.169. A dashed arrow labeled source address translation: 10.0.0.101 to 85.234.150.160 from the user with the network address, 10.0.0.101 points to the internet. A dashed line labeled destination address translation: 85.234.150.160 to 10.0.0.101 exists between internet and user with the network address, 10.0.0.101.
            -  *Network Address Translation (NAT). (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - Where the NAT device performs forwarding over selected ports only, this can be referred to as port forwarding.
                - A single static mapping is not very useful in most scenarios. Under dynamic NAT, the NAT device exposes a pool of public IP addresses. To support inbound and outbound connections between the private network and the Internet, the NAT service builds a table of public to private address mappings. Each new session creates a new public-private address binding in the table. When the session is ended or times out, the binding is released for use by another host.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/kwHNGDxfERBWQ0cjK1YlMiZjd4i13kkIjW9uNbfOcKRfNFU2sHSontB79JX7HZMf2N1pr3qCsqU2-IL4gdoIDeqjqG79GVSnPenwt6RAhosfZDdyjb4x0lJlKIDyzwA6.png)
            - Description
            - The option, One-to-One from N A T is selected under the tab, Firewall from the left menu. The right pane shows a section titled, Edit N A T 1:1 entry has fields for disabled, interface, type, external network, source or invert, source, destination or invert, and destination.
            -  *Defining a one-to-one NAT rule on an OPNsense router/firewall. This rule maps hosts using a 172.200.0.0/24 addressing scheme to 10.200.0.0/24 addresses. (Screenshot used with permission from OPNsense.)* 
            - ## 5.3.3 Port Address Translation
                - Basic NAT supports multiple simultaneous connections but is still limited by the number of available public IP addresses. Smaller companies may only be allocated a single or small block of addresses by their ISPs. In such cases, a means for multiple private IP addresses to be mapped onto a single public address would be useful. This function is provided by **Port Address Translation (PAT)**. This can be referred to as Network Address Port Translation (NAPT), NAT overloading, one-to-many NAT, many-to-one NAT, or NAT masquerade.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/W6O4T_UjROOYiWiXFlweJ9dpBSVMmU6BJLS5toDYytaZPxxydOajwNO1Jvr-OcU0SCAnHepgWaiYMbhfV2hNwXrIJvbPx1gxq1dVpw9tRIUgcK6Z8CBzkdryaDNsFGZW.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The diagram shows three P Cs with network addresses, 10.0.0.101, 10.0.0.102, and 10.0.0.103 connected to the N A P T firewall with inside local: 10.0.0.1 and inside global: 217.45.253:60103. N A P T firewall is connected to the internet web server with outside global: 77.72.206.10. Text reads as follows. Connect 10.0.0.101:1024 to comptia.org slash help dot htm 77.72.206.10:80. Connect 10.0.0.103:1024 to comptia dot org slash help dot htm 77.72.206.10:80. Open connections for 217.45.253.5:60101 and 217.45.253:60103. An arrow labeled reply to: 217.45.253.5:60101 from internet web server points to N A P T firewall. An arrow labeled index dot htm from: 77.72.206.10:80 from N A P T firewall points to the user with 10.0.0.101. An arrow labeled help dot htm from: 77.72.20.10:80 from N A P T firewall points to the user with 10.0.0.103. An arrow labeled reply to: 217.45.253.5:60103 from internet web server points to N A P T firewall.
            -  *Port Address Translation (PAT). (Images ©123RF.com.)* 
            - PAT works by allocating each new connection an ephemeral Transport layer port ID. For example, say two hosts (10.0.0.101 and 10.0.0.102) initiate a web connection at the same time. The PAT service creates two new port mappings for these requests (10.0.0.101:61101 and 10.0.0.102:61102) in its state table. It then substitutes the private IP for the public IP and forwards the requests to the public Internet. It performs a reverse mapping on any traffic returned using those ports, inserting the original IP address and port number, and forwarding the packets to the internal hosts.
            - 
            - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_nat_n09/d_conf_nat_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_nat_n09/d_conf_nat_n09.jpg)

            - ### Configure NAT 00:00-00:56
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we're going to configure](undefined)[NAT, or Network Address Translation.](undefined)[We're going to allow traffic from our](undefined)[LAN network to go to our DMZ network.](undefined)[The first thing we're going to do is configure NAT to allow](undefined)[Remote Desktop Protocol from this machine here, with the IP](undefined)[address of 10.10.10.10, through the firewall, to PC01.](undefined)
            - [The second thing we'll do is configure NAT to allow us to](undefined)[use SSH from our 10.10.10.10 machine through the firewall](undefined)[to our Kali Linux machine with the IP of 172.16.1.6.](undefined)
            - [The third thing we'll do is modify our Remote Desktop](undefined)[Protocol NAT rule to use a custom port through the LAN.](undefined)[But when it enters the DMZ, it'll use a custom port number.](undefined)[Let's get started.](undefined)
            - ### Configure RDP NAT Rule 00:56-03:31
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I'm at the dashboard of my pfSense security appliance.](undefined)[I want to come down and confirm that](undefined)[I have my three interfaces here.](undefined)[I have my WAN, LAN, and DMZ, and they all look good.](undefined)
            - [We'll go up to Firewall and select NAT from the menu.](undefined)[This takes us to the Port Forward tab by default.](undefined)[We'll go over and click on the Add button with the up arrow.](undefined)[We don't have anything configured yet, so](undefined)[it doesn't matter which button we click on.](undefined)
            - [Okay, so we're doing Remote Desktop Protocol first.](undefined)[We want to configure the interface for the LAN](undefined)[interface, so I'll pick that from the list.](undefined)
            - [Our protocol will be TCP, but I'll click on the list so](undefined)[you can see all the protocols that we can choose from.](undefined)
            - [For the destination, we want to use a LAN address here.](undefined)
            - [For our Destination Port Range, Microsoft](undefined)[Remote Desktop Protocol is listed under MSRDP.](undefined)[I can pick that from the list, and](undefined)[it'll use the default port of 3389.](undefined)[Now if, for some reason, the service isn't](undefined)[listed, we can just put in a custom port number.](undefined)[Even though MSRDP is listed, I could](undefined)[just put in the port here, '3389'.](undefined)
            - [Now for the Redirect Target port.](undefined)[This is where the traffic goes when it leaves the firewall.](undefined)[Let's look at our diagram again.](undefined)[Here, I have PC01.](undefined)[This particular PC is getting its IP via DHCP, so let's jump](undefined)[over to that machine for a minute and confirm the IP address.](undefined)[The preferred method would be to have either](undefined)[static IPs or reserved IPs configured with](undefined)[DHCP, but I like doing things the hard way.](undefined)[I'm on PC01, and I'll open a](undefined)[command prompt and enter 'ipconfig'.](undefined)[My IP is 172.16.1.101.](undefined)[That's the IP I need to enter in my settings.](undefined)[Let's go back and do that now.](undefined)
            - [I'm back here on pfSense, and I'll type in '172.16.1.101'.](undefined)[The target port is going to be MSRDP again.](undefined)[My description will be 'RDP from LAN to PC01'.](undefined)[Now, this next part is really cool.](undefined)[With a lot of devices, when you create a NAT](undefined)[rule, you have to remember to go in and create](undefined)[a rule in the firewall to allow it through.](undefined)[With pfSense, under Filter rule association, you can](undefined)[have it add the associated filter rule automatically.](undefined)[So yes, we certainly like this, and](undefined)[whenever possible, we want to use it.](undefined)[Click Save and then click Apply Changes.](undefined)
            - ### Confirm RDP Rule 03:31-05:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now, just to confirm, let's look and](undefined)[see if our firewall rule was created.](undefined)[I'm sure it's there, but I like to](undefined)[verify things before I rely on them.](undefined)[Go to Firewall and then to Rules.](undefined)[Now make sure you're on the LAN interface.](undefined)[Right down here, at the bottom,](undefined)[is the rule that was just created.](undefined)[It shows the destination IP and port.](undefined)
            - [All right, let's see that this is working with our own eyes.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and see if we can use Remote Desktop Protocol](undefined)[to get from PC02 on the LAN over to PC01, over on the DMZ.](undefined)
            - [I'm actually doing all my work from](undefined)[PC02, so this is the machine on the LAN.](undefined)[Let's open up Remote Desktop and](undefined)[see if we can make a connection.](undefined)[This part can be a little confusing.](undefined)[My LAN doesn't know anything about my DMZ.](undefined)[It doesn't know about the computers](undefined)[there or the IP address scheme.](undefined)[It's a different network.](undefined)[I need to put in the IP address of](undefined)[my LAN gateway, which is 10.10.10.1.](undefined)[When I hit that address, my NAT rule will say,](undefined)["Hey, I need to take this RDP connection, translate](undefined)[it to the 172.16 network, and forward it to PC01."](undefined)[Let's go back to PC01 on the LAN and click on Connect.](undefined)[Getting a prompt for the password is a great sign.](undefined)[I'll type in my password and press Enter.](undefined)
            - [Okay.](undefined)[It took just a few seconds, but I'm](undefined)[connected to PC01 over on the DMZ.](undefined)[My command prompt is still open, so just to be](undefined)[sure, I'll do an ipconfig, and it looks great.](undefined)[If we look up here, the IP is 10.10.10.1.](undefined)[That's the IP address of my pfSense, and](undefined)[it's where this machine thinks the connection](undefined)[is originating from because of NAT.](undefined)
            - ### Configure SSH to Use NAT 05:25-07:07
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's create another NAT rule.](undefined)[Let's go back to pfSense and go to Firewall > NAT.](undefined)
            - [Let's look at the diagram again.](undefined)[This time, I want to use NAT to allow us to use](undefined)[SSH on port 22 from PC02 through the firewall to](undefined)[our Kali Linux machine with the IP of 172.16.1.6.](undefined)[I have the program Putty, and I need to do some command](undefined)[line configurations on this machine occasionally.](undefined)
            - [Back to my pfSense.](undefined)[Let's click on Add.](undefined)[I'm on the NAT > Port Forward > Edit page again.](undefined)[Once again, for Interface, I'll pick LAN.](undefined)[SSH does use TCP, so I'll leave that set to TCP.](undefined)[Destination is the LAN address, just like Remote Desktop.](undefined)[Destination Port Range will be SSH from the list.](undefined)[Remember that the IP for the Kali machine was '172.16.1.6'.](undefined)[Redirect Target Port is also SSH.](undefined)[And for the description, I'll type,](undefined)["Allow SSH from LAN to Kali on DMZ'.](undefined)[Once again, we can have it create the rule for us.](undefined)[Click on Save and then Apply Changes.](undefined)
            - [I want to confirm that my rule is](undefined)[there, just like we did for RDP.](undefined)[Go to Firewall > Rules.](undefined)[Make sure that I'm on the LAN interface.](undefined)[Here, at the bottom, is my SSH rule that](undefined)[was created along with the NAT rule.](undefined)[I have my IP, Port 22, and my description.](undefined)
            - ### Edit NAT for Remote Desktop Protocol 07:07-09:22
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [All right.](undefined)[I've switched back over to NAT > Port Forwarding.](undefined)[The final thing I want to do is edit our](undefined)[Remote Desktop Protocol NAT settings.](undefined)[Under Actions, I'll click on the Edit icon.](undefined)[That takes us to the Edit page.](undefined)
            - [I'll scroll down.](undefined)[Under Destination Port Range, I'm](undefined)[going to change this to a custom port.](undefined)[I'm going to put in 5151 in both places here.](undefined)[Click on Save.](undefined)[Let's go to our diagram, and I'll explain what we're doing.](undefined)
            - [Over on the DMZ, I have a web server,](undefined)[and I also have my Windows 10 machine.](undefined)[I want to get to both of them using Remote Desktop Protocol.](undefined)[But when I enter the IP 10.10.10.1, NAT is](undefined)[configured to send the traffic to PC01 on the DMZ.](undefined)[So, the question is, how can I use](undefined)[RDP to get to a different machine?](undefined)[Well, that's where I can change the port that's](undefined)[hitting the 10.10.10.1 IP address with a custom port.](undefined)[Instead of using the default port](undefined)[of 3389, I'm going to use 5151.](undefined)[So, in this scenario, I'm editing the existing NAT](undefined)[configuration to have traffic go to PC01 on the DMZ.](undefined)
            - [I'm back on PC02.](undefined)[Like I said before, this is the PC that](undefined)[I'm using to configure pfSense from.](undefined)[I'll hit Apply Changes, and our NAT rule has been updated.](undefined)[Before ending the demo, I just want to confirm that this](undefined)[is working and show you how to use a custom port with RDP.](undefined)
            - [I'll open up Remote Desktop Connection again.](undefined)[At the end of the IP address for my pfSence,](undefined)[10.10.10.1, I want to add a colon (:) and](undefined)[follow that with the port number, '5151'.](undefined)[This is called a socket.](undefined)[Some call it a network socket or address socket.](undefined)[Now I'll click Connect, enter in my password,](undefined)[and wait for the connection to complete.](undefined)
            - [We've connected to our PC over on the DMZ once again,](undefined)[but this time using a custom port on the LAN side.](undefined)[I'll do an ipconfig just for the](undefined)[fun of it, and we're good to go.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 09:22-09:41
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we created NAT rules.](undefined)[First, we created a Remote Desktop connection rule.](undefined)[Then we created a NAT rule for SSH.](undefined)[Finally, we modified our Remote Desktop](undefined)[rule to use a custom port on our LAN.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
        - **5.4 Firewalls** 
            - ## 5.4.1 Firewall Uses and Types
                - The basic function of a firewall is traffic filtering. The firewall       processes traffic according to rules. Traffic that does not conform to a       rule is blocked.
                - There are many types of firewalls and many ways of implementing a       firewall. One distinction can be made between firewalls that protect a       whole network (one that is placed inline in the network and inspects all       traffic that passes through) and firewalls that protect a single host only       (one that is installed on the host and inspects only that traffic       addressed to that host). A further distinction can be made about what       parts of a packet a particular firewall technology can inspect and operate       on.
                - ## Packet Filtering Firewalls
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Packet filtering describes the earliest type of firewall. All firewalls       can still perform this basic function. A packet filtering firewall is       configured by specifying rules in a network access control list (ACL).       Each rule defines a specific type of data packet and the appropriate       action to take when a packet matches the rule. An action can be either to       deny (block or drop the packet, and optionally log an event) or to accept       (let the packet pass through the firewall). A packet filtering firewall       works at layer 3 of the OSI model to inspect the headers of IP packets.       This means that rules can be based on the information found in those       headers:
                    - **IP Filtering**—Accepting or denying traffic based on         its source and/or destination IP address.
                    - Protocol ID/type (TCP, UDP, ICMP, routing protocols, and so on).
                    - **Port Filtering/Security**—Accepting or denying a         packet based on source and destination Transport layer port numbers.
                - Port numbers are contained in TCP or UDP headers       (layer 4) rather than the IP datagram header, but packet filtering       firewalls are still almost always described as working at layer 3. They       can inspect only port numbers and not any other layer 4 header       information.
                - ACLs might be designed to control only inbound traffic or both inbound       and outbound traffic. This is also often referred to as "ingress" and       "egress" traffic or filtering. Controlling outbound traffic is useful       because it can block applications that have not been authorized to run on       the network and defeat malware, such as backdoors. Ingress and egress       traffic is filtered using separate ACLs.
                - A packet filtering firewall is stateless. This means that it does not       preserve information about the connection between two hosts. Each packet       is analyzed independently with no record of previously processed packets.       This type of filtering requires the least processing effort, but it can be       vulnerable to attacks that are spread over a sequence of packets. A       stateless firewall can also introduce problems in traffic flow, especially       when some sort of load balancing is being used or when clients or servers       need to make use of dynamically assigned ports.
                - ## Stateful Inspection Firewalls
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A circuit-level stateful inspection firewall addresses these problems by       maintaining stateful information about the session established between two       hosts (including malicious attempts to start a bogus session). Information       about each session is stored in a dynamically updated state table. A       stateful firewall operates at layer 5 (Session) of the OSI model. When a       packet arrives, the firewall checks it to confirm whether it belongs to an       existing connection. If it does not, it applies the ordinary packet       filtering rules to determine whether to allow it. Once the connection has       been allowed, the firewall allows traffic to pass unmonitored, in order to       conserve processing effort.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/K_LcqvSuM0wevl3xsIaMuP-4_D3yN1QKDFbce-azGTth9QA92Exn6plAqkT7aTesnnXv7a74Kji5O_o0ZFjoAP7pl7Ke4fN3436G2vLEF_bAuhsLJsjzUgzpR3L_Smi2.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The window shows two tabs: status and actions. Status is selected.           The data are provided in a table format whose headers read, Int, Dir,           Proto, Source, Nat, destination, state, Nat, rule, and commands.
                    -  *State table in the OPNsense firewall appliance. (Screenshot used           with permission from OPNsense.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.4.2 Firewall Selection and Placement
                - Some types of firewalls are better suited for placement at network or segment borders; others are designed to protect individual hosts. The selection of a network firewall model will largely depend on the volume of traffic it has to process. A single firewall can represent a network bottleneck if it is not able to handle the required traffic volume.
                - An appliance firewall is a stand-alone hardware firewall that performs only the function of a firewall. The functions of the firewall are implemented on the appliance firmware. This is also a type of network-based firewall and monitors all traffic passing into and out of a network segment. This type of appliance could be implemented with routed interfaces or as a layer 2/virtual wire "transparent" firewall.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/sxII7bQHS0BjipBQth8Q64__CKkffyjE3Ti3bwqLTnfz0ZA0qus-Opd2l_FcYfQha3gEn1VZG_SLpQ95OFNGyoF8bEen3tZKGziKLxJr0Hbssd0oOCs6HEXk9FrYjK4g.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The option, Dashboard is selected under the tab, Lobby from the left menu. The center pane shows a section titled, Lobby: Dashboard. It provides data on system information that includes name, versions, updates, C P U type, C P U usage, load average, uptime, current date or time, last config change, and C P U usage. The save settings button is selected. The right pane shows four sections: services, gateways, interfaces, and traffic graph. Gateways and traffic graph sections are expanded.
            -  *Status dashboard for the OPNsense open source security platform. (Screenshot courtesy of OPNsense.)* 
            - A router firewall is similar, except that the functionality is built into the router firmware. Most SOHO Internet router/modems have this type of firewall functionality, though they are typically limited to supporting a single subnet within the home network. An enterprise-class router firewall would be able to support far more sessions than a SOHO one.

            - ### Configure Network Security Appliance Access 00:00-00:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going to secure  access to our](undefined)[LAN and WAN interfaces and configure IP addresses to them.](undefined)[We're also going to work with user accounts on a network](undefined)[security appliance.  We're using a pfSense](undefined)[security  appliance, which has a graphical interface](undefined)[for  configuration that's accessible through a web browser.](undefined)
            - ### Sign In  00:26-00:37
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I'm here at the login screen, and I'll sign](undefined)[in with the default username of 'admin'.](undefined)[For this pfSense device, the default password is 'pfsense'.](undefined)[I'll click Sign In to log on.](undefined)
            - ### Configure LAN Interface 00:37-02:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The first thing I want to do is configure](undefined)[the IP address on my WAN and my LAN.](undefined)[We'll start with the LAN settings.](undefined)[Go up to Interfaces > LAN, and that](undefined)[takes us to the LAN configuration page.](undefined)[If I look here, the IPv4 Configuration](undefined)[Type is set to Static IPv4.](undefined)[DHCP is the other option there.](undefined)[I need to change it to a different IP address.](undefined)[I want to change it to '10.10.10.1', so I'll do that now.](undefined)[Now I want to confirm that my subnet](undefined)[is set to the /24 subnet mask.](undefined)[That's all I need to do on this page.](undefined)[I'll scroll down and click Save.](undefined)
            - [The page refreshes and says that the LAN](undefined)[configuration has changed, and I need to](undefined)[click Apply Changes for it to take effect.](undefined)[It reminds me to change my DHCP](undefined)[server configuration if needed.](undefined)[Since my IP address is in the same subnet range as](undefined)[it was before, my DHCP configuration is still okay.](undefined)[Just make sure the IP you assigned is not in](undefined)[the scope of IPs you've configured to be leased.](undefined)[Click Apply Changes.](undefined)
            - [Now, if you look up at my address,](undefined)[you'll see that it says 10.10.10.254.](undefined)[When I log back in, I need to go to](undefined)[the new address that I configured.](undefined)[I've found that you sometimes get a token error](undefined)[if you just try to log back in, so I'm going to](undefined)[close my web browser and open it back up.](undefined)
            - [Now let's type in '10.10.10.1'](undefined)[to get back to our Sign In page.](undefined)[I'll put my credentials back in here and click Sign In.](undefined)[I'll go back to my LAN interface, and](undefined)[you can see that my changes are there.](undefined)
            - ### Configure WAN Interface 02:15-03:14
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now let's configure our WAN settings.](undefined)[Once again, I'll go to Interfaces.](undefined)[But this time, I'll select WAN from the list.](undefined)[Right now, I'm getting an IP for my WAN interface via DHCP.](undefined)[I want to configure this with a static IP, so I'll change](undefined)[that to Static IPv4 from our IPv4 configuration type.](undefined)
            - [Now, for an IP address, I'll give it '192.168.25.254'.](undefined)[Right away, you might be saying, "Hey,](undefined)[that's a Class C Private IP address."](undefined)[If so, you're absolutely correct.](undefined)[I'm on a test network that's connected to my](undefined)[regular network, which is using the 192.168 scheme.](undefined)[I need to set my subnet mask to /24 here.](undefined)[For this demo, I'm not going to configure DHCP version 6.](undefined)[I'm going to leave everything else set to](undefined)[the defaults and click Save to continue.](undefined)
            - [I get a few reminders, just like](undefined)[we did with the LAN configuration.](undefined)[I'll click Apply Changes.](undefined)
            - ### Configure WAN DNS 03:14-04:46
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now I want to configure the DNS that my WAN will use.](undefined)[A lot of devices will have those settings](undefined)[right here, with the WAN settings.](undefined)[But with this device, we need to](undefined)[go up to System > General Setup.](undefined)
            - [I'm going to add a few DNS servers here.](undefined)[But first, let's take a look at the](undefined)[current status of our WAN interface.](undefined)[To do that, I'll go to Status > Interfaces.](undefined)[Here, we see that my DNS server is](undefined)[using the home address of 127.0.0.1.](undefined)[IPv4 network standards reserve the entire address block](undefined)[127.0.0.0/8 for loopback purposes, so this is normal.](undefined)[I want to change those to a different DNS,](undefined)[so let's go back to System > General Setup.](undefined)[Beneath DNS Server Settings, I'll put](undefined)[in the Google DNS IP of '8.8.8.8'.](undefined)[Now I'll click the Add DNS Server button, and this](undefined)[time, I'll put in the other Google DNS IP of '8.8.4.4'.](undefined)
            - [I'm going to check this box, Disable DNS Forwarder.](undefined)[For this demo, I don't want my local host,](undefined)[127.0.0.1, to be used as my first DNS server.](undefined)[Checking the box will remove the local host DNS.](undefined)[I'm only doing this to demonstrate](undefined)[what happens when you check the box.](undefined)[I'll scroll down to save these settings.](undefined)
            - [Now let's look at the status of our interfaces.](undefined)[We can see our DNS settings are](undefined)[now set to the Google DNS servers.](undefined)
            - [That wraps up configuring IP interfaces.](undefined)
            - ### Add User 04:46-06:07
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now let's add an additional user to our pfSense appliance.](undefined)[To add a user, we need to go up to System > User Manager.](undefined)[You can see here that we have just one user, the admin user.](undefined)[It's good practice not to use the](undefined)[admin account for normal maintenance.](undefined)[You usually want to create a separate account for each](undefined)[person that might mange the device, collect logs, and so on.](undefined)[We can also track the logon and logoff events](undefined)[of the users in case something happens.](undefined)
            - [I'll add the user by going over](undefined)[and clicking the Add User button.](undefined)[I'll enter in a username of 'Rachel McGaffey'.](undefined)[I'll give Rachel a password, and I'll put](undefined)[the password in a second time to confirm it.](undefined)[Now, under Full Name, I'll enter in 'Rachel McGaffey'.](undefined)[That's really all that's required.](undefined)[But if I wanted to, I could check this box](undefined)[to customize the GUI for Rachel's account.](undefined)[Let's take a look at some of those options.](undefined)
            - [After I check the box, I get some more options.](undefined)[I can change the theme for her](undefined)[account from this dropdown list.](undefined)[We can change the top navigation of the page here.](undefined)[We can change how the hostname appears in the](undefined)[menu and change the amount of dashboard columns.](undefined)[If we want things like WAN and LAN to be](undefined)[sorted alphabetically, we can change that too.](undefined)[If we have groups set up, we](undefined)[can change the memberships here.](undefined)[But we won't make any changes to these right now.](undefined)[Let's come down and click Save.](undefined)
            - ### Session Timeout 06:07-06:43
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [One thing we don't want to happen with our](undefined)[security appliance is have someone logged](undefined)[in to it make configuration changes.](undefined)[If a user gets called away from his or her](undefined)[desk and doesn't sign out, anyone can sit down](undefined)[at their computer and start messing around.](undefined)[To mitigate this risk, we want to configure Session Timeout.](undefined)[I'll click on the Settings tab.](undefined)[Under Session Timeout, I'm going](undefined)[to change this to 10 minutes.](undefined)[That is actually a long time, and you might](undefined)[want to set this to a lower amount of time.](undefined)[Having no timeout is a huge security risk.](undefined)[You'll have to judge your circumstances and](undefined)[decide what session timeout settings are best.](undefined)
            - ### Change Default Admin Password 06:43-07:27
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The last thing I want to do in this demo](undefined)[is change the default admin password.](undefined)[Default passwords for devices are](undefined)[easy to find with a simple web search.](undefined)[It's such a problem, some US states and other](undefined)[governments have forced many tech manufacturers to](undefined)[make the devices they produce have random passwords.](undefined)[Even if your device comes with a random password already](undefined)[configured, those passwords are often printed on the](undefined)[device somewhere, so it's always recommended that you](undefined)[change it that should be the very first thing you do.](undefined)
            - [To change this password for the admin](undefined)[account, I'll click on the edit icon here.](undefined)[I'll type in my new password.](undefined)[Now I'll type it a second time to confirm it.](undefined)[Come down and click Save, and that's all there is to it.](undefined)
            - ### Summary  07:27-07:50

We](undefined)[configured the LAN and WAN IP addresses.](undefined)[We changed the WAN DNS.](undefined)[We created a second account on the device.](undefined)[We configured a session timeout.](undefined)[We ended the demo by changing the default  admin](undefined)[password to something more secure.](undefined)

        - **5.5 Enterprise Network Topologies** 
            - ## 5.5.1 Hybrid Topology
                - The network topology establishes how nodes are physically and logically connected. Recall that the basic topologies are as follows:
                    - Point to point is a one-to-one link between two hosts only.
                    - Star is one-to-many links where each node relies on a central forwarding device to establish paths to other nodes.
                    - Mesh is many-to-many links that depend on each node having multiple interfaces and cable paths to other nodes.
                - These basic topologies do not always support network requirements. Often, a more complex hybrid topology is required. A **hybrid topology** is anything that uses a mixture of point-to-point, star, and mesh physical and/or logical topologies. On modern networks, hybrid topologies are often used to implement redundancy and fault tolerance or to connect sites in WANs and in enterprise campus networks:
                    - **Hierarchical star**—Corporate networks are often designed in a hierarchy, also known as a tree topology. This can be combined with a star topology to implement each node in the overall tree. The links between nodes in the tree are referred to as backbones or trunks because they aggregate and distribute traffic from multiple different areas of the network.
                    - **Hierarchical Star-mesh**—Alternatively, nodes at the top of the hierarchy can be configured in a partial or full mesh for redundancy. Switches or routers lower in the hierarchy establish star topologies that connect end systems to the network.
                - 
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/W4anTIlzRatnNM8BfVmgzmKh8sYPcInITcmf80MaPh55mKQL6cdEPp5sgOrmRQDSmDHWhhMPhD4re_MEBsHi3enRoUsbi_Nz-RbZM9J1kROEA2Fpwzd3f8k0qo-Z7gw-.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1.A hierarchical topology establishes parent slash child relationship between layers in the hierarchy.
            - 2. Hierarchical designs might use hybrid topologies. For example, the topology of the parent layer in this network is a mesh, establishing multiple paths in the network core for redundancy.
            - 3.Each child network is implemented as a star, using less cabling to connect numerous leaf nodes.
            -  *Hierarchical hybrid topology example where a mesh network distributes traffic between multiple star networks.* 
                - **Star of stars**—A WAN might be configured as a hub and spoke between a central office and branch offices, with each site implementing a star topology to connect end systems. This is also referred to as a snowflake topology.
            - ## 5.5.2 Three-Tiered Network Hierarchy
                - A hierarchical model breaks down a large and complex network design into       smaller sections based on the functions performed. Each function can be       assessed by network designers to identify the most efficient hardware and       software to use to implement it.
                - As a practical example of this type of model, many corporate office       networks follow Cisco's design principles for a **three-tiered         hierarchy**: access, distribution, and core.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Z3Rwd-In3xpbZqDdA7ujmSjIyPeOkk3JrxImZj51wpQQQojYkkIbuZURQjE-QCkZqslS1VOTanFP_FzI5XdfsGLW81gLlNUfvy31R8Udk3OPEpqbL8jRPoJMuj4_bkSE.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The diagram shows three layers labeled core, distribution, and           access. Two W A N router or firewalls are connected to the internet.           Each W A N router or firewall is connected to the same pair of layer 3           switches in the core layer. The firewalls are interconnected and the           layer 3 switches are also interconnected. Each layer 3 switch in the           core layer is connected to the same four layer 3 switches via 40 G b p           s in the distribution layer. The region till this represents routing.           The first and second switches are interconnected. The third and fourth           switches are interconnected. Each layer 3 switch in the distribution           layer is connected to the same pair of workgroup switches in the           access layer via 10 G b p s. Each workgroup switch is connected to           three hosts via 1 G b p s. The region below the distribution layer           represents switching.
                    -  *Core, distribution, and access layers in three-tiered network           architecture. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - ## Access/Edge Layer
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The **access         or edge layer** allows end user devices, such as computers,       printers, and smartphones to connect to the network. The access layer is       implemented for each site using structured cabling and wall ports for       wired access and access points for wireless access. Both are ultimately       connected to workgroup switches. Switches deployed to serve the access       layer might also be referred to as LAN switches or data switches. End       systems connect to switches in the access/edge layer in a star topology.       There are no direct links between the access switches.
                - ## Distribution/Aggregation Layer
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The **distribution         or aggregation layer** provides fault-tolerant interconnections       between different access blocks and either the core or other distribution       blocks. Each access switch has full or partial mesh links to each router       or layer 3 switch in its distribution layer block. The distribution layer       is often used to implement traffic policies, such as routing boundaries,       filtering, or quality of service (QoS).
                - The **layer           3 capable switches** used to implement the       distribution/aggregation layer have different capabilities to the layer 2       workgroup switches used in the access tier. Rather than 1 Gbps access port       and 10 Gbps uplink ports, as would be typical of a workgroup switch, basic       interfaces on an aggregation switch would be 10 Gbps and uplink/backbone       ports would be 40 Gbps (or possibly 40 Gbps/100 Gbps). Layer 3 switches       work on the principle of "route once, switch many," which means that once       a route is discovered, it is cached with the destination MAC address, and       subsequent communications are switched without invoking the routing       lookup. While a router uses a generic processor and firmware to process       incoming packets, a layer 3 switch uses an **application-specific         integrated circuit (ASIC)**. This can have an impact on the       relative performance of the two types of devices. Layer 3 switches can be       far faster, but they are not always as flexible. Layer 3 switches cannot       usually perform WAN routing and work with interior routing protocols only.       Often layer 3 switches support Ethernet only.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Gy3NbeJfpt98mfGKppKMO1V-RUmE8HvFehmkHH3gs3x2TJtwocp5bqBJto09MTXppOurWw-e1EzQ2mf6Ok1gKM6xw6G8wE_J2h9QLdvwmRyzmMrx1tYBMcecOhmLWxxI.png)
                -  *An example of a core/distribution switch. (Image © 123RF.com.)* 
                    - ## Core Layer
                        - [Size]();-[H2]()
                    - The **core         layer** provides a highly available network backbone. Devices such       as client and server computers should not be attached directly to the       core. Its purpose should be kept simple: provide redundant traffic paths       for data to continue to flow around the access and distribution layers of       the network. Routers or layer 3 switches in the core layer establish a       full mesh topology with switches in distribution layer blocks.
                    - ## Collapsed Core
                        - [Size]();-[H2]()
                    - Medium-sized networks might not need separate core and distribution       layers. In a two-tier or **collapsed           core** model, a monolithic core layer is implemented as       a full mesh. This is impractical if there are large numbers of core       switche *s* , making the design less scalable *.* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
        - **5.6 Virtual LANs** 
            - ## 5.6.1 Virtual LANs and Subnets
                - Modern Ethernet networks are built using switches. In its default configuration, every port on a switch will be in the same local segment or, put another way, in the same broadcast domain. At the Network layer, this group is identified as either an IP network or as a subnet within an IP network. Any host within a broadcast domain can contact any other host using the same logical addressing scheme (IP subnet) and by hardware/MAC addressing.
                - If too many hosts are attached to the same switch, broadcast traffic can become excessive and reduce performance. At layer 2, **virtual LANs (VLANs)** are a means of addressing this issue. Each interface on a managed switch can be assigned a VLAN ID. Using VLANs means that different groups of computers on the same cabling and attached to the same switch(es) can appear to be in separate LAN segments. Each VLAN is a separate broadcast domain.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_vlan_overview_n09/v_vlan_overview_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_vlan_overview_n09/v_vlan_overview_n09.jpg)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment
            - 
            - 00:001. Default Broadcast Domain in Layer Two Switches
            - CC
            - 1.0x
            - Volume Control
            - Interactive ScriptTheater ModeFull Screen
                - Copy Video Transcript
                - Print Video Transcript
            - ## Interactive Script
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - close interactive script
            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Default Broadcast Domain in Layer Two Switches 00:00-00:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [By default, every port on a layer two](undefined)[switch belongs to the same broadcast domain.](undefined)[This means if one host on this LAN sends a packet to](undefined)[the switch, the switch replicates or broadcasts the](undefined)[packet to all the other active ports on the switch.](undefined)
            - ### Creating VLANs 00:15-00:36
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To create VLANs you need to assign individual](undefined)[ports on a switch to the broadcast domain you want.](undefined)[Let's keep it simple and say that you want a VLAN for the](undefined)[sales department and a VLAN for the engineering department.](undefined)[You can create a VLAN for each department by assigning](undefined)[the hosts in the sales department to VLAN two and the](undefined)[hosts in the engineering department to VLAN 20.](undefined)
            - ### Isolation and Communication Between VLANs 00:36-01:01
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [This isolates each department's hosts to](undefined)[their own virtual network, meaning they](undefined)[won't see the traffic from the other's VLAN.](undefined)[Each VLAN will also have their own Mac address table in](undefined)[this case, but we'll discuss that a little bit later.](undefined)[Now, if a host in the sales department tries to](undefined)[send a packet to an IP address on the engineering](undefined)[subnet, it won't work because communication](undefined)[between VLANs can only happen on layer three.](undefined)
            - ### Routing Between VLANs 01:01-01:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To get around this limitation, you could add a](undefined)[router to one of the switches ports and](undefined)[configure it to route data between the subnets.](undefined)[Or you could use a layer three switch which](undefined)[provides both switching and routing functions,](undefined)[then the routing would occur internally.](undefined)
            - ### Expanding VLANs Across Switches 01:15-01:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [OK, now let's expand our example a bit.](undefined)[Imagine you also have members of the sales](undefined)[department whose offices aren't physically](undefined)[close to the other sales team members.](undefined)[In this case, these Members would be connected](undefined)[to a different physical switch, but they could](undefined)[still be configured to be part of VLAN 2.](undefined)
            - ### Connecting Different VLANs 01:32-01:50
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The same is true if you had members of the engineering](undefined)[department connected to a different switch.](undefined)[In the first group of engineers, the physical](undefined)[switches can be connected through their glance.](undefined)[To do this, you'd configure the second sales team's ports](undefined)[to VLAN two and second group of engineers' ports to VLAN 20.](undefined)
            - ### Data Transmission Between VLANs 01:50-02:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [For example, let's say that Jeff in sales sends data](undefined)[to Kim in engineering, the packet would go to the](undefined)[VLAN 2 port that Jeff's computer is connected to.](undefined)[The switch would then forward the packet to the](undefined)[router, which would then forward the packet to VLAN 20.](undefined)
            - ### Trunk Porting Solution 02:23-02:51
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [This becomes very cumbersome if you have a lot of VLANs.](undefined)[The solution to this is trunk porting a trunk port](undefined)[is a port configuration that allows multiple VLANs.](undefined)[In this case both VLAN 2 and VLAN](undefined)[20 to connect through a single port.](undefined)[It keeps each VLAN's traffic in its own lane trunk.](undefined)[Ports are also called tagged ports.](undefined)[The ports that allow traffic from only one](undefined)[VLAN are called access or untagged ports.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - At layer 3, subnetting is the process of logically dividing an IP network into smaller subnetworks (subnets), with each subnet having a unique address. A subnetting scheme can be applied to represent the VLAN design in the layer 3 topology.
            - For example, the following subnet design allocates separate subnets (`10.0.1.0` and `10.0.2.0`) for the two VLANs configured on Switch A and for the serial WAN links configured between Router A and Routers B and C (`10.0.3.0` and `10.0.4.0`). Routers B and C also have a subnet each for their local networks (`10.0.5.0` and `10.0.6.0`).
            - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/YP5Ncv6X5c5b-q9GdJSl9ArF5CedMkx4R1gIzpd_kqkKrO0WB4J1jt99BNdAEMXGDTryGv09ILnyEe4TUhLqjlLMHTlz94mLJLIIuqKe1cKEKAj_YXXWkbkaUKtvzA-J.png)
            - Description
            - The diagram shows switch A connected to three V L A N 10s via the I P address slash subnet mask, 10.0.1.0 slash 24. Switch A is also connected to three V L A N 20s via 10.0.2.0 slash 24. Switch A is connected to router A. Router A is connected to router B via 10.0.3.0 slash 24. Router A is connected to router C via 10.0.4.0 slash 24. Router B is connected to switch B via 10.0.5.0 slash 24. Router C is connected to switch C via 10.0.6.0 slash 24.
            -  *Subnet design. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - Apart from breaking up broadcast domains, VLANs and subnets can be used to achieve other network design goals:
                - Many organizations have more than one site with WAN links between them. The WAN link normally forms a separate subnet.
                - It is useful to divide a network into logically distinct zones for security and administrative control. VLANs isolate a group of hosts, allowing incoming and outgoing traffic for the group to easily be filtered at the router.
                - Some hosts and appliances have similar performance characteristics. VLANs and subnets can be used to group these hosts and optimize their network performance more easily.
            - ## 5.6.2 Virtual LAN IDs and Membership
                - Implementing VLANs can reduce broadcast traffic when a network has expanded beyond a certain number of hosts or users. As well as reducing the impact of broadcast traffic, from a security point of view, each VLAN can represent a separate zone. VLANs are also used to separate nodes based on traffic type and the need for quality of service. For example, it is commonplace to put all VoIP handsets on a voice VLAN to minimize interference coming from nodes that are sending email or downloading large files on the same network. The switches and routers can then be configured to give the VoIP VLAN priority over VLANs that contain ordinary client PCs.
                - VLANs are configured on switches. The VLAN with ID 1 is referred to as the default VLAN. Unless configured differently, all ports on a switch default to being in VLAN 1.
                - The simplest means of assigning a node to a VLAN is by configuring the port interface on the switch with a VLAN ID in the range 2 to 4094. For example, from the switch management interface, ports 1 through 10 could be configured as a VLAN with the ID 10 and ports 11 through 20 could be assigned to VLAN 20. Host A connected to port 2 would be in VLAN 10, and Host B connected to port 12 would be in VLAN 20. Host A and Host B would not be able to communicate directly, even though they are connected to the same switch. Each VLAN is typically configured with its own subnet address and IP address range. Communications between VLANs must go through an IP router or layer 3 capable switch.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/p_2rFeAKK2CpweLDvFU-CZ0qshAiczbxjjeDyx34h-u8yz9p6YTIP5b5sBD9u64jgb88HqhXkQ72VmHddwFRglOfr8kB3GJxeS22FLk4ILUDqF0SkpHS82aXQ37i6LXM.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The output has two tables. The first table provides data on V L A N name, status, and ports. The second table provides data on V L A N, type, S A I D, M T U, parent, ring number, bridge number, stp, bridge mode, trans 1, and trans 2.
            -  *Cisco switch showing port 1 assigned to VLAN 111, ports 2–11 in VLAN 112, and so on. (Image © and Courtesy of Cisco Systems, Inc. Unauthorized use not permitted.)* 
            - VLANs 2–1001 are referred to as the normal range. VLANs 1002–1005 are reserved. Extended VLAN IDs (1006–4094) are not available on older switch OS versions.
                - A VLAN database is the list of VLANs configured on a switch. A VLAN is created using the following commands from global configuration mode:
                - `vlan 16`
                - `name VLAN16`
                - The following commands put port 1-13 into VLAN 16:
                - `interface range gigabitEthernet 0/1-13`
                - `switchport access vlan 16`
                - Do note that on Cisco switches, there is an explicit vlan database command. This invokes a legacy configuration mode that is now deprecated. VLAN database mode only supports the normal range 2–1001.
                - The `show vlan` command reports the VLAN IDs configured on the switch, plus the ports assigned to them. You can use `no vlan` to delete a VLAN.
                - 
                -

            - ### Intro 00:00-00:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [When you configure a switch out of the box,](undefined)[you'll most likely have to set up an IP and some](undefined)[basic settings so that the switch will be useable.](undefined)[Granted, a DHCP IP address can be left as-is, but in most](undefined)[cases, you don't want to have the IP address change on you.](undefined)[A DHCP reservation or a static address can be](undefined)[set, depending on how your network is configured.](undefined)
            - ### Find the Switch  00:25-01:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Locating your switch could be troublesome](undefined)[if you don't know where to start.](undefined)[If your network is already in place, DHCP](undefined)[IP addressing is most likely present.](undefined)[You should be able to hook up your switch](undefined)[and find the address it was assigned.](undefined)[However, if DHCP isn't present, connecting to](undefined)[the switch via console port may be required.](undefined)[In this demo, we're going to find our switch's DHCP address.](undefined)[We happen to know our MAC address](undefined)[ends in 28:A5 and the vendor is CISCO.](undefined)[Let's launch Angry IP Scanner.](undefined)[First, I am going to modify the fetchers so that the](undefined)[MAC address and MAC vendor are included with my search.](undefined)[The IP range is automatically set to the range of the](undefined)[computer I'm using, so I'm just going to click 'Start'.](undefined)[Once this is complete, we'll be able](undefined)[to see how many hosts are alive.](undefined)[If I scroll down, you can see here I have a](undefined)[device that's CISCO branded and has the same](undefined)[ending MAC address we were looking for.](undefined)[Most likely, this is the correct device.](undefined)[So let's try to access this IP in the web browser.](undefined)
            - ### Set the IP  01:26-02:31
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A factory default switch will require you to](undefined)[set up a new user upon login for the first time.](undefined)[We're just going to use 'ciscoAdmin' and](undefined)[then put in a password and confirm it.](undefined)[Once this is set, we can navigate to the menu of the switch.](undefined)[Currently the IP address of the switch is](undefined)[set to DHCP, which means it could change.](undefined)[Let's change it to static by going to 'Administration',](undefined)['Management Interface', and 'IPv4 Interface'.](undefined)[You can see it's set to Dynamic, which is DHCP.](undefined)[When 'Static' is selected, the grayed area](undefined)[goes away and we're able to modify the address.](undefined)[Since I know my DHCP range is 192.168.30.100](undefined)[to .130, I'll set it to an address outside](undefined)[that range, such as 192.168.30.90.](undefined)[The Netmask and Gateway are set correctly, as that](undefined)[information was already obtained when it was set to Dynamic.](undefined)[Let's apply this setting to the running config.](undefined)[Once this is changed, you'll notice it logs you out of](undefined)[the switch and automatically switches to the newly set IP.](undefined)[We're going to log in again](undefined)[because we're not quite done yet.](undefined)
            - ### Default VLAN 02:31-03:02
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Normally, Cisco and some other](undefined)[brands set the default VLAN to 1.](undefined)[For a basic setup, this will work.](undefined)[However, using VLAN 1 as your main VLAN isn't very secure.](undefined)[Most people know that VLAN 1 is the default VLAN,](undefined)[like a default username and a password on a router.](undefined)[To change our default VLAN, we must go to 'VLAN](undefined)[Management' and then 'Default VLAN Settings'.](undefined)[Just for demo purposes, we're going to switch this to '2'.](undefined)[This number can be anything between](undefined)[1-4094, depending on what the user decides.](undefined)[Next, we'll just click 'Apply'.](undefined)
            - ### Save Changes 03:02-03:47
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now that our settings are set up for the IP](undefined)[and the default VLAN, let's save the config.](undefined)[An easy way is to click on the flashing red 'Save'.](undefined)[We want to make sure our source is the running config](undefined)[while the destination is always the startup config.](undefined)[When we apply and click 'OK', it](undefined)[will save to the startup config.](undefined)[For specific changes to happen,](undefined)[the switch requires a reboot.](undefined)[Under the 'Administration' tab,](undefined)[there will be an option for reboot.](undefined)[Since we're ready to reboot, I'm](undefined)[going to click this now and 'OK'.](undefined)[We're going to pause the recording](undefined)[since it takes a while to reboot.](undefined)
            - [Alright, our switch is online, so](undefined)[let's try and see if we can log in.](undefined)[It looks good and we're back into the switch.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 03:47-03:59
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we described how to search for a](undefined)[switch, set a static IP address, and change the](undefined)[default VLAN to something other than VLAN 1.](undefined)

            - ### Intro 00:00-00:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A VLAN, which stands for virtual local area](undefined)[network, is a broadcast domain that's isolated.](undefined)[VLANS are created inside of a](undefined)[switch, router, or even a hypervisor.](undefined)[Normally, VLANs can't talk to each other unless](undefined)[routing rules are set up to allow them to do so.](undefined)
            - ### Create VLANs 00:23-01:17
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In order to create VLANs on this switch, we need](undefined)[to go to 'VLAN Management' and 'VLAN Settings'.](undefined)[Next, we're going to click 'Add', set the VLAN](undefined)[ID to '30', and set the VLAN Name to 'Network'.](undefined)[We're going to do the same thing for two other](undefined)[VLANS. VLAN 40, which will be for cameras, and](undefined)[VLAN 50, which is for IOT devices.](undefined)[When we're done there, we should](undefined)[have VLANs 30, 40, and 50 set.](undefined)[A common thing that's done to keep things simple](undefined)[is to match up the IP scheme with the VLAN number.](undefined)[For example, our switch IP address is 192.168.30.90,](undefined)[and our main network is on the VLAN ID of 30.](undefined)
            - ### Change Port Mode 01:17-02:17
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now that we have our VLANS set, we need](undefined)[to prep our ports for our VLAN 30 network.](undefined)[As we go to 'Interface Settings', every](undefined)[port on the switch is set to Trunk.](undefined)[The switch will function and work correctly this way.](undefined)[But we don't need Trunk mode on everything.](undefined)[First, I'm going to select 'Port 1' and click 'Edit'.](undefined)[Next, we're going to change 'Trunk' to 'Access',](undefined)[click 'Apply', and close once this is done.](undefined)[We want to make sure 'Port 1' is still selected](undefined)[and go to the bottom to 'Copy Settings'.](undefined)[The copy tool saves time so that you don't](undefined)[have to individually change each port.](undefined)[Port 1 is our copy port, so we need](undefined)[to copy these changes to '2-26'.](undefined)[In our case, 27 and 28 are reserved for uplink ports.](undefined)[When we click 'Apply', ports 1-26](undefined)[will now be access-only ports.](undefined)[This port mode allows only one VLAN](undefined)[on it, and that's what we want.](undefined)
            - ### Apply VLAN to Ports 02:17-03:17
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To properly tie a VLAN to a port,](undefined)[we must create a membership.](undefined)[Let's select 'Port to VLAN'.](undefined)[The filter needs to be changed to](undefined)[the VLAN in question, which is '30'.](undefined)[Selecting 'Go' applies the filter and](undefined)[allows us to adjust the membership type.](undefined)[All ports will say Excluded, which means](undefined)[it's not associated with that VLAN.](undefined)[For ports 1-26, we need to change this to 'Untagged'.](undefined)[We don't want to select Tagged for this](undefined)[VLAN because network frames from a computer](undefined)[typically don't have VLAN tags on them.](undefined)[We're going to fast forward here](undefined)[since this isn't a quick copy button.](undefined)[Great!](undefined)[All of our ports are set to Untagged,](undefined)[so all we need to do is click 'Apply'.](undefined)[Now when we look at the 'Port VLAN](undefined)[Membership', ports 1-26 have only VLAN 30.](undefined)
            - [To save our changes, we're going to](undefined)[select the flashing red 'Save' button.](undefined)[Now, apply the running config to the startup](undefined)[config, and then click 'OK' on the pop-up window.](undefined)[Simple as that.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 03:17-03:27
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we discussed how to create a VLAN, change our](undefined)[port mode to Access, and apply a VLAN to multiple ports.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll

            - ### Configure Switch IP and VLAN - CLI 00:00-00:31
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [When you configure a switch out of the box,](undefined)[you'll most likely want to set up an IP address](undefined)[and some basic settings before using it.](undefined)[Granted, a DHCP IP address can be left as-is.](undefined)[But in most cases, you want to set](undefined)[a static IP address on the device.](undefined)[A DHCP reservation or static address can be set](undefined)[depending on how your network is configured.](undefined)[In our case, we're going to configure the switch](undefined)[as if we're connected to the console port.](undefined)
            - ### Default VLAN 00:31-01:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Normally, Cisco and other brands set the default VLAN to 1.](undefined)[For a basic setup, this works.](undefined)[But using VLAN 1 as your main VLAN isn't very secure.](undefined)[Most people know that VLAN 1 is the default VLAN,](undefined)[like a default username and password on a router.](undefined)[To see which VLANs are configured, type 'show vlan'.](undefined)[We can see we only have VLAN 1 set up,](undefined)[so we'll need to create another one.](undefined)[First, we need to enter config mode by typing 'config t'.](undefined)[Typing 'int vlan 13' will create the VLAN](undefined)[and also enter the mode to configure it.](undefined)[Next, we should name it something,](undefined)[so let's type 'name network'.](undefined)[This way we know it's our main network VLAN.](undefined)[The 'end' command gets us out of config mode.](undefined)[Let's check or VLAN list with the 'show vlan' command.](undefined)[Now we have 1 and 13 listed for VLANs.](undefined)[That's great.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Ports 01:24-02:10
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now that our VLAN is there, we need to](undefined)[configure the ports to use that VLAN.](undefined)[Enter config mode with 'config t'.](undefined)[This time, we're going to change settings](undefined)[on a range of ports with 'int range gi1-27'.](undefined)[These ports will only be used for](undefined)[regular computer access on our network.](undefined)[But this might not be the same on your network.](undefined)[Since only one VLAN needs to be associated with](undefined)[this type of use, we need to switch the port to](undefined)[access mode with 'switchport mode access'.](undefined)[Now we can set the untagged VLAN of 13 on](undefined)[these ports with 'switchport access vlan 13'.](undefined)[Let's type 'end' to get out of config mode and then 'show](undefined)[vlan' to see which ports are associated with VLAN 13.](undefined)[Awesome!](undefined)[Now ports 1 through 27 are only on VLAN 13.](undefined)
            - ### Uplink Port 02:10-02:59
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Our regular access ports are now configured, but](undefined)[we still have one port left as our uplink port.](undefined)[Normally, uplink ports are trunk ports](undefined)[that allow for multiple VLANs on a port.](undefined)[This is how tagged VLAN traffic is](undefined)[received between switches, for example.](undefined)[First, we need to enter config mode with 'config](undefined)[t' and then go to interface 28 with 'int gi28'.](undefined)[Config changes are now only specific to port 28.](undefined)[The 'switchport mode trunk' command](undefined)[sets the port to a trunk port.](undefined)[Finally, using 'switchport trunk allowed vlan add](undefined)[13' adds a tagged VLAN of 13 to this switch port.](undefined)[Let's type 'end' and then 'show](undefined)[vlan' to see our associated ports.](undefined)[Now you can see gi28 has a tagged VLAN of 13.](undefined)
            - ### Set IP Address  02:59-04:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [It's a good idea that you have your VLAN settings in place](undefined)[before switching the default IP address to a different VLAN.](undefined)[Right now, our switch has a DHCP address.](undefined)[But we don't want to keep that address, as it could change.](undefined)[A DHCP reservation or static are two methods](undefined)[to prevent this address from changing.](undefined)[Using 'show ip interface' shows](undefined)[the currently set IP address.](undefined)[We'll disregard the mismatch alert message,](undefined)[as that config problem is from another switch.](undefined)[Let's enter config mode by typing 'config t'.](undefined)[Since we want our IP address on VLAN 13, we need](undefined)[to enter this VLAN's config with 'int vlan 13'.](undefined)[Simply type the IP address and subnet mask of your](undefined)[choosing, such as 'ip address 192.168.30.90 255.255.255.0'.](undefined)[There'll be a warning to make sure](undefined)[you have the correct settings.](undefined)[Enter 'Y' when you see this.](undefined)[Keep in mind that after this change, it might](undefined)[take a moment for this IP address to update on](undefined)[the switch, so we're just going to fast forward.](undefined)[Now that our IP address is changed, let's](undefined)[check it by typing 'show ip interface'.](undefined)[Our IP address is now updated and is on VLAN 13.](undefined)
            - ### Save Changes 04:15-04:30
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [When you're done making changes, it's crucial](undefined)[to save changes to the startup config.](undefined)[Otherwise, when the switch reboots,](undefined)[it'll lose the changes you made.](undefined)[The 'copy running-config startup-config' command](undefined)[brings the changes you made over to the startup config.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 04:30-04:43
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we showed you how to set up a](undefined)[VLAN, apply that VLAN to multiple switch ports,](undefined)[and change the default IP address for the switch.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 5.6.3 Trunking and IEEE 802.1Q
                - On a large network, a single switch will not provide enough ports for all the hosts that need to be connected to the network. This means that multiple switches must be interconnected to build the network fabric. Multiple switches may also be deployed to provide redundant links. The interconnections between switches are referred to as **trunks**. One of the ports on each switch would be configured as a trunk port for this purpose.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/ZBIt4l0Ko8rDB3eIH-tRug2z4ja3TYyaXbEtRwimonDY3BhMT9Yz58PmaWkQZCcH80pYIhInqqY7tgVhFJ89OBczwgTHEOiJw17AmkCgQ0fz3iz3EFPUz1WJg8Oh1jqj.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The diagram shows first floor and second floor. In the first floor, three PCs are connected to a switch via VLAN 10 and three other PCs are connected to the same switch via VLAN 20. The switch is connected to another switch via a trunk. This switch lies between the first and second floors. In the second floor, the second switch is connected to a third switch via trunk. Three PCs are connected to the third switch via VLAN 10 and three other PCs are connected to the third switch via VLAN 20.
            -  *VLAN trunk link. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - When frames designated for different VLANs are transported across a trunk, the VLAN ID (VID) of each frame must be preserved for the receiving switch to forward it correctly. VIDs are normally defined by the IEEE **802.1Q** standard. Under 802.1Q, per-VLAN traffic is identified by a tag inserted in the Ethernet frame between the Source Address and EtherType fields. The tag contains information about the VID (from 1 to 4,094) and priority (used for QoS functions). The EtherType value is set to identify the frame as 802.1Q.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/VaE0LZb6MqCEkCh4rDZwJiP3ClfUeZiNIyqvv8FtEZaniJE0_kOrwCLbV6t_vUJFxSxOj0qhNBbbcIr9S-KXofUcT8nEHbW96mN019CoUa6ZJKOqYkWYYXOCetbBj1xt.png)
            - Description
            - The header fields are destination MAC, source MAC, 802.1 Q tag, ether type, payload, and FCS.
            -  *Construction of an 802.1Q (VLAN-tagged) Ethernet frame.* 
            - 
            - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_trunking_n09/d_conf_trunking_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_trunking_n09/d_conf_trunking_n09.jpg)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play

                - Video Next Segment
