            - ### Configure Trunking 00:00-00:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we're going to take a look at trunking.](undefined)[This is a common term you'll hear when](undefined)[setting up or configuring enterprise networks.](undefined)[Essentially, when a port is trunked, that means its](undefined)[purpose is to transmit VLAN tags for Ethernet frames.](undefined)[Multiple VLANS will be able to pass through a trunk port.](undefined)
            - ### Defaults	 00:23-00:52
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Switches have defaults applied to them out of the](undefined)[box, so we need to take a look at which defaults](undefined)[are set and what we need to change them to.](undefined)[On this switch, our VLAN settings](undefined)[will be under 'VLAN Management'.](undefined)[We're interested in interface settings.](undefined)[This will define all the settings](undefined)[for each switch port on the switch.](undefined)[If I select 'Port Number 1' and click 'Edit', I](undefined)[have many options to change for this single port.](undefined)[I know this may look intimidating, but the](undefined)[concept we're discussing today can be applied](undefined)[to almost any Layer 2 or 3 managed switch.](undefined)
            - ### Change VLAN Mode 00:52-01:30
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The default settings out of the box for all ports is Trunk.](undefined)[Yes, this will work as-is.](undefined)[However, most devices connecting to a](undefined)[switch only need an access VLAN mode.](undefined)[Data from a PC usually sends untagged Ethernet frames,](undefined)[but it can still be on a single VLAN in Access Mode.](undefined)[Previously, we set ports 1-26 to Access](undefined)[Mode with an untagged VLAN of 30.](undefined)[Those ports will only be able to talk on VLAN 30,](undefined)[which is the VLAN of our main computer network.](undefined)[Ports 27 and 28 are the ones we're interested](undefined)[in, since they are our uplink ports.](undefined)[Both ports get the same VLANs assigned to them since](undefined)[the same VLANS are on other switches in the network](undefined)
            - ### Trunking and VLAN Assignment 01:30-02:02
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In order to have a properly setup trunked port,](undefined)[we need to make sure the interface is in trunked](undefined)[mode and has the appropriate VLANS assigned to it.](undefined)[Let's select Port 27 and click 'Edit'.](undefined)[You can see there's multiple options for VLAN mode.](undefined)[Our port is already in trunk.](undefined)[However, a general port could work like a trunked port.](undefined)[The difference is general supports tagged and](undefined)[untagged members of one or more VLANS while](undefined)[trunk only supports one untagged member of a](undefined)[VLAN and multiple tagged members of a VLAN.](undefined)[We're going leave it as trunk and close it.](undefined)
            - ### VLAN Assignment 02:02-02:47
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, we're going to go to 'Port VLAN Membership'.](undefined)[Here we can see which VLANs are assigned to which ports.](undefined)[As we scroll down, we can see our trunk VLANs](undefined)[have only the default VLAN assigned to them.](undefined)[When I select 'Port 27' and click 'Join VLAN', this window](undefined)[here gives me the option to add more VLANs to this port.](undefined)[We want to add 30, 40, and 50, as we want all these](undefined)[VLANS to pass through this trunked uplink port.](undefined)[There's an untagged VLAN of 2 still on this](undefined)[port, but we're just going to leave that for now.](undefined)[Click 'Apply' and then close it.](undefined)[The same settings would be applied to](undefined)[port 28 just like we did with port 27.](undefined)[Don't forget to save your changes.](undefined)[Click on the flashing red button over here and click](undefined)['Apply' and then 'OK' on the second window that pops up.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:47-02:57
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we covered VLAN modes, trunked ports,](undefined)[and how to assign multiple VLANS to a trunked port.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 5.6.4 Tagged and Untagged Ports
                - If a switch port will only ever participate in a single VLAN, that port can be configured as untagged. This is also referred to as an access port or host port. An untagged/access port uses the following **port tagging** logic:
                    - If a frame is addressed to a port in the same VLAN on the same switch, no tag needs to be added to the frame.
                    - If the frame needs to be transported over a trunk link, the switch adds the relevant 802.1Q tag to identify the VLAN, and then forwards the frame over the trunk port.
                - Conversely, a tagged port will normally be one that is operating as a trunk; that is, capable of transporting traffic addressed to multiple VLANs using the 802.1Q frame format. A trunk might be used to connect switches or to connect a switch to a router. In some circumstances, a host attached to a port might need to be configured to use multiple VLANs and would need to be attached to a trunk port, rather than an access port. One example of this is a virtualization host with multiple guest operating systems. The virtual servers might need to be configured to use different VLANs.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.6.5 Voice VLANs
                - Voice over IP (VoIP) transmits voice traffic as data packets, rather than       over circuit-based transmission lines. The bandwidth and latency       requirements of voice traffic mean that it is often necessary to       prioritize it over other types of data packets. This can be accomplished       using a dedicated VLAN for voice traffic. However, in many cases, VoIP has       been implemented into network infrastructures that were originally       designed for just desktop and laptop computers, with limited numbers of       physical network wall ports.
                - To accommodate the lack of dedicated wall ports for handsets, most VoIP       endpoints incorporate an embedded switch with just two external ports. The       handset is connected via its uplink port to the wall port and via the       structured cabling to an access switch. The PC or laptop is connected to       the handset via the other port. The handset forwards data traffic from the       PC to the access switch as untagged frames. The handset sends voice       traffic over the same physical link but uses 802.1Q tagged frames.
                - Normally, for a switch interface to process tagged frames, it would have       to be configured as a trunk port. This adds a lot of configuration       complexity, so most switches now support the concept of a **voice         or auxiliary VLAN** to distinguish the PC and VoIP traffic without       having to configure a trunk. In the following example, the interface       configuration assigns traffic from the PC to VLAN 100 and the voice       traffic to VLAN 101:
                - `interface GigabitEthernet0/0`
                - `switchport mode access`
                - `switchport access vlan 100`
                - `switchport voice vlan 101`
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/PUEd7YQSf-Sw2T5QFm-5yRaY3_XZGukcFcGckLXHIGJVNeyMrKGae5pcBduhJmh6xxsRgfKbabO1icG1aH8ZDF1h0_bK1agWh9369uo52qdL-O_K08pORwoe40dxeQKS.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The diagram shows a port labeled e t h 0 of P C connected to a port           labeled P C of V o I P endpoint via a dashed line labeled untagged P C           traffic. A port labeled L A N of V o I P endpoint connected to a port           labeled V L A N 101 of switch via a line labeled 802.1 Q tagged VoIP           traffic. A port labeled L A N connected to a port labeled V L A N 100           of switch via a dashed line labeled untagged P C traffic.
                    -  *Sharing a single physical wall port between a PC and VoIP handset.           The handset and switch interface configuration allow VoIP traffic to           be assigned to a different VLAN than the PC's data traffic. (Images ©           123RF.com.)* 
                - The switch will only accept tagged frames that match the configured voice       VLAN ID. To avoid having to configure this manually, the voice VLAN ID and       other configuration parameters can be communicated to the handset using a       protocol such as Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP).
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.6.6 Default VLAN and Native VLAN
                - The VLAN with ID 1 is referred to as the **default VLAN**. This cannot be changed. Unless configured differently, all ports on a switch default to being in VLAN 1. When you are implementing VLANs, you should avoid sending user data traffic over the default VLAN. It should remain unused or used only for inter-switch protocol traffic, where necessary. For example, spanning tree traffic would be permitted to run over the default VLAN. Make sure that unused ports are not assigned to VLAN 1.
                - A **native VLAN** is one into which any untagged traffic is put when receiving frames over a trunk port. When a switch receives an untagged frame over a trunk, it assigns the frame to the native VLAN. Untagged traffic might derive from legacy devices such as hubs or older switches that do not support 802.1Q encapsulated frames. The native VLAN is initially set with the same VLAN ID (VID) as the default VLAN (VID 1). You can and should change this, however, to make the native VID any suitable ID. This should not be the same as any VLAN used for any other data traffic. The same native VID should be configured for the trunk port on both switches.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.6.7 VLAN Routing
                - Many networks are segmented using the VLAN feature of managed switches.       Traffic between VLANs must be routed. There are various ways of       accomplishing this.
                - ## Subinterfaces
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - One method is to deploy a router with a single interface (a one-armed       router or router on a stick) connected to a trunk port on the switch. The       trunk port carries all the VLAN-to-VLAN traffic that must be routed. The       router's physical interface is configured with multiple **subinterfaces**.       Each subinterface is configured with a specific VLAN ID and IP address.       The subinterface acts as the default gateway for its VLAN/subnet. The       router forwards inter-VLAN traffic between the subinterfaces.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/HISGDuirrSFnpDYvFvb0lTe2B_tQJVOt653qfXOJTqEReDOAV--i2LE1RpDjH_Dpu_I83jzdrT3_8IXo1ZZkXRbGh7C9ajjhWZfQ96plxy4UOyQHlSeN2Mt16mTCEDfD.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The diagram shows a router with V L A N 16 sub interface, V L A N 32           sub interface, and V L A N 48 subinterface connected to a physical           interface. The physical interface is connected to a switch via a           trunk. The switch is connected to three hosts, .17, .18, and .19 of V           L A N 16 in the network address, 198.51.100.16 slash 28. The switch is           connected to three hosts, .33, .34, and .35 of V L A N 32 in the           network address, 198.51.100.32 slash 28. The switch is connected to           three hosts, .49, .50, and .51 of V L A N 48 in the network address,           198.51.100.48 slash 28.
                    -  *Router-on-a-Stick topology with subinterfaces serving each           VLAN/subnet. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - The following commands would configure a subinterface for VLAN16 on G0,       using the last available host address as the default gateway:
                - `interface G0.16`
                - `encapsulation dot1Q 16`
                - `ip address 198.51.100.30 255.255.255.240`
                - Remember that the choice of default gateway is by       convention. Many organizations use the first available host address. In       these examples, we're using the last available address (just to focus       attention on how many addresses are available in any given subnet). The       key is to apply the same convention consistently across the network.
                - 
                - Be aware that it's not necessary for the router to       have a single physical interface. This is just a conventional example. It       could have multiple physical interfaces each with subinterfaces connected       to different switches. It could also have a WAN interface.
                - ## Switch Virtual Interfaces
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Passing traffic between a router appliance and the switch over a trunk       link is relatively inefficient and does not scale well to tens of VLANs.       Consequently, enterprise networks usually deploy layer 3 switches in the       core and distribution layers of their networks. A layer 3 capable switch       is one that is optimized for routing between VLANs. It can use static and       dynamic routing to identify which VLAN a packet with a given destination       IP address should be forwarded to.
                - With layer 3 switches, each VLAN can be assigned a **Switch           Virtual Interface (SVI)** to act as the default gateway.       For example, if the topology shown in the previous figure were implemented       using a single layer 3 switch rather than a router plus layer 2 switch,       the SVI for VLAN 16 would be configured as follows:
                - `interface VLAN16`
                - `ip address 198.51.100.30 255.255.255.240`
                - The hosts in that VLAN would be configured with 198.51.100.30 as the       default gateway.
                - Do be aware that a layer 3 switch could also be       configured with subinterfaces. Any port on a layer 3 switch can be       designated as routed rather than switched using the no switchport command.       To distinguish the concepts independently of device types, remember that       an SVI is bound to a VLAN and doesn't require a physical interface (it's       like configuring a virtual router for the virtual LAN); a subinterface is       bound to a physical interface and then allocated a VLAN ID.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
        - **5.7 Routing and VLAN Troubleshooting** 
            - ## 5.7.1 Routing Table Issues
                - If you can ping a host's default gateway, but you cannot ping some or all hosts on remote networks, then you should suspect a routing issue. In many cases, this will be because a router has gone offline, and there is no alternative path to the network. Verify that the router is powered on, that cabling to interface ports is correct, and that each interface is up and configured with the correct IP address. If the router's interfaces are working and correctly configured, next investigate the routing topology.
                - If you suspect a problem with router configuration and the network topology, use `traceroute` to try to identify where the network path is failing and the `route` or `show route` commands to investigate the routing tables of intermediate systems at that point in the path.
                - When inspecting a routing table, you can use `show ip route w.x.y.z` to check for the presence of a route to a specific IP network. A **missing route** may arise because a required static routing entry has not been entered or has been entered incorrectly. Missing routes may also arise because a router fails to communicate with its neighbors and so does not receive routing protocol updates. You might start troubleshooting this by pinging the router nodes that are neighbors of the system with the issue to check basic connectivity. If there is a network path and the neighbors are up, you would investigate the protocol configuration (perhaps there is an authentication issue or incorrect parameter).
                - If all expected routes are present, there may be a priority problem. Remember that route selection uses the following factors:
                    1. The most specific path is preferred. For example, if there are paths to 198.50.100.0/28 via 198.50.100.30 and 198.59.100.0/24 via 198.50.100.254, the path to 198.50.100.0/28 will be preferred for destination addresses from .1 to .30 because /28 is a longer prefix than /24.
                    2. If there are paths with equal prefixes, the path with the lowest administative distance will be selected. Administrative distance is a measure of how trustworthy the source of the root is. Directly connected and static routes have lower AD values than routing protocols.
                    3. If there are identical paths with equal AD, the path with the lowest metric value is preferred.
                - Investigate any paths with overlapping ranges, such as the /24 and /28 example quoted. These are likely to indicate an error, especially if they come from different sources. For example, a misconfigured static route might be in conflict with a learned route.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 5.7.2 Default Route and Routing Loop Issues
                - Each end host must be configured with a default gateway so that it can communicate with remote hosts. The host's routing table should contain an entry for 0.0.0.0/0 that uses the router's IP address as the path. If there is no entry for 0.0.0.0/0, check that the default gateway is configured. If the host cannot contact remote networks, verify that you can ping the default gateway address. If the default gateway router is up, verify its configuration. If the router configuration is faulty or the router or one of its interfaces is down, it's likely that multiple hosts will have connectivity problems.
                - A default route can also be configured on a router. This might be used as the path to a service provider's routers to contact external networks. Misconfigured default routes or propagating default routes to other routers can be the cause of routing loops.
                - A **routing loop** occurs when two routers use one another as the path to a network. Packets caught in a routing loop circle around until the TTL expires. One symptom of a potential routing loop is for routers to generate ICMP Time Exceeded error messages.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/u_X5g4L5HnwLE5bJNmxGEECHqnf0IDi-aCKRhEl23rhFo7Z47EwRvhwKWalaQ-5ENOkYlCi-cGh6Y21iLubPsMx08o1SoSodlc68ZdPY5GmxgVQcZxVFBzGvXoFLByNs.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1. Interface G 0 on Router B goes down, so it remove routes that use that interface from its routing table.
            - 2. Router C provides an update to Router B with routes to the missing networks.
            - 3. When Router C tries to send a packet to 10.0.1.0 slash 24 via G 0. Router B routes the packet back as according to its routing table. Router C is the next hop interface for 10.0.1.0 slash 24. A loop forms.
            - 4. This loop would be prevented by split horizon. Router C learned the routes to 10.0.1.0 slash 24 and 10.0.2.0 slash 24 from Router B and so should not advertise them back.
            -  *A routing loop created between Routers B and C. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - Routing protocols use various mechanisms to prevent loops. For example, distance vector protocols use the following mechanisms:
                - **Maximum hop count**—If the cost exceeds a certain value (16 in RIP), the network is deemed unreachable. A poison route is one advertised with a hop count of 16. This can provide an explicit failure notice to other routers.
                - **Holddown timer**—If a node declares a network unreachable, its neighbors start a holddown timer. Any updates about that route received from other nodes are discarded for the duration of the timer. This is designed to ensure that all nodes have converged information about an unreachable network.
                - **Split horizon**—Prevents a routing update from being copied back to the source. In the example above, this would prevent Router C from sending an update about a route to Router A via Router B to Router B.
                - Link state protocols try to ensure that each node has a consistent view of the network through continual, timely updates flooded to all nodes in the routing domain. A loop in a link state routing domain typically indicates that updates are not being propagated correctly.
                - You can use `traceroute` to diagnose a routing loop by looking for IP addresses that appear multiple times in the output.
            - ## 5.7.3 VLAN Assignment Issues
                - When you configure a virtual LAN (VLAN), the switch ports assigned to that VLAN are in a segmented network. The VLAN is likely to be assigned its own subnet address. Any device connecting to a port in the VLAN must have an appropriate IP configuration for that subnet in terms of IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS servers. Hosts in the VLAN must use the router to contact other VLANs or other remote networks.
                - The VLAN's router (default gateway) is likely to be configured as a type of virtual interface. If the router has a single physical trunk link to the switch hosting the VLAN, then that virtual interface will be configured as a subinterface of the physical interface, such as `interface G0.16`. If a layer 3 switch is used to implement the VLAN, the default gateway address will usually be configured as a Switched Virtual Interface (SVI), such as `interface VLAN16`. Use `show` commands on the router or switch to verify that these parameters have been set correctly and that ports have been assigned to the correct VLANs.
                - A host must be physically connected to the correct switch port configured with the appropriate VLAN ID. If the switch port is configured with the correct VLAN ID, and the host is still assigned to the wrong VLAN, you should suspect a physical cabling problem. Verify that the cable from the patch panel is connected to the correct switch port. If there are continuing problems, verify that the patch panel labeling correctly identifies the wall jack that the computer is connected to.
                - If you cannot diagnose the issue by looking at the configuration, use `ping`to test connectivity. You can use exactly the same process as you would a physical LAN:
                    1. Ping the loopback address, then the host's own IP address to verify that TCP/IP is working and that the host's IP is correctly configured.
                    2. Ping the default gateway to verify that it is contactable. Optionally, also ping another host on the same VLAN/subnet to verify that local communications can be established. If you cannot ping the default gateway, look for a problem with either the host or switch/router configuration. If you can rule out these and there is no connectivity with any local hosts, check that the patch cable for the workstation's wall port is connected to the correct switch port and for any other physical cable issues.
                    3. Ping a remote host or server to verify that routing is available. If you cannot ping any remote hosts, verify the switch and router configuration. Determine if the problem is isolated to a single VLAN or is more widespread across the network. If you can ping some remote hosts but not others, use `traceroute`to determine the cause.
                - Hosts in each VLAN must be able to reach Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) servers and Domain Name System (DNS) servers. One option is to place these as hosts within the VLAN, but that could mean provisioning many servers. More typically, they will be in a server VLAN, and hosts in the client VLAN must use the default gateway to contact them. In this setup, a DHCP relay must also be configured. Network service configuration is discussed in more detail in the next module.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - 00:001. Troubleshoot WAN Issues

            - ### Troubleshoot WAN Issues  00:00-00:33
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we're going to investigate](undefined)[troubleshooting commands available in Cisco IOS to help](undefined)[diagnose WAN connectivity problems. In the exhibit, you](undefined)[see that we have two routers, Router A and Router B.](undefined)
            - [They're both connected over a point-to-point WAN](undefined)[circuit using their serial interfaces. Both routers](undefined)[have been configured, but there remains a lack of](undefined)[connectivity. We cannot ping across the link.](undefined)[We need to diagnose why that is.](undefined)
            - ### Check for Connectivity  00:33-00:51
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's start with Router A.](undefined)[We'll enter 'ping 192.168.1.2'. That is the IP](undefined)[address on the other end of this connection.](undefined)[We can see that there is a connectivity problem](undefined)[between the two routers. Based on that, let's enter a](undefined)[few show commands to try to determine the root cause.](undefined)
            - ### Display Router A Config  00:51-02:35
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We'll start by typing 'show interfaces' and](undefined)[specifying the serial interface that we're using.](undefined)[So, we'll add 's0/0/0' to the show interfaces](undefined)[command and press Enter. On top, we see](undefined)[Serial0/0/0 is up, but the line protocol is down.](undefined)
            - [When you have an up, down situation, that](undefined)[usually means it's a layer 2 issue.](undefined)[That gives us a starting point.](undefined)
            - [We can see it has an IP address of 192.168.1.1](undefined)[assigned. Also, we see that the encapsulation](undefined)[is set to PPP. This is all good information.](undefined)[It's worth noting, however, that PPP is](undefined)[not the default encapsulation type on](undefined)[serial interfaces.  HDLC is the default.](undefined)
            - [The down situation could be the result of HDLC running on](undefined)[the other end. It doesn't matter which one you choose, as](undefined)[long as they're identical in their settings on both ends.](undefined)
            - [That's something that we'll have to check on the other](undefined)[end to make sure that the encapsulations match. If we](undefined)[enter 'show IP interface brief', we can see the same](undefined)[situation on the bottom. It's in an up, down state.](undefined)[There's definitely something wrong here. It could be](undefined)[on Router A. It could be on the remote end. We don't](undefined)[have enough information at this point to be sure.](undefined)
            - [A good next step is to use a 'show run' on the](undefined)[local router and look at the serial interface again.](undefined)[We can see the same IP](undefined)[address and mask we saw earlier and encapsulation PPP.](undefined)[We knew we were using that.](undefined)
            - [A new piece of information is this PPP authentication CHAP.](undefined)[So, this interface has been configured to authenticate with](undefined)[the other end. Again, not a problem, actually, a good thing.](undefined)[But, we need to verify the same has](undefined)[been configured on the other end.](undefined)
            - ### Display Router B Config  02:35-02:49
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Maybe now is a good time to switch over to Router](undefined)[B and look at its configuration. We'll resume a](undefined)[Telnet connection that we had with Router B.](undefined)[We'll execute that same command](undefined)[again, 'show run int s0/0/0'.](undefined)
            - ### Compare Configs  02:49-03:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Both configurations are onscreen now,](undefined)[so we can do a side-by-side comparison.](undefined)[That will be helpful. We can see the IP address and](undefined)[the mask. Just focusing on that, they do look like](undefined)[they are compatible, 1.1 and 1.2 with a common mask.](undefined)[There's no reason from an IP standpoint](undefined)[that they should not be able to communicate.](undefined)
            - [They're both configured for PPP as well as](undefined)[authentication CHAP. We can also see a clock](undefined)[rate has been set on the Router B serial port.](undefined)[That tells us that it's the DCE end, the WAN](undefined)[connection. Nothing jumps out onscreen as being a problem.](undefined)
            - ### Display the Username and Password  03:24-03:58
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, we'll broaden the show command.](undefined)[We'll enter 'show run' and see what else might](undefined)[be in the config. We'll go to the next page.](undefined)
            - [Now we can see a username command here. This is](undefined)[required for PPP authentication CHAP. Username Router A](undefined)[is the device on the other end of this serial connection,](undefined)[the host name of that device. So, that looks accurate.](undefined)
            - [Then password secret.](undefined)[So, secret is the shared string that must match on both](undefined)[ends of this connection. Let's make a note of that](undefined)[and verify that the same is configured on the other end.](undefined)
            - ### Compare the Router A Configs  03:58-04:41
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's return to Router A and enter that same show](undefined)[command, 'show run'. Page down once, twice,](undefined)[and there's the username command. It looks okay so](undefined)[far. Router B is the other end of this connection.](undefined)
            - [But notice, the password has been mistyped. In this](undefined)[case, we have an extra letter e in the word secret.](undefined)[These strings do not match.](undefined)[That's something we'll have to fix. That's](undefined)[an easy fix. Let's do that right now.](undefined)
            - [Let's go back to global config and re-execute](undefined)[the username command, being more careful this](undefined)[time to type it in correctly. There we go.](undefined)
            - ### Reinitialize the Authentication Sequence  04:41-05:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We'll perform a shutdown of the port to reinitialize](undefined)[the authentication sequence, but prior to doing so,](undefined)[we'll enter a 'terminal monitor' command.](undefined)[That will redirect console messages](undefined)[to this Telnet-connected session.](undefined)[Let's enter 'debug ppp authentication' to](undefined)[activate a debug of PPP authentication.](undefined)
            - [Alright. Now, let's go back to that](undefined)[interface, enter 'shut' and wait for it to go down.](undefined)[We'll enter 'no shut' to bring it back up.](undefined)[You should very quickly see a flood](undefined)[of authentication debug messages.](undefined)[On the very bottom, you can see that the](undefined)[status of the interface has been changed to up.](undefined)
            - [This is what we're looking for, success messages on the](undefined)[bottom. If authentication was failing, as it was prior, you](undefined)[would see failure messages on the bottom here, indicating a](undefined)[password string mismatch. Because we're done with](undefined)[debugging, we can turn that off by entering 'undebug all'.](undefined)
            - ### Verify Configs  05:39-06:03
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's repeat a couple of these show commands. For](undefined)[example, 'ip interface brief'. Well, that's better](undefined)[on the bottom. We now have an up, up state.](undefined)[So far, it looks as if the password mismatch](undefined)[was the cause of the problem we were having.](undefined)
            - [Another cause of up, down could be an encapsulation](undefined)[mismatch. Once again, if we had PPP on one end and HDLC on](undefined)[the other, that would also result in an up, down state.](undefined)
            - ### Verify Connections  06:03-07:00
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's enter 'ping 192.168.1.2' to get across](undefined)[the link and see if we fixed everything there.](undefined)[It works. Yes, we fixed everything there.](undefined)
            - [The reason we used ping is that though even we are in](undefined)[an up, up state, it does not mean we don't still have](undefined)[a problem above layer 2. That's why we looked at the](undefined)[IP addresses and verified that they are compatible.](undefined)[Even though layer 2 might be in good shape, you could](undefined)[still have two IP addresses that simply don't exist on](undefined)[the same subnet, and thus still could not communicate.](undefined)
            - [That's why we troubleshoot from the bottom up, which means](undefined)[starting with layer 1. Are the devices connected Is there](undefined)[a cable unplugged Are the devices connected properly?](undefined)[Are the pinouts correct?](undefined)
            - [If the cabling looks good, then we move to layer 2. Layer 2](undefined)[in this demo included the encapsulation types and the](undefined)[authentication process. Once those were fixed, layer](undefined)[3 required only IP compatibility for things to start working.](undefined)
            - ### Summary  07:00-07:09
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we performed troubleshooting on WAN circuits.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
        - [Size]();-[H3]()
    - ### 6.0 Implementing Network Services
        - **6.1 Transport and Application Layer Protocols** 
            - ## 6.1.1 Transport Layer Ports and Connections
                - The first three layers of the OSI model are primarily concerned with basic connectivity, address, and forwarding. Protocols at the Transport layer (layer 4) are concerned with delivery of multiplexed application data. Transport layer headers don't describe how to forward a packet across a network. They instruct a host what to do with the data in a packet, and optionally, how to verify that it is complete.
                - A TCP/IP host may be running multiple services or communicating with multiple servers, clients, or peers in parallel. This means that incoming packets must be directed to the appropriate service or application. To facilitate this, each application protocol is assigned a unique identification number called a **port**. A host can operate multiple ports simultaneously.
                - Port numbers 0 through 1,023 are preassigned by the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA) to "well-known" server applications. These port assignments are documented at [iana.org/assignments/service-names-port-numbers/service-names-port-numbers.xhtml](https://www.iana.org/assignments/service-names-port-numbers/service-names-port-numbers.xhtml). Other server applications have been registered in the port range 1,024 through 49,151.
                - The remaining ports (up to 65,535) are designated for private or dynamic use. As well as the server application needing a port, each client application must assign its own port number to track its requests. Client ports are also referred to as ephemeral ports or source ports.
                - OS implementations of TCP/IP have not always conformed to these recommendations. For example, early versions of Windows and UNIX/Linux used 1,024–5,000 for client ports. Modern Linux kernels often use 32,768–60,999.
                - The port number is used in conjunction with the source IP address to form a **socket**. Each socket is bound to a software process. Only one process can operate a socket at any one time. A connection is formed when a client socket requests a service from the server socket. A connection is uniquely identified by the combination of server port plus IP address and client port plus IP address. A server socket can therefore support multiple connections from a number of client sockets.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/zPvRnkGWkMylLvovQkL6s-2D-DqxTPBRJBPXz0ztlm4fgwDOU0_5Zir2kD5bAhvvEkCzIBg31uI6hvZACxQkStoVilPAdi5GwA340H2iofsp6856vfTH5QIKgjBObnVC.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1.A server {192.168.1.254} opens T C P ports 80 and 143 to run separate web and mail application services.
            - 2.A client host (192.168.1.1) runs a web browser and email client application.
            - 3.The browser open an ephemeral port 47747 to connect to the server port 80, Where the email application connects to the server port 143 at the same time, using ephemeral port 47748.
            - 4. Each combination of source socket (I P address plus port) and destination socket from a separate connection listed in the host’s state table.
            - 5. Server replies are directed to the ephemeral port that opened the connection, allowing the data to be received by the correct application on the client.
            - 6. A second client can open connections at the same time – note that a client may open multiple connections to the same server port.
            - 7. The combination of source IP plus source port uniquely identifies each client socket.
            -  *Multiplexing application ports as sockets at the Transport layer. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - ## 6.1.2 Transmission Control Protocol
                - The **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** works at the Transport layer to provide connection-oriented, guaranteed communication using acknowledgments to ensure that delivery has occurred. If packets are missing, they can be retransmitted. TCP can be used for unicast transmission only.
                - TCP takes data from the Application layer as a stream of bytes and divides it up into segments, each of which is given a header. The TCP segments become the payload of the underlying IP datagrams. The use of sequencing, acknowledgments, and retransmissions means that TCP requires numerous header fields to maintain state information. The main fields in the header of a TCP segment are the following:
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 6.1.3 TCP Handshake and Teardown
                - A TCP connection is typically established to transfer a single file, so a client session for something like a webpage (HTTP) might involve multiple TCP connections being opened with the server. These connections are managed using handshake transactions, which make use of a number of **TCP flags**.
                - ## TCP Three-Way Handshake
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A connection is established using a three-way handshake:
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/GYbFxH_55Vm1fehZ0QXSWpvdGNOtnkPiEBULsgKr7jE1Yr_PNCI7DehHh4yK_jtUoPV-5PUT64OFj1c8wN8up_C9GKjbafAcJHZAhCq-yFI83gthHeTD-DwfOUYIkMop.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The screenshot provides data in a tabular format whose column headers are number, time, source, destination, protocol, length, and info. Destination is highlighted.
            -  *Observing the three-way handshake with the Wireshark protocol analyzer. (Screenshot courtesy of Wireshark.)* 
                1. The client sends a segment with the TCP flag SYN set to the server with a randomly generated sequence number. The client enters the SYN-SENT state.
                2. The server, currently in the LISTEN state (assuming it is online), responds with a SYN/ACK segment, containing its own randomly generated sequence number. The server enters the SYN-RECEIVED state.
                3. The client responds with an ACK segment. The client assumes the connection is ESTABLISHED.
                4. The server opens a connection with the client and enters the ESTABLISHED state.
            - Servers can (usually) support thousands or even millions of TCP connections simultaneously.
            - The sending machine expects regular acknowledgments for segments it sends and, if a period elapses without an acknowledgment, it assumes the information did not arrive and automatically resends it. This overhead makes the system relatively slow. Connection-oriented transmission is suitable when reliability and data integrity are important.
            - ## TCP Connection Teardown
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - There are also functions for resetting a connection and (in some implementations) keeping a connection alive if no actual data is being transmitted (hosts are configured to time out unused connections). To close a connection, also referred to as teardown, the following basic steps are performed:
                1. The client sends a FIN segment to the server and enters the FIN-WAIT1 state.
                2. The server responds with an ACK segment and enters the CLOSE-WAIT state.
                3. The client receives the ACK segment and enters the FIN-WAIT2 state. The server sends its own FIN segment to the client and goes to the LAST-ACK state.
                4. The client responds with an ACK and enters the TIME-WAIT state. After a defined period, the client closes its connection.
                5. The server closes the connection when it receives the ACK from the client.
            - Some implementations may use one less step by combining the FIN and ACK responses into a single segment operation.
            - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/SVpo73k6kgr2Ft1WwHiDV8zGBPL40bxRFU4ADliFvAC9b515-4G8N-Mc9vDs6O-8vmYGxGzBfxX0RcRtcGkaVbcZsVZJjKkGt8WXnn9-a6FDOO34nrgd0dbh48MnsPl0.png)
            - Description
            - The output shows active connections in a table format. The table provides data on protocol, local address, foreign address, state, and P I D.
            -  *Observing TCP connections with the netstat tool. (Screenshot used with permission from Microsoft.)* 
            - A host can also end a session abruptly using a reset (RST) segment. This would not be typical behavior and might need to be investigated. A server or security appliance might refuse connections using RST, a client or server application might be faulty, or there could be some sort of suspicious scanning activity ongoing.
            - ## 6.1.4 User Datagram Protocol
                - The **User Datagram Protocol (UDP)** also works at the Transport layer, but unlike TCP, it is a connectionless, nonguaranteed method of communication with no acknowledgments or flow control. There is no guarantee regarding the delivery of messages or mechanism for retransmitting lost or damaged packets. If an application uses UDP and needs reliability mechanisms, these must be implemented in the Application layer or software logic.
                - UDP is suitable for applications that send small amounts of data in each packet and do not require acknowledgment of receipt. It is used by Application layer protocols that need to send multicast or broadcast traffic. It may also be used for applications that transfer time-sensitive data but do not require complete reliability, such as voice or video. Using small packets means that if a few are lost or arrive out of order, they only manifest as minor glitches in playback quality. The reduced overhead means that overall delivery is faster.
                - This table shows the structure of a UDP header.
                - The header size is 8 bytes, compared to 20 bytes (or more) for TCP.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 6.1.5 netstat
                - The **netstat** command allows you to check the state of ports on the local host. You can use `netstat` to check for service misconfigurations, such as a host running a web or FTP server that a user installed without authorization. You may also be able to identify suspicious remote connections to services on the local host or from the host to remote IP addresses.
                - On Windows, used without switches, the command outputs active TCP connections, showing the local and foreign addresses and ports. Using the `-a` switch displays all open ports, including both active TCP and UDP connections and ports in the listening state.
                - On Linux, running `netstat` without switches shows active connections of any type. If you want to show different connection types, you can use the switches for Internet connections for TCP (`-t`) and UDP (`-u`), raw connections (`-w`), and UNIX sockets/local server ports (`-x`). Using the `-a` switch includes ports in the listening state in the output. `-l` shows only ports in the listening state, omitting established connections.
                - For example, the following command shows listening and established Internet connections (TCP and UDP) only: `netstat -tua`.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/ZFJgVA8l6CO_VhrDC-XP8CBKDgX6sWqgcQn-SbEqX45nGzcFL5kwcp2OS8r8wD2RfxPxdQEj8gxxz0mu0731m6Ctiuiv_U4tT2IV5EhP5z-XKdcDpNQM9gCz4v2_UUjm.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The output shows active internet connections in a table format. The table provides data on protocol, received Q, send Q, local address, foreign address, and state.
            -  *Linux netstat output showing active and listening TCP and UDP connections.* 
            - On both Windows and Linux, `-n` displays ports and addresses in numerical format. Skipping name resolution speeds up each query. On Linux, using `-4` or `-6` filters sockets by IPv4 or IPv6 addresses respectively. In Windows, use the `-p` switch with the protocol type (`TCP`, `TCPv6`, `UDP`, or `UDPv6`).
                - Another common task is to identify which software process is bound to a socket. On Windows, `-o` shows the process ID (PID) number that has opened the port, while `-b` shows the process name. In Linux, use `-p` to show the PID and process name.
                - `netstat -s` reports per protocol statistics, such as packets received, errors, discards, unknown requests, port requests, failed connections, and so on. The tool will report Ethernet statistics using `-e` (Windows) or `-I` (Linux). `netstat -r` displays the routing table.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/3d73qNuPzdOoVG1WKLybjQ_ngNcMlwQ9CCeb25nQgdIyK5p1HZChr0YhRa1k1JG6acOZQKN0bfmKabFb3yKARDIQ1_EtPc98a3RsIRYCxMRvQmfLlRt7lVXaiUL5MuSD.png)
                -  *Linux netstat interface statistics showing receive and transmit packets numbers plus errors and dropped packets.* 
            - `netstat` can also be set to run continuously. In Windows, run `netstat nn`, where  *nn * is the refresh interval in seconds (press `Ctrl+C` to stop); in Linux, run `netstat -c`.
                - The Linux netstat command is part of the deprecated net-tools package. The preferred package iproute2 contains a number of different commands to replace netstat functionality. Most of the port scanning functions are performed by ss, while interface statistics are reported by nstat.
                - 
                - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_netstat_n09/d_netstat_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment
            -

        - **6.2 Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol** 
            - ## 6.2.1 DHCP Process

            - ### Network Device Requirements 00:00-00:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Every device on a network requires certain](undefined)[configurations so it can communicate properly.](undefined)[At a minimum, each device must have a unique](undefined)[IP address, subnet mask, and default gateway.](undefined)[The device might also need a specific Domain Name](undefined)[System or DNS assignment, and maybe some Windows](undefined)[naming service or wins server information.](undefined)[This all depends on the specific network you use.](undefined)
            - ### Manual vs. DHCP Configuration 00:25-00:40
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [If you only have a few systems, it's not very](undefined)[hard to configure these options manually.](undefined)[The problem is if you have to manage a large network,](undefined)[manually configuring each device just isn't practical.](undefined)[That's where at DHCP comes in.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - The **Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)** provides an automatic method for allocating an IP address, subnet mask, and optional parameters, such as the default gateway and DNS server addresses, when a host joins the network.
            - A host is configured to use DHCP by specifying in the TCP/IP configuration that it should automatically obtain an IP address.
            - 
            - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_dhcp_process_n09/v_dhcp_process_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)

            - ### Introduction  00:01-00:08
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [When a new device connects to your network,](undefined)[it goes through a four-step process to obtain](undefined)[the IP configuration from the DHCP server.](undefined)
            - ### DHCP Process 00:08-00:47
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The first step is DHCP discover, where the](undefined)[device sends out a DHCP request on the broadcast](undefined)[address, looking for any DHCP servers.](undefined)[The DHCP server gets the request and](undefined)[responds with the DHCP offer packet.](undefined)[This packet contains the DHCP server's information.](undefined)[The client then sends a DHCP request packet to the server.](undefined)[The final step is the server sending a DHCP](undefined)[ACK packet, or DHCP acknowledge packet.](undefined)[This assigns the client's IP configuration and allows](undefined)[the client to start communicating on the network.](undefined)
            - ### DHCP Lease Renewal 00:47-01:05
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [When the device's lease time is halfway](undefined)[up, the renewal process takes place.](undefined)[This process skips the discover and offer steps.](undefined)[The client sends the DHCP request packet](undefined)[to the server, which responds with the DHCP](undefined)[ACK packet and renews the IP configuration.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/-r2ShtF1UTO5z-xVEuYHpfJcuU0odXQAX6sQkPB8JdDRMXW-j0k6hYCQWdL7rArhjgZyfsqBq2joN6btqUkkBtZYRSFYBVY482yPIQtbp_gFfzUBm5ePJRPMWnspPLP7.png)
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
                - 1.D H C P DISCOVER
                - M A C: A A
                - Source I P: 0.0.0.0
                - Destination I P: 255.255.255.255
                - 2.D H C P OFFER
                - M A C: A A
                - Source I P: 192.168.1.254
                - Destination I P: 255.255.255.255
                - I P: 192.168.1.101
                - Netmask: 255.255.255.0
                - Server: 192.168.1.254
                - Leases time: 48 hours
                - 3.D H C P REQUEST
                - M A C: A A
                - Source I P: 0.0.0.0
                - Destination I P: 255.255.255.255
                - I P: 192.168.1.101
                - Netmask: 255.255.255.0
                - Requested parameters
                - 4.D H C P PACK
                - M A C: A A
                - Source I P: 192.168.1.254
                - Destination I P: 255.255.255.255
                - I P: 192.168.1.101
                - Netmask: 255.255.255.0
                - Server: 192.168.1.254
                - Gateway: 192.168.1.254
                - Other requested parameters.
            -  *DHCP Discover, Offer, Request, Ack process. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                1. When a DHCP client initializes, it broadcasts a DHCPDISCOVER packet to find a DHCP server. All communications are sent using UDP, with the server listening on port 67 and the client on port 68.
                2. Presuming it has an IP address available, the DHCP server responds to the client with a DHCPOFFER packet, containing the address and other configuration information.
                3. The client may choose to accept the offer using a DHCPREQUEST packet—also broadcast onto the network.
                4. Assuming the offer is still available, the server will respond with a DHCPACK packet. The client broadcasts an ARP message to check that the address is unused. If so, it will start to use the address and options; if not, it declines the address and requests a new one.
            - The IP address is leased by the server for a limited period only. A client can attempt to renew or rebind the lease before it expires. If the lease cannot be renewed, the client must release the IP address and start the discovery process again.
            - Sometimes, the DHCP lease process is called the DORA process: Discover, Offer, Request, and Ack(nowledge).
            - ## 6.2.2 DHCP Server Configuration
                - A DHCP server must be allocated a static IP address and configured with a range (or pool) of IP addresses and subnet masks plus option values to allocate.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/euxUAgn78xdF7QWKVPb65gmz7kJToSAiWkTDs4zyIM4RRWVEFOv1SeVxS3d86QKW98_t8VY4XvCIRQqabbBfeSQen1w81YoOVeLQLOKx7k3Dw-4kVBMa3GQCzyqz1kJJ.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The left pane shows network tab selected to list several options from which D H C P settings is selected. The right pane shows two tabs: quick setup and advanced. Advanced tab is selected. D H C P settings are listed. It provides details of D H C P server, I P address pool, address lease time, default gateway, primary D N S, and secondary D N S. Save button is at the bottom.
            -  *Configuring DHCP on a TP-LINK wireless access point. (Screenshot courtesy of TP-Link Technologies Co., Ltd.)* 
            - A range of addresses and options configured for a single subnet is referred to as a **scope**. To define a scope, you must provide a start and end IP address along with a subnet mask. The server maintains a one-to-one mapping of scopes to subnets. That is, no scope can cover more than one subnet, and no subnet can contain more than one scope.
                - The multifunction device shown only supports a single scope. The DHCP server must be placed in the same subnet as its clients. More advanced DHCP servers might be configured to manage multiple scopes. Where a server provides IP configuration for multiple subnets/scopes, it must choose the pool to service each request based on the subnet from which the request originated.
                - There is no mechanism for a client to choose between multiple servers. Therefore, if multiple DHCP servers are deployed—for fault tolerance, for instance—they must either be configured with non-overlapping split scopes or use a failover mechanism. DHCP for multiple subnets is usually handled by configuring relay agents to forward requests to a central DHCP server.
                - 
                - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_dhcp_n09/d_conf_dhcp_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment

            - ### DHCP Server 00:00-00:35
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're](undefined)[going to configure a DHCP server.](undefined)[DHCP stands for Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol.](undefined)[It's a client/server protocol that automatically provides](undefined)[a host with its IP address and other information, such as](undefined)[the subnet mask, DNS, and default gateway.](undefined)[The server I'm working on is a domain controller, has Active](undefined)[Directory installed, and has been configured as a DNS server.](undefined)[Your network may be configured](undefined)[differently than the one I'm using.](undefined)
            - ### Verify Current Server IP Information 00:35-01:30
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Before we get started, there are a](undefined)[few things we've done to this server.](undefined)[The first thing is that this server](undefined)[has been assigned a static IP address.](undefined)[As we've learned previously, there are two ways to get an](undefined)[IP address from a DHCP server statically or dynamically.](undefined)[A DHCP server can't assign itself a valid IP address.](undefined)
            - [The second thing we need to do is find out what our](undefined)[subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS servers are.](undefined)[In our case, our DNS server is also](undefined)[this server, so that's what we'll use.](undefined)[Let's take a look at that first to](undefined)[confirm and document that information.](undefined)[The fastest way to do this is to bring up](undefined)[a Command Prompt and type 'ipconfig /all'.](undefined)[Let's go to the search box and type in](undefined)['cmd' to bring up the Command Prompt.](undefined)[Once the command line interface](undefined)[comes up, we type 'ipconfig /all'.](undefined)[We can now see how this server's](undefined)[IP information is configured.](undefined)
            - ### Install the DHCP Role 01:30-02:34
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We're ready to start installing DHCP on this server.](undefined)[To do this, we have to add the DHCP role.](undefined)[We need to do this from Server Manager.](undefined)[If Server Manager isn't open, you can go to](undefined)[the search box, type in 'Server Manager',](undefined)[and then open it from the search results.](undefined)
            - [Now that we have Server Manager open, we click on 'Add Roles](undefined)[and Features' to bring up the Add Roles and Features wizard.](undefined)[Click on 'Next' to continue.](undefined)[We'll do a role-based or feature-based installation.](undefined)[Click on 'Next'.](undefined)[We'll make sure our server is](undefined)[selected down here in the list.](undefined)[There's only one server on the network,](undefined)[so it's the only one we can pick.](undefined)[Click 'Next'.](undefined)[We'll come down here and select 'DHCP' to add the DHCP role.](undefined)[Click the 'Add Features' button to add DHCP](undefined)[features to support the DHCP role on the server.](undefined)[Our server is telling us that we need some additional](undefined)[features, so we'll accept these and continue.](undefined)[Click 'Next'.](undefined)[Click 'Next' again and then click 'Install'.](undefined)[We'll wait just a minute while](undefined)[the DHCP server role is installed.](undefined)
            - ### Complete DHCP Configuration 02:34-03:51
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Okay, the role installation is](undefined)[complete, but it's not configured yet.](undefined)[The next thing we need to do is actually click on](undefined)[this option right here 'Complete DHCP Configuration'.](undefined)[We have to authorize this DHCP server](undefined)[because of a problem with rogue DHCP servers.](undefined)[What could happen is somebody could either maliciously or](undefined)[accidentally set up a DHCP server, just like we're doing](undefined)[right now, and then connect it to a production network.](undefined)[It could start handing out IP addresses to the various](undefined)[clients on the network, and it's not supposed to do that.](undefined)[This step is to launch the post-install wizard.](undefined)[We'll hit 'Next'.](undefined)[We have to specify which domain and the user credentials](undefined)[that'll be used to authorize this DHCP server.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and click 'Commit' and 'Close'.](undefined)
            - [I want to confirm that my DHCP service is running.](undefined)[To do this, I'm going to go up to 'Tools', select](undefined)['Services', go down to 'DHCP Server', and confirm that](undefined)[it's running and that it's set to start automatically.](undefined)[We're good to go.](undefined)[The DHCP service is now available on this](undefined)[server, but it hasn't been configured.](undefined)[It can't do much of anything because we](undefined)[haven't told it what exactly we want it to do.](undefined)[We need to fix that now.](undefined)
            - ### Configure a DHCP Scope 03:51-06:07
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's go over to 'Tools'.](undefined)[Let's go to 'DHCP' and bring up the DHCP Management Console.](undefined)[Here's our DHCP server.](undefined)[We're going to be working with IP version 4.](undefined)[Notice over here, under IPv4, that we don't have any scopes.](undefined)[We have no addresses to hand out,](undefined)[and we need to define a scope.](undefined)[These scopes will basically say that these are](undefined)[the IP addresses you can hand out, these are the](undefined)[IP addresses you can't hand out, and these are the](undefined)[options you can deliver with your IP addresses.](undefined)[Let's right-click on 'IPv4' and click on 'New Scope'.](undefined)[The New Scope wizard appears.](undefined)[Click 'Next'.](undefined)[We need to give the scope a name.](undefined)[Let's call it 'Network-A' and put](undefined)['Internal' for the description.](undefined)[Click 'Next'.](undefined)[We have to specify the IP addresses](undefined)[that this DHCP server will hand out.](undefined)[Let's hand out a range from](undefined)['192.168.1.1' to '192.168.1.199'.](undefined)
            - [We'll use the default subnet mask of '255.255.255.0'.](undefined)[Click 'Next'.](undefined)[Notice that we've got a little problem 'we're](undefined)[handing out addresses between 192.168.1.1 and .199.](undefined)[We've got other devices on that subnet](undefined)[that are statically assigned an IP address.](undefined)[If that's the case, you can't](undefined)[hand those addresses out via DHCP.](undefined)[Otherwise, you're going to get an IP address conflict.](undefined)[For example, we have 192.168.1.10 assigned to](undefined)[the interface on the server I'm working on.](undefined)[We can't hand that IP address out via DHCP because](undefined)[that's the address of this server and so it's taken.](undefined)[Let's also assume we have a few other devices with](undefined)[static IPs between 192.168.1.1 and 192.168.1.20.](undefined)
            - [Let's enter '192.168.1.1' to](undefined)['192.168.1.20' and add that exclusion.](undefined)[That'll block those addresses so](undefined)[that they aren't handed out via DHCP.](undefined)[A better way of solving this problem would've been to](undefined)[start the range of IP addresses that our DHCP server](undefined)[would hand out from 192.168.1.21 and go to 192.168.1.199,](undefined)[but I wanted to show you how to create an exclusion.](undefined)
            - ### DHCP Leases 06:07-06:57
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now we can specify the lease duration.](undefined)[You can specify how long DHCP clients are allowed](undefined)[to keep an IP address from the DHCP server before](undefined)[they either have to renew it or give it up.](undefined)[We're going to leave it set to the default of just 8 days.](undefined)[Be aware that if you decrease the lease time, it](undefined)[does increase network traffic a little bit because](undefined)[clients have to continually renew their IP addresses.](undefined)[But decreasing the lease time also makes it so](undefined)[that you use your addresses more efficiently.](undefined)[We'll just leave it at the default for our purposes.](undefined)[Click 'Next'.](undefined)
            - [We need to specify whether or not](undefined)[we want to configure DHCP options.](undefined)[We do want to, but we're not going to do it right](undefined)[now, so I'm going to turn this option to 'No'.](undefined)[I'll configure these later.](undefined)[And we're done.](undefined)[I'll click 'Finish', and now we have a scope defined.](undefined)
            - ### DHCP Reservations 06:57-08:12
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In addition, we have an option](undefined)[down here to define reservations.](undefined)[Reservations are IP addresses that have to be assigned](undefined)[to a specific host in the network every single time.](undefined)[They can't be assigned to anybody else.](undefined)[These reservations are based on the host's MAC address.](undefined)[We can come over here, right-click,](undefined)[and select 'New Reservation'.](undefined)[We can specify an IP address that we want to always be](undefined)[assigned to the same host 192.168.1.21, for example.](undefined)[If we did that, we'd then come down here and enter in the](undefined)[MAC address of the host that will always get this IP address.](undefined)[That way, the next time that host comes](undefined)[up, this IP address would be assigned.](undefined)[Why would we do this?](undefined)[Let's say we have an employee who takes their](undefined)[notebook computer home every night and works](undefined)[from home, but we want that employee's notebook](undefined)[computer to have a specific IP address.](undefined)[We can't assign that notebook with a static IP](undefined)[because it wouldn't be able to communicate with the](undefined)[employee's home network, so we create a reservation](undefined)[so that employee gets the same IP address every time.](undefined)[This also assures that that employee is guaranteed a](undefined)[connection if we were to run out of IP address leases.](undefined)[We're not actually going configure](undefined)[reservations today, so we'll click 'Close'.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Scope Options 08:12-09:47
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Notice that there's also an option for scope options.](undefined)[Scope options are additional information that](undefined)[can be delivered via DHCP to our DHCP clients.](undefined)[By default, if we don't configure these, all](undefined)[we're going to give our clients is an IP](undefined)[address and subnet mask, and that's not good.](undefined)[They'll also need DNS information to resolve domain names.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and create options that'll deliver](undefined)[those along with the IP address and subnet mask.](undefined)
            - [First, we want to set our default gateway.](undefined)[I'll put in that gateway information that I found](undefined)[earlier from when we did an ipconfig on our server.](undefined)[That gateway was 192.168.1.1.](undefined)[We add it, and then we need to](undefined)[configure our DNS server as well.](undefined)[As you may recall, I have this server configured](undefined)[as a DNS server, so we need to add it, too.](undefined)[It's 192.168.1.10.](undefined)[Now, whenever the clients receive an IP](undefined)[address from this DHCP server, they'll](undefined)[also receive this additional information.](undefined)
            - [We're almost ready to go.](undefined)[However, notice here, under the](undefined)[scope, we have a red down arrow.](undefined)[That means the scope isn't activated yet.](undefined)[It's all defined but not completely ready to go.](undefined)[The DHCP server isn't using it to](undefined)[hand out IP addressing information.](undefined)[We need to activate it.](undefined)[I'll select it, right-click, and select 'Activate'.](undefined)[I think we're ready to go.](undefined)[The DHCP is running, and the scope is defined.](undefined)[The next step is to test it on a client and see if we](undefined)[successfully receive an IP address and configuration.](undefined)
            - ### Obtain an IP via DHCP on the Client 09:47-10:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's go to our Windows 10 computer.](undefined)[This workstation has been set to obtain an](undefined)[IP address automatically from a DHCP Server.](undefined)[I've opened a command line interface and](undefined)[am going to do an 'ipconfig /release' to](undefined)[release any IP address that I may have.](undefined)[Now I'm going to do an 'ipconfig /renew'.](undefined)[You'll notice that, right away, our workstation](undefined)[went out and grabbed the first IP address in the](undefined)[scope that we configured it to hand out.](undefined)[Remember that we excluded 192.168.1.1](undefined)[through .20 for statically assigned hosts.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 10:23-10:47
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
