            - ### Configure a DHCP6 Server 00:00-00:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going to](undefined)[set up DHCP for IP version 6.](undefined)[The first thing I want to do is make sure](undefined)[that the DHCP server has an address that](undefined)[matches the scope of the IP addresses](undefined)[it will give out to the clients.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and look at our IPv6 address.](undefined)
            - ### Confirm Static IPv6 00:23-00:51
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I'm here on the server. I'm going to](undefined)[click on the Ethernet address. I'm going to right-click my](undefined)[network adapter, go into Properties, and go to my IPv6](undefined)[settings. You can see I've set a static IP address of FC00](undefined):-[1.](undefined)[The network ID, or the subnet prefix length,](undefined)[is 64, and I'm using this same server for my DNS.](undefined)[We'll need to create a scope for](undefined)[IP version 6 that matches this address.](undefined)
            - [I'm going to click Cancel and exit all of these windows.](undefined)
            - ### Configure an IPv6 Scope 00:51-01:58
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We'll go up under Tools and go into DHCP.](undefined)[I'm going to expand this, slide this over,](undefined)[and expand these.](undefined)[To add a scope, we'll just right-click](undefined)[IP version 6 and make a new scope.](undefined)[It says, Welcome to the wizard.](undefined)[I'll give my scope a name.](undefined)[I'm just going to call it CorpNetIPv6 and click Next.](undefined)[I want to go ahead and give the network ID](undefined)[(in IP version 6, we call that the prefix).](undefined)[We'll keep the same prefix that we used](undefined)[on the server, which is FC00](undefined):-[.](undefined)[Let's type that in, 'FC00](undefined)↔['.](undefined)
            - [IP version 6 addresses are eight blocks of](undefined)[four-digit hexadecimal numbers.](undefined)[The prefix is half of that, which is four blocks.](undefined)[In my case, I'm using FC00](undefined):-[.](undefined)[The double colons mean that all](undefined)[of those values are zeros.](undefined)[I could put zeros in there, and it would look like this:](undefined)[FC00:0000:0000:0000.](undefined)[But IPv6 will drop them](undefined)[and display it as FC00](undefined)↔[ anyway,](undefined)[so let's get rid of those zeros.](undefined)
            - [I'm going to go ahead and click Next.](undefined)
            - ### Add Exclusions 01:58-02:20
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [After I click Next, it says, Do you](undefined)[want to add any exclusions?](undefined)[These are addresses that won't be distributed by the server.](undefined)[In our case, I'm going to add in the number 1,](undefined)[which is my server address, and I'll end it at 100.](undefined)[These are IPs that I will use for static assignments.](undefined)[Everything else in this particular](undefined)[network will be good to go. I'll](undefined)[go ahead and click Next.](undefined)
            - ### Scope Lease 02:20-03:08
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [It asks me about the scope lease.](undefined)[What's really different about IP version 6 is that we](undefined)[have a preferred lifetime and a valid lifetime.](undefined)[The preferred lifetime is always](undefined)[less than the valid lifetime.](undefined)[The default preferred lifetime is eight days.](undefined)[The default valid lifetime is 12 days.](undefined)[The preferred lifetime is the amount of time](undefined)[the client can use this new IP address](undefined)[to create new conversations and work as normal.](undefined)
            - [When the preferred lifetime expires,](undefined)[it will still be able to use that IP address](undefined)[to finish off existing conversations,](undefined)[but it won't be allowed to initiate new conversations.](undefined)
            - [I'm going to leave this as the default and click Next.](undefined)[Then it says, Great. You've set up your scope.](undefined)[Do you want to activate it?](undefined)[�We'll leave it on Yes and then click Finish.](undefined)[You can see my scope is set up.](undefined)
            - ### IPv6 Scope Options 03:08-03:54
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [IP version 6 scopes use scope options just like IP version 4 scopes.](undefined)[If you wanted to set up DHCP so that it hands out](undefined)[additional options, we would set those up here.](undefined)[I can go into Configure Options.](undefined)[I'll expand out IPv6, right-click on Scope Options,](undefined)[select Configure Options, and then enter in whatever is needed.](undefined)[I can put in routers, different](undefined)[domain lists, or refresh time.](undefined)[There aren't as many options for IP version 6](undefined)[as you would see for IP version 4, but they are out there.](undefined)[In fact, we could do a domain search list.](undefined)[Let's make it 'CorpNet.xyz'.](undefined)[This will populate the domain suffix list](undefined)[that we would normally see in the network adapter.](undefined)
            - [Let's go over to our client](undefined)[and see what happens with IP version 6.](undefined)
            - ### Obtain IPv6 on a Client 03:54-04:30
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Here we are on our Windows 10 client.](undefined)[I'm just going to do a quick ipconfig release,](undefined)['ipconfig /release6'. Press Enter.](undefined)[You can see that it currently has an fe8 address,](undefined)[which is a link local address, sort of the](undefined)[IP version 6 equivalent of APIPA in IP version 4.](undefined)[Right now, I don't have an IPv6 address from the server.](undefined)[The renew option is very similar to that, as well.](undefined)[I can do an ipconfig /renew6, and it will go](undefined)[out and contact the DHCP server.](undefined)[You can see right away it picked up an IP version 6](undefined)[address in the scope that we had set up.](undefined)
            - ### Unique Local Addresses 04:30-04:49
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [One thing to keep in mind is that I used](undefined)[unique local addresses for this demo.](undefined)[These addresses are not expected to be](undefined)[routable out on the global internet](undefined)[and are comparable to IPv4 private addresses.](undefined)[When implementing your IP version 6 scheme,](undefined)[make sure it will suit your current and future needs.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 04:49-05:02
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[We configured a DHCP6 server with a new scope.](undefined)[We than went to our workstation and](undefined)[obtained an IP version 6 IP address via DHCP.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 6.2.3 DHCP Options
                - Along with an address scope, you also need to define other parameters,       such as lease time and options.
                - ## DHCP Lease Time and Available Leases
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The client can renew the lease when at least half the lease's period has       elapsed (T1 timer) so that it keeps the same IP addressing information. If       the original DHCP server does not respond to the request to renew the       lease, the client attempts to rebind the same lease configuration with any       available DHCP server. By default, this happens after 87.5% of the lease       duration is up (T2 timer). If this fails, the client releases the IP       address and continues to broadcast to discover a server.
                - A long lease time means the client does not have to renew the lease       often, but the DHCP server's available pool of IP addresses is not       replenished frequently. Where IP addresses are in short supply, a short       lease period enables the DHCP server to allocate addresses previously       assigned to hosts that are now not active on the network.
                - A Windows client can be forced to release a lease       by issuing a command such as ipconfig. In Linux, the utility dhclient is       often used for this task, though modern distributions might use       NetworkManager or systemd-networkd.
                - ## DHCP Options
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - When the DHCP server offers a configuration to a client, at a minimum it       must supply an IP address and subnet mask. Typically, it will also supply       other IP-related settings, known as **DHCP           options**. Each option is identified by a tag byte or       decimal value between 0 and 255 (though neither 0 nor 255 can be used as       option values). Some widely used options include the following:
                    - The default gateway (IP address of the router).
                    - The IP address(es) of DNS servers that can act as resolvers for name         queries.
                    - The DNS suffix (domain name) to be used by the client.
                    - Other useful server options, such as time synchronization (NTP), file         transfer (TFTP), or VoIP proxy.
                - A set of default (global) options can be configured on a server-wide       basis. Default options can be overridden by setting scope-specific       options.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 6.2.4 DHCP Reservations and Exclusions
                - One disadvantage of the standard dynamic assignment method is that it does not guarantee that any given client will retain the same IP address over time. There are some cases where it would be advantageous for certain hosts, such as network printers or wireless access points, to retain their IP addresses.
                - One solution is to configure static assignments, using IP addresses outside the DHCP scope. Alternatively, statically assigned addresses can be assigned from a specially configured exclusion range if this is supported by the server. While these solutions are functional, they lose the advantages of centralized configuration management.
                - An alternative approach is to create a **reservation**. A reservation is a mapping of a MAC address or interface ID to a specific IP address within the DHCP server's address pool. When the DHCP server receives a request from the given interface, it always provides the same IP address. This is also referred to as static or fixed address assignment. An automatically allocated reservation refers to an address that is leased permanently to a client. This is distinct from static allocation as the administrator does not predetermine which specific IP address will be leased.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
        - **6.3 APIPA and SLAAC** 
            - ## 6.3.1 Automatic Private IP Addressing
                - A host's IP configuration can either be applied statically, or it can use an autoconfiguration method. Autoconfiguration on an IPv4 network usually means using a Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server.
                - **Automatic Private IP Addressing (APIPA)** was developed by Microsoft as a means for clients that could not contact a DHCP server to communicate on the local network anyway. If a Windows host does not receive a response from a DHCP server within a given time frame, it selects an address at random from the range 169.254.1.1 to 169.254.254.254.
                - These addresses are from one of the address ranges reserved for private addressing (169.254.0.0/16). The first and last subnets are supposed to be unused.
                - This type of addressing is referred to as link local in standards documentation (RFC 3927).
                - APIPA has no mechanism for assigning default gateway or DNS server addresses. Hosts using APIPA are restricted to communicating on the local network.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 6.3.2 IPv6 Interface Autoconfiguration and Testing
                - In IPv6, an interface must always be configured with a link local       address. One or more routable addresses can be assigned to the interface       in addition to the link local address. As with IPv4, you can either assign       a routable IPv6 address statically or use an automatic addressing scheme.       Static address configuration would generally be reserved to routers and       possibly some types of servers.
                - ## Neighbor Discovery Protocol and Router Advertisements
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The Neighbor Discovery (ND) Protocol performs some of the functions on an       IPv6 network that ARP and ICMP perform under IPv4. The main functions of       ND are as follows:
                    - **Address autoconfiguration**—Enables a host to         configure IPv6 addresses for its interfaces automatically and detect         whether an address is already in use on the local network, by using         neighbor solicitation (NS) and neighbor advertisement (NA) messages.
                    - **Prefix discovery**—Enables a host to discover the         known network prefixes that have been allocated to the local segment.         This facilitates next-hop determination (whether a packet should be         addressed to a local host or a router). Prefix discovery uses router         solicitation (RS) and **router           advertisement (RA)** messages. An RA contains information about         the network prefix(es) served by the router, information about         autoconfiguration options, plus information about link parameters, such         as the MTU and hop limit. Routers send RAs periodically and in response         to a router solicitation initiated by the host.
                    - **Local address resolution**—Allows a host to discover         other nodes and routers on the local network (neighbors). This process         also uses neighbor solicitation (NS) and neighbor advertisement (NA)         messages.
                    - **Redirection**—Enables a router to inform a host of a         better route to a particular destination.
                - ## Stateless Address Autoconfiguration
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - IPv4 has a system for generating link local addresses, but these are not       routable outside the local network. Consequently, IPv4 depends heavily on       the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) for address       autoconfiguration. IPv6 uses a more flexible system of address       autoconfiguration called **stateless         address autoconfiguration (SLAAC)**:
                    - The host generates a link local address and uses Neighbor Discovery         (ND) messages to test that it is unique.
                    - The host listens for a router advertisement (RA) or transmits a router         solicitation (RS) using ND protocol messaging. The router can either         provide a network prefix, direct the host to a DHCPv6 server to perform         stateful autoconfiguration, or perform some combination of stateless and         stateful configuration.
                - ## ICMPv6
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - IPv6 uses an updated version of ICMP. The key new features are the       following:
                    - **Error messaging**—ICMPv6 supports the same sort of         destination unreachable and time exceeded messaging as ICMPv4. One         change is the introduction of a Packet Too Big class of error. Under         IPv6, routers are no longer responsible for packet fragmentation and         reassembly, so the host must ensure that they fit in the MTUs of the         various links used.
                    - **Informational messaging**—ICMPv6 supports ICMPv4         functions, such as echo and redirect, plus a whole new class of messages         designed to support ND and MLD, such as router and neighbor         advertisements and solicitations.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - 00:001. Lease Address and Auto Configuration in IPV6

            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Lease Address and Auto Configuration in IPV6 00:00-00:31
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Stateless Address Auto Configuration (SLAAC) is a method](undefined)[of IPV6 host configuration where each device will get its](undefined)[own IPV6 address without the need of a DHCP server.](undefined)[It's a really convenient and easy](undefined)[way to configure these IPV6 networks.](undefined)[Now, in Windows 11 and the latest version of Windows](undefined)[10, this is configured automatically on your device.](undefined)[However, some networks may have older devices in use.](undefined)
            - ### Configuring or Verifying IPV6 Setup 00:31-01:34
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's take a look at how to either](undefined)[configure or verify that this is set up.](undefined)[We'll open up the Control Panel,](undefined)[then open up Network and Internet.](undefined)[Next, we go to the Network and Sharing](undefined)[Center, and we will change adapter settings.](undefined)[We need to open up the properties of the Ethernet adapter](undefined)[that will be used in order to communicate on the network.](undefined)[Once we do that, we'll open up the IPV6 properties.](undefined)[Here, we can see that "Obtain an](undefined)[IPV6 address" is already checked.](undefined)[We also see that "Obtain DNS server](undefined)[address automatically" is checked as well.](undefined)[Once we open up the advanced tab, we can also](undefined)[see that "Append primary and connection-specific](undefined)[DNS suffixes" is already selected and "Registers](undefined)[connections' addresses in DNS."](undefined)[This means that the computer will automatically create its](undefined)[own IPV6 address and register it with the DNS server in](undefined)[order to properly forward and route data on an IPV6 network.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 6.3.3 DHCPv6 Server Configuration
                - IPv6's Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC) process can locate the default gateway and generate a host address with a suitable network prefix automatically. In this context, the role of a DHCP server in IPv6 is different. DHCPv6 is often just used to provide additional option settings, rather than leases for host IP addresses. The format of messages is different, but the process of DHCP server discovery and address leasing (if offered) is fundamentally the same. As IPv6 does not support broadcast, clients use the multicast address `ff02::1:2` to discover a DHCP server. DHCPv6 uses ports 546 (clients) and 547 (servers), rather than ports 68 and 67 as in DHCPv4.
                - In stateless mode, a client obtains a network prefix from a router advertisement and uses it with the appropriate interface ID. The router can also set a combination of flags to tell the client that a DHCP server is available. If so configured, the client solicits a DHCPv6 server using the multicast address ff02:-1:2 and requests additional configuration information.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/b1CU3KBLOLe2jZhIacThL4BdHVMjETtMizCSUeOeHa6O49UebmMnY_j9Y8-bdgNKdjJrkUshkL2m5upV34iwS1VmYP16o9BHQjqwKT9ONMxB1LZwXfvNWAscWt9AUzwr.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1. NDP Router solicitation “I need information form the router.”
            - 2. NDP Router Advertisement “Here’s the network information so you can create your I P v 6 address but you need to get other information such as D N S addresses from a DHCPv6 server.”
            - 3. DHCPv6 Solicit Message “I need a DHCPv6 Server” “Multicast address ff02:-1:2.”
            - 4. DHCPv6 Advertise Message “I’m a DHCPv6 Server.”
            - 5. DHCPv6 Information Request Message “I need some extra IP information.”
            - 6. DHCPv6 Reply Message “Here is your extra information” DNS, SIP, SNTP, Domain options etc.
            -  *DHCPv6 stateless mode. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - By contrast, stateful mode means that a host can also obtain a routable IP address from a DHCPv6 scope. In either mode, a DHCPv6 server can be used to supply options information, such as DNS server addresses, DNS suffix/domain lists, time servers, and so on.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/20jcta3Z84rv_DJcB4iUdVmwp1omI5yzEEpX9nf8vM3WLTM3vK4K0JtPVnCiXHeZE1lgEYThm7psn0gE7U1Rp7RcNK7WzYRHq7nk2kkMXztiuj1nxOO4jrB-emK0KE7m.png)
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1. NDP Router Solicitation “I need information from the router.”
            - 2. NDP Router Advertisement “I can't help you. Ask a DHCPv6 server for all your information.”
            - 3. DHCPv6 Solicit Message “I need a DHCPv6 server.” “multicast address ff02:-1:2.”
            - 4. DHCPv6 Advertise Message “I’m a DHCPv6 Server.”
            - 5. DHCPv6 Information Request Message “I need IP addressing and extra information.”
            - 6. DHCPv6 Reply Message “Here is your I P address from my scope and extra information” DNS, SIP, SNTP, Domain options etc.
            -  *DHCPv6 stateful mode. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - Configuring the scope requires you to define the network prefix and then any IP addresses that are to be excluded from being offered. All other addresses that are not explicitly excluded can be offered. The host must still listen for a router advertisement to obtain the network prefix and configure a default gateway. There is no mechanism in DHCPv6 for setting the default route.
                - When using stateful DHCPv6, it is possible to configure a static reservation. However, a DHCPv6 reservation doesn't use a MAC address. Instead, each system generates a host DHCP Unique Identifier (DUID) plus an Identity Association Identifier (IAID) for each interface.
            - 
        - **6.4 DHCP Relay and Troubleshooting** 
            - ## 6.4.1 DHCP Relay and IP Helper
                - Normally, routers do not forward broadcast traffic. This means that each broadcast domain must be served by its own DHCP server. On a large network with multiple subnets, this would mean provisioning and configuring many DHCP servers. To avoid this scenario, a **DHCP relay** agent can be configured to provide forwarding of DHCP traffic between subnets. Routers that can provide this type of forwarding are described as RFC 1542 compliant.
                - The DHCP relay intercepts broadcast DHCP frames, applies a unicast address for the appropriate DHCP server, and forwards them over the interface for the subnet containing the server. The DHCP server can identify the original IP subnet from the packet and offer a lease from the appropriate scope. The DHCP relay also performs the reverse process of directing responses from the server to the appropriate client subnet.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/jBQqIzqab-yDpLVgmBi2Ytj8y3e90i6k96gqEPMgeDo59ygswJFqyxbsdbDkFl9yMHdEEjwUg9lX7P9VJWLHQgsaRSAFnIv6_A3LpQtPJFIq1DA_ToIu-rNoQmuTZNxf.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The illustration shows router or D H C P relay agent connected to three switches via eth0, eth1, and eth2 in the network addresses, 10.1.0.0 slash 24, 10.1.10.0 slash 24, and 10.1.20.0 slash 24. The first switch is connected to clients. The second switch is connected to clients. The third switch is connected to D H C P server (10.1.0.200).
            -  *Configuring a DHCP relay agent. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - 
            - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_dhcp_relay_n09/v_dhcp_relay_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_dhcp_relay_n09/v_dhcp_relay_n09.jpg)

                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment

            - ### Initial DHCP Discovery 00:00-00:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [When a client first connects to the network,](undefined)[it sends out a DHCP discover packet.](undefined)[This packet is a UDP broadcast sent out](undefined)[on Port 67 to all devices on the network](undefined)[asking if there's a DHCP server available.](undefined)
            - ### Local DHCP Server Response 00:15-00:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now, if the DHCP server is on our local](undefined)[network, it will respond and start the process.](undefined)[However, if the server is not on our local network,](undefined)[this DHCP discover packet will hit the router and drop](undefined)[because the router won't forward on a broadcast message.](undefined)
            - ### Introduction of DHCP Relay 00:32-00:42
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [This is where the DHCP relay comes in.](undefined)[Now on our network we'll configure our router to](undefined)[act as a DHCP relay this time when the client sends.](undefined)
            - ### DHCP Relay in Action 00:42-00:58
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [About the DHCP discover broadcast message on UDP port 67,](undefined)[our router will recognize the packet and forward it on.](undefined)[The server sees the unicast message and sends back the](undefined)[DHCP offer packet, but it's sent to the relay agent.](undefined)
            - ### Relay Agent Processing 00:58-01:16
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The relay agent puts the DHCP information](undefined)[into a DHCP broadcast packet and sends it](undefined)[back out to the network on UDP port 68.](undefined)[The client receives the packet and then sends](undefined)[out the DHCP request packet to the relay](undefined)[agent, who forwards that onto the server.](undefined)
            - ### Final DHCP Acknowledgement 01:16-01:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Finally, the server sends back the DHCP act](undefined)[and passes it through the DHCP relay agent.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - This **IP helper** functionality can be configured on routers to allow set types of broadcast traffic (including DHCP) to be forwarded to an interface. The IP helper function supports the function of the DHCP relay agent. For example, in the diagram, hosts in the 10.1.20.0/24 and 10.1.10.0/24 subnets need to use a DHCP server for autoconfiguration, but the DHCP server is located in a different subnet. The router is configured as a DHCP relay agent, using the following commands to enable forwarding of DHCP broadcasts on the interfaces serving the client subnets:
            - `interface eth1`
            - `ip helper-address 10.1.0.200`
            - `interface eth2`
            - `ip helper-address 10.1.0.200`
            - UDP forwarding is a more general application of the same principle. As well as DHCP, it is used for the Network Time Protocol (NTP) and other broadcast-based applications.
            - 
            -

            - ### DHCP Relay Agent Setup Overview 00:00-01:53
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going](undefined)[to configure a DHCP relay agent.](undefined)[Before we jump onto our servers and start configuring](undefined)[things, let's take a moment to look at our network setup.](undefined)
            - [I have two subnets on this network.](undefined)[On the left, I have Network-A with](undefined)[the network address 192.168.0.0/24.](undefined)[And over to the right, I have Network-B](undefined)[with the network address 192.168.5.0/24](undefined)
            - [These two networks are separated by this Windows 2016](undefined)[server that's configured with Routing and Remote Access.](undefined)[I have PCs on each network.](undefined)[And on Network-A, I also have another Windows](undefined)[server that's acting as my DNS and DHCP server.](undefined)[My router's job is to keep packets from going back and](undefined)[forth between networks unless that's their destination.](undefined)[The router won't forward broadcast traffic between the](undefined)[two networks, and that's a problem in this scenario.](undefined)
            - [You see, PC2 is configured as a DHCP client.](undefined)[And when PC2 is turned on, it starts looking for an](undefined)[IP address by sending out a DHCP broadcast request.](undefined)[The problem is that this broadcast never makes](undefined)[it past the router, and our DHCP server, over](undefined)[here, will never see that request sent by PC2.](undefined)
            - [Now, PC1 doesn't have a problem because it's](undefined)[on Network-A, the same as the DHCP server.](undefined)[Only devices on Network-B are going to have issues.](undefined)[Eventually, PC2 will give up and fail over to APIPA.](undefined)[To fix this, we need to go into both servers](undefined)[and make some changes so that this works, and](undefined)[that's what we're going to do next.](undefined)
            - [One quick note is that this router could be something other](undefined)[than a Windows server, such as a Layer 3 switch that can be](undefined)[configured as a DHCP Relay Agent or another routing device.](undefined)[Okay, let's get started.](undefined)
            - ### Set Up the DHCP Server with new Scope 01:53-03:27
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I'm on our DHCP server.](undefined)[The first thing we want to do before we set up](undefined)[the relay agent on the router is make a DHCP](undefined)[scope to accommodate the clients on Network-B.](undefined)[I'm going to go up to Tools and select DHCP.](undefined)[I'm going to right-click and do a New Scope,](undefined)[click Next, and we'll call this scope Network-B.](undefined)[We'll set it to run from 192.168.5.100](undefined)[to 192.168.5.199 and click Next.](undefined)
            - [I'm not going to exclude any IPs](undefined)[since my scope is from 100 to 199.](undefined)[I'll have those first 99 addresses for](undefined)[any static addresses that I'll need.](undefined)[I'll hit Next.](undefined)
            - [I'll leave the default lease set](undefined)[to eight days and click Next.](undefined)
            - [Yes, we will configure these options now.](undefined)
            - [I'll type in my router address, which is 192.168.5.1.](undefined)[That's the interface that connects the router to Network-B.](undefined)[I'm going to go ahead and add that in.](undefined)
            - [I'm fine with the domain, CorpNet.xyz.](undefined)[My DNS server is 192.168.0.2.](undefined)[No Wins Server.](undefined)[I'll go ahead and activate the](undefined)[scope, click Next, and then Finish.](undefined)
            - [Now I have two scopes set up, one for my 0.0 network,](undefined)[Network-A, and one for my 5.0 network, Network-B.](undefined)[Now let's go over to our router](undefined)[and configure out DHCP Relay.](undefined)
            - ### Configure the Router 03:27-04:47
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Okay.](undefined)[I'm on my router, and I'm going to set](undefined)[it up to relay DHCP broadcast messages.](undefined)[Let's go over to Local Server, and you can](undefined)[see my two IP addresses and my two interfaces.](undefined)[I'm going to go up into Tools and](undefined)[open up Routing and Remote Access.](undefined)
            - [The first thing I need to do is add in the DHCP relay agent.](undefined)[I'm going to right-click General](undefined)[and do a new routing protocol.](undefined)[The one we want is DHCP relay agent,](undefined)[which is already highlighted for me.](undefined)
            - [The next thing I need to do is tell the DHCP relay agent](undefined)[which network it should listen to for DHCP broadcasts.](undefined)[I'm going to right-click and do a New Interface.](undefined)[In this case, I want it to listen on the](undefined)[5.0 network, which is actually Ethernet 2.](undefined)[I'll click OK and then OK again.](undefined)[Now that my DHCP relay agent is listening on](undefined)[the 192.168.5.0 network, the last piece is for](undefined)[me to tell it where to send those DHCP packets.](undefined)[I'm going to right-click, go to](undefined)[Properties, and say, "All right.](undefined)[You're going to relay this to 192.168.0.2,](undefined)[which is the IP address of my DHCP server."](undefined)[I add that in and click OK.](undefined)[That should be it.](undefined)[Now let's test it out on PC2 and see if we can](undefined)[successfully obtain an IP address from our DHCP Server.](undefined)
            - ### Test on PC2 04:47-05:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Okay, I'm on PC2 on Network-B.](undefined)[I'm going to open up a command prompt and enter](undefined)['Ipconfig /release' and then 'Ipconfig /renew'.](undefined)[You can see that I obtained the IP address 192.168.5.100,](undefined)[the first IP address in our scope for Network-B.](undefined)[It gave me my default gateway.](undefined)[Now let's do 'Ipconfig /all'.](undefined)[Here you can see my DHCP server is 192.168.0.2,](undefined)[and I have also contained my DNS information here.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 05:24-05:53
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demonstration, we looked at our](undefined)[network setup and determined that we needed](undefined)[to relay DHCP broadcasts through our router.](undefined)
            - [First, we set up a DHCP scope for our Network-B](undefined)[subnet, and then we configured our router to](undefined)[send those DHCP requests to our DHCP server.](undefined)[Then we verified that our client is able to](undefined)[receive an IP address from our DHCP server.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 6.4.2 DHCP Issues
                - A Windows host that is configured to use dynamic addressing but that fails to obtain a lease will revert to an automatic IP address (APIPA) configuration and select an address in the 169.254.0.0/16 range. Linux might use link local addressing, set the address to unknown (0.0.0.0), or leave the interface unconfigured.
                - Possible reasons for a client to fail to obtain a lease include the following:
                    - The DHCP server is offline. If your DHCP servers go offline, users will continue to connect to the network for a period and thereafter start to lose contact with network services and servers as they come to try to renew a lease.
                    - No more addresses available (DHCP scope exhaustion). Create a new scope with enough addresses or reduce the lease period. A shorter lease period can mitigate exhaustion issues in networks with high client turnover, such as guest Wi-Fi. IP Address Management (IPAM) software suites can be used to track address usage across a complex DHCP infrastructure.
                    - The router between the client and DHCP server doesn't support BOOTP forwarding. Either install RFC 1542-compliant routers or add another type of DHCP relay agent to each subnet or VLAN.
                - If you reconfigure your DHCP servers and their scopes, you will need to plan for the fact that not all clients' IP configurations will be updated when the server scopes are edited and could be left with an expired IP, default gateway, or DNS server address. You can mitigate this by lowering the lease duration in advance of changes, forcing all clients to renew, or running parallel settings for a period.
                - Also be aware that address pool exhaustion might be a symptom of a malicious attack.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
        - **6.5 Domain Name System** 
            - ## 6.5.1 Host Names and Domain Names
                - The Internet Protocol uses a binary IP address to locate a host on an internetwork. The dotted decimal (IPv4) or hex (IPv6) representation of this IP address is used for configuration purposes, but it is not easy for people to remember. For this reason, a "friendly" name is also typically assigned to each host. There are two types of names: host names and fully qualified domain names (FQDNs).
                - A **host name** is assigned to a computer by the administrator, usually when the OS is installed. The host name needs to be unique on the local network.
                - To avoid the possibility of duplicate host names on the Internet, a **fully qualified domain name (FQDN)** is used to provide a unique identity for the host belonging to a particular network. An example of an FQDN might be `nut.widget.example.`An FQDN is made up of the host name and a domain suffix. In the example, the host name is `nut,` and the domain suffix is `widget.example.` This domain suffix consists of the domain name `widget` within the top-level domain (TLD) `.example`. A domain suffix could also contain subdomains between the host and domain name. The trailing dot or period represents the root of the hierarchy.
                - When you are configuring name records, an FQDN must include the trailing period to represent the root, but this can be omitted in most other use cases.
                - A domain name must be registered with a registrar to ensure that it is unique within a top-level domain. Once a domain name has been registered, it cannot be used by another organization. The same domain name may be registered within different top-level domains, however—`widget.example.` and `widget.example.uk.` are distinct domains, for instance.
                - Numerous hosts may exist within a single domain. For example: `nut`, `bolt`, and `washer` might all be hosts within the `widget.example.` domain. Given that, FQDNs must follow certain rules:
                    - The host name must be unique within the domain.
                    - The total length of an FQDN cannot exceed 253 characters, with each label (part of the name defined by a period) no more than 63 characters (excluding the periods).
                    - A DNS label should use letter, digit, and hyphen characters only. A label should not start with a hyphen. Punctuation characters such as the period (.) or forward slash (/) should not be used.
                    - DNS labels are not case-sensitive.
                - Additionally, Internet registries may have their own restrictions.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 6.5.2 DNS Hierarchy
                - The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is a global hierarchy of distributed name server databases that contain information on domains and hosts within those domains. At the top of the DNS hierarchy is the root, which is represented by the null label, consisting of just a period (.). There are 13 root level servers (A to M).
                - Immediately below the root lie the top-level domains (TLDs). There are several types of top-level domains, but the most prevalent are generic (such as .com, .org, .net, .info, .biz), sponsored (such as .gov, .edu), and country code (such as .uk, .ca, .de). DNS is operated by ICANN ([icann.org](https://www.icann.org/)), which also manages the generic TLDs. Country codes are generally managed by an organization appointed by the relevant government.
                - Information about a domain is found by tracing records from the root down through the hierarchy. The root DNS servers have complete information about the top-level domain servers. In turn, these servers have information relating to servers for the second level domains. No name server has complete information about all domains. Records within the DNS tell them where an authoritative name server for the missing information is found.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/vztVZCeKL2BxPXtcCdVjh52-aubg_4JlqnUT9_H9oOto0lDSZb2_Xx8r-F-L4RpOVtaEBuJliqgwkX15ZuNtuOu4ZfSXOPd53hHla8WSY2f_tVlTOF9pVCkaA20qI3lM.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The illustration shows six levels. Root: dot. Top level domain: uk, net, and com. Country code subdomain: co. Domain: 515 web and 515 support. Subdomain: corp. Resource records: w w w and p c. Example: www dot 515 web dot net and p c dot corp dot 515 support dot com. Dot leads to uk, net, and com. uk leads to co. net leads to 515 web. com leads to 515 support. 515 web leads to w w w. 515 support leads to corp. corp leads to p c.
            -  *DNS hierarchy. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - An FQDN reflects this hierarchy, from most specific on the left (the host's resource record with its name:IP address mapping) to least specific on the right (the TLD followed by the root). An example is  `pc.corp.515support.com.`
            - ## 6.5.3 Name Resolution Using DNS
                - The signal for the name resolution process to commence occurs when a user presents an FQDN (often within a web address) to an application program, such as a web browser. The client application, referred to as a stub resolver, checks its local cache for the mapping. If no mapping is cached, it forwards the query to its local name server. The IP addresses of one or more name servers that can act as resolvers are usually set in the TCP/IP configuration. The resolution process then takes place as follows:
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/TUXujuDbdr2D3pgMahXAlXfYUB6muq_9RsBEITIEI4StwBRzhWU78RSGxA34DO7NFFc_kRSsN783r2mGJoTn6J6BhavQqQwsqrzbBQfFJrhMsCEPD2VfxhWmexY-dx_R.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1. Client queries www dot 515 web dot net
            - 2. Local name server is not authoritative is 515 web dot net and does not have a cached record. It is configured to perform recursive querying and so contacts a root server using a pre-configured list of I P addresses.
            - 3. Root server is not authoritative for the record but returns the server for the .net T L D.
            - 4. net T L D is not authoritative but returns the name server for 515 web dot net.
            - 5. The 515 web dot net name server is authoritative for the domain and returns the requested www dot 515 web dot net host record.
                - 6. The local server caches the record and responds to the client.
                - 7. The client now has an IP address for www dot 515 web dot net, which it caches.
            -  *DNS name resolution process. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - Most queries between name servers are performed as **iterative lookups**. This means that a name server responds to a query with either the requested record or the address of a name server at a lower level in the hierarchy that is authoritative for the namespace. It makes no effort to try to make additional queries to locate information that it does not have. In the figure, at steps 3 and 4, the root server and .net name server simply pass the querying server the address of an authoritative name server. They do not take on the task of resolving the original query for www.515web.net.
                - A **recursive lookup** means that if the queried server is not authoritative, it does take on the task of querying other name servers until it finds the requested record or times out. The name servers listed in a client's TCP/IP configuration accept recursive queries. This is the type of querying performed by the corp.515support.com name server.
                - A DNS server may be configured to only perform recursive querying (a resolver), or it may perform recursive querying and maintain zone records, or it may only maintain zone records. Usually the roles are split, especially if the servers are open to the Internet. Most Internet-accessible DNS servers disable recursive queries. Recursive resolvers are typically only accessible by authorized clients—subscribers within an ISP's network or clients on a private LAN, for instance.
            - ## 6.5.4 Resource Record Types
                - DNS name servers maintain the DNS namespace in zones. A single zone namespace might host records for multiple domains. Conversely, subdomains within a domain might be managed as multiple zones, possibly hosted on multiple servers.
                - A DNS zone will contain numerous **resource records**. These records allow a DNS name server to resolve queries for names and services hosted in the domain into IP addresses. Resource records can be created and updated manually (statically), or they can be generated dynamically from information received from client and server computers on the network.
                - The Start of Authority (SOA) record identifies the primary **authoritative name server** that maintains complete resource records for the zone. The primary name server can be used to modify resource records. The SOA also includes contact information for the zone and a serial number for version control.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/-tSAA2HPK_wa3MiYyYN54C90o2gu3gmftZsLxutoSx1SS0dISkjTSnad25yJqy1nMO4mhRC2grTRCch6LQnj9xQ_8vtDOabIxrpTc-voJblV8jtn0Qdx7NhWaRnUG5sk.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The screenshot shows six tabs at the top from which start of authority (SOA). It includes fields for serial number, primary server, responsible person, refresh interval, retry interval, expires after, minimum (default) TTL, and TTL for this record.
            -  *Configuring a Start of Authority record in Windows DNS. (Screenshot courtesy of Microsoft.)* 
            - Name server (NS) records identify authoritative DNS name servers for the zone. As well as the primary name server, most zones are configured with secondary name servers for redundancy and load balancing. Secondary name servers hold read-only copies of resource records but can still be authoritative for the zone.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/xG67GdIzhRhz_uEULELRx2qFO5AiypKFpAj_gAiDtl-osRZuuy9XPkT-mXg3f0U104N3nRWAiMgndkPvIkC7s8MvqBcgfdqt7yanbblaBRUyACxMUkjmnv8a2uQIqyck.png)
                -  *Resource records configured on a BIND DNS server.* 
            - ## 6.5.5 Host Address and Canonical Name Records
                - An address (A) record is used to resolve a host name to an IPv4 address. An AAAA record resolves a host name to an IPv6 address.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/xf04smphzjzc75hZtZ07Qstuwbd1YPYEqLyWAZxKqyNdUGma9mly0mYsxKZNPa_KV7Jo1f2FSk93qOhbnGz5IY1dydsr0Gt5pJgYx-GyJ82rzj3r7ZWZe41J9ZQxzJkp.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - In the left pane, corp dot 515 support dot com under forward lockup zones of D C 10 is selected. The right pane shows details in a table format. The header of the table are name, type, data, and timestamp.
            -  *Both types of host records (A and AAAA) plus a CNAME record in Windows Server DNS. (Screenshot courtesy of Microsoft.)* 
            - DNS uses the UDP transport protocol over port 53 by default, and UDP has a maximum packet size of 512 bytes. Due to the larger address sizes of IPv6, AAAA records can exceed this limit. This can result in UDP packets being fragmented into several smaller packets. This can result in these packets being blocked by firewalls if they are not configured to expect them. Network administrators should check that their DNS servers can accept these transmissions and that intermediary components are not blocking them.
                - A canonical name (CNAME) (or alias) record is used to configure an alias for an existing address record (A or AAAA). For example, the IP address of a web server with the host record `lamp` could also be resolved by the alias `www`. CNAME records are also often used to make DNS administration easier. For example, an alias can be redirected to a completely different host temporarily during system maintenance.
                - Multiple different-named resource records can refer to the same IP address (and vice versa in the case of load balancing).
                - There is nothing to stop an administrator configuring multiple address records to point different host names to the same IP address. Using CNAME records is usually considered better practice, however. It is also possible to configure multiple A or AAAA records with the same host name but different IP addresses. This is usually done as a basic load balancing technique, referred to as round robin DNS.
                - 
            - A name server can be configured to allow automatic creation, updating, and deletion of host records using Dynamic DNS (DDNS). DDNS allows a client or DHCP server to configure records, rather than requiring the DNS server administrator to create and update them manually. In Windows, running ipconfig /registerdns causes a client to attempt to use DDNS.
            - ## 6.5.6 Mail Exchange, Service, and Text Records
                - A mail exchange (MX) record is used to identify an email server for the domain. In a typical network, multiple servers are installed to provide redundancy, and each one will be represented with an MX record. Each server record is given a preference value with the lowest numbered entry preferred. The host identified in an MX record must have an associated A or AAAA record. An MX record must not point to a CNAME record.
                - While most DNS records are used to resolve a name into an IP address, a Service (SRV) record contains the service name and port on which a particular application is hosted. SRV records are often used to locate VoIP or media servers. SRV records are also an essential part of the infrastructure supporting Microsoft’s Active Directory; they are used by clients to locate domain controllers, for instance. As with MX, SRV records can be configured with a priority value.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/aFU-oFUrv1Kq0jrVL1uPuBR10tA7qaHRvoyjA3K_KGk4z_cpAKWjuVzVuoS9nETcmEWTWHC1rh4FSOCBQj25uGf6ryRwJVT5nxQdKuKHC36d3f7baxhdgPED0cAVcdvy.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - In the left pane, dash t c p under corp dot 515 support dot com of forward lockup zones is selected. The right pane shows details in a table format. The header of the table are name, type, data, and timestamp.
            -  *SRV records in Windows Server DNS. (Screenshot courtesy of Microsoft.)* 
            - A TXT record is used to store any free-form text that may be needed to support other network services. A single domain name may have many TXT records, but most commonly they are used as part of Sender Policy Framework (SPF) and DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM). An SPF record is used to list the IP addresses or names of servers that are permitted to send email from a particular domain and is used to combat the sending of spam. DKIM records are used to decide whether you should allow received email from a given source, preventing spam and mail spoofing. DKIM can use encrypted signatures to prove that a message really originated from the domain it claims.
            - ## 6.5.7 Pointer Records
                - A DNS server may have two types of zones: forward lookup and reverse lookup. Forward lookup zones contain the resource records listed previously. For example, given a name record, a forward lookup returns an IP address; an MX record returns a host record associated with the domain's mail services. Conversely, a **reverse DNS** query returns the host name associated with a given IP address. This information is stored in a reverse lookup zone as a pointer (PTR) record.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/85USFSwUfu7QiVMEXmXk81-Z_8Za9_oXrg3kHptYormRMALRJuF5M_3kjX05GC2Cr7zpohmpm-t9tnLUCfbZkdhk-prJs5c4Mf0EXKVYpHGVMGlVjKGBYWIUI2bqUDkY.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - In the left pane, a folder under reverse lockup zones lockup zones is selected. The right pane shows details in a table format. The header of the table are name, type, data, and timestamp.
            -  *Reverse lookup zone and pointer records in Windows Server DNS. (Screenshot courtesy of Microsoft.)* 
            - Reverse DNS querying uses a special domain named by the first three octets of IP addresses in the zone in reverse order and appended with `in-addr.arpa`. The name server is configured with a reverse lookup zone. This zone contains PTR records consisting of the final octet of each host record. For example, the reverse lookup for a host record containing the IP address 198.51.100.1 is:
                - `1.100.51.198.in-addr.arpa`
                - IPv6 uses the ip6.arpa domain; each of the 32 hex characters in the IPv6 address is expressed in reverse order as a subdomain. For example, the IPv6 address:
                - `2001:0db8:0000:0000:0bcd:abcd:ef12:1234`
                - is represented by the following pointer record:
                - `4.3.2.1.2.1.f.e.d.c.b.a.d.c.b.0.0.0.0.0.0.0.0.0.8.b.d.0.1.0.0.2.ip6.arpa`
            - ## 6.5.8 DNS Server Configuration
                - DNS is essential to the function of the Internet. Windows Active Directory and most Linux networks also require a DNS service to be running and correctly configured. It is important to realize that there are different kinds of DNS servers however, fulfilling different roles in network architecture.
                - ## DNS Server Types
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A DNS server is usually configured to listen for queries on UDP port 53. Some DNS servers are also configured to allow connections over TCP port 53, as this allows larger record transfers (over 512 bytes). Larger transfers might be required if IPv6 is deployed on the network or if the DNS servers are using a security protocol (DNSSEC).
                - A name server can maintain primary and/or secondary zones:
                    - Primary means that the zone records held on the server are editable. A zone can be hosted by multiple primary servers for redundancy. As the zone records are editable on all primaries, changes must be carefully replicated and synchronized. It is critically important to update the serial number for each change.
                    - Secondary means that the server holds a read-only copy of the zone. This is maintained through a process of replication known as a **zone transfer** from a primary name server. A secondary zone would typically be provided on two or more separate servers to provide fault tolerance and load balancing. Again, the serial number is a critical part of the zone transfer process.
                - A name server that holds complete records for a domain can be defined as authoritative. This means that a record in the zone identifies the server as a name server for that namespace. Both primary and secondary name servers are authoritative.
                - Servers that don't maintain a zone (primary or secondary) are referred to as cache-only servers. A non-authoritative answer from a server is one that derives from a cached record, rather than directly from the zone records.
                - ## DNS Caching
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Each resource record can be configured with a default **time to live (TTL)** value, measured in seconds. This value instructs resolvers how long a query result can be kept in cache. Setting a low TTL allows records to be updated more quickly but increases load on the server and latency on client connections to services. Some common TTL values include 300 (five minutes), 3,600 (one hour), 86,400 (one day), and 604,800 (one week).
                - **DNS caching** is performed by both servers and client computers. In fact, each application on a client computer might be configured to manage its own DNS cache. For example, separate web browser applications typically maintain their own caches rather than relying on a shared OS cache.
                - If there is a change to a resource record, server and client caching means that the updated record can be relatively slow to propagate around the Internet. These changes need to be managed carefully to avoid causing outages. Planning for a record change involves reducing the TTL in the period before the change, waiting for this change to propagate before updating the record, and then reverting to the original TTL value when the update has safely propagated.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_dns_n09/d_conf_dns_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment
            -

            - ### Configure DNS 00:00-01:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going to](undefined)[work with the Domain Name System, or DNS.](undefined)[DNS translates host names and domain names](undefined)[into IP addresses that our computer systems](undefined)[can actually use to send and](undefined)[receive information on the network.](undefined)
            - [For example, if I open up a web browser,](undefined)[enter the URL www.testout.com,](undefined)[and hit Enter, I'm able to access the testout.com website.](undefined)[The important thing to remember is that the IP](undefined)[stack on this workstation actually has](undefined)[no clue what www.testout.com means](undefined)[it just sees a bunch of text.](undefined)[Before you can access the TestOut website,](undefined)[a DNS server must translate that domain name,](undefined)[www.testout.com, into an IP address](undefined)[that can receive a request.](undefined)
            - [My workstation is currently set up](undefined)[to use a DNS server on the internet.](undefined)[This is very common in situations where](undefined)[you're using an internet service provider of some sort;](undefined)[they usually provide you with some type](undefined)[of DNS server address that you can use for name resolution.](undefined)[You don't have to if you don't want to.](undefined)[In fact, if you're running networks for](undefined)[any kind of organization, you'll likely](undefined)[be required to manage and maintain your own DNS server.](undefined)[That way, you can resolve host names on the internet](undefined)[and within your organization, as well.](undefined)[We're going to do just that in this demonstration.](undefined)
            - ### Install DNS 01:26-02:57
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I've switched over to my CorpNet-DC1 server.](undefined)[This server is currently running the](undefined)[DHCP service for my network segment.](undefined)[I'm going to add a second role to it, DNS.](undefined)[I'm doing this on a single box because my network is tiny.](undefined)[I just have a few hosts on this network segment;](undefined)[therefore, CorpNet-DC1 can easily handle the load.](undefined)[On a large, heavily used network,](undefined)[you probably wouldn't want to assign](undefined)[too many services to the same server.](undefined)
            - [Let's go ahead and add the DNS server](undefined)[role to our Windows server.](undefined)[I do that by accessing Server Manager,](undefined)[selecting Add Roles and Features, and selecting Next.](undefined)[Keep the Role-based or feature-based installation selected.](undefined)[This server is the only one in the list.](undefined)[Select Next.](undefined)[Select DNS as the role we want to install.](undefined)[Select Add Features. Click Next a](undefined)[few more times. Finally, Install.](undefined)[We will close these dialog boxes.](undefined)[All right. The DNS server role's been installed.](undefined)[I'm going to select Close.](undefined)
            - [The DNS server role has been installed,](undefined)[but it is not currently configured.](undefined)[To do that, I need to access my DNS server management tool.](undefined)[If I expand it, you can see that I have](undefined)[two different types of zones,](undefined)[forward lookup zones and reverse lookup zones.](undefined)[Currently, I have no zones configured whatsoever,](undefined)[and we'll need to change that.](undefined)[Before we do, let's talk about the difference](undefined)[between forward and reverse lookup zones.](undefined)
            - ### Forward Lookup Zone 02:57-03:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A forward lookup zone allows you to](undefined)[resolve a host name such as testout.com](undefined)[into an IP address such as 67.192.129.239](undefined)[(or whatever the IP address for testout.com happens to be).](undefined)[I don't have that IP address memorized.](undefined)[That's why we use DNS in the first place-](undefined)[-because it's hard for human beings](undefined)[to remember complex IP addresses.](undefined)[We remember host names like testout.com much more easily.](undefined)
            - ### Reverse Lookup Zone 03:23-03:42
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A reverse lookup zone does just](undefined)[the opposite of a forward zone.](undefined)[Instead of resolving a host name into an IP address,](undefined)[it resolves an IP address into a host name.](undefined)[We can send an IP address to a DNS server](undefined)[and have it return the host name](undefined)[associated with that IP address.](undefined)
            - ### Primary and Secondary Zones 03:42-04:57
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [As you can see here, we do not have any zones defined.](undefined)[Obviously, the first thing we need to do is](undefined)[define a forward lookup zone and reverse lookup zone.](undefined)[Right-click on Forward Lookup Zone. Then select New Zone.](undefined)[Select Next in the overview window.](undefined)[We have to specify what type of zone we want to create,](undefined)[either a primary zone or a secondary zone.](undefined)[A primary zone is an authoritative zone.](undefined)[It's the zone where you can add records,](undefined)[remove records, and change records.](undefined)[A secondary zone is simply a copy of a primary zone.](undefined)[It's usually provided for fault tolerance purposes](undefined)[and increases the performance of our DNS.](undefined)[The important thing to remember about](undefined)[a secondary zone is the fact that](undefined)[it cannot be edited directly](undefined)[If you need to make](undefined)[a change, you need to make the change to the primary zone, which](undefined)[will then be transferred to the](undefined)[secondary zone through a zone transfer.](undefined)[For our purposes today, we're](undefined)[going to create a primary zone.](undefined)
            - [Let's name the zone CorpNet.xyz. Select Next.](undefined)[We'll use the default file name for the zone.](undefined)[Next.](undefined)[Here we have the option of specifying](undefined)[whether we're going to allow the zone](undefined)[to be updated dynamically from a DHCP server.](undefined)[We do want our DNS to be updated by DHCP.](undefined)[Click Next and then Finish.](undefined)
            - ### Create a Reverse Lookup Zone 04:57-06:27
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [All right. We have the CorpNet.xyz](undefined)[zone. Let's go ahead and create a reverse lookup zone as well.](undefined)[Right-click on Reverse Lookup Zone and click New Zone.](undefined)[Select Next in the screen.](undefined)[It will be a primary zone. Select Next.](undefined)[We want to create an IPv4 reverse lookup zone.](undefined)[Select Next.](undefined)
            - [This is where reverse zones get really confusing.](undefined)[A lot of this confusion comes from their name.](undefined)[A forward lookup zone is really straightforward.](undefined)[For example, we created the CorpNet.xyz](undefined)[zone, and it makes sense.](undefined)[A reverse lookup zone is based on an](undefined)[IP address, not a domain name.](undefined)[We have to name it using the IP address of the](undefined)[associated forward lookup zone.](undefined)[To do this, you have to know the network address,](undefined)[where the hosts in that zone reside.](undefined)[My host resides on the 192.168.0.0 network.](undefined)[Therefore, to name my zone, I enter '192.168.0'](undefined)[in the network ID field (assuming that the last](undefined)[octet is a zero, so we don't have to put it in).](undefined)
            - [Notice down here, when I do this, that the](undefined)[reverse lookup zone name is automatically](undefined)[created for me. But notice that it's backwards,](undefined)[it starts with 0.168.192.](undefined)[If you see a domain name that looks like this,](undefined)[you know automatically that it is a reverse lookup zone.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and select Next.](undefined)[We'll just use the default file name for the zone.](undefined)[Next.](undefined)[We'll allow dynamic updates from a DHCP server.](undefined)[Finish.](undefined)[We have our forward and reverse lookup zones.](undefined)
            - ### Name Server Records 06:27-06:55
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [If we go into our forward lookup zone, CorpNet.xyz,](undefined)[you'll see that we don't have any A records](undefined)[in our forward lookup zone. To change this, we right-click](undefined)[CorpNet.xyz, select New Host, and type in the IP address of our server,](undefined)['192.168.0.2'. We have the option to create a pointer record,](undefined)[but I'm not going to do this at this time.](undefined)[We will select Add Host, OK, and Done.](undefined)
            - ### Pointer Record Creation 06:55-08:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In addition, if we come down here,](undefined)[we can expand our reverse lookup zone,](undefined)[and we can see some records that](undefined)[have been automatically created.](undefined)[We have an A record in our forward lookup zone](undefined)[for CorpNet-DC1, but we do not have a pointer record](undefined)[in our reverse lookup zone that points back to CorpNet-DC1.](undefined)[We need to fix that.](undefined)
            - [Let's right-click on our reverse lookup zone,](undefined)[and let's go to New Pointer Record.](undefined)[We enter the IP address that we want to](undefined)[create the record for, '192.168.0.2'.](undefined)[Then we browse to and select which host](undefined)[in the forward lookup zone this](undefined)[resolves to, and it resolves to our host.](undefined)[We now have a pointer record that](undefined)[will resolve 192.168.0.2 into CorpNet-DC1.CorpNet.xyz.](undefined)[In addition, we have a forward lookup record,](undefined)[an A record that resolves CorpNet-DC1 to 192.168.0.2.](undefined)
            - [You can add new records if you wish.](undefined)[Right-click on CorpNet.xyz. Select New Host.](undefined)[Let's add a record for our CorpNet-DC2 server,](undefined)[which uses an IP address of 192.168.0.3.](undefined)[Notice, down here, there's an option](undefined)[to have your associated pointer record](undefined)[automatically created for you.](undefined)[That's a really useful feature.](undefined)[If I mark that option and then select](undefined)[Add Host, the record is created.](undefined)[We have our CorpNet-DC2 record here](undefined)[that resolves the host name into an IP address.](undefined)[At this point, we're ready to test our server](undefined)[to make sure it's working properly.](undefined)
            - ### Check Workstation Settings  08:28-09:40
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's switch over to our CorpNet-PC3 workstation.](undefined)[This workstation is currently configured](undefined)[with a static IP, and I have manually](undefined)[configured the DNS for a computer from the internet.](undefined)[I want to use the DNS that I just configured](undefined)[and also obtain an IP address from my DHCP server.](undefined)
            - [I'm going to go to the Network and Sharing Center.](undefined)[I'm going to change the settings on](undefined)[both of these to obtain the information automatically.](undefined)[Let's close all of these windows.](undefined)[Let's go to a command line and release our address lease,](undefined)[and then renew it to get the new](undefined)[IP address of the DHCP server.](undefined)
            - [Come down to the Search box. Type 'CMD' and Enter.](undefined)[Let's do an ipconfig /release,](undefined)[an ipconfig /renew,](undefined)[and an ipconfig /all.](undefined)[Let's scroll up.](undefined)[Notice that the DNS server address](undefined)[that we received from the DHCP server is 192.168.0.2.](undefined)[Now, let's type CLS and clear the screen.](undefined)
            - ### Test a DNS Server 09:40-11:12
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's take a moment to test our DNS server.](undefined)[One tool is the nslookup tool.](undefined)[If I type in 'nslookup' and press Enter,](undefined)[you'll see it tells me the name of the DNS server](undefined)[that I am currently using and its IP address.](undefined)
            - [What happens if we need to resolve a host name](undefined)[that's not configured on that DNS server-](undefined)[-for example, the host name of an internet server?](undefined)[Let's enter www.google.com.](undefined)[It's still able to resolve it.](undefined)[Notice here that it resolves the](undefined)[IP version 6 address and the IP version 4 address.](undefined)
            - [How did our DNS server know how to resolve www.google.com?](undefined)[There's no record in our forward lookup](undefined)[or our reverse lookup zone for www.google.com.](undefined)[Let's go over here, in our DNS server manager.](undefined)[Notice there's an option here called Root Hints.](undefined)[If I double-click on it, it provides a list](undefined)[of all the IP addresses of root level](undefined)[servers on the internet that this DNS server](undefined)[can use to resolve host names](undefined)[that it's not authoritative for.](undefined)[Because the DNS server is not authoritative for that zone,](undefined)[it sent a request to the appropriate root-level DNS server,](undefined)[which pointed it to the correct DNS server.](undefined)[It contacted that DNS server and asked it](undefined)[to resolve www.google.com, pulled the information back,](undefined)[and sent it back to the workstation for us.](undefined)
            - [By doing this, any DNS server that's properly configured](undefined)[can resolve both host names that it is authoritative for](undefined)[and host names it's not authoritative for.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 11:12-11:37
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [OK, that is it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demo, we worked with DNS.](undefined)[First, we talked about DNSs role and function.](undefined)[We talked about how you can add the](undefined)[DNS role to a server in your network.](undefined)[We added A records and pointer records](undefined)[to our forward and reverse lookup zones.](undefined)[We talked about how to use nslookup to](undefined)[test our DNS server.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 6.5.9 Internal vs External DNS
                - As well as making sure that resource records for the managed domain(s) are accurate, administrators should ensure that DNS services are highly available and secure, to prevent DNS spoofing, where an attacker is able to supply false name resolutions to clients.
                - A company will use primary and secondary name servers to maintain authoritative zone records for the domains that it manages. Internal DNS zones refer to the domains used on the private network only. These name records should only be available to internal clients. For example, a company might run a Windows Active Directory network using the domain name
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - corp.515support.com
                - . The zone records for the subdomain
            - corp.515support.com
                - would be served from internal name servers. This would allow a client PC (
            - pc1.corp.515support.com
                - ) to contact a local application server (
            - crm.corp.515support.com
                - ). The name servers hosting these internal subdomain records must not be accessible from the Internet.
                - External DNS zones refer to records that Internet clients must be able to access. For example, the company might run web and email services on the domain
            - 515support.com
                - . In order for Internet hosts to use a web server at
            - www.515support.com
                - or send email to an
            - @515support.com
                - address, the zone records for 515support.com must be hosted on a name server that is accessible over the Internet.
                - Companies must also provide name resolution services to support their internal clients contacting other domains. The function of a resolver is to perform recursive queries in response to requests from client systems (stub resolvers). If a name server is not authoritative for the requested domain, it can either perform a recursive query to locate an authoritative name server or it can forward the request to another name server. A recursive resolver must be configured with a root hints file so that it can query the whole DNS hierarchy from the root servers down. DNS servers should allow recursive queries only from authorized internal clients. It is also a good idea to separate the DNS servers used to host zone records from ones used to service client requests for nonauthoritative domains.
                - It is possible for the same DNS server instance to perform in both name server and resolver roles, but more typically these functions are separated to different servers for security reasons.
                - As an alternative to recursion (or to supplement it), name servers can be configured to resolve queries via forwarding. A forwarder transmits a client query to another DNS server and routes the replies it gets back to the client. A conditional forwarder performs this task for certain domains only. For example, you might configure a DNS server that is authoritative for the local private network (internal DNS), but that forwards any requests for Internet domains to an external DNS resolver run by your ISP.
            - ## 6.5.10 DNS Security
                - DNS is a critical service that should be configured to resist spoofing and poisoning attacks. These attacks mean that a threat actor changes the record returned by a DNS query to point to a different IP address, potentially redirecting the victim machine to connect to a malicious host.
                - ## DNS Security Extensions
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - **DNS Security Extensions (DNSSEC)** help to mitigate against spoofing and poisoning attacks on DNS servers by providing a validation process for DNS responses. With DNSSEC enabled, the authoritative server for the zone creates a "package" of resource records (called an RRset) signed with a private key (the Zone Signing Key). When another server requests a secure record exchange, the authoritative server returns the package along with its public key, which can be used to verify the signature.
                - The public Zone Signing Key is itself signed with a separate Key Signing Key. Separate keys are used so that if there is some sort of compromise of the Zone Signing Key, the domain can continue to operate securely by revoking the compromised key and issuing a new one.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Pc267GHLjbhpeCRZsEeVo_fM1TOWxVk5UBgd63v8B_Mxq64_kuNy6hWG4uNVBEJD18MIROEJEtAaVFZAdgtWfsewlSdQYuUO2op2wDKfjvvybKTSDox2kvviI2qS4uw-.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The Action menu is selected from the menus at the top. The left pane shows classroom dot local is selected from the Forward Lookup Zones folder. The right pane provides data on name, type, date, and timestamp.
            -  *Windows Server DNS services with DNSSEC enabled. (Screenshot used with permission from Microsoft.)* 
            - The Key Signing Key for a particular domain is validated by the parent domain or host ISP. The top-level domain trusts are validated by the Regional Internet Registries, and the DNS root servers are self-validated, using a type of M-of-N control group key signing. This establishes a chain of trust from the root servers down to any particular subdomain.
                - ## DNS Client Security
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Where DNSSEC validates the records held by a name server, DNS client security uses transport encryption to prevent an on-path threat actor tampering with responses to DNS queries. There are two main protocols for securing DNS queries:
                    - **DNS over transport layer security (DoT)**—This uses Transport Layer Security (TLS) to validate the resolver name server's digital certificate. This mitigates the risk of a threat actor using a rogue DNS server to spoof the legitimate one. If the client trusts the certificate, the subsequent DNS traffic will be encrypted. DoT works over TCP port 853.
                    - **DNS over hypertext transfer protocol secure (DoH)**—This also validates the resolver certificate and encrypts the DNS traffic but does so by encapsulating it within HTTP Secure packets. This uses the HTTPS standard port TCP/443, which completely disguises the fact that the client is making DNS queries. The downside is that the additional HTTP headers add overhead to each query and response.
                - As well as protecting against malicious attacks, DoH and DoT provide better privacy. Plain text queries can be read by anyone operating a network appliance in the path between the client and resolver. Encrypting the queries and responses prevents this type of snooping. Conversely, administrators of a corporate network need to ensure that clients use authorized resolvers and will often prefer to monitor DNS traffic.
                - 
                - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_dns_cache_n09/d_conf_dns_cache_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)

                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment

            - 00:001. Configure DNS Caching on Linux
