            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Configure DNS Caching on Linux 00:00-00:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [DNS caching is the temporary storage](undefined)[for records of recently visited domains.](undefined)[Domains you haven't visited aren't in cache.](undefined)[By default, DNS caching isn't turned](undefined)[on in Redhat Enterprise Linux.](undefined)[In order to do so, you need to install additional](undefined)[software to make this happen, like dnsmasq.](undefined)[It's a DNS server, but you can use it](undefined)[in standalone mode to process caching.](undefined)
            - ### Install 00:28-00:52
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To install dnsmasq, we type the 'yum install](undefined)[dnsmasq' command and select 'y' since this is OK.](undefined)[If you want to skip this message, you can](undefined)[include the 'y' in the install command.](undefined)[There's some GPG key importing required because this](undefined)[is the first time installing packages for this server.](undefined)[So just click 'y' on both.](undefined)
            - ### Configure dnsmasq 00:52-02:38
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, we need to configure some settings for this to work.](undefined)[The 'cd /etc' command brings us into the directory](undefined)[where most of the configuration files are stored.](undefined)[Using 'vi /etc/dnsmasq.conf' allows us to](undefined)[edit the configuration file for dnsmasq.](undefined)[There are a lot of settings already in here, but](undefined)[they'll be commented out with the pound sign.](undefined)[If you already know which config you want to enter,](undefined)[you can just add it to the bottom of the file.](undefined)[Enter edit mode by hitting 'I'.](undefined)[The 'interface=lo' command will](undefined)[point to our loopback interface.](undefined)[Next, we need to type in 'bind-interfaces'.](undefined)[The config for listening is 'listen-address=127.0.0.1',](undefined)[and we'll specify 'cache-size=1000'.](undefined)[This can be adjusted if more entries need to be](undefined)[cached, but this should be enough for testing.](undefined)['resolve-file=/etc/resolv.dnsmasq' is](undefined)[where dnsmasq will look for DNS servers vs.](undefined)[the general resolv.conf.](undefined)[And lastly, we need to set 'no-poll'.](undefined)[Make sure you save this with ':wq!'.](undefined)
            - [If you noticed, we set a resolv-file,](undefined)[but that file doesn't exist yet.](undefined)[To create it, we just need to](undefined)[type in 'vi /etc/resolv.dnsmasq'.](undefined)[This automatically creates a new](undefined)[file if said file doesn't exist.](undefined)[All we need to put in here is 'nameserver 192.168.30.1'.](undefined)[This would be the DNS server you'd](undefined)[specified in 'resolv.conf', most likely.](undefined)[Our DNS server we just listed probably](undefined)[won't be the same in another network.](undefined)[Save this and then exit the config file.](undefined)
            - ### Change Local Settings 02:38-03:13
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Since we'll be using dnsmasq, we want to make](undefined)[sure everything is processed through there.](undefined)[The general resolv.conf needs to be changed,](undefined)[otherwise dnsmasq won't be able to process any](undefined)[DNS requests, so we do 'vi /etc/resolv.conf'.](undefined)[Notice there are two name servers](undefined)[listed in this config file.](undefined)[Go ahead and comment out both of the existing name servers.](undefined)[Next, we'll add another name server below and type](undefined)['nameserver 127.0.0.1', which is the loopback.](undefined)[Save your settings and exit the config file.](undefined)
            - ### Enable and Test 03:13-04:16
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Even though we set up the configuration](undefined)[for dnsmasq, the service isn't running yet.](undefined)[Let's check the status with 'systemctl status dnsmasq'.](undefined)[Notice it's inactive and not running.](undefined)[We need to set it to auto-start](undefined)[with 'systemctl enable dnsmasq'.](undefined)[This way, whenever this server is rebooted,](undefined)[this server will start automatically.](undefined)[Next, we need to start it by](undefined)[typing 'systemctl start dnsmasq'.](undefined)[You may've noticed how I saved some time for typing.](undefined)[All I did was arrow up and replace](undefined)[the needed text with my next command.](undefined)
            - [We're going to clear our screen and then run some](undefined)[quick tests to see if we can process any DNS requests.](undefined)[If I do an 'nslookup' on 'google.com' or even 'redhat.com',](undefined)[you can see both domains returned with an IP address.](undefined)[This means our DNS resolving is working correctly.](undefined)
            - [Note that some additional configuration may](undefined)[be needed if you're using Network Manager.](undefined)[However, we aren't in this demo.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 04:16-04:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we configured and](undefined)[tested DNS caching with dnsmasq.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll

        - **6.6 DNS Troubleshooting** 
            - ## 6.6.1 Client DNS Issues
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
            -

            - 00:001. Symptom of Name Resolution Problems
            - CC
            - 1.0x
            - Volume Control
            - Interactive ScriptTheater ModeFull Screen
                - Copy Video Transcript
                - Print Video Transcript
            - ## Interactive Script
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - close interactive script
            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Symptom of Name Resolution Problems 00:00-00:19
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A common symptom of name resolution](undefined)[problems is when users on your network open](undefined)[a web browser and try to access a web page.](undefined)[But when they do, they get an error message](undefined)[indicating that the server can't be reached.](undefined)[This is usually because the IP protocol didn't](undefined)[have any idea where it was supposed to go.](undefined)
            - ### Testing DNS Name Resolution 00:19-00:33
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A quick and easy way to test DNS name resolution is](undefined)[to try pinging the desired site by its IP address.](undefined)[If you can ping with the host IP address, you know that](undefined)[basic connectivity exists between those two systems.](undefined)
            - ### Diagnosing with DNS Name 00:33-00:52
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Once that's done, try pinging the same](undefined)[host, but this time using the DNS name.](undefined)[If the ping fails, you know there's a pretty good chance](undefined)[that you have a DNS name resolution problem, since the](undefined)[ping to the IP address verified that there was a network](undefined)[connectivity between the sender and the receiver.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - A DNS issue is typically indicated when a host can ping a server by its IP address, but not by its name.
            - When a host receives a client request to access a name and it does not have the IP mapping cached, it asks a name server configured as a resolver to perform the lookup and return the IP address. As name resolution is a critical service, most hosts are configured with primary and secondary name server resolvers for redundancy. The server addresses are entered as IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. In a majority of cases, these addresses are likely to be autoconfigured via DHCP.
            - If a single client is unable to resolve names, the issue is likely to lie with that client's configuration. In Windows, you can view the name servers configured as resolvers using `ipconfig /all`. In Linux, the DNS server addresses are recorded in `/etc/resolv.conf`. Typically, a package such as NetworkManager or systemd-networkd would add the entries. Entries added directly will be overwritten at reboot.
            - If a host cannot resolve names, check that the correct name server addresses have been configured and that you can ping them. If there are configuration errors, either correct them (if the interface is statically configured) or investigate the automatic addressing server. If there are connectivity errors, check the network path between the host and its name servers.
            - If multiple clients are affected, the issue is likely to lie with the server service (or the way a subnet accesses the server service). Check that the server configured as a DNS resolver is online and available (that you can ping the server from the client). Bear in mind that DHCP might be configuring DNS server settings incorrectly. Check the server options or scope options configuration on the DHCP server as well.
            - While we are focusing on name resolution via DNS here, note that a host can use multiple methods, especially on Windows workgroup networks. Link Local Multicast Name Resolution (LLMNR) and Multicast DNS (mDNS) are modified forms of DNS that allow clients to perform name resolution on a local link without needing a server.
            - 
            - Hosts have a system DNS configuration, but apps such as browsers might use separately configured name servers.
            - ## 6.6.2 Name Resolution Issues
                - If some DNS queries work from the client, but others don't, then the       problem is more complex. When you perform a successful connectivity test       by IP address, and have verified that the host's DNS resolvers are       working, and the target host or service still does not respond to pinging       it by name, you need to check for a fault within the name resolution       process.
                - ## Name Resolution Methods
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - To troubleshoot name resolution, you should establish exactly how the       process works on that specific host. A host can use a variety of methods       to resolve a name to an IP address. In very general terms, these will be       as follows:
                    1. Check local name caches. One complication here is that there are         different types of cache and separate caches for individual         applications, such as web browsers. On Windows, you can use `ipconfig           /displaydns` and `ipconfig /flushdns` to monitor and         clear the system's DNS cache.
                    2. Check HOSTS. The HOSTS file is a static list of host name to IP         address mappings. The local resolver is likely to try to use any HOSTS         file mappings first (or the mappings might be cached automatically). The         default location under Windows is `%SystemRoot%\system32\drivers\etc\`,         while under Linux it is usually placed in the `/etc`directory.         In most cases, HOSTS should not contain any entries (other than the         loopback address). Any static entries in HOSTS could be the cause of a         name resolution issue. The file can also be used for troubleshooting.
                - Any text preceded by the # symbol in a **HOSTS**       file is a comment and will not be processed. To verify a name resolution       problem, edit the HOSTS file and place the correct name and IP address       record in the file for the test host. When you ping that name, if that is       successful, it suggests a name resolution service problem.
                    1. Verify DNS records using the `nslookup` or `dig`         tools. There might be some discrepancy between the records returned by         the resolver compared to the records configured on the authoritative DNS         server that maintains the zone.
                - Use the `nslookup` or `dig` utilities to check       what records are returned by the resolver. If trying to connect to an       Internet resource, compare these records to those returned by public       resolvers (such as Google's servers at 8.8.8.8). Consider whether       clients have cached a record that has been changed recently.       Reconfiguration of DNS records should be planned and implemented carefully       to avoid caching problems.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 6.6.3 nslookup
                - Name resolution troubleshooting typically involves testing multiple clients and servers. The use of caching and the distributed nature of the system means that configuration errors can occur in several different places.
                - You might start investigating a name resolution issue by verifying the name configured on a host. In Windows, you can use the command `ipconfig /all` to display the FQDN of the local host. In Linux, you can use the command `hostname --fqdn`.
                - On a local network, each host is normally configured with a DNS suffix. For example, PC1 might be configured as part of a Windows network with the suffix ad.example.local. If this suffix is not set correctly, some name queries could fail.
                - You can troubleshoot DNS name resolution with the **nslookup command**:
                - `nslookup -Option Host DNSServer`
                    -  *Host * can be either a host name, domain name, FQDN, or IP address.  *DNSServer * is the IP address of a server used to resolve the query; the default DNS server is used if this argument is omitted.  *Option * specifies an nslookup subcommand. For example, the following command queries Google's public DNS server (8.8.8.8) for information about 515support.com's mail records:
                - `nslookup -type=mx 515support.com 8.8.8.8`
                - If `nslookup` is run without any arguments (or by specifying the server only with `nslookup – DNSServer`), the tool is started in interactive mode. You can perform specific query types and output the result to a text file for analysis.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Ao4sji3uqcbxjKQPc3ejAADVlssYfmvhfvWFk7uG6XYvz-YWPQf5MtWbeKYDPU7lm1zU3Sz73gjfb5kCU9hc5nhIl6B_pDmCBTKg8uUVE4T-CcCvKkdrSCHV_UFuhwmz.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The three n s lookup commands are as follows. n s lookup dash type is equal to m x comptia dot org 0.0.0.0; n s lookup dash type is equal to n s comptia dot org 0.0.0.0; n s lookup dash type is equal to m x comptia dot org n s 1 dot comptia dot org.
            -  *The first two nslookup commands identify comptia.org's MX and primary name server records using Google's public DNS resolver (8.8.8.8). Note that the answers are non-authoritative. The third command queries CompTIA's name server for the MX record. This answer is authoritative. (Screenshot courtesy of Microsoft.)* 
            - The Windows PowerShell environment provides a more sophisticated scripted environment that you can use to issue cmdlets to test DNS name resolution (and change DNS settings as well, if required). PowerShell provides a cmdlet called Resolve-DnsName, which allows a more flexible method of testing name resolution than nslookup, as it allows testing of the different methods of name resolution (HOSTS file, DNS cache, and DNS server).
            - 
            - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_nslookup_n09/d_nslookup_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_nslookup_n09/d_nslookup_n09.jpg)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment

            - ### Use nslookup  00:00-00:17
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we'll cover the nslookup utility to test](undefined)[name resolution with your DNS server system.](undefined)[nslookup is available on both windows and linux platforms.](undefined)
            - ### Confirm IP Address Settings 00:17-00:44
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Before we do anything with these utilities, let's](undefined)[check the IP configuration of the Windows workstation.](undefined)[We want to make ensure that it got the correct](undefined)[DNS server address when it received its IP](undefined)[addressing configuration from the DHCP server.](undefined)[To do that, at the command prompt, type 'ipconfig /all'.](undefined)[We should see that the](undefined)[DNS server address is set to 192.168.1.10.](undefined)[That is what we want; it's working correctly.](undefined)
            - ### Use nslookup Passive Mode and Interactive Mode 00:44-01:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To test whether the DNS server can resolve host names](undefined)[properly, enter 'nslookup' at the command prompt.](undefined)[You have two modes you can run nslookup in.](undefined)
            - [First, there's passive mode.](undefined)[You provide the host name you want](undefined)[resolved, such as nslookup demo.local.](undefined)[In this case, nslookup will send the query to](undefined)[the DNS server for the host name demo.local.](undefined)[It returns the response it gets from the DNS server.](undefined)[Now exit.](undefined)[That's one way to use nslookup.](undefined)
            - [You can also use it in interactive mode.](undefined)[To do that, you enter 'nslookup' at the prompt.](undefined)[Here, you can enter as many host names as you like and](undefined)[have nslookup continually resolve them until you're done.](undefined)[When you're done, you can enter 'exit'](undefined)[at the caret prompt to exit nslookup.](undefined)
            - ### Use Authoritive Lookup 01:28-02:08
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's enter 'nslookup'.](undefined)[After we do that, you'll notice the host](undefined)[name and address of the DNS server that this](undefined)[workstation used to resolve host names.](undefined)[The DNS server address is 192.168.1.10.](undefined)[Its host name is dc1.demo.local.](undefined)[Let's use that DNS server to resolve dc2.demo.local.](undefined)[We'll spell out the fully qualified](undefined)[domain name and press Enter.](undefined)[It sends that domain name to the DNS server and it responds](undefined)[with the IP address that's been assigned to that host name.](undefined)[In this case, 192.168.1.11.](undefined)
            - [In addition to resolving host names in zones that](undefined)[this DNS server is authoritative for, we can also](undefined)[look up names that it's not authoritative for.](undefined)
            - ### Use nslookup for Non-Authoritative Lookup 02:08-03:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [For example, we could enter](undefined)[testout.com at the command prompt.](undefined)[The response we get says, "Non-authoritative answer."](undefined)[We did not get that up here because the DC1 DNS](undefined)[server is authoritative for the demo.local zone.](undefined)[But it is not authoritative for the testout.com zone.](undefined)[Notice it was still able to resolve](undefined)[this DNS name into this IP address.](undefined)
            - [How did it do that?](undefined)[It could because the DNS server](undefined)[is configured with root hints.](undefined)[Root hints are IP addresses of](undefined)[root-level DNS servers on the internet.](undefined)[These root-level servers are preconfigured to send queries](undefined)[to domain names that they are not authoritative for.](undefined)
            - [In this case, this DNS server was not](undefined)[authoritative for testout.com, so it sent](undefined)[that query to a root level name server.](undefined)[That server pointed the DC1 DNS server to the](undefined)[DNS server that is authoritative for testout.com.](undefined)
            - [Our DNS server then sent the request to the](undefined)[DNS server that's authoritative for testout.com](undefined)[and asked it to resolve the host name.](undefined)[That remote server resolved the name and](undefined)[sent the response back to our DNS server.](undefined)[Our sever returned the resolved name to the](undefined)[workstation through the nslookup command.](undefined)
            - [We will exit here.](undefined)
            - ### Summary  03:26-03:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demo, we used nslookup to perform both](undefined)[authoritative and non-authoritative DNS records lookups.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 6.6.4 dig
                - **Domain Information Groper (dig)** is a command line tool for querying DNS servers that ships with the BIND DNS server software published by the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) ([isc.org/downloads/bind](https://www.isc.org/bind/)).
                - `dig` can be run pointing at a specific DNS server; otherwise, it will use the default resolver. Without any specific settings, it queries the DNS root zone. A simple query uses the syntax: `dig host`. This will search for the address record for the host, domain, or FQDN or PTR record for an IP address.
                - The following command example directs the resolve request to the specific DNS server identified after the @ symbol. This can be an FQDN or IP address.
                - `dig @ns1.isp.example host`
                - Other examples of `dig` are to display all the resource records about a domain or just specific ones such as Mail Exchange:
                - `dig @ns1.isp.example host all`
                - `dig @ns1.isp.example host MX`
                - `dig` often generates a lot of information, so it is possible to add parameters to the end of the command like `+nocomments` or `+nostats`, which will reduce the output.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/HWRpHGz0zPPxOVs8VjYPhmc3Z8rKq0I4NQalaMq4sbmFWndniEn2nOkqE3rczT9zetk__vQMJkdSGo5LLG-tXoiUod7VuPKjnfHo9gdde3yiG3BwgOLgz1q21Xlc5DWg.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The screenshot shows output for two commands: dig plus no c m d plus no e d n s plus no question 515 support dot com M X; dig plus no c m d plus no e d n s plus no question mail dot 515 support dot com.
            -  *Using dig to locate MX and A records.* 
            - You can use dig on Windows by downloading the BIND DNS server package and installing it using the tools-only option.
            - 
            - 
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_dig_n09/d_dig_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_dig_n09/d_dig_n09.jpg)

            - ### Review dig Lookup Types  00:07-02:56
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's look at a second utility called dig.](undefined)[The dig command is not available by default on Windows](undefined)[systems, but it is available by default on Linux systems.](undefined)
            - [In order to use dig, let's switch](undefined)[to an Ubuntu Desktop Linux system.](undefined)[We'll use it to send DNS name resolution](undefined)[queries to our DC1 name server.](undefined)[Just as we did with the Windows system, we want to](undefined)[make sure that the IP configuration is set properly.](undefined)[We'll use the ifconfig command at the](undefined)[command prompt of the Linux system.](undefined)
            - [We can see that we get the address,](undefined)[192.168.1.101, from the DHCP server.](undefined)[Notice, however, that there is no DNS information there.](undefined)[On Linux systems, the DNS information](undefined)[is stored in a file named resolv.conf.](undefined)[To view the contents of that file, we enter](undefined)['cat /etc/resolv.conf' at the command prompt.](undefined)[We see the DNS information down here.](undefined)[Let's type 'clear' to clear the screen.](undefined)
            - [If you enter 'dig' at the command prompt, by default it](undefined)[uses the name server specified in the /etc/resolv.conf file.](undefined)[If, however, you want to use a different DNS server, you can](undefined)[do that by entering an at sign (@) after the command followed](undefined)[by the IP address of the DNS server you want to use.](undefined)
            - [For example, on the remote subnet, we have a](undefined)[DNS server with the IP address 192.168.0.2.](undefined)[If we want to use it instead of the Windows](undefined)[server 192.168.1.10, we put an at sign and then](undefined)[the IP address of that server on the same line.](undefined)[Then all name resolution requests go to that server instead](undefined)[of the default name server specified in the resolv.conf file.](undefined)[For now, we don't want to do that.](undefined)[We'll use the 192.168.1.10 name server.](undefined)
            - [We'll enter 'dig' at the command prompt followed](undefined)[by the type of lookup we want to perform.](undefined)[For example, we enter 'A' to do an A record lookup](undefined)[followed by the DNS name that we want to look up.](undefined)[For example, 'dc2.demo.local'.](undefined)[It performs an A record lookup on that host name.](undefined)
            - [If we want to perform a reverse lookup by looking up](undefined)[the pointer record, the PTR record, in the reverse](undefined)[zone, we enter 'dig ptr' followed by the IP address](undefined)[of the host that we want to do the reverse lookup on.](undefined)[Instead of resolving the host name into the IP](undefined)[address, it resolves the IP address into the host name.](undefined)
            - [We can also perform a cname lookup or alias lookup by](undefined)[entering 'dig cname' at the command prompt followed by](undefined)[the alias that we want to resolve into a host name.](undefined)[If we want to resolve a mail server record,](undefined)[we enter 'dig mx' followed by the domain name](undefined)[of the mail server that we want to resolve.](undefined)[For today, let's resolve an A record.](undefined)
            - ### Resolve an A Record 02:56-05:18
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We'll enter 'dig a dc1.demo.local'.](undefined)[Press Enter.](undefined)[This sends the query to the name server.](undefined)[It responds with the corresponding record from the zone.](undefined)[Notice that, compared to nslookup, dig](undefined)[displays a little more information.](undefined)[For example, down here, we have the question section.](undefined)[In other words, this is the request](undefined)[for name resolution that we sent.](undefined)[We sent this domain name.](undefined)[We're looking for an internet record](undefined)[and we're looking for an A record.](undefined)[In response, here's what we receive:](undefined)[dc1.demo.local resolves to 192.168.1.10.](undefined)
            - [Down here, we see the query time, how long it](undefined)[took to make that resolution (zero milliseconds),](undefined)[and the IP address for the server that was used](undefined)[to resolve the domain name into the IP address.](undefined)[Notice there's a number 53 over here.](undefined)[It specifies that the DNS server is listening on port 53.](undefined)[That is the default port used by DNS servers.](undefined)[Then the timestamp, or the response,](undefined)[and the size of the message.](undefined)[Let's clear the screen.](undefined)
            - [Now let's do a non-authoritative lookup.](undefined)[We do the same thing, enter 'dig'.](undefined)[We'll point out here that if you don't](undefined)[specify a type of lookup to perform, by](undefined)[default, dig will perform an A record lookup.](undefined)
            - [We don't have to include the 'a' here.](undefined)[We enter 'dig' and let's do testout.com.](undefined)[Press Enter.](undefined)[Just as with nslookup, the DNS server receives](undefined)[the response, recognizes that it is not](undefined)[authoritative for that zone, and goes out and](undefined)[queries a root level name server on the internet.](undefined)[It responds with the IP address of the DNS server](undefined)[that is authoritative for the testout.com zone.](undefined)
            - [The DNS server then sends that name resolution request](undefined)[to the server that is authoritative for testout.com.](undefined)[It receives the response and then forwards the](undefined)[response to the dig command on the Linux workstation.](undefined)
            - [You can see here that we have the same information.](undefined)[Here's the question that was sent, testout.com.](undefined)[Just as before, we have the DNS](undefined)[server on port 53 and the timestamp.](undefined)[As we mentioned earlier, because we didn't](undefined)[specify a particular type of lookup to perform,](undefined)[by default dig performed an A record lookup.](undefined)[We got the answer that told us testout.com](undefined)[resolves to the IP address of 40.386.96.177.](undefined)
            - ### Summary  05:18-05:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demo, we talked about using dig to perform both](undefined)[authoritative and non-authoritative DNS records lookups.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll

        - [Size]();-[H3]()
    - ### 7.0 Explaining Application Services
        - **7.1 Application Security and Time Synchronization** 
            - ## 7.1.1 Transport Layer Security
                - One of the critical problems for the provision of services is that TCP/IP       application protocols were originally devised without any security       mechanisms. Without security, there is no authentication of the servers       running the applications (or of the clients accessing them), and all data       is sent in plaintext. This makes these services highly vulnerable to       spoofing, eavesdropping, and unauthorized modification. **Transport         Layer Security (TLS)** was developed as an IETF standard to solve       this issue.
                - TLS works as a layer between the Application and Transport layers of the       TCP/IP stack, or, in OSI terms, at the Session layer. It's normally used       to authenticate and encrypt TCP connections. When it is used with the HTTP       application, it is referred to as HTTP Secure (HTTPS). TLS can also be       used to secure other TCP application protocols, such as DNS, NTP, FTP,       POP3/IMAP, SMTP, and LDAP. The secure form of the protocol typically uses       a different port than the insecure version.
                - TLS can also be used with UDP, referred to as       Datagram Transport Layer Security (DTLS), most often in virtual private       networking (VPN) solutions.
                - To implement TLS, the server is installed with a **digital           certificate** issued by some trusted **certificate           authority (CA)**. When a client connects to a secure       service, a TLS handshake is performed. During the handshake, the server       provides its certificate to the client. The cryptographic data in the       certificate proves the identity of the server, assuming that the client       also trusts the CA. The certificate contains the **public           key** part of a public/private encryption key pair. The       **private           key** is kept a secret known only to the server.
                - If authentication is successful, the server and client use the key pair       in the digital certificate and a chosen cryptographic **cipher           suite** within the TLS protocol to set up an encrypted       tunnel. Even though someone else might know the public key and be in a       position to record traffic passing between the server and client, they       cannot decrypt the contents of the tunnel without obtaining the server's       private key. This means that the communications cannot be read or changed       by a third party.
                - The latest versions of TLS can use a mechanism       called Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS). When this is configured, not even       obtaining the server's private key allows decryption of captured packets.
                - TLS has been developed through a number of versions, with TLSv1.3 being       current at the time of writing. A server and client must be able to agree       on a compatible version. As older versions can contain serious weaknesses,       many servers are configured to allow only TLSv1.3 or TLSv1.2.       Additionally, the client and server must be able to agree on a mutually       supported cipher suite.
                - TLS itself was developed from an older protocol       called Secure Sockets Layer (SSL). SSL is now completely obsolete.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 7.1.2 Network Time Protocol
                - Many applications on networks require hosts to be synchronized to the       same time. These include authentication and auditing/logging mechanisms,       scheduling applications, and backup software. The **Network         Time Protocol (NTP)** enables the synchronization of these       time-dependent applications. NTP works over UDP on port 123.
                - Top-level NTP servers (stratum 1) obtain the Coordinated Universal Time       (UTC) via a direct physical link to an accurate clock source, such as an       atomic clock accessed over the **Global           Positioning System (GPS)**. An NTP server that       synchronizes its time with a stratum 1 server over a network is operating       at stratum 2. Each stratum level represents a step away from the accurate       clock source over a network link. These lower stratum servers act as       clients of the stratum 1 servers and as servers or time sources to lower       stratum NTP servers or client hosts. Most switches and routers can be       configured to act as time servers to local client hosts and this function       is also typically performed by network directory servers. It is best to       configure each of these devices with multiple reference time sources (at       least three) and to establish them as peers to allow the NTP algorithm to       detect drifting or obviously incorrect time values.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/qHqGkh0xbAyPqlzSxz1sI8ttYFqkNBVteQwKXPXXEaoft5yq-vpyxbnjQ3LIKbN1jGhYXNwZfmgYV-ot7HRHaTtIuwV4niRQ6s9QdYxcDA1-yj1de9TII_2RUf2lDTWL.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The illustration shows three atomic clock sources (G P S atomic clock           sources) in stratum 0 connected to each N T P server via direct links           in stratum 1. Each N T P server is connected to same four N T P           servers in stratum 2 via N T P network links. The N T P servers are           connected to local network application servers and workstations via S           N T P network links.
                    -  *Stratum 1 NTP servers are directly connected to an accurate clock           source. Each stratum level below one represents a network hop away           from that accurate time source. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - Client hosts (application servers and workstations) usually obtain the       time by using a modified form of the protocol called Simple NTP (SNTP).       SNTP works over the same port as NTP. A host that supports only SNTP       cannot act as a time source for other hosts. In Windows, the Time Service       can be configured by using the `w32tm`command. In Linux, the       ntp package can be configured via`/etc/ntp.conf`.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_ntp_n09/v_ntp_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_ntp_n09/v_ntp_n09.jpg)
                - Video Previous Segment

                - Video Control Pause

            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Introduction to NTP 00:00-00:14
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The cool thing about NTP is that it's incredibly accurate.](undefined)[Instead of using time zones, NTP uses](undefined)[Coordinated Universal Time or UTC.](undefined)[Each device is responsible for converting](undefined)[the time to its local time zone.](undefined)
            - ### How NTP Works 00:14-00:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [NTP sends time data through UDP packets to all](undefined)[connected devices, and the devices can be synched](undefined)[up within two milliseconds of each other.](undefined)
            - ### Stratum Levels in NTP 00:24-00:41
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Our first level is called Stratum 0.](undefined)[This is the authoritative time source that](undefined)[we use to configure the NTP server with.](undefined)[Stratum one is the NTP server that's](undefined)[attached to the authoritative time source.](undefined)[Each subsequent NTP server connected](undefined)[on adds a new stratum level.](undefined)
            - ### Adjusting for Time Delays 00:41-00:58
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now, because there will always be a slight delay](undefined)[between stratum levels as packets are sent back and](undefined)[forth, each level calculates the amount of time it](undefined)[takes for the NTP packets to travel and adjust its](undefined)[internal clock so it can maintain an accurate time.](undefined)
            - ### Public NTP Servers 00:58-01:12
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Public NTP servers are also used by individual](undefined)[users to update their computer times.](undefined)[For example, Microsoft maintains a list of](undefined)[public NTP servers that devices can reach out to](undefined)[in order to automatically update their clocks.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - Time drift is when a system’s clock begins to deviate from the source       clock. NTP can use two methods to deal with time drift:
                - **Slew method**—If the time is off by only a few         seconds, NTP adjusts the time a few milliseconds at a time to get it         back on track. Slewing is a slower, methodical method of correcting the         time, but the risk of problems occurring is much less.
                - **Slam method**—If the time is off by more than a few         seconds and slewing will take too long, NTP will hard reset the time.         While this is a quick and immediate fix, slamming can cause some         programs to not function properly.
            - If a server or host is configured with the incorrect time, it may not be       able to access network services. Authentication and other security       mechanisms will often fail if the time is not synchronized on both       communicating devices. In this situation, errors are likely to be generic       failure or invalid token type messages. Always try to rule out time       synchronization as an issue early in the troubleshooting process.
            - If a local stratum 1 server cannot be implemented       on the local network, the time source can be configured using one or more       public NTP server pools, such as time.google.com, time.windows.com,       time.apple.com, time.nist.gov, or pool.ntp.org.
            - To mitigate risks from unauthorized time sources or manipulation of       synchronization data, NTP can be protected using Transport Layer Security       (TLS). **Network           Time Security (NTS) **works over TCP port 4460. NTS       servers may also support ports 3443 and 4443, which were specified in       early draft versions of the protocol.
            - ## 7.1.3 Precision Time Protocol
                - NTP is accurate enough for many network services, but not for the most       timing critical application requirements. Networks supporting industrial       processes, 5G cellular data, medical devices, market trading and financial       services, or broadcasting use the **Precision           Time Protocol (PTP)**. Where NTP can produce millisecond       precision, PTP is capable of nanosecond precision. PTP can also be seen as       a general replacement for NTP. It is defined in the IEEE 1588 standard.
                - PTP can use layer 2 messaging plus hardware clocks in compatible network       adapters and switches to ensure greater levels of accuracy than NTP can       support. It uses mechanisms to measure and account for delay.
                - PTP uses the following clock types:
                    - Grandmaster clock is the authoritative time source within a PTP         domain.
                    - Boundary clock is one with interfaces in multiple PTP segments.
                    - Ordinary clock is one with a single PTP interface.
                - When two clocks are connected, one interface has a timeTransmitter role,       and the other has a timeReceiver role. The grandmaster clock's interfaces       are always timeTransmitter. A boundary clock would have the timeReceiver       role on its interface with the grandmaster and the timeTransmitter role on       other interfaces. Ordinary clock interfaces are usually timeReceiver.
                - Additionally, transparent clocks can be deployed. These can measure path       delay and adjust P2P messages to compensate.
                - PTP can also be deployed as a layer 3 protocol over       IP, but it will not work as accurately as a layer 2 implementation with       PTP-compatible hardware-timestamping adapters and switches.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - ### Configure an NTP Server 00:00-00:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The Network Time Protocol, or NTP, is](undefined)[used to ensure relatively close time](undefined)[synchronization between computer systems.](undefined)[In some cases, even small differences in time can be](undefined)[detrimental to the proper function of various services.](undefined)[In this demonstration, I'm going to configure a](undefined)[Windows Server 2019 system to act as an NTP server, one](undefined)[where other machines can query for an accurate time.](undefined)
            - ### Configure the NTP Service 00:29-02:18
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [First, I'll need to make sure the service](undefined)[is configured to start automatically.](undefined)[Then I'll use regedit to configure parameters.](undefined)[To get to the Services Settings](undefined)[window, I'll just use some shortcuts.](undefined)[If I open Task Manager and click on the Services](undefined)[tab, I can click Open Services at the bottom.](undefined)[Next, I need to open the properties](undefined)[of the Windows Time service.](undefined)[Great, it's already set to Automatic.](undefined)[Now, let's set things up the way we want them.](undefined)[I'll click Start, then type 'registry'](undefined)[and click on the Registry Editor app.](undefined)[If I expand HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE > SYSTEM >CurrentControlSet](undefined)[> Services > W32Time >Parameters, I can right-click on the](undefined)[Type entry in the pane on the right and click Modify.](undefined)[It has to read NTP in the Value data box.](undefined)[Under W32Time\TimeProviders, I click on NtpServer,](undefined)[then modify the Enabled key to be a 1 instead of a 0.](undefined)[One last change in regedit, in the left pane.](undefined)[We'll click Config and set AnnounceFlags to 5.](undefined)
            - [At this point, we just need to restart the](undefined)[service for the changes we made to take effect.](undefined)[In the Services window, right-click](undefined)[on Windows Time and select Restart.](undefined)[Your Windows server will now respond to NTP requests.](undefined)
            - [That isn't the only way to configure a Windows](undefined)[2019 server to be an NTP server, though.](undefined)[PowerShell can also do the same thing.](undefined)[Using the 'Set-ItemProperty' command, I can change registry](undefined)[settings that I previously changed with the Registry Editor.](undefined)[I'm not going to demonstrate that right](undefined)[now, but be aware that there is more than](undefined)[one way to configure services on Windows.](undefined)
            - ### NTP and Firewalls 02:18-02:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [There's one other thing to consider when setting up](undefined)[an NTP server: NTP typically runs on UDP port 123, so](undefined)[the firewall must allow those incoming connections.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:28-02:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[I've shown you how to configure a Windows](undefined)[Server 2019 system as an NTP server.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - 
            -
