            - ### Configure NTP on Linux 00:00-00:21
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Network Time Protocol, or NTP, is a](undefined)[protocol used to sync time on computers.](undefined)[Any system, regardless of whether it's Linux or](undefined)[Windows, has some form of time synchronization.](undefined)[Today, we're going to look at two ways](undefined)[of syncing time on a Linux system.](undefined)
            - ### NTP Service 00:21-01:58
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The first package we're going to check out is ntp.](undefined)[This is a legacy service that's being](undefined)[deprecated in newer versions of Redhat and](undefined)[Ubuntu, but it's still relevant for now.](undefined)[To install it, we need to type 'yum -y install ntp'.](undefined)[This installs all the libraries,](undefined)[configs, and daemons needed.](undefined)[To check the status, we use 'systemctl status](undefined)[ntpd', and we see that it's not running yet.](undefined)[This is normal, as we want to make sure](undefined)[the config is in place before we run it.](undefined)[It's located in /etc, so if we type 'vi](undefined)[/etc/ntp.conf', it'll allow us to view the](undefined)[existing config related to the ntp package.](undefined)
            - [Out of the box, this config will](undefined)[only be for the local system.](undefined)[But it's possible to have this Redhat](undefined)[server as a time server for other systems.](undefined)[Typically, you wouldn't go through the extra effort](undefined)[to make a time server unless it were GPS synced, which](undefined)[provides better accuracy than an internet time server.](undefined)[The config we're interested in is the server list.](undefined)[Whenever you specify an NTP server, you first start](undefined)[with 'server', the server's name, and lastly, 'iburst'.](undefined)[This sends a burst of requests to the time server at](undefined)[startup, which usually results in a faster time sync.](undefined)[The maxpull option could be included here, but](undefined)[I wouldn't set it too low, otherwise you may be](undefined)[blacklisted from the public time servers.](undefined)[Entries like 'maxpull 10' would pull](undefined)[NTP messages every 1024 seconds.](undefined)[6 would be 64 seconds and so on.](undefined)[We're just going to leave the](undefined)[default time servers as they are.](undefined)[If your organization has specific time server](undefined)[requirements, this is where you'd make those changes.](undefined)[Let's exit out of this.](undefined)
            - ### Systemctl 01:58-02:54
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - ['systemctl enable ntpd' enables](undefined)[the daemon to start up at boot.](undefined)[That way we don't forget to start.](undefined)[The 'systemctl start ntpd' command initializes](undefined)[the daemon and runs the NTP protocol for us.](undefined)[Now when we check the daemon's status with 'systemctl](undefined)[status ntpd', we can see that it's running.](undefined)[This only tells us information about the daemon](undefined)[and not much related to time sync, which is why we](undefined)[need to use ntpq to query for ntp-related info.](undefined)[When we issue the 'ntpq -p' command, it](undefined)[prints a list of peers and their states.](undefined)[Notice when you see the servers you're](undefined)[syncing against, they're not the same as](undefined)[the servers you specified in your config.](undefined)[This is OK because when you sync against a](undefined)[DNS name with the word "pool", it means that](undefined)[there's a pool of servers under that name.](undefined)[Commands related to ntpq can be found](undefined)[in the man page by typing 'man ntpq'.](undefined)[And here you can see the '-p'](undefined)[flag we just used, among others.](undefined)
            - ### View Stats 02:54-03:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Just so you know a little about the stats](undefined)[listed, we're going to break it down.](undefined)[Most of these measurements are in milliseconds,](undefined)[so items such as delay is the round trip delay](undefined)[it takes or latency of the time packets.](undefined)[The offset refers to the difference in time](undefined)[between the local host and the time server.](undefined)[Lastly, jitter is the variance in time](undefined)[between the local host and the time server.](undefined)[The lower the jitter, the better.](undefined)[After running the command to check the status again,](undefined)[we notice that it'll take a while for this clock to](undefined)[get in sync, so your stats may not be good initially.](undefined)
            - [Now let's look at another service used for NTP.](undefined)
            - ### Chrony Service 03:28-04:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Chrony is a newer implementation of the NTP protocol.](undefined)[Not only can Chrony perform better in a](undefined)[congested network, it can synchronize the](undefined)[clock quicker and with better accuracy.](undefined)[All around, this is better than using the ntp service.](undefined)[Keep in mind that you can't use](undefined)[both of them at the same time.](undefined)[If you were using the ntp service prior to this, you'd](undefined)[need to uninstall it first and then install Chrony.](undefined)[To do so, you type 'yum -y install chrony'.](undefined)[The configuration is also located in](undefined)[/etc, so type 'vi /etc/chrony.conf'.](undefined)[Now, up at the top are the time](undefined)[server pools that Redhat uses.](undefined)[Just like the ntp package, if you had](undefined)[your own NTP servers, you could input them](undefined)[or change some internet time server pools.](undefined)[Both iburst and maxpull still apply even in this config.](undefined)[We're just going to keep the](undefined)[defaults, so let's exit this now.](undefined)
            - ### Enable Chrony 04:25-06:18
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To enable our chronyd daemon at startup,](undefined)[we type 'systemctl enable chronyd'.](undefined)[Next, we start it up by typing 'systemctl start chronyd'.](undefined)[To check the status, we can do 'systemctl status chronyd'.](undefined)[Checking the status after starting a daemon is always](undefined)[good so you can see if there are errors popping up.](undefined)[From the logs at the bottom of the status, it](undefined)[looks like we have some good startup messages.](undefined)[The commands to check the status of Chrony's sync](undefined)[aren't the same, as they're unique to Chrony.](undefined)['chronyc sources' shows you all the](undefined)[current NTP servers you're syncing against.](undefined)[They may vary, as you're pulling from a pool of](undefined)[internet time servers that might not always be the same.](undefined)[Also, 'chronyc sourcestats' provides you with](undefined)[more statistics related to each time server.](undefined)[You'll notice some stats are similar, but these](undefined)[stats from Chrony provide more granular details.](undefined)[A good reference is to look at the man page.](undefined)[If we do a forward slash and type 'sourcestats', we can](undefined)[look at the details related to the command we just ran.](undefined)[These pages are very descriptive and](undefined)[explain what you may be looking for.](undefined)[Right here is a good example where they give](undefined)[definitions for each field in the sourcestats](undefined)[command, so you know what you're reading.](undefined)[Let's exit this.](undefined)[Just to check our time sync again,](undefined)[let's type 'chronyc sources'.](undefined)[Generally, when our reach is 37,](undefined)[all syncs are in good standing order.](undefined)[The characters off to the left-hand side of the time](undefined)[servers serve a purpose by giving you certain details.](undefined)[For example, the time server with a * indicates](undefined)[which time server you're sync'd against.](undefined)[The + would be other acceptable sources,](undefined)[while the - indicates an acceptable source](undefined)[that's excluded from the algorithm.](undefined)[There are a few other characters that aren't](undefined)[present currently, so be sure to do your research](undefined)[so that you know what you're looking at.](undefined)
            - [After setting up either the NTP service](undefined)[or Chrony, make sure there are no firewall](undefined)[rules restricting the use of UDP port 123.](undefined)[Generally, outbound system firewall](undefined)[traffic isn't blocked by default.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 06:18-06:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we showed you two different NTP protocol](undefined)[services and details related to time sync in Linux.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - 
        - **7.2 Web, File, Print, and Database Services** 
            - ## 7.2.1 Hyper Text Transfer Protocol
                - Websites and web applications are perhaps the most useful and ubiquitous of network services. Web technology can be deployed for a huge range of functions and applications, in no way limited to the static pages of information that characterized the first websites. The foundation of web technology is the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)**. HTTP enables clients (typically web browsers) to request resources from an HTTP server. A client connects to the HTTP server using an appropriate TCP port (TCP/80, by default) and submits a request for a resource, using a uniform resource locator (URL). The server acknowledges the request and either responds with the data or with an error message.
                - ## HTTP Headers and Payload
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The response and request formats are defined in the HTTP header. The HTTP payload is usually used to serve HyperText Markup Language (HTML) webpages, which are plain text files with coded tags describing how the page should be formatted. A web browser can interpret the tags and display the text and other resources associated with the page, such as binary picture or sound files linked to the HTML page.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Fc9ziMbobh8XK5f1VlKYu2_d2tb-rbJ_HwrSdUkC5HG2Jv71Zy-kf_yMd6k4HFczl4vFB0y9mW9AiNFEEy4ZuLOvIb9icXyGECo9rsqMYJoWWFM_XuK8wMLupjcTQebo.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The Comp T I A shows network tab at the top selected. A table provides a list of data from which one of them is selected. A small pane on the right lists response headers.
            -  *Using Firefox's web developer tools to inspect the HTTP requests and response headers involved in serving a typical modern webpage. (Screenshot courtesy of Mozilla Foundation.)* 
            - HTTP also features a forms mechanism (POST) that enables a user to submit data from the client to the server. HTTP is nominally a stateless protocol; this means that the server is not required to preserve information about the client during a session. However, the basic functionality of HTTP servers is also often extended by support for scripting and programmable features (web applications). Servers can also set text file cookies to preserve session information. These coding features, plus integration with databases, increase flexibility and interactivity, but also increase the attack surface and expose more vulnerabilities.
                - Many argue that HTTP is a stateful protocol. Version 2 of HTTP adds more state-preserving features (blog.zamicol.com/2017/05/is-http2-stateful-protocol-application.html).
                - ## Web Servers
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Most organizations have an online presence, represented by a website. In order to run a website, it must be hosted on an HTTP server connected to the Internet. Larger organizations or SMEs with the relevant expertise may host websites themselves, but more typically, an organization will lease a server or space on a server from an ISP. The following types of hosting packages are common:
                    - **Dedicated server**—The ISP allocates your own private server computer. This type of service is usually unmanaged (or management comes at additional cost).
                    - **Virtual private server (VPS)**—The ISP allocates you a virtual machine (VM) on a physical server. This is isolated from other customer instances by the hypervisor.
                    - **Cloud hosting**—Your website is run on a cloud over several hardware computers, allowing more scalability if demand patterns change.
                    - **Shared hosting**—Your website is hosted within a private directory on a shared server. Performance can be severely affected by other sites hosted on the server, because all the sites are competing for the same resources.
                - The main web server platforms are Apache, Microsoft Internet Information Services (IIS), and NGINX.
            - ## 7.2.2 HTTP Secure
                - Plaintext HTTP is highly vulnerable. A modern browser will warn users before initiating an unencrypted connection (or may refuse such connections altogether). HTTP protected by Transport Layer Security (TLS) is referred to as HTTP Secure (HTTPS). HTTPS encrypted traffic is sent over TCP port 443 (by default), rather than the open and unencrypted port 80. A web browser will open a secure session to a server providing this service by using a URL starting with https://, and it will also show a padlock icon in the address bar to indicate that the connection is secure. The padlock icon allows inspection of the site's security data, including the certificate authority (CA) that issued the certificate.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/rj1G_y67Xm-P5e2dC5-HXIAxekPhi7txHTnKtNfZ4qXf0-MUcxB9DdZofiq54r7jVe9V8Rk3GPgc1WMub2GlksmC5dGgIQ5EHn28F5xC_xcVaJa4_AwBONKnSs22TPOm.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The screenshot shows the selection tool placed over the H T T P S padlock icon. The information reads, website identification and website permissions.
            -  *HTTPS padlock icon. (Screenshot courtesy of Microsoft.)* 
            - ## 7.2.3 File Transfer Protocol
                - It is often necessary to transfer files to and from appliances or servers from a remote host. Many methods of remote file access use some form of the **File Transfer Protocol (FTP)**. While HTTPS-based web services and web applications can now offer file downloads to end users, FTP is still often used to perform the administrative upload/download of files to and from servers and appliances. For these uses, it is important to secure the FTP session.
                - ## Active Versus Passive FTP
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - An FTP client connects to TCP port 21 on an FTP server and opens a chosen dynamic client port number ( *n* ). The TCP port 21 control port is used to transfer commands and status information, but not for data transfer. Data transfer can operate in one of two modes: active or passive. In active mode, the client sends a `PORT` command specifying its chosen data connection port number (typically  *n* +1), and the server opens the data connection between the chosen client port and TCP port 20 on the server.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/4FOD5JDFDumKfo8Q-H7ng8pliSowBeuCXJm1X5gwUshCRfpKovaV3hqLKm4qXfPxzZii8P2SZbIKHQdbHaLExiLqxqltoZKhen8tuOACor4zmjh0E0Jh1M-IMF1EPqWt.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The illustration shows a FTP client and a FTP server. An arrow labeled control connection (P O R T) from 1030 of FTP client points to 21 of FTP server. An arrow labeled data connection from 20 of FTP server points to 1031 of FTP client.
            -  *FTP in active mode. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - In passive mode, the client opens a data port (again, typically  *n* +1) and sends the `PASV` command to the server's control port. The server then opens a random high port number and sends it to the client using the `PORT` command. The client then initiates the connection between the two ports.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/9GjzXGq1vs9ZWxWUADAnN3mE74tmHdLsDwqt3P2dCGbRN4tdfiodbOVrVTbjVdLGLbeWSECprV0cawmrHRdW-gd25dkxOeCRmnhLRN82XnhblE1IiMD_1ZC4koH-t-MV.png)
            - Description
            - The illustration shows a FTP client and a FTP server. An arrow labeled control connection (P A S V) from 1030 of FTP client points to 21 of FTP server. An arrow labeled data connection from 1031 of FTP client points to 1041 of FTP server. A dashed arrow labeled 1041 from 21 of FTP server points to 1030 of FTP client.
            -  *FTP in passive mode. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - Active FTP poses a configuration problem for some firewalls, as the server is initiating the inbound connection, but there is no way of predicting which port number will be utilized. However, not all FTP servers and clients can operate in passive mode. If this is the case, check that firewalls installed between the client and server can support active stateful inspection firewalls (SPIs).
                - Another problem is that the control connection can remain idle when the data connection is in use, meaning that the connection can be "timed out" by the firewall (or other routing device).
                - ## Trivial File Transfer Protocol
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The **Trivial File Transfer Protocol (TFTP)** is a connectionless protocol running over UDP port 69. Consequently, TFTP does not provide the guaranteed delivery offered by FTP and is only suitable for transferring small files. Also, it only supports reading (`GET`) and writing (`PUT`) files, not directory browsing, file deletion, or any of the other features of FTP. A TFTP server is most commonly used by legacy network appliances (switches, routers, diskless workstations, and printers) to download configuration files. It can also be used as a backup and restore method for configuration files. However, TFTP has no security mechanisms, and appliances are no longer as resource constrained as they were in the early days of networking. Consequently, secure protocols are now preferred for these functions.
            - ## 7.2.4 Secure File Transfer Protocol
                - **Secure FTP (SFTP)** addresses the privacy and integrity issues of FTP by encrypting the authentication and data transfer between client and server. In SFTP, a secure link is created between the client and server using Secure Shell (SSH) over TCP port 22. Ordinary FTP commands and data transfer can then be sent over the secure link without risk of eavesdropping. This solution requires an SSH server that supports SFTP plus SFTP client software.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/3Y1kU0yEJ1kxEVUFlQQZhU0_daBnAeXvz0pb_wBIbYg0BK4tWP25cEdFc_ZKEyehZ-iUW4sHqLcenbwo0VEZUyU9lB3Gw1nsP5B1e_2qz47q3Flrgy2SwBZPzNWcx4qh.png)
                -  *WinSCP SFTP client. (Screenshot courtesy of WinSCP.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Another means of securing FTP is to use the connection security protocol SSL/TLS. There are two means of configuring FTP over TLS:
                - **Explicit TLS (FTPES)**—Use the `AUTH TLS` command to upgrade an insecure connection established over TCP port 21 to a secure one. This protects authentication credentials. The data connection for the actual file transfers can also be encrypted (using the `PROT` command).
                - **Implicit TLS (FTPS)**—Negotiate an SSL/TLS tunnel before the exchange of any FTP commands. This mode uses TCP port 990 for the control connection.
                - FTPS is tricky to configure when there are firewalls between the client and server. Consequently, FTPES is usually the preferred method.
            - ## 7.2.5 Server Message Block
                - File and print services allow network clients to share access to disk and       printer resources.
                - On a Windows network, the File/Print Sharing Service is provided by the **Server         Message Block (SMB)** protocol. SMB allows a host to share its       directories/files and printers to make them available for other machines       to use. Support for SMB in UNIX- or Linux-based machines and network       attached storage (NAS) appliances is provided by using the Samba software       suite (samba.org/samba/what_is_samba.html),       which allows a Windows client to access a Linux host as though it were a       Windows file or print server. Samba also allows Linux hosts to access       file/printer shares hosted on Windows.
                - On legacy networks, SMB ran as part of an older network services protocol       called NetBIOS on TCP port 139. If no legacy client support is required,       however, SMB is more typically run directly over TCP port 445. SMB should       be restricted to use only on local networks. It is important that any       traffic on the NetBIOS port ranges (137–139) and port 445 be blocked by a       perimeter firewall.
                - SMB version 3 supports message encryption, which can be enabled on a file       server or on a per-share basis. An encrypted share can only be accessed by       an SMB 3.0 or higher client.
                - SMB has gone through several updates, with SMB3 as       the current version. SMB1 has very serious security vulnerabilities and is       now disabled by default on current Windows versions (docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/storage/file-server/troubleshoot/detect-enable-and-disable-smbv1-v2-v3).
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 7.2.6 Network Attached Storage
                - A **network attached storage (NAS)** appliance is a device dedicated to performing a file server role. A NAS uses a bespoke operating system, typically based on Linux, and will usually be operated via a web app. A NAS appliance is accessed via an IP address or domain name. Copy or backup operations are performed at file level using an application protocol, such as Server Message Block (SMB) or File Transfer Protocol (FTP).
                - Most NAS devices support some level of Redundant Array of Independent Disks (RAID). In a RAID array, information is spread between disks so that if one fails, the logical volume and its data will remain available.
                - The main drawback of NAS is that it shares bandwidth with other network applications. Adding a NAS to an already overwhelmed network increases network traffic and may result in unacceptable delays for users and applications to access data. On networks with adequate bandwidth, however, a NAS is a quick and easy way of adding shared storage.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/iqziX1jhwNQSgp-Owo5CxPEx8ck1JRfcZjQHmF-MPoXhSkwc7YxgvF8XpkJ-sOf_27klbjMPIAmNd9B3nTvhJ6ztbpZPCCWEEB07SDbIau3sHcc8E-Kh4v7UgZHrHb99.png)
                -  *Network attached storage. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_nas_n09/d_conf_nas_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_nas_n09/d_conf_nas_n09.jpg)
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
            - 
            - 00:001. Configure a NAS for Data Backups
            - 1.0x
            - Volume Control
            - Interactive ScriptTheater ModeFull Screen
                - Copy Video Transcript
                - Print Video Transcript
            - ## Interactive Script
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - close interactive script
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 7.2.7 Database Services
                - A database provisions information in a format that can be read and updated through some type of query language. There are two main types of databases. Relational databases store information in tables with rows (records) and columns (fields). Relationships between data fields in different tables is created using key fields that uniquely identify each record. Relational databases are operated using **Structured Query Language (SQL)**. SQL defines commands such as SELECT to retrieve information or UPDATE to change it.
                - SQL has been implemented in relational database management system (RDBMS) platforms by several different vendors. As well as providing an implementation of SQL, an RDBMS provides management tools and often a GUI to use to operate the database. A remote access protocol allows a client to connect to the database server over the network and allows replication traffic to move between database servers. Replication is a means of synchronizing the data held on each server. Each RDBMS uses a different TCP port to distinguish it as an application service:
                    - Oracle's remote data access protocol SQL*Net uses TCP/1521.
                    - Microsoft SQL Server uses TCP/1433.
                    - The open source MySQL platform uses TCP/3306. The MariaDB platform forked from MySQL uses the same port.
                    - The open source PostgreSQL platform uses TCP/5432.
                - These are the principal ports. An RDBMS is likely to use other TCP or UDP ports for additional functions.
                - By default, these ports are insecure. However, the RDBMS server can be installed with a certificate and configured to enable TLS transport encryption. The connection is still made over the same port. Either the server or the client can be configured to require encryption and drop the connection if a valid security profile is not available. Optionally, the client can also be installed with a certificate and the server configured to refuse connections from clients without a valid certificate.
                - The other type of database is referred to as NoSQL or "not only SQL." Rather than highly structured relational tables, NoSQL data can use a variety of formats, such as key-value pairs or wide columns (where rows do not have to have the same set of fields). NoSQL databases are typically accessed using an application programming interface (API) over HTTPS.
                - All the RDBMS platforms also provide support for NoSQL datastores. There are also dedicated NoSQL platforms, such as MongoDB, Amazon DynamoDB, and CouchDB.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
        - **7.3 Email and Voice Services** 
            - ## 7.3.1 Simple Mail Transfer Protocol
                - Electronic mail enables a person to compose a message and send it to another user on their own network (intranet) or anywhere in the world via the Internet. Email uses separate mail transfer and mailbox access protocols:
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/FF8oysS3fzcgQDROiB978mXNOK9mwjxqc1mg5cZLLF9gcCKlGZ18ue7HFid6Ce-j3iQXylKOf38V0wvedQw9srH7jlr81yICgurh3bNF3UQvxPs09v7aAQN8J0bXx3Zx.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1.Client submits a new message for delivery to the local S M T P server over the secure port 587. The message is copied to the sent items folder on the local I M P server using the secure port 993.
            - 2.The local S M T P server uses D N S to lookup the M X record listing an I P address for the remote recipient domain and establishes a session with the remote S M T P server over the unencrypted port 25.
            - 3.If the remote server accepts the message. It copies it to the inbox folder of the user’s mailbox hosted on an I M P server.
            - 4.The remote user’s mail client connects to its I M A P server over secure port 993 to download the message.
            -  *Operation of delivery and mailbox email protocols. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - The **Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP)** specifies how email is delivered from one system to another. The SMTP server of the sender discovers the IP address of the recipient SMTP server by using the domain name part of the recipient's email address. The SMTP servers for the domain are registered in DNS using mail exchange (MX) and host (A/AAAA) records.
                - SMTP does not queue messages indefinitely. If there is a communication problem, the SMTP server retries at regular intervals before timing out and returning a non-delivery report (NDR) to the sender. The NDR will contain an error code indicating the reason the item could not be delivered. SMTP provides no mechanism for the persistent storage of messages.
                - SMTP communications can be secured using TLS. This works much like HTTPS with a certificate on the SMTP server and a negotiation between client and server about which cipher suites to use. There are two ways for SMTP to use TLS:
                    - **STARTTLS**—This is a command that upgrades an existing insecure connection to use TLS. This is also referred to as explicit TLS or opportunistic TLS. This method is now deprecated but does remain in widespread use.
                    - **Implicit TLS**—This establishes the secure connection before any SMTP commands (HELO, for instance) are exchanged. Implicit TLS is now considered the preferred method.
                - Typical SMTP configurations use the following ports and secure services:
                    - **Port 25**—Used for message relay between SMTP servers, or message transfer agents (MTAs). If security is required and supported by both servers, the STARTTLS command can be used to set up the secure connection.
                    - **Port 465**—Used for SMTP Submission with implicit TLS. SMTP Submission is a subset of SMTP that allows the message submission agent (MSA) part of a mail client to transfer messages for delivery by a server.
                    - **Port 587**—Used for SMTP Submission with explicit TLS. Servers configured to support port 587 should use STARTTLS and require authentication before message submission.
                - Mail clients can use port 25 to submit messages to the server for delivery, but this is not best practice. Use of port 25 is typically reserved for relay between servers.
            - ## 7.3.2 Internet Message Access Protocol
                - SMTP is useful only to deliver mail to hosts that are permanently available. When a message is received by an SMTP server, it delivers the message to a mailbox server. This could be a separate machine or a separate process running on the same server. A mailbox access protocol allows the user's client email software to operate the mailbox.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/v5cVQhYQWdR0Txm5KW0VOzVWllO03oEZmLK3FEssmb-XNHUXVTgFg3l6YSGGJ5ZZY8DXLy54M4ARmDJVwH2w27fGvhBWFUqu-Y_J_rPVk6WU7X_xdszSrOcGRc-h2u79.png)
                -  *Configuring mailbox access protocols on a server. (Screenshot courtesy of Mozilla Foundation.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - **Internet Message Access Protocol (IMAP)** is the most widely used mail retrieval protocol. IMAP supports permanent connections to a server and connecting multiple clients to the same mailbox simultaneously. It also allows a client to manage the mailbox on the server (to organize messages in folders and to control when they are deleted, for instance) and to create multiple mailboxes.
                - A client connects to an IMAP server over TCP port 143, but this port is insecure. Connection security can be established using a TLS. The default port for IMAPs is TCP/993.
                - In a Windows environment, the proprietary Messaging Application Programming Interface (MAPI) protocol is typically used to access Microsoft Exchange mailboxes. MAPI uses HTTPS as a secure transport protocol.
            - ## 7.3.3 Voice and Video Services
                - **Voice         over IP (VoIP)**, web conferencing, and video teleconferencing       (VTC) solutions have become standard methods for the provision of business       and social communications. Many networks are upgrading from legacy voice       services to IP-based protocols and products.
                - ## Private Branch Exchange
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Legacy voice services use the public switched telephone network (PSTN). A       residential telephone installation would be serviced by a simple box       providing a one- or two-line analog interface to the local exchange. This       analog interface is also referred to as the plain old telephone service       (POTS). Each line provides a single channel for an incoming or outgoing       call. A typical business requires tens or hundreds of lines for voice       communications, let alone capacity for data communications. Historically,       this requirement would have been facilitated by a digital trunk line, also       referred to as a time division multiplexing       (TDM) circuit. A TDM can multiplex separate voice and data channels for       transmission over a single cable.
                - A **private         branch exchange (PBX)** is an automated switchboard providing a       single connection point for an organization's voice lines. A TDM-based PBX       connects to the telecommunications carrier over a digital trunk line,       which will support multiple channels (inward and outward calls). The PBX       allows for the configuration of the internal phone system to direct and       route calls to local extensions and provides other telephony features such       as call waiting, music on hold, and voicemail.
                - ## VoIP-Enabled PBX
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - TDM-based PBXes are being replaced by hybrid and fully IP/VoIP PBXes. For       internal calls and conferences, a VoIP PBX establishes connections between       local VoIP endpoints with data transmitted over the local Ethernet       network. A VoIP PBX can also route incoming and outgoing calls from and to       external networks. This might involve calls between internal and external       VoIP endpoints, or with voice telephone network callers and receivers. A       VoIP PBX will also support features such as music on hold and voicemail.
                - A TDM PBX is supplied as vendor-specific hardware. A VoIP PBX can be       implemented as software running on a Windows or Linux server. Examples of       software-based solutions include 3CX ([3cx.com](https://www.3cx.com/))       and Asterisk ([asterisk.org](https://www.asterisk.org/)).       There are also hardware solutions, where the VoIP PBX runs on a router,       such as Cisco Unified Communications Manager (cisco.com/c/en/us/products/unified-communications/unified-communications-manager-callmanager/index.html).
                - A VoIP PBX would normally be placed at the network edge and be protected       by a firewall. Internal clients connect to the PBX over Ethernet data       cabling and switching infrastructure, using Internet Protocol (IP) at the       Network layer for addressing. The VoIP PBX uses the organization's       Internet link to connect to a VoIP service provider, which facilitates       inward and outward dialing to voice-based telephone networks.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/2ry-VA21wrL6dt_-M_zPQO3cCmkieqHzFpHLk6FRclhUEywBdJmqa4IZhzcjZi7qnj2pTI7ssjMiEeZtayTqQf9saU52PZcaMvflmdlHHr1KBPqkMpsZRa1UFr9UXUFY.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The edge router or firewall is connected to internet. V o I P service           provider in the internet is connected to telephone network. The L A N           router is connected to a switch. The switch is connected to two groups           of wireless V o I P endpoints. One of the groups has an access point.
                    -  *A VoIP PBX facilitates internal IP calls and calls to and from           external VoIP networks and the landline and cellular telephone           networks. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 7.3.4 VoIP Protocols
                - Voice and video services can be challenging to support because they require response times measured in milliseconds (ms). Delayed responses will result in poor call or video quality. This type of data can be one-way, as is the case with media streaming, or two-way, as is the case with VoIP and VTC.
                - The protocols designed to support real-time services cover one or more of the following functions:
                    - **Session control**—Used to establish, manage, and disestablish communications sessions. They handle tasks such as user discovery (locating a user on the network), availability advertising (whether a user is prepared to receive calls), negotiating session parameters (such as use of audio/video), and session management and termination.
                    - **Data transport**—Handles the delivery of the actual video or voice information.
                    - **Quality of service (QoS)**—Provides information about the connection to a QoS system, which in turn ensures that voice or video communications are free from problems, such as dropped packets, delay, or jitter.
                - ## Session Initiation Protocol
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The **Session Initiation Protocol (SIP)** is one of the most widely used session control protocols. SIP endpoints are the end user devices (also known as user agents), such as IP-enabled handsets or client and server web conference software. Each device, conference, or telephony user is assigned a unique SIP address known as a SIP Uniform Resource Identifier (URI). Examples of SIP URIs include:
                - `sip:jaime@515support.com`
                - `sip:2622136227@515support.com`
                - `sip:jaime@2622136227`
                - `meet:sip:organizer@515support.com;ms-app=conf;ms-conf-id=subg42`
                - There is also a tel: URI scheme allowing SIP endpoints to dial a landline or cell phone. A tel: URI can either use the global (E.164) format (such as tel:+1-866-8358020) or a local format (for internal extensions).
                - SIP typically runs over UDP or TCP ports 5060 (insecured) and 5061 (SIP-TLS). SIP has its own reliability and retransmission mechanisms and can thus be seen to benefit most from the lower overhead and reduced latency and jitter of UDP. Some enterprise SIP products use TCP anyway.
                - ## Real-Time Transport Protocol and RTP Control Protocol
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - While SIP provides session management, the actual delivery of real-time data uses different protocols. The principal one is Real-time Transport Protocol (RTP). RTP enables the delivery of a stream of media data via UDP, while implementing some of the reliability features usually associated with TCP communications. RTP works closely with the RTP Control Protocol (RTCP). Each RTP stream uses a corresponding RTCP session to monitor the quality of the connection and to provide reports to the endpoints. These reports can then be used by the applications to modify codec parameters or by the network stacks to tune quality of service (QoS) parameters.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_voip_server_n09/d_conf_voip_server_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_conf_voip_server_n09/d_conf_voip_server_n09.jpg)

            - 00:001. Configure a VoIP Server

            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Configure a VoIP Server 00:00-00:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Voice over Internet Protocol, or VoIP, is the](undefined)[ability to make phones calls over the internet.](undefined)[Landlines or telephone lines used to be the main](undefined)[method to receive phone calls, but VOIP and cell phones](undefined)[have replaced much of the existing infrastructure.](undefined)[Not only can they provide more options,](undefined)[they can even save you money as well.](undefined)
            - ### Server Setup 00:26-01:31
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Today, we're going to be working with an](undefined)[open-source VOIP server called FreePBX.](undefined)[We can deploy this simply with an](undefined)[ISO on a virtual or physical machine.](undefined)[It's also possible to purchase an appliance](undefined)[for FreePBX, but this isn't required.](undefined)[First, we need to change our server's name.](undefined)[We really don't want the label VoIP server for our name.](undefined)[Go to 'Settings' and then 'Advanced Settings'.](undefined)[We're going to scroll to the bottom](undefined)[where we see 'System Identity'.](undefined)[Let's change that to 'Testout VoIP'.](undefined)[After we click 'Submit', 'Apply Config'](undefined)[at the top will save the running config.](undefined)[Next, we're going to go to 'Admin' and then 'System Admin'.](undefined)[There's also options to change the Hostname here.](undefined)[Let's change this to 'testoutpbx.testout.com'](undefined)[and click 'Update Hostname'.](undefined)[Under Time Zone, if you want to put your actual](undefined)[time zone instead of UTC, you'd set that here.](undefined)[It's a good idea to have the FreePBX](undefined)[server and the VoIP phones in sync.](undefined)
            - ### IP and Port Configuration 01:31-04:30
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Typically, when FreePBX is built, it's set to DHCP.](undefined)[We want to make sure this is static so that any firewall](undefined)[rules or phones connecting to this IP will work.](undefined)[Leaving the DHCP in place could result in](undefined)[an IP change if a reservation isn't set up.](undefined)[These options are in 'Admin' and 'System Admin'.](undefined)
            - [On the right-hand side, you'll see 'Network Settings'.](undefined)[Click that.](undefined)[We want to change this to 'Static' and then pick a](undefined)[different IP address that isn't in our DHCP range.](undefined)[The rest of the settings can be left as is;](undefined)[these are the right settings for our network.](undefined)[Saving this interface brings up a window reminding](undefined)[us of the risks related to changing these settings.](undefined)[Click 'Save and Apply'.](undefined)[Since the IP has changed, we have to go to the new one.](undefined)[We just change our IP to '150' on](undefined)[the end and it'll refresh the screen.](undefined)[It's possible that it might prompt us for a re-login.](undefined)
            - [Now that that's out of the way, we](undefined)[need to change some more settings.](undefined)[Under 'Settings' and 'Asterisk SIP Settings', we're](undefined)[interested specifically in the NAT settings here.](undefined)[If these aren't set right, you could have problems.](undefined)[For example, we want to define our external (public) IP](undefined)[address and define all our local networks to use FreePBX.](undefined)[You can see tabs for chan_pjsip and chan_sip.](undefined)[The chan_pjsip driver is a newer one that hasn't](undefined)[been used by many SIP trunk providers yet.](undefined)[A SIP trunk is a media service provided by companies to](undefined)[trunk your phones to a public-switched phone network.](undefined)[Without a SIP trunk, all you could do](undefined)[would be dial internal business extensions.](undefined)[We know that our SIP trunk provider is only](undefined)[using chan_sip, so we need to update our ports.](undefined)[Basically, we need to switch the port 5060,](undefined)[which is the chan_pjsip one, to use](undefined)[something else so that chan_sip can have it.](undefined)
            - [On our chan_pjsip tab, we scroll down](undefined)[and change the port to say '5260'.](undefined)[When we submit the config, it does give us a](undefined)[note telling us Asterisk will need to be](undefined)[restarted so the port changes can take effect.](undefined)[Let's apply the config before proceeding.](undefined)[The easiest way to do this is to go to](undefined)[our console session for this server.](undefined)[We're going to open PuTTY and go to '192.168.30.150'.](undefined)[Next, we provide the root username](undefined)[and password for the server.](undefined)[Then all we need to type is 'fwconsole restart'.](undefined)[This will run through restarting Asterisk.](undefined)[Since this process takes several iterations](undefined)[to complete, we're going to fast forward.](undefined)
            - [Now that our changes are done, we](undefined)[can show you the final product.](undefined)[On our chan_pjsip, the port is 5160.](undefined)[And if we go to the chan_sip tab, our port is 5060.](undefined)[Basically, we just flipped the ports](undefined)[since weren't not using chan_pjsip.](undefined)
            - ### SIP Trunks 04:30-06:35
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, we're going to look at the](undefined)[area where we configure SIP trunks.](undefined)[Under 'Connectivity' and 'Trunks' is](undefined)[where we'd define our SIP trunk vendor.](undefined)[The nice thing is that there's a](undefined)[built-in vendor called SIPStation.](undefined)[So, if you do use SIPStation, it's very easy to](undefined)[configure the SIP trunk with little to no hassle.](undefined)
            - [Our SIPStation trunks are already defined,](undefined)[but we were unable to show the wizard due](undefined)[to the personal information needed.](undefined)[If you didn't use SIPStation and you wanted to](undefined)[add another vendor, you'd typically click 'Add](undefined)[Trunk' and then 'Add SIP (chan_sip) Trunk'.](undefined)[The SIP trunk provider should give you all the information](undefined)[you need or a tutorial on how to set it all up.](undefined)[For example, if you want to set up a trunk name, the](undefined)[phone number associated with your SIP trunk channel](undefined)[would be listed on the outbound caller ID here.](undefined)[On the 'sip Settings' tab, you'd](undefined)[provide a Trunk Name and PEER Details.](undefined)[These details provide the proper connection details,](undefined)[username, and password to connect to your SIP trunk provider.](undefined)[You'd put similar details on the Incoming tab as well.](undefined)[Once a trunk is defined, you can then](undefined)[configure the Inbound and Outbound routes.](undefined)[This is necessary.](undefined)[Otherwise, FreePBX doesn't know where](undefined)[to route incoming and outgoing calls.](undefined)[Under 'Connectivity' and 'Inbound Routes', you can see that](undefined)[we have one phone number defined from our SIPStation wizard.](undefined)[If you go to the edit icon, that number is](undefined)[defined as the DID number because we're using DID](undefined)[verification for the destination SIPStation trunk.](undefined)[If we go to 'Connectivity' and then 'Outbound](undefined)[Routes', we see the SIPStation-Out route, too.](undefined)[There are three outbound routes configured, like](undefined)[the E911 one here, our normal SIPStation outbound](undefined)[route, and an international outbound route as well.](undefined)[We're just going to deal with the general](undefined)[outbound route, so let's click the edit icon.](undefined)
            - [At the bottom, you can see which trunks this is tied to.](undefined)[There are two trunks listed for redundancy.](undefined)[Under dial patterns is where you](undefined)[configure patterns for outbound calls.](undefined)[These are the default for SIPStation, but if your company](undefined)[has a need to switch these patterns, they could do so here.](undefined)
            - ### Extensions 06:35-08:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Our server is mostly ready.](undefined)[The last thing we need to look at is extensions.](undefined)[To do so, let's go to 'Applications' and 'Extensions'.](undefined)[As you can see, we have none.](undefined)[To add one, we can click 'Add](undefined)[Extension' or 'Quick Create Extension'.](undefined)[The Quick Create option makes things](undefined)[simpler, so let's go with that.](undefined)
            - [The Type will be 'chan_sip' since](undefined)[our provider is using that one.](undefined)[The Extension Number typically shouldn't be the number 1.](undefined)[We're going to start at 100, but your organization](undefined)[can start on another one if they want.](undefined)
            - [Display Name would be the name](undefined)[of the person using the phone.](undefined)[You can specify the Outbound Caller ID, but this would](undefined)[override the caller ID when going out of a trunk.](undefined)[For now, we're not going to set that.](undefined)[Next, we can put in an email,](undefined)[such as 'djackson@testout.com'.](undefined)
            - [On Step 2, there isn't much to configure.](undefined)[However, I am going to point out some options.](undefined)[Enable Find Me/Follow Me is a cool option](undefined)[that can ring another phone, like your](undefined)[cell phone, if you aren't at your desk.](undefined)[On the bottom here, we can change the Voicemail](undefined)[PIN on creation or just do that later.](undefined)[Click 'Finish'.](undefined)[Now our extension is created.](undefined)
            - [Let's go back into it by clicking the edit icon.](undefined)[First, the area that says Secret will need](undefined)[the password to authenticate to FreePBX.](undefined)[As we scroll down, I want to look at User Manager Settings.](undefined)[By default, the extension and the username will be the](undefined)[same unless you click the box for Use Custom Username.](undefined)[As you hover over the password, it becomes visible.](undefined)[The username and the user's secret will be](undefined)[needed to authenticate with a phone on FreePBX.](undefined)[You don't want to confuse the password listed at the](undefined)[bottom here, as this won't be used on the VOIP phone.](undefined)[Don't forget to save any config changes made.](undefined)[Let's click the 'Apply Config' button.](undefined)
            - [Now that we have all these settings in place,](undefined)[we should be able to configure more extensions.](undefined)[Keep in mind that you'll need to do some server hardening](undefined)[to make sure that this FreePBX server is secure.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 08:32-08:44
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we showed you how to set up a](undefined)[FreePBX server, provided an overview of SIP](undefined)[trunks, and set up a VoIP phone extension.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 7.3.5 VoIP Phones
                - A VoIP/SIP endpoint can be implemented as software running on a computer or smartphone or as a dedicated hardware handset. **VoIP phones** use VLAN tagging to ensure that the SIP control and RTP media protocols can be segregated from normal data traffic. In a typical voice VLAN configuration, the LAN port on the handset is connected to the wall port, while the PC is connected to the PC port on the handset. The two devices share the same physical link, but data traffic is distinguished from voice traffic by configuring separate VLAN IDs.
                - Handsets can use Power over Ethernet (PoE), if available, to avoid the need for separate power cabling or batteries. There are also wireless handsets that work over 802.11 Wi-Fi networks.
                - Connection security for VoIP works in a similar manner to HTTPS. To initiate the call, the secure version of SIP (SIPS) uses digital certificates to authenticate the endpoints and establish a TLS tunnel. The secure connection established by SIPS can also be used to generate a master key to use with the secure versions of the transport and control protocols.
                - When you are installing a new handset, you should also test that the connection works and that the link provides sufficient call quality. Most service providers have test numbers to verify basic connectivity and perform an echo test call, which replays a message you record so that you can confirm voice quality.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - ### Configure a VoIP Phone 00:00-00:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A Voice over IP, or VoIP, phone is a phone that's](undefined)[specifically used with a VoIP telephone system.](undefined)[This phone hooks up to a RJ45 connector rather than](undefined)[to a RJ11 one, which is a standard telephone line.](undefined)[There are many brands of VoIP phones](undefined)[available, but today, we're just going to look](undefined)[at one from this particular vendor Polycom.](undefined)
            - ### Initial Setup 00:25-01:57
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [What you see here is a web browser for](undefined)[the Polycom phone we have on our network.](undefined)[You should enable DHCP by default so that you can go onto](undefined)[the phone's screen to obtain the assigned IP address.](undefined)[Our address happens to be 192.168.30.102.](undefined)[When we log in, the default password is 456.](undefined)[Just like the note up here says, we suggest](undefined)[that you change the default admin password.](undefined)
            - [This model is a VVX 400.](undefined)[Typically, VoIP phones are more universal when it comes to](undefined)[business phone systems because they aren't tied to one brand.](undefined)[First, let's go to 'Preferences' and then 'Date and Time'.](undefined)[It's a good idea to have the VoIP server and phone](undefined)[synced to the same Network Time Protocol, or NTP, server.](undefined)[Simple Network Time Protocol, or SNTP, is similar to NTP.](undefined)[It's basically implemented when a full](undefined)[NTP implementation isn't necessary.](undefined)[Since we don't have an NTP server](undefined)[on site, let's set this up.](undefined)
            - [Our first server will be 'pool.ntp.org', and the](undefined)[second one will be 'north-america.pool.ntp.org'](undefined)[since we're in North America.](undefined)[We're going to set the time zone and alternate the](undefined)[same because we don't want an alternate time zone.](undefined)[You can use the overrides, but if you don't have](undefined)[an NTP server on your network, it won't matter.](undefined)[Also, we can see that daylight](undefined)[savings time is enabled already.](undefined)[Click 'Save'.](undefined)
            - ### Line Settings 01:57-04:00
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To configure this phone on a FreePBX VoIP server,](undefined)[we need to go to 'Settings' and then 'Lines'.](undefined)[The Display Name is typically the name](undefined)[of the person who's using the phone.](undefined)[The Address and Label can be the phone's extension.](undefined)[The number of line keys are for multiple](undefined)[direct numbers or even shared numbers.](undefined)[In some businesses, this might be set to 6, for example,](undefined)[if you have six total lines to set up per phone.](undefined)[SRTP is a protocol that adds more security](undefined)[features to the phone's communications.](undefined)[We're just going to leave the defaults for now.](undefined)
            - [Under 'Authentication', our domain will be](undefined)[the FreePBX server, which is 192.168.30.150.](undefined)[The User ID is the extension on the FreePBX server.](undefined)[To grab this information, we're going](undefined)[to hop over to our Freepbx server tab.](undefined)[Under 'Applications and Extensions', we have one](undefined)[extension listed here that we already created.](undefined)[Extension 100 is for a user called Daniel Jackson.](undefined)[If we go into the 'Edit mode', there's more](undefined)[information listed about this extension.](undefined)[The secret here is the password we'll use](undefined)[to authenticate to the FreePBX server.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and copy that.](undefined)
            - [Now back to our Polycom phone.](undefined)[We can enter the user ID of '100' and paste our password.](undefined)[We'll enter same IP under the Server 1](undefined)[tab for the address '192.168.30.150'.](undefined)[The port doesn't need to be set, as the default is 0.](undefined)
            - [The last part we need to configure is the Message Center.](undefined)[Although it's called that, it's really just our voicemail.](undefined)[To access this on a FreePBX server, we use *97 in](undefined)[both the subscription address and callback contact.](undefined)[Let's set the callback mode to 'Contact'.](undefined)[Having this setup allows for the voicemail](undefined)[buttons on the phone to work properly.](undefined)[Click 'Save' at the bottom.](undefined)[Great!](undefined)[Our phone is set up and ready to go.](undefined)
            - ### Troubleshooting 04:00-04:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [There may be times where troubleshooting](undefined)[is required for these phones.](undefined)[There are some tools here to help](undefined)[with figuring out what the problem is.](undefined)[Under 'Diagnostics', we can view and download logs.](undefined)[If there were errors or problems occurring,](undefined)[we could go here and see if there were some](undefined)[logs related to the issue we were having.](undefined)[Under 'Utilities', there are a few options, such as](undefined)[Import & Export Configuration, Phone Backup & Restore,](undefined)[Software Upgrade, and even Restart and Reboot.](undefined)[You might think those two last](undefined)[options are the same, but they're not.](undefined)[Restart just restarts the application on the](undefined)[phone, while Reboot restarts the kernel and the](undefined)[application, which results in a longer boot time.](undefined)

        - **7.4 Disaster Recovery and High Availability** 
            - ## 7.4.1 Disaster Recovery Concepts
                - A **disaster         recovery plan (DRP)** addresses large-scale network outage       incidents. These will typically be incidents that threaten the performance       or security of a whole site. A DRP should accomplish the following:
                    - Identify scenarios for natural and non-natural disasters and options         for protecting systems.
                    - Identify tasks, resources, and responsibilities for responding to a         disaster. Disaster recovery focuses on tasks such as switching services         to failover systems or sites and restoring systems and data from         backups.
                    - Train staff in the disaster planning procedures and how to react well         to adverse events.
                - Testing system resilience and incident response effectiveness are crucial       for organizations to recover from disruptions and maintain business       continuity. By conducting various tests, organizations can identify       potential vulnerabilities, evaluate the efficiency of their recovery       strategies, and improve their overall preparedness for real-life       incidents.
                    - **Tabletop             exercises** involve teams discussing and working         through hypothetical scenarios to assess their response plans and         decision-making processes. These exercises help identify knowledge,         communication, and coordination gaps, ultimately strengthening the         organization's incident response capabilities. For example, a tabletop         exercise might be an earthquake that destroys processing ability at a         primary site, testing failover to an alternate processing location.
                    - **Validation tests** involve performing simulations of         failovers. This tests that services can be restored using backup         configurations and data. Validation tests can also test metrics for         recovery time. They can also reveal any unexpected problems, such as         dependencies between services not being met during the failover process.
                - Where disaster recovery focuses on plans for specific scenarios, a **business         continuity plan (BCP)** or continuity of operations plan (COOP) is       a collection of processes and resources that enable an organization to       maintain normal business operations in the face of some adverse event.       Continuity planning activity focuses on the functions performed by a       business or other organization:
                    - Business impact analysis (BIA) identifies mission essential and         primary business functions and the risks that would arise if the         organization cannot fulfill them.
                    - IT contingency planning (ITCP) or IT service continuity planning         (ITSCP) ensures that these functions are supported by resilient IT         systems, working to identify and mitigate all single points of failure         from a process or function.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 7.4.2 Disaster Recovery Metrics
                - Disaster recovery planning is governed by a variety of metrics that express how reliable services are and how long it takes to recover from critical events.
                - One of the key properties of a resilient system is availability. Availability is the percentage of time that the system is online, measured over a certain period, typically one year. The corollary of availability is downtime; that is, the percentage or amount of time during which the system is unavailable.
                - **High availability** is a characteristic of a system that can guarantee a certain level of availability. The **Maximum Tolerable Downtime (MTD)** metric states the requirement for a business function. Downtime is calculated from the sum of scheduled service intervals (Agreed Service Time) plus unplanned outages over the period. High availability might be implemented as 24x7 (24 hours per day, seven days per week) or 24x365 (24 hours per day, 365 days per year). For a critical system, availability will be described as two-nines (99%) up to five- or six-nines (99.9999%).
                - A system where there is almost no scheduled downtime and outages are extremely rare is also referred to as continuous availability. This sort of availability is required when there is not just a commercial imperative, but a danger of injury or loss of life associated with systems failure. Examples include networks supporting medical devices, air traffic control systems, communications satellites, networked autonomous vehicles, and smart traffic signaling systems.
                - The MTD metric sets the upper limit on the amount of recovery time that system and asset owners have to resume operations. Additional metrics can be used to govern recovery operations:
                    - **Recovery time objective (RTO).** This is the period following a disaster that an individual IT system may remain offline. This represents the maximum amount of time allowed to identify that there is a problem and then perform recovery (restore from backup or switch in an alternative system, for instance).
                    - **Work recovery time (WRT)**. Following systems recovery, there may be additional work to reintegrate different systems, restore data from backups, test overall functionality, and brief system users on any changes or different working practices so that the business function is again fully supported.
                - RTO+WRT must not exceed MTD!
                - **Recovery point objective (RPO). ** This is the amount of data loss that a system can sustain, measured in time units. That is, if a database is destroyed by a virus, an RPO of 24 hours means that the data can be recovered from a backup copy to a point not more than 24 hours before the database was infected.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/x5GpXnCewWL7cHZGOnBO2PoDxzwV8xQoXfqv-jXsO5VjDBKXMhuemlOCh0yFPPrHkoVXZNv4GRgClP1lCorgdcbNAzXGRRlf0anHnm5zBEY8LsJti8M1r8-RdjbnFOxa.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - --------------------- Portal ---------------------Availability
                - 99.9999% #Availability

            - ### Set Up NIC Teaming 00:00-00:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we're going to take a look at NIC teaming.](undefined)[I'm working on a virtual Windows Server,](undefined)[and I have two network cards installed.](undefined)[When you set up a NIC team, you do it inside Server Manager.](undefined)[I'll go to the local server, and you](undefined)[can see that NIC teaming is disabled.](undefined)[If I click on it, it brings up the NIC Teaming dialog box.](undefined)[The first thing that I want to do is click on 'Tasks'.](undefined)[Then I want to make a new team.](undefined)[We'll give our team a name; I'll type in 'Team1'.](undefined)[Then we select the adapters that'll be a part of the team.](undefined)[On a physical machine, I can have up](undefined)[to 32 network cards working together.](undefined)
            - ### Switches and NIC Teams 00:39-01:43
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's go down here and look at these additional properties.](undefined)[The first thing we're going to](undefined)[talk about is our teaming mode.](undefined)[I'm going to expand 'Additional Properties' down here.](undefined)
            - [First, we have Switch Independent, which](undefined)[means they're plugged into different switches.](undefined)[I can do aggregation, or I can just use it for failover.](undefined)[Maybe I just want to make sure that if one network](undefined)[card or one switch fails, the other one is available.](undefined)
            - [Now since this is a virtual](undefined)[machine, we have some limitations.](undefined)[Only certain options will be](undefined)[allowed, depending on the setup.](undefined)
            - [One of those options is Static Teaming, which means](undefined)[the adapters are plugged into the same switch.](undefined)[We're going to coordinate with the switch to let it](undefined)[know that these two adapters are teamed together,](undefined)[and we're going to aggregate the bandwidth.](undefined)[Let's get the combined bandwidth of both ports.](undefined)[You also have the option of using LACP, or Link](undefined)[Aggregation Control Protocol, which means that](undefined)[the computer will coordinate with the switch and](undefined)[figure out the teaming automatically.](undefined)
            - [We'll go with 'Switch Independent' since that's](undefined)[the only option with this virtual machine.](undefined)[Now let's go to 'Load Balancing Mode'.](undefined)
            - ### Load Balancing Mode 01:43-02:04
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Here we see Address Hash.](undefined)[Again, we have limited options](undefined)[because this is a virtual machine.](undefined)[Address Hash means it's going to make a hash](undefined)[for each TCP stream and make sure that each](undefined)[stream is processed by one of the adapters.](undefined)[We do this to try to avoid packets arriving out of order.](undefined)[Once again, this option is one that's compatible with our](undefined)[server running as a virtual machine, so I'll select that.](undefined)
            - ### Standby Adaptor 02:04-02:13
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's look at 'Standby Adaptor'.](undefined)[I can have them all active, or I can](undefined)[have up to one backup for the whole team.](undefined)[We're just going to leave them all active and click 'OK'.](undefined)

            - [Let's go in and look at our](undefined)[network adaptors to confirm this.](undefined)[You can see these two adapters here](undefined)[and over here show up as Team1.](undefined)
            - [Now let's go into a Command Prompt](undefined)[and see what it looks like there.](undefined)[I'll type in 'ipconfig'.](undefined)[I can see there's only one adapter (as far as the](undefined)[operating system is concerned) and it's named Team1.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:49-03:08
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demo, we enable NIC teaming and tied two](undefined)[network cards together to combine their bandwidth.](undefined)[We also saw how combining the network](undefined)[cards can provide failover for our server.](undefined)[And finally, we saw how the team appeared on](undefined)[the operating system as if it were one adapter.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 7.4.3 Disaster Recovery Sites
                - Within the scope of business continuity planning, disaster recovery plans (DRPs) describe the specific procedures to follow to recover a system or site to a working state. A disaster could be anything from a loss of power or failure of a minor component to human-made or natural disasters, such as fires, earthquakes, or acts of terrorism.
                - Providing redundant devices and spares or network links allows the spare devices to be swapped in if existing systems fail. Enterprise-level networks often also provide for spare sites. A spare site is another location that can provide the same (or similar) level of service. A disaster or systems failure at one site will cause services to failover to the alternate processing site. Disaster recovery planning must demonstrate how this will happen, what checks need to be made to ensure that failover has occurred successfully (without loss of transactional data or service availability), and how to revert to the primary site once functionality is restored there.
                - Site resiliency is described as hot, warm, or cold:
                    - A **hot site** can failover almost immediately. It generally means that the site is already within the organization's ownership and is ready to deploy. For example, a hot site could consist of a building with operational computer equipment that is kept updated with a live dataset.
                    - A **warm site** could be similar but with the requirement that the latest dataset will need to be loaded.
                    - A **cold site** takes longer to set up. A cold site may be an empty building with a lease agreement in place to install whatever equipment is required when necessary.
                - Clearly, providing redundancy on this scale can be very expensive. Sites are often leased from service providers. However, in the event of a nationwide emergency, demand for the services is likely to exceed supply! Another option is for businesses to enter into reciprocal arrangements to provide mutual support. This is cost-effective but complex to plan and set up.
                - For many companies, the most cost-effective solution is to move processing and data storage to a cloud site. A cloud operator should be able to maintain hot site redundancy so that a disaster in one geographic area will not disrupt service, because the cloud will be supported by a datacenter in a different region.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 7.4.4 Fault Tolerance and Redundancy
                - Switching services over to a disaster recovery site should be a rare       occurence. A resilient network should be able to deal with routine faults       by provisioning highly available systems. A fault is usually defined as an       event that causes a service to become unavailable. Each IT system will be       supported by assets, such as servers, disk arrays, switches, routers, and       so on. Each asset is susceptible to faults. Key performance indicators       (KPIs) can be used to determine the reliability of each asset and assess       whether goals for MTD, RTO, and RPO can be met. Some of the main KPIs       relating to component reliability are as follows:
                    - **Mean           time between failures (MTBF)** represents the expected lifetime         of a product. The calculation for MTBF is the total operational time         divided by the number of failures. For example, if you have 10         appliances that run for 50 hours and two of them fail, the MTBF is 250         hours/failure (10*50)/2.
                    - **Mean           time to failure (MTTF)** expresses a similar metric for         non-repairable components. For example, a hard drive may be described         with an MTTF, while a server, which could be repaired by replacing the         hard drive, would be described with an MTBF. The calculation for MTTF is         the total operational time divided by the number of devices. For         example, say two drives were installed in the server in a RAID array.         One had failed after 10 years, but had never been replaced, and the         second failed after 14 years, bringing down the array and the server.         The MTTF of the drives is (10+14)/2 = 12 years.
                - MTTF/MTBF can be used to determine the amount of       asset redundancy a system should have. A redundant system can failover to       another asset if there is a fault and continue to operate normally. It can       also be used to work out how likely failures are to occur.
                    - **Mean           time to repair (MTTR)** is a measure of the time taken to correct         a fault so that the system is restored to full operation. This can also         be described as mean time to replace or recover. MTTR is calculated as         the total number of hours of unplanned maintenance divided by the number         of failure incidents. This average value can be used to estimate whether         a recovery time objective (RTO) is achievable.
                - A system that can experience failures in individual components and       subsystems and continue to provide the same (or nearly the same) level of       service is said to be fault tolerant. Fault tolerance is often achieved by       provisioning redundancy for critical components to eliminate single points       of failure. A redundant or failover component is one that is not essential       to the normal function of a system but that allows the system to recover       from the failure of another component. Examples of devices and solutions       that provide fault tolerance include the following:
                    - **Redundant spares**—Components such as power supplies,         network cards, drives (RAID), and cooling fans provide protection         against hardware failures. A fully redundant server configuration is         configured with multiple components for each function (power,         networking, and storage). A faulty component will then automatically         failover to the working one.
                    - **Network links**—If there are multiple paths between         switches and routers, these devices can automatically failover to a         working path if a cable or network port is damaged.
                    - **Uninterruptible power supplies (UPSs) and standby power           supplies**—Provide power protection in the event of complete         power failure (blackout) and other types of building power issues.
                    - **Backup strategies**—Provide protection for data.
                    - **Cluster services**—A means of ensuring that the total         failure of a server does not disrupt services generally.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 7.4.5 Load Balancers
                - Where NIC teaming allows load balancing at the component level, a **load balancer** can be deployed as a hardware appliance or software instance to distribute client requests across server nodes in a farm or pool. You can use a load balancer in any situation where you have multiple servers providing the same function. Examples include web servers, front-end email servers, and web conferencing, video conferencing, or streaming media servers. The load balancer is placed in front of the server network and distributes requests from the client network or Internet to the application servers. The service address is advertised to clients as a virtual server. This is used to provision services that can scale from light to heavy loads, provision fault tolerant services, and to provide mitigation against distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/4Fnt6MYxtj0o0WK8Y3Uenbu2cIKYJd6s-WlmwyLBl91mxGsQaEUUhzoFk5ii_YWI8jbUU3UUKU187QxpyJWGJ8bMJNxUxcntrU5Z2Sc2scn_NeDqoVJv5XI7k8gNpe8u.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1. Client connects to the virtual server over the Internet.
            - 2.The load balancer selects a web server instance to handle the connection and forwards the traffic.
            - 3.A persistence mechanism can keep the client connected to the same server, if it is available.
            -  *Topology of basic load balancing architecture. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - There are two main types of load balancers:
                - **Layer 4 switch**—Basic load balancers make forwarding decisions on IP address and TCP/UDP header values, working at the Transport layer of the OSI model.
                - **Layer 7 switch (content switch)**—As web applications have become more complex, modern load balancers need to be able to make forwarding decisions based on application-level data, such as a request for a particular URL or data types like video or audio streaming. This requires more complex logic, but the processing power of modern appliances is sufficient to deal with this.
                - We are used to associating switches with layer 2 (Ethernet), but appliances can perform switch-like forwarding at layer 3, layer 4, and layer 7. These are collectively referred to as multilayer switches.
                - 
                -
