            - ### Scan with nmap 00:00-00:41
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Sometimes network resources aren't accessible for](undefined)[administration and troubleshooting directly, even](undefined)[if you are an IT administrator on the network.](undefined)[For example, some servers might be managed by a development](undefined)[team for testing builds or staging new product deployments.](undefined)
            - [When another team controls the resources, you might not](undefined)[have the passwords or credentials necessary to troubleshoot](undefined)[systems when they are reportedly not responding.](undefined)[In this demonstration, we'll use nmap, and the](undefined)[graphical version of nmap called Zenmap, to](undefined)[verify the availability of a service running on a](undefined)[machine without accessing the machine directly.](undefined)
            - ### Use nmap  00:41-01:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [First, let's explain what nmap is.](undefined)[nmap is an open-source tool for scanning](undefined)[hosts on a network for open service ports.](undefined)[Using various methods, nmap tries to create a](undefined)[network connection with a host on network ports.](undefined)
            - [If successful, the port is open.](undefined)[That means traffic can reach it and some service responded.](undefined)[If not, it is either not in use or something,](undefined)[such as a firewall, is preventing network traffic](undefined)[from being replied to by a service on the machine.](undefined)
            - [In our scenario, the Linux system at 10.20.30.41](undefined)[is hosting a website used for collaboration.](undefined)[The site is configured to run over HTTP on port 80.](undefined)[Users have reported that it is not available.](undefined)
            - ### Scan a Host 01:23-02:17
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [For this demonstration, we'll use an Ubuntu](undefined)[Linux system with nmap and Zenmap both installed.](undefined)[nmap is a command-line tool.](undefined)[It has a significant number of options and scripts](undefined)[that can be used to probe a host for information.](undefined)
            - [From a terminal, we will launch a simple scan of the](undefined)[host in question to see if it responds to any traffic.](undefined)[We'll use the command 'nmap 10.20.30.41'.](undefined)[Note that some nmap commands require root privileges.](undefined)[This one does not.](undefined)[The response came back showing that the "Host seems down."](undefined)
            - [As a suggestion, the nmap command has recommended running](undefined)[nmap with the -Pn option to see if that is really true.](undefined)[The -Pn option tells nmap to perform the](undefined)[scan without performing host discovery,](undefined)[assuming that all hosts queried are up.](undefined)[As you can see, the output shows that the host is](undefined)[indeed up, and that port 22 for the SSH service is open.](undefined)
            - ### Use Zenmap 02:17-03:13
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We'll show you that last command](undefined)[using the graphical tool, Zenmap.](undefined)[The interface is very helpful in showing](undefined)[you the nmap commands that will be executed.](undefined)[That's what Zenmap does: it uses a graphical](undefined)[interface to choose options and the output of the](undefined)[nmap command, which is run in the background.](undefined)
            - [In the target field, we'll enter the IP address to scan.](undefined)[Here we can see the command.](undefined)[The dropdown here shows some already configured scan types.](undefined)[We'll leave this Profile as it is but change](undefined)[the command to include the scan we used from](undefined)[the terminal: nmap -Pn and the IP address.](undefined)[Then, we'll press Enter.](undefined)[The scan runs.](undefined)[We get the same results, as expected.](undefined)
            - [Based on the output we've seen here, we can conclude](undefined)[that either the web service running on the host is](undefined)[not working, or that the firewall is blocking it.](undefined)[We could, then, contact someone who administers that system](undefined)[and give them that information so they can resolve the issue.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 03:13-03:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[We've shown you how to use nmap and](undefined)[Zenmap for simple troubleshooting of a](undefined)[remote system by scanning for open ports.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 8.2.4 Discovery Protocols
                - Nmap is a relatively complex app that needs a Windows or Linux computer       to run it. Switch, router, and access point appliances can run simpler       discovery protocols to identify other devices on the same local (Data       Link) network.
                - ## Cisco Discovery Protocol
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - **Cisco           Discovery Protocol (CDP)** runs by default on all Cisco       switch, router, and access point hardware. It uses Data Link layer       multicast messaging to send status announcements over local interfaces       every 60 seconds. CDP uses the multicast address `01:00:0c:cc:cc:cc`.       Each device keeps a cache table of the data compiled from announcements it       has received.
                - The command `show cdp neighbors` reports information from the       CDP cache. CDP can report device ID/host name, IOS version, interface       addresses and statistics, VLAN information, and Power over Ethernet usage.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/EVX_TYTzQws9Qlw4O6LlQUZYz9ngo1zNcyk9-4WD1rNFAy_t3nJnifR1ar3l70bQCek2crS_UtKlLQHTjSwWw3Q7_atckOodn9YJj5Xgoi-Qai-OngJn7zmzf3_nPYcs.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The output shows capability codes and a table. Capability codes. R:           router. T: trans bridge. B: source route bridge. S: switch. H: host.           I: I G M P. R: repeater. P: phone. The table provides data on device I           D, local interface, hold time, capability, platform, and port I D.
                    -  *Output from show cdp neighbors command listing router physical           interface plus subinterfaces and a number of switches (these are           actually VoIP handsets with embedded voice VLAN switches). (Image ©           and Courtesy of Cisco Systems, Inc. Unauthorized use not permitted.)* 
                - ## Link Layer Discovery Protocol
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - CDP performs a very useful function, but as a proprietary protocol is       restricted to use on Cisco hardware or hardware from licensed vendors. The       IEEE **Link           Layer Discovery Protocol (LLDP)** is a standards-based       approach that offers similar functionality. LLDP uses the multicast       address `01:80:c2:00:00:0e`. It sends announcements every 30       seconds, by default.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 8.2.5 Performance Monitoring
                - Network monitoring tools fulfill a wide range of functions beyond host       and service discovery. As input, they can capture and analyze traffic,       monitor interface and device metrics, and consolidate log data. As output,       they can alert you to events, help you define baselines, analyze traffic       patterns and congestion, determine upgrade and forecast needs, and       generate reports for management.
                - ## Performance Metrics
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - When you are monitoring a network host or appliance, several **performance         metrics** can tell you whether the host is operating normally:
                    - **Bandwidth**—This is the rated speed of all the         interfaces available to the device, measured in Mbps or Gbps. For wired         Ethernet links, this will not usually vary, but the bandwidth of WAN and         wireless links can change over time.
                    - **Utilization/throughput**—This is the actual amount of         data transferred. Utilization expresses this as a percentage of the         bandwidth, while throughput is the amount of data transferred per unit         of time.
                    - **CPU and memory**—Devices such as switches and routers         perform a lot of processing. If CPU and/or system memory utilization         (measured as a percentage) is consistently very high, an upgrade might         be required. High CPU utilization can also indicate a problem with         network traffic.
                    - **Storage**—Some network devices require persistent         storage (typically, one or more flash drives) to keep configuration         information and logs. Storage is measured in MB or GB. If the device         runs out of storage space, it could cause serious errors. Servers also         depend on fast input/output (I/O) to run applications efficiently.
                - ## Baseline Metrics
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - **Baseline           metrics** establish the level of resource utilization at       a point in time, such as when the system was first installed. This       provides a comparison to measure system responsiveness later. For example,       if a company is expanding a remote office that is connected to the       corporate office with an ISP's basic tier package, the baseline can help       determine if there is enough reserve bandwidth to handle the extra user       load, or if the basic package needs to be upgraded to support higher       bandwidths.
                - Reviewing baselines is the process of evaluating whether a baseline is       still fit for purpose or whether a new baseline should be established.       Changes to the system usually require a new baseline to be taken.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 8.2.6 Availability Monitoring
                - An       **availability monitor** triggers an alert or alarm if a       host or service experiences an outage or other unscheduled downtime. These       tools are also referred to as heartbeat monitors or uptime monitors. Most       work by sending a probe to the target service and checking for a non-error       response. For example, an HTTP service should return a 200 status code       when a resource is available. Some monitors may also be configured to       check the expiry date of digital certificates.
                - When you are troubleshooting unresponsive service issues, they will       usually manifest with multiple clients being unable to connect. There can       be any number of underlying causes, but consider some of the following:
                    - The application or OS hosting the service has crashed (or there is a         hardware or power problem).
                    - The server hosting the service is overloaded (high CPU/memory/disk I/O         utilization/disk space utilization). Try throttling client connections         until the server resources can be upgraded.
                    - There is congestion in the network, either at the client or server end         (or both). Use `ping`or `traceroute`to check         the latency experienced over the link and compare to a network         performance baseline. Again, throttling connections or bandwidth may         help to ease the congestion until higher bandwidth links can be         provisioned.
                    - A broadcast storm is causing loss of network bandwidth. Switching         loops causes broadcast and unknown unicast frames to circulate the         network perpetually, as each switch repeatedly floods each frame. A         broadcast storm may quickly consume all link bandwidth and crash network         appliances (check for excessive CPU utilization on switches and hosts).         The Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) is supposed to prevent such loops, but         this can fail if STP communications between switches do not work         correctly, either because of a fault in cabling or a port/transceiver or         because of a misconfiguration. Ports can also be configured with storm         control. This will start to drop broadcasts and unknown unicasts if they         reach a certain level.
                    - Network congestion or high host CPU/memory utilization may also be a         sign that the service is being subject to a denial of service (DoS)         attack. Look for unusual access patterns (for example, use GeoIP to         graph source IP addresses by country and compare to baseline access         patterns).
                - If users on a LAN cannot connect to an external       service, such as a cloud application, use a site such as       isitdownrightnow.com to test whether the issue is local to your network or       a problem with the service provider site.
                - 
                - Be proactive in monitoring service availability so       that you can resolve problems before they affect large numbers of clients.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 8.2.7 Configuration Monitoring
                - Configuration management processes ensure that all network appliances are       in a known state. Recall that there are various configuration states:
                    - The baseline or golden configuration is a template for the state that         a given device should be in.
                    - The production configuration is the state that the device is actually         in. Also, a device could have a running configuration that is different         from its startup configuration.
                    - A backup configuration is a point-in-time copy of a running or startup         configuration.
                - A **configuration           monitor** generates logs, alerts, or alarms when there       is a change to a device's production configuration. Some tools may be       capable of identifying line-by-line differences between production and       baseline configurations.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_monitor_utilization_n09/d_monitor_utilization_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_monitor_utilization_n09/d_monitor_utilization_n09.jpg)

            - ### Utilization Monitoring 00:00-00:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we'll cover utilization monitoring.](undefined)[This is something you should do on your](undefined)[network hosts, such as workstations and servers,](undefined)[as well as your network infrastructure devices,](undefined)[such as switches, routers, firewalls, and so on.](undefined)
            - [We don't have time to cover all of](undefined)[these devices in this demonstration.](undefined)[We'll look at monitoring utilization](undefined)[on this Windows workstation.](undefined)[The principles are the same with all devices.](undefined)[The process you'll use will vary depending on the device.](undefined)
            - ### Task Manager 00:32-01:01
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [On a Windows workstation, there are two tools you can use](undefined)[to monitor utilization: Task Manager and Resource Monitor.](undefined)[Let's first look at Task Manager.](undefined)[To start Task Manager, right-click](undefined)[the task bar and select Task Manager.](undefined)
            - [By default, the applications that are running are displayed.](undefined)[This is a list of applications](undefined)[that are running on the system.](undefined)[There's not a lot you can do or see](undefined)[here as far as monitoring utilization.](undefined)[If you want more information, you can go](undefined)[down to More Details here, at the bottom.](undefined)
            - ### Process Tab 01:01-01:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [On the left, we start with the Process tab.](undefined)[Here you have four columns: CPU, Memory, Disk, and Network.](undefined)[If you want a quick look at the processes that are using](undefined)[the most resources, you can click here and sort the column.](undefined)
            - [This is useful if, for example, you want to](undefined)[see how much memory an application is using.](undefined)[From here, you can also right-click a process and](undefined)[get a menu for more options, such as ending the task.](undefined)[You can also select Search Online](undefined)[to read more about the process.](undefined)
            - ### Performance Tab 01:29-04:02
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [On the Performance tab, you see several parameters.](undefined)[First, there's CPU utilization.](undefined)[You can see overall CPU usage.](undefined)[Here, you can see the current percent of utilization.](undefined)[This graph, over here, represents the](undefined)[average of all the CPUs in the system.](undefined)[Down here, you can see the maximum speed, how many sockets](undefined)[we have, how many cores, and the logical processors.](undefined)
            - [This gives you an idea of what's happening with the CPU.](undefined)[Occasional spikes here are nothing to be concerned about.](undefined)[Obviously, the more applications you have](undefined)[running, the more CPU you're going to use.](undefined)
            - [Let's look at Memory.](undefined)[Here's how much physical memory is installed on the system.](undefined)[This line shows how much of the physical memory is in use.](undefined)[Down here are the numbers that tell you what's going on.](undefined)
            - [If we go down here, to Ethernet,](undefined)[we can monitor network utilization.](undefined)
            - [There are other tabs in Task Manager,](undefined)[so let's take a quick look them.](undefined)
            - [We have App History.](undefined)[By default, the App History tab displays usage only](undefined)[for modern and Universal Windows Platform apps.](undefined)[Since we don't use these apps very](undefined)[often, it's not going to show a lot.](undefined)
            - [Under Startup, we can see the impact that apps and](undefined)[programs have on the system when it's starting up.](undefined)[If there's a program you don't need every time](undefined)[you use the system and you want your system to](undefined)[start up faster, you can disable the program by](undefined)[right-clicking it and selecting Disable.](undefined)
            - [For example, this program here,](undefined)[Skype, isn't necessary on the system.](undefined)[We can disable it.](undefined)[Be careful not to disable something like your](undefined)[antivirus from starting when Windows boots.](undefined)
            - [Under Users, you can see who's logged in.](undefined)[If this were a system that multiple users](undefined)[logged in to, there could be times when](undefined)[another user did not log off completely.](undefined)[In that case, that user's account is using resources](undefined)[in the background, potentially slowing down the system.](undefined)
            - [The Details tab shows a few more](undefined)[options than the Process tab.](undefined)[It also allows you to access some advanced options.](undefined)[For example, if you want Explorer to have a higher](undefined)[priority than whatever is set by default, you can](undefined)[right-click it and set the priority higher.](undefined)[You then get a dialog box asking you to confirm.](undefined)[We'll select Change priority.](undefined)
            - [The last tab is Services.](undefined)[Under here, you can see all the](undefined)[services running on the system.](undefined)[One useful thing here is that you can](undefined)[start, stop, or restart a service.](undefined)
            - [Okay, let's go back to the Performance tab.](undefined)[All the tabs in Task Manager are quite useful.](undefined)[If you need a quick glance at what's going](undefined)[on in the system, you use Task Manager.](undefined)
            - ### Resource Monitor 04:02-04:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [However, if you want to see more detail, you can](undefined)[use a second tool, and that's Resource Monitor.](undefined)[If your system is not working correctly or efficiently,](undefined)[you should do a detailed analysis of what's going on.](undefined)[You use Resource Monitor because](undefined)[it displays more information.](undefined)
            - [Let's go down here and click Open Resource Monitor.](undefined)[Let's close Task Manager.](undefined)
            - ### Overview Tab 04:24-04:56
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [On the Overview tab, you get a summary](undefined)[view of what's going on with the system.](undefined)[Basically, it's a lot of the same](undefined)[information that we saw in Task Manager.](undefined)[We can look at CPU.](undefined)[Here we see a list of all the](undefined)[processes running on the system.](undefined)
            - [We can get an overview of the overall utilization.](undefined)[Notice over here, there are graphs showing what's going on.](undefined)[Here's the CPU.](undefined)[Below that we have the disk.](undefined)[We also have network utilization.](undefined)[And finally, at the bottom, we have memory utilization.](undefined)[Again, if you want more detail,](undefined)[you can go to a specific tab.](undefined)
            - ### CPU Tab 04:56-05:17
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's go up to the CPU tab and click it.](undefined)[We can see all the processes running on this CPU.](undefined)[If we click Services, we can see a list of](undefined)[the services that are running on this system.](undefined)[On the right, we can see graphs](undefined)[for the CPU utilization total.](undefined)[It displays the amount of CPU used by](undefined)[services on the system, and it graphs](undefined)[utilization for the two cores in the CPU.](undefined)
            - ### Memory Tab 05:17-05:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Likewise, we can go to the Memory tab.](undefined)[We see a list of all the processes running on](undefined)[the system and how much memory each one is using.](undefined)[We have a graph that shows the memory](undefined)[allocation in the system: how much is in use,](undefined)[how much is in standby, and how much is free.](undefined)
            - [Up here, we can see what's using the most memory.](undefined)[This is useful if you're troubleshooting a problem](undefined)[where you have excessive memory utilization.](undefined)[By clicking the various columns, you can see](undefined)[the processes that are using the most memory.](undefined)
            - ### Disk Tab 05:45-05:52
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [On the Disk tab, we can see the processes](undefined)[that are currently accessing the disk.](undefined)[Here, at the bottom, we can see](undefined)[the drives and storage usage.](undefined)
            - ### Network Tab 05:52-06:56
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We can also go to the Network tab and see the](undefined)[processes that are currently using the network.](undefined)
            - [Under Network Activity, we can see the services and](undefined)[processes running on the system and how much they're using.](undefined)[We can see the services that are sending and receiving.](undefined)
            - [Let's go down here.](undefined)[Under TCP Connections, we can see the connections that are](undefined)[currently established between this system and another system.](undefined)[We can see the process that established the connection.](undefined)[We can see the IP address of the local network board](undefined)[and the port that is in use on the local system.](undefined)[We can also see the remote system that we're connected](undefined)[to and the port that is in use on the remote system.](undefined)
            - [One useful thing down here, especially from a](undefined)[security standpoint, is the list of listening ports.](undefined)[These are network ports that are](undefined)[currently open on the local system.](undefined)[You can see which port it is and the](undefined)[process associated with that port.](undefined)
            - [Over to the right, just like all the other](undefined)[tabs, there are graphs that provide a quick](undefined)[visual representation of what's going on.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 06:56-07:11
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[We talked about monitoring utilization.](undefined)[We first looked at using Task Manager to](undefined)[monitor CPU, memory, disk, and network.](undefined)[Then we explored looking at those same](undefined)[parameters using Resource Monitor.](undefined)

        - **8.3 Simple Network Management Protocol** 
            - ## 8.3.1 SNMP Agents and Monitors
                - Stand-alone devices may have a web console to use for performance and       environmental monitoring. Local access is not scalable to managing tens or       hundreds of devices, however.
                - The Simple         Network Management Protocol (SNMP) is a widely used framework for       remote management and monitoring of servers and network appliances. SNMP       consists of agents and a monitoring system.
                - ## SNMP Agents
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The agent is a process (software or firmware) running on a switch,       router, server, or other SNMP-compatible network device. A device running       an SNMP agent is referred to as a managed device. The agent maintains a       data store called a **management           information base (MIB)** that holds variables relating       to the activity of the device, such as the number of frames per second       handled by a switch. Each parameter stored in a MIB is referred to by a       numeric Object Identifier (OID). OIDs are stored within a tree structure.       Part of the tree is generic to SNMP, while part can be defined by the       device vendor.
                - An agent is configured with the community string or community name of the       computers allowed to manage the agent and the IP address or host name of       the server running the management system. The community string acts as a       rudimentary type of password. An agent can pass information only to       management systems configured with the same community string. There are       usually two community strings; one for read-only access and one for       read-write access (or privileged mode).
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/X8MAFjNBld3bauSj6-0Hfvnvj-VK5bcxDDeoPcjqTWsZY129XotVZ8EwYOe-xuAP7VZ75O6EHF91_g035qbvVJVv4WfUQu0iLw6UwRQ52e_aDvdwYvYHBxAR0nqRVUd-.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The screenshot shows a window titled, Services: net-S N M P. The           General tab is selected. The settings of the general tab are as           follows: enable S N M P service, S N M P community, S N M P location,           S N M P contact, add agent X support, layer 3 visibility, display           version in O I D, and listen I Ps.
                    -  *Configuring an SNMP agent on an OPNsense security appliance.           (Screenshot courtesy of OPNsense.)* 
                - ## SNMP Monitor
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - An SNMP monitor is management software that provides a location from       which you can oversee network activity. The monitor polls agents at       regular intervals for information from their MIBs and displays the       information for review. It also displays any trap operations as alerts for       the network administrator to assess and act upon as necessary. The monitor       can retrieve information from a device in two main ways:
                    - **Get**—The software queries the agent for a single         OID. This command is used by the monitor to perform regular polling         (obtaining information from devices at defined intervals).
                    - **Trap**—The agent informs the monitor of a notable         event, such as port failure. The threshold for triggering traps can be         set for each value.
                - The monitor can be used to change certain variables using the Set       command. It can also walk an MIB subtree by using multiple Get and Get       Next commands. This is used to discover the complete layout of an MIB.       Device queries take place over UDP port 161; traps are communicated over       UDP port 162.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/ZUaoY7U9zQJ_jb1KD3wmllrL1nKgakdCFglttxGlCN91_oBESQh_BKMo84AErZ9JJPg9lExiUJr8B3O1dJaY9LP9GEit3qkg6AUN7lvLi1lACmiZiLNJMrlGinQBAh3t.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The diagram shows an access point (1), a server (2), a printer (6),           and a PC connected to a switch (3). The switch is connected to a           router (5). The router is connected to a voice gateway (4) and           internet. Another router (9) is also connected to the internet. This           router is connected to a switch (8). This switch is connected to a           server, a P C, and a voice switch (7). This switch is connected to a           trap receiver. The trap receiver has a S N M P manager. The S N M P           manager sends G E T slash S E T request to master agent. The G E T           slash S E T response from master agent leads to S N M P manager. An           arrow labeled trap from master agent points to trap receiver. Agents 1           to 8 have double-headed arrows between them and master agent. Agent 9           sends trap to trap receiver. Agent 9 sends G E T slash S E T request           to trap receiver. Trap receiver sends G E T slash S E T response from           trap receiver to agent 9.
                    -  *SNMP collects information from network devices for diagnostic           purposes. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 8.3.2 SNMP Security
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            -

            - ### Monitoring Protocol Components 00:00-NaN:NaN
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [As an MP relies upon several key](undefined)[components to perform its function.](undefined)[First is the SNMP manager.](undefined)[The SNMP manager is responsible for](undefined)[collecting data from network hosts that are](undefined)[being monitored using the SNMP protocol.](undefined)[The SNMP manager aggregates the information and displays](undefined)[an overview of the current status of the network.](undefined)
            - ### SNMP Agents 00:20-NaN:NaN
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next are the SNMP agents.](undefined)[The SNMP agents are small software applications](undefined)[that get installed on the network hardware that](undefined)[you want to monitor, such as servers, printers,](undefined)[switches, routers, and even firewalls.](undefined)[The agent is configured to monitor the device; they report](undefined)[events that happened on the device back to the SNMP manager.](undefined)[Devices with SNMP agents running are called managed devices.](undefined)
            - ### Management Information Base 00:43-NaN:NaN
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Finally, we have the management](undefined)[information base, or the MIB.](undefined)[The MIB defines and organizes the](undefined)[parameters, called variables, that the agents](undefined)[will monitor on their respective devices.](undefined)[The SNMP manager uses the MIB to determine](undefined)[what data it will gather from the agent.](undefined)[The MIB is a hierarchical, structured definition](undefined)[of variables, similar to a database schema.](undefined)[The MIB is not an actual database; it simply defines](undefined)[the structure of the information used by SNMP.](undefined)[You can use SNMP walk messages to traverse](undefined)[through the hierarchical MIB structure.](undefined)[Each variable in MIB has a unique](undefined)[number assigned to it, called the OID.](undefined)[SNMP operates at the application layer in the](undefined)[OSI model, and it runs on ports 161 and 162.](undefined)
            - ### SNMP Communications 01:28-NaN:NaN
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [SNMP communications can occur in three ways on your network.](undefined)[The first option is polling using pull mode.](undefined)[The SNMP manager periodically polls the SNMP agents to](undefined)[see what is happening with their respective devices using](undefined)[UDP port 161; these are referred to as get messages.](undefined)[It queries them for specific pieces](undefined)[of information using the MIB.](undefined)[For example, if the monitored device is a server, it may](undefined)[query the agent to see what CPU utilization looks like.](undefined)[The second option is to implement traps.](undefined)[With traps, you configure your SNMP](undefined)[agents with certain thresholds.](undefined)[Whenever the agent then detects that a threshold has](undefined)[been exceeded, it sends an SNMP trap message to the SNMP](undefined)[manager on UDP Port 162 instead of waiting to be polled.](undefined)[The third option is to use SNMP set commands.](undefined)[These set commands are generated by the SNMP](undefined)[manager and are used to actually modify information](undefined)[on the monitored hosts through the SNMP agent.](undefined)[For each variable you want to set, you need to specify the](undefined)[variable to update using its OID number and the MIB, the](undefined)[data type, and the value you want to assign to the variable.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - Many networks run SNMP v2c. This protocol version has no support for       robust authentication or encryption. When using SNMP v2c, apply the       following guidelines:
                - SNMP v2c **community             strings** are sent in plaintext and should not be         transmitted over the network if there is any risk of interception.
                - Use difficult-to-guess community strings; never leave the community         string blank or set it to the default.
                - Use access control lists to restrict management operations to known         hosts (that is, restrict to one or two host IP addresses).
            - SNMP v3 supports encryption and strong user-based authentication. Instead       of community strings, the agent is configured with a list of usernames and       access permissions. When authentication is required, the SNMP message is       signed with a hash of the user's passphrase. The agent can verify the       signature and authenticate the user using its own record of the       passphrase.
            - If authNoPriv mode is used, packets are not encrypted. authPriv enables       encryption using the credential as a key.
            - 
        - **8.4 Event Management** 
            - ## 8.4.1 Network Device Logs
                - Network device logs are one of the most valuable sources of performance, troubleshooting, and security auditing information. A single logged event consists of metadata, such as the date and time, category, and event ID, plus a description and contents of error or informational output. For example, you can use a system log to troubleshoot an IP conflict by looking for TCP/IP events or to determine when and why a system was shut down.
                - While the specifics of what and where events are logged can vary widely from platform to platform, it is possible to discern some general log types, including system, security, application, and performance or traffic.
                - ## System and Application Logs
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A system log records startup events plus subsequent changes to the configuration at an OS level. This will certainly include kernel processes and drivers but could also include core services.
                - By contrast, an application log records data for a single specific service, such as DNS, HTTP, or a database. Note that a complex application could write to multiple log files. For example, the Apache web server logs errors to one file and access attempts to another.
                - ## Audit Logs
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - An audit log records use of authentication and authorization privileges. It will generally record success/fail type events. An audit log might also be described as an access log or security log. Audit logging might be performed at an OS level and at a per-application level.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/TtDzwPY3z-1YKa8xMOAdWkaeT7jajnNVeg-5B1WiOxzVh4zNjrQCFE1VQTjFtF8ltnrqKlN8yle0uAiRt2Xu-Z1jMUItsnzqWwvrEjo9zmjbNTQM_Ym43iVQgNs_z2Ki.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The window has a drop-down at the top set to d s t underscore port. The window shows data in a table format whose headers read, interface, time, source, destination, protocol, and label.
            -  *Viewing audit logs on an OPNsense security appliance. (Screenshot courtesy of OPNsense.)* 
            - Audit logs typically associate an action with a particular user. This is one of the reasons that it is critical that users not share logon details. If a user account is compromised, there is no means of tying events in the log to the actual attacker.
                - ## Performance/Traffic Logs
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Performance and traffic logs record metrics for compute, storage, and network resources over a defined period.
                - 
                -

            - 00:001. Configuring Remote Logging

            - ### Configuring Remote Logging  00:00-01:33
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [There are many aspects of the syslog daemon that you can](undefined)[modify to customize how your log files are configured.](undefined)[You could have warning messages going into](undefined)[one file, error messages going into](undefined)[another, or separate them based on program.](undefined)[But one of the cool things I think the](undefined)[syslog daemon can do is log to a remote host.](undefined)[This basically allows us to write our logs not only to](undefined)[our local system but also to a log host somewhere else.](undefined)[This is a great benefit for the administrator.](undefined)[You can set up a log host in your network](undefined)[somewhere and all the logs from all the](undefined)[systems you support go into that log host.](undefined)[If somebody's having a problem, instead of having to](undefined)[use SSH in their system to look at their log files,](undefined)[you can have one central location to view them.](undefined)[This can be very helpful in keeping](undefined)[history and tracking your log files.](undefined)[Syslog servers are also very helpful when](undefined)[there has been an attack on your network.](undefined)
            - [That's what we're going to do here.](undefined)[We're going to configure the syslog](undefined)[daemon to log to a remote host.](undefined)[I have two different Linux systems running.](undefined)[I have a RedHat system running here that will](undefined)[serve as my log host and I have a CentOS system](undefined)[running here that will serve as my log client.](undefined)[The log messages from the CentOS system](undefined)[are going to be saved on our syslog server](undefined)[which happens to be our Redhat system.](undefined)[Keep in mind that different Linux](undefined)[distributions may be slightly different.](undefined)[But the same concepts apply.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Log Host  01:33-03:54
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's configure the log host first.](undefined)[The first thing we need to do is check](undefined)[to see if the rsyslog daemon is running.](undefined)[Type 'systemctl status rsyslog'.](undefined)[As you can see everything appear to be in order.](undefined)[We need to change to our root user account, so I'll](undefined)[do a 'su- root' command and enter the password.](undefined)[We're going to edit the configuration file](undefined)[with the 'vi /etc/rsyslog.conf' command.](undefined)[As we scroll down, we're going to uncomment some modules.](undefined)[These are the imudp module and the imtcp module.](undefined)[These modules enable the syslog daemon](undefined)[to listen for incoming syslog messages.](undefined)
            - [Down at the bottom in the rules area we have a](undefined)[template we're going to use for our remote logging.](undefined)[This template isn't here by default so it has to be added.](undefined)[This allows the remote logs to be placed in](undefined)[their own folder by host name and program name.](undefined)[If you don't set up a template, all syslog](undefined)[messages from remote hosts will go in the](undefined)[/var/log/messages of the syslog server.](undefined)[We're going to uncomment this by removing the pound symbols.](undefined)[Let's save it by typing ':wq!'.](undefined)
            - [For these changes to take effect, we must restart the](undefined)[rsyslog daemon by typing 'systemctl restart rsyslog'.](undefined)[Since there will be incoming traffic, we need to modify](undefined)[the firewall to accept incoming messages on port 514.](undefined)
            - [To do so, type 'firewall-cmd](undefined)[--permanent --add-port=514/udp'.](undefined)[When you press Enter you see that it was a success.](undefined)[Now we do the same thing with the TCP protocol](undefined)[with "arrow up" and remove UDP for TCP.](undefined)[The changes won't be active until we reload the](undefined)[firewall with the 'firewall-cmd --reload' command.](undefined)
            - [I'm going to do a netstat to make sure the](undefined)[syslog daemon is listening on port 514.](undefined)
            - [Type 'netstat -tulnp | grep "rsyslo"' and press Enter.](undefined)[This shows us that rsyslogd has](undefined)[port 514 open for UPD and TCP.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Log Client  03:54-05:08
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We're going to venture over to our CentOS](undefined)[client server that's going to be sending](undefined)[syslog messages to our RedHat server.](undefined)[First, we check to make sure the rsyslog daemon](undefined)[is running by typing "systemctl status rsyslog".](undefined)[We're good to go.](undefined)
            - [Just like our syslog server, we're going to](undefined)[modify the configuration file for rsyslog.](undefined)[Let's type 'sudo vi /etc/rsyslog.conf'.](undefined)[Enter the password and press Enter.](undefined)[We're going to scroll all the way to](undefined)[the bottom to this configuration file.](undefined)
            - [Just so that I know what this is, I'm going](undefined)[to put a comment in there which is "#syslog](undefined)[server" and type '*.* @@192.168.0.55:514'.](undefined)[The *.* is a wildcard that sends all](undefined)[syslog messages to the syslog server.](undefined)[The IP address listed is the IP of the syslog](undefined)[server and 514 is the port specified to use.](undefined)[We save this with ':wq!' and Enter.](undefined)
            - ['sudo systemctl restart rsyslog' allows the rsyslog](undefined)[daemon to grab the new settings we just edited.](undefined)
            - ### Test Syslog Messages 05:08-06:03
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now this is the fun part.](undefined)[We will actually get to see a message go over to](undefined)[our syslog server from our CentOS client server.](undefined)[To write a test message, we type 'logger](undefined)[this is a test message' and press Enter.](undefined)[Let's use 'sudo tail -f /var/log/messages'](undefined)[to see the message locally.](undefined)[Now that we see the local message, let's go over to](undefined)[our syslog server with 'cd /var/log/centos-server1/'.](undefined)[I'm going to do a quick 'll' to list](undefined)[what log files have already been written.](undefined)[Since we did a logger command under the TestOut user,](undefined)[we're going to tail that log file to look for our](undefined)[newly created test message with 'tail -f testout.log'.](undefined)[As you can see, it's the exact same message](undefined)[that was on our CentOS client server.](undefined)
            - ### Summary  06:03-06:33
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demonstration, we talked about how to](undefined)[configure a log host with Linux on a network.](undefined)[We first looked at the steps you need to complete in order](undefined)[to configure the system that's going to function as the log](undefined)[host to receive logging messages from another system.](undefined)[Then we looked at the log client and configured it](undefined)[to send its log messages not only to its own log](undefined)[files, but also to send a copy to our log host.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 8.4.2 Log Collectors and Syslog
                - When an event is generated by a device, it might be logged to the console or to a local file. Accessing the console or log files on individual devices is impractical on a large network. A log collector receives event messages forwarded from numerous devices to a single storage location. As well as collecting logs, the system can be configured to run one or more status and alerting dashboards.
                - **Syslog** is an example of a protocol and supporting software that facilitates log collection. It has become a de facto standard for logging events from distributed systems. For example, syslog messages can be generated by Cisco routers and switches, as well as UNIX or Linux servers and workstations. A syslog collector usually listens on UDP port 514.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/dHPOHZsAsxXATpuMsOffsDeQ4c6dVsg2PEK_1G_oTUaTsJoxu0Oey17Gb5XCHCxVRPfEKp-suSPxmtYyFHy2zVFf9S3tKBBu0QjpiLRLAUc0RIMlKnef4z7bvzuaZfVo.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The screenshot shows a dialog box titled, edit destination overlapping the window. The dialog box shows fields for enabled, transport, applications, levels, facilities, hostname, port, and description. Two buttons, cancel and save are at the bottom.
            -  *Configuring an OPNsense security appliance to transmit logs to a remote syslog server. The syslog server has the IP address 10.1.16.242 and listens on UDP port 514. (Screenshot courtesy of OPNsense.)* 
            - As well as a protocol for forwarding messages to a remote log collector, Syslog provides an open format for event data. A syslog message comprises a PRI code, a header containing a timestamp and host name, and a message part. The PRI code is calculated from the facility and a severity level. The message part contains a tag showing the source process plus content. The format of the content is application dependent. It might use space- or comma-delimited fields or name/value pairs, such as JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) data.
            - 
            -

            - [We do have to touch the router in order](undefined)[for this to work.](undefined)[What we're going to do is I'm in Global Config](undefined)[as you can see right here.](undefined)[We'll type out 'Service' and then I'll hit the 'Tab' key](undefined)[there so I can save some time.](undefined)['Time stamps.' We're going to do log, date time, milliseconds.](undefined)[We're also going to do our service. Time stamps,](undefined)[and we're going to do debug. Or](undefined)[this happened an hour ago."](undefined)

            - ### Debugging 02:01-03:13
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I'm going to type 'logging trap'](undefined)[and we're going to do 'debugging.'](undefined)[If you remember there are eight different levels,](undefined)[zero through seven, of the types of messages](undefined)[that we could get.](undefined)[Anywhere from emergency at a level zero](undefined)[all the way up to what we're doing, debugging,](undefined)[on level seven.](undefined)
            - [You may not want to do debugging in your infrastructure](undefined)[you may just want to do warnings, or errors](undefined)[or critical messages that are coming across](undefined)[on your server.](undefined)[I'm picking number seven or the severity level seven](undefined)[is what they call debugging.](undefined)[Not only will I do seven it will do everything below that.](undefined)[If we were to pick warning](undefined)[which is a severity level of four,](undefined)[it would do everything between zero and four.](undefined)
            - [You're going to get less information](undefined)[but sometimes that's better right?](undefined)[Having too much information can be a bad thing sometimes.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and we're going to](undefined)[turn on debugging here.](undefined)[I'm going to hit 'Enter. We're all done.](undefined)[That's all there is to it to turn on logging.](undefined)[I'm going to do a 'do show run' so we can](undefined)[look at the running config.](undefined)
            - [You can see the service time stamps](undefined)[right up here that we turned on for logging](undefined)[and debugging for date and time.](undefined)[Then we'll get down here to the bottom](undefined)[and you'll see what we're trapping for,](undefined)[and you'll see the IP address that we're sending](undefined)[this information to.](undefined)

            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 8.4.3 Event Prioritization and Alerting
                - Devices can generate thousands of events per hour. There must be a system       for prioritizing those that require an immediate response over those that       are nonurgent or informational only. Most logging systems categorize each       event. For example, in Windows, system and application events are defined       as Informational, Warning, or Critical, while audit events are categorized       as Success or Fail.
                - Syslog severity levels are as follows:
                - The **logging         level** configured on each host determines the maximum       level at which events are recorded or forwarded. For example, if the       logging level for remote forwarding is set to 4, events that are level 5,       6, or 7 are  *not*  forwarded.
                - An automated event management system can be configured to generate some       sort of **alert**. An alert can indicate when certain event       types of a given severity are encountered. Alerts can also be generated by       setting thresholds for performance counters. Examples include packet loss,       link bandwidth drops, number of sessions established, delay/jitter in       real-time applications, and so on. Finally, an alert can reveal an       anomaly, or patterns of behavior or usage that are not consistent with       normal activity. Most network monitors also support heartbeat tests so       that you can receive an alert if a device or server stops responding to       probes.
                - Setting alerts is a matter of balance. On the one hand, you do not want       performance to deteriorate to the point that it affects user activity; on       the other hand, you do not want to be overwhelmed by alerts.
                - You can also make a distinction between alerts and notifications. An       alert means that the system has matched some sort of pattern or filter       that should be recorded and highlighted. A **notification **means       that the system sends a message to advertise the occurrence of the alert.       A low priority alert may simply be displayed in the system dashboard. A       high priority alert might use some sort of active notification messaging,       such as emailing a system administrator, sending a text message (SMS) to a       phone, or triggering a physical alarm signal.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/j5dLOM0n001NgVZPP-_3Kp5LT1zDLS2TdDEoqrMLPJLlwT9uyZxlChxqmYX1JGTttvxF83KaNeRIhkB2mGRTipyp5cGOfV9C1w58sCp8em7MOAtbQNc4WWfMOkCKHdr1.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - A dialog box titled, Action Options overlaps the window. The dialog           box includes sections for threshold, frequency, log action activity,           and dynamic content enhancement. Three buttons, O K, Cancel, and Help           are at the top-right. O K is selected.
                    -  *Configuring alert and notification settings in EventSentry SIEM.           (Screenshot courtesy of NETIKUS.NET Ltd.)* 
                - There should be some process for acknowledging and dismissing alerts as       they are generated. A serious alert may need to be processed as an       incident and assigned a job ticket for formal investigation. If an alert       is a false positive, it can be dismissed. If the management system or       dashboard is allowed to become cluttered with old alerts, it is much more       difficult to identify new alerts and gauge the overall status of the       network.
                -

            - ### Logging Events on pfSense 00:00-00:33

            - ## 8.4.4 Security Information and Event Management
                - As distinct from collection, log aggregation refers to normalizing data from different sources so that it is consistent and searchable. Aggregation can also condense repetitive, individual events to a summary event that counts the number of instances. **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** is designed to integrate network and security monitoring through automated collection, aggregation, and analysis of log data. The core function of a SIEM tool is to aggregate logs from multiple sources. In addition to logs from Windows and Linux-based hosts, this could include devices such as switches, routers, firewalls, intrusion detection sensors, vulnerability scanners, malware scanners, and databases.
                - To perform log aggregation, SIEM software features connectors or plug-ins to interpret (or parse) data from distinct types of systems and to account for differences between vendor implementations. Usually parsing will be carried out using regular expressions tailored to each log file format to identify attributes and content that can be mapped to standard fields in the SIEM's reporting and analysis tools. Another important function is to normalize date/time zone differences to a single timeline.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/3A6I4cJ69imsVjxDXcNASkzYB_9qF8Vnn0glzhxxnL9AmRQHNY2_CyJ2SUDEpGqbv55zGlOlJ6aKUfj6wE5ECP9uLnlA6wmlfpb323mhMPYVTiw_cM3HX0w0MJjflgjF.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The dashboard shows an area graph representing alert level evolution and a donut chart representing top M I T R E attacks. Total: 245. Level 12 or above alerts: 1. Authentication failure: 0. Authentication success: 107.
            -  *Wazuh SIEM dashboard—Configurable dashboards provide a high-level status view of network security metrics. (Screenshot used with permission from Wazuh Inc.)* 
            - Different monitoring and logging products can also be integrated using an application programming interface (API). An API makes the functions of a particular product available to scripts. For example, a configuration monitor could have an API that allows a SIEM to initiate a scan and return the results directly to the SIEM.
            -

            - ### Data Collection Process 00:00-00:33
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Everything starts with the collectors.](undefined)[Log collectors are responsible for gathering event logs](undefined)[from security appliances, host systems, and applications.](undefined)[We can also add sensors to the system to gather other data,](undefined)[such as capturing network packets or data inputs from](undefined)[the different systems on the network.](undefined)[This data will be sent to event collectors.](undefined)[The collector sends all data and logs to the SIEM.](undefined)
            - [The SIEM software takes this data, reads and analyzes](undefined)[it, and separates it into categories](undefined)[such as logon attempts, database entries, port](undefined)[scans, network congestion, and more.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 8.4.5 Log Reviews
                - Monitoring involves viewing traffic, protocols, and events in real time. Network and log reviewing, or analysis, involves later inspection and interpretation of the captured data to determine what was happening on the network during the capture. Only referring to the logs after a major incident misses the opportunity to identify performance problems or security issues early and to respond proactively.
                - Not all performance incidents will be revealed by a single event. One of the features of log analysis and reporting software should be to identify trends. Examining each event in a log file makes it difficult to spot a trend. Plotting data as a graph is particularly helpful as it makes it easier to spot trends, spikes, or troughs in a visualization of events rather than the raw data. Most performance monitors, log collectors, and SIEMs can plot metrics in a graph.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/KkfubmLkH55Hr3dhMEi0_hakO247qBP3hsxsw5szZLM-eMbD51hR0PF_sreWI1R_WNbggGXyzHu-wBzoqSl_oCl0D7Mb3vC91Qb3mcN30cruMrDMCd9Q8ojFSUxiFIH2.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The left pane shows system monitor under reports of performance selected. The right pane shows the performance report graph.
            -  *Data retrieved from a performance log file. (Screenshot courtesy of Microsoft.)*

                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we'll discuss auditing device logs.](undefined)[and we'll use our Cisco managed switch as an example.](undefined)[Now, auditing device logs requires two things:](undefined)[First, we must enable logging so that we have log](undefined)[events that are being captured on the device.](undefined)[And second, we take the time to review or](undefined)[audit the logs to see what's happening.](undefined)[Let's see how this is done.](undefined)
            - ### Log Settings 00:25-00:54
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [First, let's enable logging by clicking](undefined)[Administration, System Log, then Log Settings.](undefined)[You'll see that logging is currently enabled.](undefined)[This particular device has three types of logging.](undefined)[You can save log events in RAM, the random-access](undefined)[memory which will be lost after the system reboots.](undefined)[You can also save log events in flash memory which](undefined)[means that they'll be stored after the system reboots.](undefined)[And, finally, you can also aggregate, or compile,](undefined)[your logs and send them to a SYS log server.](undefined)
            - ### Event Aggregation 00:54-01:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To enable event aggregation, we enable the Syslog](undefined)[aggregator by checking the box and entering a time](undefined)[in seconds for Max Aggregation Time.](undefined)[Will enter 300 here, which will mean 5 minutes.](undefined)
            - [If you have a set of events that happen](undefined)[repeatedly for example, the same event keeps](undefined)[happening every five seconds what will happen is the](undefined)[aggregator will say this event happened 15 times.](undefined)[Instead of seeing a separate entry for each similar](undefined)[event, you'll see the event happened and the number](undefined)[of times it happened within that aggregation period.](undefined)[We'll click apply to enable logging with aggregation.](undefined)
            - ### Remote Log Servers 01:28-01:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()

            - ### RAM Memory Logging 01:45-02:55
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [As you'll notice there are several levels of](undefined)[logging on this device starting with Emergency.](undefined)[Let's talk about what each of these mean.](undefined)
            - [The Emergency level of severity](undefined)[means the system is not usable.](undefined)[An alert means that there's some](undefined)[sort of action that's needed.](undefined)[An event with critical severity means that](undefined)[the system is in a critical condition.](undefined)[An error means that there's an error in the system.](undefined)[Warning means that a system warning has occurred and a](undefined)[notice means that the system is functioning properly, but](undefined)[it just wants to let you know that something has happened.](undefined)[The informational level log is about device information.](undefined)[And then if you're having an issue with the device, you can](undefined)[log detailed debugging information about a particular event.](undefined)[As you'll notice we don't have debug information selected.](undefined)[But in our RAM logging , we're logging](undefined)[all of the other device events.](undefined)[Selecting a severity level causes all of](undefined)[those events higher to be selected as well.](undefined)[For example, we'll choose informational, and notice all](undefined)[the more critical events above it are selected, too.](undefined)
            - [We can also specify the Originator Identifier in](undefined)[our log messages by choosing None, or by hostname,](undefined)[IP address, or a description of your choice.](undefined)
            - ### Flash Memory Logging 02:55-03:11
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [For flash memory logging which will be available after a](undefined)[system reboot, we're logging only a few top severity levels.](undefined)[These events are aggregating in our error log every 300](undefined)[seconds, and then sent off to our defined SYS log server.](undefined)[Now let's take a look at some](undefined)[of the events in the event log.](undefined)

            - 
        - **8.5 Packet Capture and Analysis** 
            - ## 8.5.1 Packet Capture
                - A protocol analyzer is one of the most important tools used for network support. A protocol analyzer allows inspection of traffic received by a host or passing over a network link. A protocol analyzer depends on a **packet sniffer**. A sniffer captures frames moving over the network medium.
                - Often the terms "sniffer" and "protocol analyzer" are used interchangeably, but be aware that they might be implemented separately.
                - A basic software-based sniffer installed to a host will simply interrogate the frames received by the network adapter by installing a special driver. This allows the frames to be read from the network stack and saved to a file on disk. They also support filters to reduce the amount of data captured.
                - There are three main options for connecting a sniffer to the appropriate point in the network:
                    - **SPAN (switched port analyzer)/****port mirroring**—This means that the sensor is attached to a specially configured port on the switch that receives copies of frames addressed to nominated access ports (or all the other ports). This method is not completely reliable. Frames with errors will not be mirrored, and frames may be dropped under heavy load.
                    - **Passive test access point** (**TAP**)—This is a box with ports for incoming and outgoing network cabling and an inductor or optical splitter that physically copies the signal from the cabling to a monitor port. There are types for copper and fiber optic cabling. Unlike a SPAN, no logic decisions are made so the monitor port receives every frame—corrupt or malformed or not—and the copying is unaffected by load.
                    - **Active TAP**—This is a powered device that performs signal regeneration (again, there are copper and fiber variants), which may be necessary in some circumstances. Gigabit signaling over copper wire is too complex for a passive tap to monitor, and some types of fiber links may be adversely affected by optical splitting. Because it performs an active function, the TAP becomes a point of failure for the links in the event of power loss.
                - A TAP will usually output two streams to monitor a full-duplex link (one channel for upstream and one for downstream). Alternatively, there are aggregation TAPs, which rebuild the streams into a single channel, but these can drop frames under very heavy load.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 8.5.2 tcpdump
                - **tcpdump** is a command line packet capture utility for Linux, providing a user interface to the libpcap library. The basic syntax of the command is:
                - `tcpdump -i eth0`
                - ...where `eth0`is the interface to listen on (you can substitute with the keyword `any`to listen on all interfaces of a multi-homed host). The utility will then display captured packets until halted manually (by pressing `Ctrl`+`C`). The operation of the basic command can be modified by switches. For example, the `-w` and `-r` switches write output to a file and read the contents of a capture file respectively. The `-v`, `-vv`, and `-vvv` can be used to increase the amount of detail shown about each frame while the `-e` switch shows the Ethernet header.
                - tcpdump is often used with some sort of filter expression:
                    - **Type**—Filter by `host`, `net`, `port`, or `portrange`.
                    - **Direction**—Filter by source (`src`) or destination (`dst`) parameters (`host`, `network`, or `port`).
                    - **Protocol**—Filter by a named protocol rather than port number (for example, `arp`, `icmp`, `ip`, `ip6`, `tcp`, `udp`, and so on).
                - Filter expressions can be combined by using Boolean operators:
                    - `and (&&)`
                    - `or (||)`
                    - `not (!)`
                - Filter syntax can be made even more detailed by using parentheses to group expressions. A complex filter expression should be enclosed by quotes. For example, the following command filters frames to those with the source IP 10.1.0.100 and destination port 53 or 80:
                - `tcpdump -i eth0 "src host 10.1.0.100 and (dst port 53 or dst port 80)"`
                - 
                - Refer to tcpdump.org for the full help and usage examples. ngrep (github.com/jpr5/ngrep) is another useful packet capture and analysis tool. As well as the standard filter syntax, it supports use of regular expressions (regexr.com) to search and filter capture output. You can also use the netcat tool (nmap.org/ncat) to copy network traffic from one host to another for analysis.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 8.5.3 Protocol Analyzers
                - A **protocol         analyzer** works in conjunction with a packet capture or sniffer       tool. You can either analyze a live capture to analyze frames as they are       read by a sniffer or open a saved capture (.pcap) file. Most protocol       analyzer tools bundle a sniffer component with the analyzer in the same       software package.
                - One function of a protocol analyzer is to parse each frame in a stream of       traffic to reveal its header fields and payload contents in a readable       format. Analyzing protocol data at the frame or packet level will help to       identify protocol or service misconfigurations. **Wireshark** ([wireshark.org](https://www.wireshark.org/)) is an open source       graphical packet capture and analysis utility, with installer packages for       most operating systems. Having chosen the interfaces to listen on, the       output is displayed in a three-pane view, with the top pane showing each       frame, the middle pane showing the fields from the currently selected       frame, and the bottom pane showing the raw data from the frame in hex and       ASCII.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Sl_CIRjiw-hEdTqnMoawFs9_EEI8ZVzYRfivMwusyrTap1Hv1hoWtp67fTzXyxyMhvKoFB4bjFr39htFXhBe6ZQWtRAG98BrXpO9RrKi3CUOVFmsybLB50x1Mhdog4Wz.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - A table at the top lists the number, time, source, destination,           protocol, length, and info. In the output below, request: login and           password and three lines of output are highlighted.
                    -  *Wireshark protocol analyzer. (Screenshot courtesy of Wireshark.)* 
                - As a live stream or capture file can contain hundreds or thousands of       frames, you can use display filters to show only particular frames or       sequences of frames. Another useful option is to use the **Follow           TCP Stream** context command to reconstruct the packet       contents for a TCP session.
                - Another function of a protocol analyzer is to perform traffic analysis.       Rather than reading each frame individually, you use the tool to monitor       statistics related to communications flows, such as bandwidth consumed by       each protocol or each host, identifying the most active network hosts,       monitoring link utilization and reliability, and so on. In Wireshark, you       can use the **Statistics** menu to access traffic analysis       tools.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/LiUsr4Kk0C2paNke76hkrPvutLq-8DIlySktNgZhNM7vQlu4COeU_koHItY_BD7CKt61QsxaU2qEOXDb6r553ik9DSNTgLDk8CBYSouXEUyCLpmA_5K4bTjwssUZIKnq.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The window shows the following tabs at the top: Ethernet 19, I P v 4           12, I P v 6 9, T C P 92, and U D P 163. I P v 4 12 is selected. Data           is provided in a table format. The column headers read, address A,           address B, packets, bytes, packets A to B, bytes A to B, packets B to           A, bytes B to A, rel start, and duration. Close button at the bottom           is selected.
                    -  *Using the Conversations option from Wireshark's Statistics tools.           (Screenshot courtesy of Wireshark.)* 
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Tn6WzlLGnnqWcNhi2eEyJ5pV0vGTu1aNCyL6fXgkgwqIRA6Vy8164wkZKlAMSNXirc0RxlJG4lSyCTZR5w_pDPqz0Nm4-WsHagpBbHmU-gN-9zj28Yj1IdK2Ahq09FlJ.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The window shows data in a table format whose headers are protocol,           percent packets, packets, percent bytes, bytes, and bits per second.           Frame is expanded to list Ethernet. Ethernet is expanded to list           Internet protocol version 6 and Internet protocol version 4. Internet           protocol version 4 is expanded to list several protocols from which           user datagram protocol and transmission control protocol are expanded.           Close button is selected.
                    -  *Using the Protocol Hierarchy tool in Wireshark to view the most           active protocols on a network link. This sort of report can be used to           baseline network activity. (Screenshot courtesy of Wireshark.)*

                - ### Analyzing Network Traffic  00:00-00:44
                    - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Wireshark is a network packet analyzer that](undefined)[tries to capture network packets and display the](undefined)[data they carry in as much detail as possible.](undefined)
            - [Network professionals use Wireshark to troubleshoot](undefined)[network problems, examine security problems, verify](undefined)[network devices, debug network issues, and more.](undefined)[Wireshark can be installed on Windows,](undefined)[Linux, and other operating systems.](undefined)[It's not a firewall or intrusion detection system,](undefined)[and it doesn't keep bad things from happening.](undefined)[But it does a great job of monitoring](undefined)[and measuring activity on a network.](undefined)
            - [We're going to take a very brief look at Wireshark.](undefined)[There are entire courses on Wireshark, and as you progress](undefined)[professionally, you'll want to learn more about the program.](undefined)
            - ### Wireshark Interface 00:44-02:25

            - [The main toolbar gives you quick access](undefined)[to the most common Wireshark tasks.](undefined)[The two most common items here are the shark fin,](undefined)[or Start, which starts capturing packets, and this](undefined)[square, which turns red when Wireshark is capturing.](undefined)
            - [Below that, there's the Filter toolbar.](undefined)[We'll come back to this in a few minutes.](undefined)
            - [Down here, there's the Packet List pane.](undefined)[This is where all the captured packets are displayed.](undefined)[It only takes a few minutes to see hundreds or](undefined)[thousands of packets, depending on your network traffic.](undefined)[Each line here is a packet that Wireshark has](undefined)[captured, and it's separated in columns up here.](undefined)[You can separate packets by source or destination](undefined)[IP addresses or categorize them in other ways.](undefined)[Whichever one is selected up here will display more](undefined)[detailed information down in the Packet Detail pane.](undefined)
            - [The Packet Detail pane shows all the](undefined)[details about the selected packet.](undefined)[I can come down here, expand the](undefined)[different areas, and see more details.](undefined)[For those of you who love the OSI model, you'll be very](undefined)[happy to hear that this is listed in the OSI order.](undefined)
            - [Farther down, we have the Packet Bytes pane.](undefined)[This is a hexadecimal dump of the selected packets.](undefined)[Sometimes, you can see readable](undefined)[information over here, in ASCII.](undefined)[If the bytes aren't readable, this](undefined)[information is replaced with periods.](undefined)
            - [And finally, at the bottom, we have the status bar.](undefined)

            - ### Display Filters 02:25-04:44

            - [One way to filter is to use the Expression Builder.](undefined)[For example, let's say I want to filter dhcp.](undefined)[If I look, bootp and dhcp are actually the same thing.](undefined)[If I expand this out, I get lots of things to choose from.](undefined)[I'm going to just leave it as-is.](undefined)

Let's](undefined)[see if we can just do a find for that.](undefined)[I'll click on Find Next until I](undefined)[see something worth looking at.](undefined)[Here, it looks like I found something.](undefined)[It's the word log with the username Admin listed.](undefined)[This is followed by pwd and the](undefined)[password the person typed in.](undefined)[So, with Wireshark, we were able](undefined)[to see a cleartext password.](undefined)[We could also filter other things,](undefined)[like FTP, to find cleartext passwords.](undefined)
