                - Copy Video Transcript
                - Print Video Transcript
            - ## Interactive Script
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - close interactive script
            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### DoS Attacks 00:00-01:05
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [DoS attacks use a single computer with a](undefined)[single connection to attack a single target.](undefined)
            - [The attacker uses some sneaky method to send a large number](undefined)[of legitimate-looking requests to the server to make sure it](undefined)[can't determine which requests are valid, and which aren't.](undefined)
            - [This barrage of requests overwhelms the system](undefined)[to the point that the server can't manage the](undefined)[capacity, making it inaccessible to other users.](undefined)
            - [The attacker uses compromised websites and](undefined)[emails to distribute specially designed](undefined)[malware to poorly secured devices.](undefined)[This malware provides an access point to the](undefined)[attacker, which they can use to control the device.](undefined)[These zombie devices are recruited](undefined)[to cooperative teams called botnets.](undefined)[The attacker's goal is to recruit as many zombie machines as](undefined)[possible, often creating botnets of thousands of computers.](undefined)[When an attacker is ready to strike, he commands his army of](undefined)[machines to launch a coordinated attack at a target system.](undefined)[And with the advancement of the Internet of](undefined)[Things, it's important to note that zombie](undefined)[devices aren't limited to desktops and laptops.](undefined)
            - ### Any device can be hacked 01:05-01:14
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Any device that can communicate](undefined)[over the internet can be hacked.](undefined)[This includes security cameras, DVR](undefined)[players, and even kitchen appliances.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - When we describe attacks, there's a focus on       purposefully malicious threat actors, but do be aware of inadvertent,       accidental, and non-malicous threats. For example, a user might cause       accidental DoS by connecting two wall ports and creating a switching loop.       Users can also create inadvertent vulnerabilities. For example, shadow IT       (devices or apps used in the workplace without authorization) could be       vectors for exploits that aren't mitigated by security controls.
            - ## 9.2.3 Distributed DoS Attacks and Botnets
                - A **         distributed DoS (DDoS) ** attack is launched simultaneously by       multiple hosts. Some types of DDoS attack simply aim to consume network       bandwidth, denying it to legitimate hosts. Others cause resource       exhaustion on the hosts processing requests, consuming CPU cycles and       memory. This delays processing of legitimate traffic and could potentially       crash the host system completely. For example, a SYN flood attack works by       withholding the client's ACK packet during TCP's three-way handshake. The       client's IP address is spoofed, meaning that an invalid or random IP is       entered so the server's SYN/ACK packet is misdirected. A server can       maintain a queue of pending connections. When it does not receive an ACK       packet from the client, it resends the SYN/ACK packet a set number of       times before timing out the connection. The problem is that a server may       only be able to manage a limited number of pending connections, which the       DoS attack quickly fills up. This means that the server is unable to       respond to genuine requests.
                - ## Distributed Reflection Dos/Amplification Attacks
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A more powerful TCP SYN flood attack is a type of distributed reflection       DoS (DRDoS) or amplification attack. In this attack, the adversary spoofs       the victim's IP address and attempts to open connections with multiple       servers. Those servers direct their SYN/ACK responses to the victim       server. This rapidly consumes the victim's available bandwidth.
                - One example of this technique is to bombard a victim network with       responses to bogus DNS queries. One of the advantages of this technique is       that while the request is small, the response to a DNS query can be made       to include a lot of information, so this is a very effective way of       overwhelming the bandwidth of the victim network with much more limited       resources on the attacker's network. The Network Time Protocol (NTP) can       be abused in a similar way.
                - ## Botnets
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A **           botnet **  is a group of compromised hosts that can be       used to launch DDoS and DRDoS attacks. A threat actor will first       compromise one or two machines to use as handlers or herders. The handlers       are used to compromise hundreds, thousands, or even millions of zombie       hosts and install DDoS tools on them (the bots). To compromise a host, the       attacker must install malware that opens a backdoor remote connection. The       attacker can then use the malware to install bots and trigger the zombies       to launch the attack at the same time. The network established between the       handlers and the bots is called a **           command and control (C2 or C&C)**  network.
                - Any type of Internet-enabled device is vulnerable to compromise. This       includes web-enabled cameras, SOHO routers, smart TVs, and other       appliances. This is referred to as an Internet of Things (IoT) botnet.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 9.2.4 Malware Attacks
                - Many of the intrusion attempts perpetrated against computer networks depend on the use of malicious software, or **malware**. Malware can be defined simply as software that does something bad, from the perspective of the system owner. There are many types of malware, but they are not classified in a rigorous way, so some definitions overlap or are blurred. Some malware classifications, such as Trojan, virus, and worm, focus on the vector used by the malware. The vector is the method by which the malware executes on a computer and potentially spreads to other network hosts. Another complicating factor with malware classification is the degree to which its installation is expected or tolerated by the user. The following categories describe some types of malware according to vector:
                    - **Viruses and worms**—These represent some of the first types of malware and spread without any authorization from the user by being concealed within the executable code of another process. Viruses infect files, while worms can infect processes running in system memory.
                    - **Trojan**—Malware concealed within an installer package for software that appears to be legitimate. This type of malware does not seek any type of consent for installation and is actively designed to operate secretly.
                    - **Potentially unwanted programs (PUPs)/Potentially unwanted applications (PUAs)**—Software installed alongside a package selected by the user or perhaps bundled with a new computer system. Unlike a Trojan, the presence of a PUP is not automatically regarded as malicious. It may have been installed without active consent or consent from a purposefully confusing license agreement. This type of software is sometimes described as grayware rather than malware.
                - Other classifications are based on the payload delivered by the malware. The payload is an action performed by the malware other than simply replicating or persisting on a host. Examples of payload classifications include spyware, rootkit, remote access Trojan (RAT) or backdoor, and ransomware.
                - As malware has continued to be developed for criminal intent and security software became better able to detect and block known viruses, worms, and Trojans, malware code and techniques have become more sophisticated. The term "fileless" has gained prominence to refer to these modern types of malware. Fileless is not a definitive classification, but it describes a collection of common behaviors and techniques:
                    - Fileless malware does not write its code to disk. The malware uses memory-resident techniques to run in its own process, within a host process or dynamic link library (DLL), or within a scripting host. This does not mean that there is no disk activity at all, however. The malware may change registry values to achieve persistence (executing if the host computer is restarted). The initial execution of the malware may also depend on the user running a downloaded script, file attachment, or Trojan software package.
                    - Fileless malware uses lightweight **shellcode** to achieve a backdoor mechanism on the host. The shellcode is easy to recompile in an obfuscated form to evade detection by scanners. It is then able to download additional packages or payloads to achieve the threat actor's objectives. These packages can also be obfuscated, streamed, and compiled on the fly to evade automated detection.
                    - Fileless malware may use "live off the land" techniques rather than compiled executables to evade detection. This means that the malware code uses legitimate system scripting tools, notably PowerShell and Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI), to execute payload actions. If they can be executed with sufficient permissions, these environments provide all the tools the attacker needs to perform scanning, reconfigure settings, and exfiltrate data.
                - The terms "**advanced persistent threat (APT)**" and "advanced volatile threat (AVT)" can be used to describe this general class of modern fileless/live off the land malware. Another useful classification is low-observable characteristics (LOC) attack. The exact classification is less important than the realization that adversaries can use any variety of coding tricks to effect intrusions and that their tactics, techniques, and procedures to evade detection are continually evolving.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_respond_attacks_n09/d_respond_attacks_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_respond_attacks_n09/d_respond_attacks_n09.jpg)

            - 00:001. Respond to Network Attacks
            - CC

            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Respond to Network Attacks 00:00-00:31
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Network attacks are something we often hear about.](undefined)[Attacks can be a distributed denial of service](undefined)[attack (DDoS) also known as a ping attack.](undefined)[In a DDoS attack, unwanted traffic from an unknown](undefined)[IP address, or even traffic from an infected](undefined)[network computer, can overwhelm network resources.](undefined)
            - [There are many ways that a DDoS attack can be implemented.](undefined)[In this demo, we show you what you](undefined)[can do to respond to such attacks.](undefined)
            - ### Show traffic 00:31-01:01
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we will use Pfsense.](undefined)[To see the active traffic, you can use pfTop](undefined)[which is located under Diagnostics tab.](undefined)[Using this tool, you can see the](undefined)[current inbound and outbound traffic.](undefined)
            - [Because this is a test system,](undefined)[we don't have a lot of traffic.](undefined)[Symptoms you should look for are an excessive](undefined)[number of packets sent or traffic from an](undefined)[unknown IP address in the source or destination.](undefined)[Let's say there is an IP address that is suspicious,](undefined)[and we want to know where it is coming from.](undefined)
            - ### Use IP Address Lookup 01:01-01:23
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's lookup this IP address, 1.0.3.104.](undefined)[There are many sites you can use to look](undefined)[up a public IP address such as this one.](undefined)[When we enter the IP address and click Search, we](undefined)[can see more details related to this IP address.](undefined)[Because this is a test system and international](undefined)[work isn't done on it, it's unlikely this IP](undefined)[address needs access this network.](undefined)
            - ### Block IP Address 01:23-03:12
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's go back to our Pfsense Firewall.](undefined)[In order to block an IP address on](undefined)[a firewall, you must create a rule.](undefined)[The rule defines what to allow or what to deny.](undefined)[To set this, let's go to Firewall then Rules.](undefined)[If the network has been thoroughly hardened,](undefined)[there will probably be a lot of rules.](undefined)[The rules listed here are defaults.](undefined)
            - [To add a rule to the bottom, click the Add down arrow.](undefined)[We could add it to the top, depending on it's priority.](undefined)[The action we want to use is Block.](undefined)[If we use Reject, it sends a reject message back to](undefined)[the sender letting them know we rejected the traffic.](undefined)
            - [Typically, a block is preferred since it](undefined)[blocks the traffic and does it silently.](undefined)[We will need to set the protocol](undefined)[if the traffic is only TCP or UDP.](undefined)[Since we want to cover everything related](undefined)[to blocking this IP, we'll Select any.](undefined)
            - [Next, we'll define the source we want to block.](undefined)[To define a single IP address, we](undefined)[must select Single host or alias.](undefined)[The nice thing about aliases is you can bundle whole](undefined)[networks, multiple IP addresses, ports and more.](undefined)
            - [Since we have only one IP address](undefined)[to block, we will enter it here.](undefined)[The destination will be any because this rule is](undefined)[specifically for inbound traffic from this IP address.](undefined)[If we were concerned about traffic going from the network](undefined)[to this IP address, we would do the reverse of this.](undefined)[We would put the IP address in the](undefined)[destination instead with the source.](undefined)
            - [It's best practice to write the description with](undefined)[enough detail so that you know what the rule does.](undefined)[Once complete, we click Save.](undefined)[The changes won't be in effect until](undefined)[we click this Apply changes button.](undefined)[We see that the rule was applied successfully.](undefined)
            - ### Use Geo Blocking 03:12-04:13
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [There are occurrences where the](undefined)[attacker will change the IP address.](undefined)[This happens frequently.](undefined)[An attacker can use a range of](undefined)[addresses from the same country.](undefined)[If we were not concerned about blocking the entire](undefined)[country, we could use something like geo blocking.](undefined)[Let's do that.](undefined)
            - [Under Firewall, we'll go to pfBlockerNG.](undefined)[This is an added package that must be installed.](undefined)[It isn't here by default.](undefined)[Over here there is GeoIP.](undefined)[Once we click it, we can see we](undefined)[have a whole list of countries.](undefined)[There are two lists because its shows ipv4 and ipv6.](undefined)
            - [We'll block everything from the country of](undefined)[China since this country doesn't have a reason](undefined)[to contact any IP addresses on this network.](undefined)[You could do the same for other countries.](undefined)[Be mindful of what you are blocking because there are](undefined)[cases where US websites use cloud servers internationally.](undefined)
            - [The List action will be to Deny Both.](undefined)[That way traffic to and from the country will be blocked.](undefined)[You do, however, have many options to select from.](undefined)[Let's click Save at the bottom and we're done.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 04:13-04:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we looked at how to view active traffic on](undefined)[the firewall, block IP addresses, and geo block countries.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - 
        - **9.3 Spoofing Attacks** 
            - ## 9.3.1 On-Path Attacks
                - An **on-path**       attack is a specific type of spoofing attack where a threat actor       compromises the connection between two hosts and transparently intercepts       and relays all communications between them. The threat actor might also       have the opportunity to modify the traffic before relaying it.
                - On-path attacks are also known by the term       "Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)." Such terms are non-inclusive and/or use       inappropriate or vague metaphors and are deprecated in the latest CompTIA       exam objectives documents. The terms "Manipulator in the Middle," "Machine       in the Middle," and "Adversary in the Middle (AitM)" are also used as       replacements.
                - ## MAC Spoofing and IP Spoofing
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A host can arbitrarily select any MAC and/or IP address and attempt to       use it on the network. While each network interface has a burned-in MAC       address, this can be changed to any arbitrary value using packet crafting       software. A threat actor might exploit this to spoof the value of a valid       MAC or IP address to try to circumvent an access control list or       impersonate a legitimate server. For this type of attack to succeed, the       threat actor must normally disable the legitimate host or there will be       duplicate addresses on the network, which will have unpredictable results.
                - IP spoofing is also used in most denial of service       (DoS) attacks to mask the origin of the attack and make it harder for the       target system to block packets from the attacking system. In this type of       spoofing, the threat actor does not care that they will not receive       replies, so it is different from an on-path attack.
                - ## ARP Spoofing and ARP Poisoning
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - **ARP           spoofing** is a common means of perpetrating an on-path       attack. It works by broadcasting unsolicited ARP reply packets, also known       as gratuitous ARP replies, with a source address that spoofs a legitimate       host or router interface. Because ARP has no security, all devices in the       same broadcast domain as the rogue host trust this communication and       update their MAC:IP address cache table with the spoofed address. The ARP       cache is said to be poisoned. Because the threat actor broadcasts endless       ARP replies, it overwhelms the legitimate interface.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/qEphuKm90Z1QAm5WqvN7kol3HDIJQMHiLk88MZawMhOK1EH-puy3wJg2bJIdVeLs8zO4dHrWPgdmk0IVa5r5i_gI-MCtKAKEtiC74Vsmnm74MqbmlH9Uo5DPWYiVhqm1.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - A table at the top lists the number, time, source, destination,           protocol, length, and info. In the output below, destination and           transmission control protocol are highlighted.
                    -  *Observing ARP poisoning in a Wireshark packet capture. (Screenshot           courtesy of Wireshark.)* 
                - The usual target will be the subnet's default gateway. If the attack is       successful, all traffic destined for remote networks will be sent to the       attacker. The threat actor can then perform an on-path attack to monitor       the communications and continue to forward them to the router to avoid       detection. The attacker could also modify the packets before forwarding       them. ARP poisoning could also perform a DoS attack by not forwarding the       packets.
                - ARP spoofing can be difficult to detect without closely monitoring       network traffic. However, attempts at ARP spoofing are likely to cause       sporadic communications difficulties, such as an unreachable default       gateway. In such cases, performing network captures and examining ARP       packets may reveal the poison packets, as will examining local ARP caches       for multiple IP addresses mapping to the same MAC address.
                - Technically, ARP spoofing is the broadcast of the       unsolicited ARP replies, while ARP poisoning is the injection of spoofed       MAC:IP mappings into the victim cache. The terms are often just used       interchangeably, however. Be aware that ARP poisoning could include other       methods of injecting fake mappings, such as the local host being infected       with malware.
                - 
                - While IPv6 does not use ARP, it is also vulnerable       to layer 2 spoofing if the unencrypted Neighbor Discovery (ND) Protocol is       used. Abuse of this can be used for router advertisment (RA) spoofing.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 9.3.4 MAC Flooding Attack
                - Where ARP poisoning is directed at hosts, **MAC         flooding** is used to attack a switch. The intention of the       attacker is to exhaust the memory used to store the switch's MAC address       table. The switch uses the MAC address table to determine which port to       use to forward unicast traffic to its correct destination. Overwhelming       the table can cause the switch to stop trying to apply MAC-based       forwarding and flood unicast traffic out of all ports, working as a hub.       This makes sniffing network traffic easier for the threat actor.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_mac_flood_n09/v_mac_flood_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment
            -

            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Introduction to MAC Flooding 00:00-00:12
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The first attack to examine is MAC flooding.](undefined)[Switches maintain MAC address tables, sometimes](undefined)[called content access memory tables or CAM tables,](undefined)[to track workstations and ports associations.](undefined)
            - ### MAC Address Tables and Data Forwarding 00:12-00:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Switches learn which workstation is connected to](undefined)[each port, and that information is stored in the](undefined)[MAC address table and used when switches forward.](undefined)[For example, let's say that workstation A](undefined)[wants to send information to Workstation D.](undefined)[The frame has a source address ending in A's and the](undefined)[destination address is the address ending with D's.](undefined)[When A sends the information, the Switch](undefined)[examines the destination address and sends](undefined)[the data out port one to its destination.](undefined)[Workstations B and C aren't involved,](undefined)[so they never see the information.](undefined)
            - ### Potential Attack Scenario 00:45-01:00
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Suppose an attacker wants to see all](undefined)[data passing through this switch.](undefined)[Considering that a switch in normal operation only](undefined)[sends data to the intended recipient, the attacker](undefined)[would only be able to see data destined for his or](undefined)[her workstation, which is very unlikely.](undefined)
            - ### MAC Flooding Attack 01:00-01:22
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [But the attacker can flood the switch with fake MAC](undefined)[address information and fill up the MAC address table.](undefined)[Once the switch can no longer store any more MAC](undefined)[addresses, the switch enters a fail-open mode and](undefined)[behaves the same as a network hub or layer one device.](undefined)[This means that any packet coming](undefined)[in now goes out to all switch ports.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 9.3.6 VLAN Hopping Attacks
                - **VLAN hopping** is an attack designed to send traffic to a VLAN other than the one the host system is in. This exploits the default VLAN feature of 802.1Q. Default VLANs are designed to provide compatibility with non-VLAN capable switches. The attacker, using a device placed in the default VLAN, crafts a frame with two VLAN tag headers. The first trunk switch to inspect the frame strips the first header, and the frame gets forwarded to the target VLAN. Such an attack can only send packets one way but could be used to perform a DoS attack against a host on a different VLAN.
                - A VLAN hopping attack can also be launched by attaching a device that spoofs the operation of a switch to the network and negotiating the creation of a trunk port. As a trunk port, the attacker's device will receive all inter-VLAN traffic.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/videos/v_vlan_hop_n09/v_vlan_hop_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/image/default-video.png)
                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment
            - 
            - 00:001. Introduction to Double Tagging
            - CC
            - 1.0x
            - Volume Control

                - Print Video Transcript

                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - close interactive script
            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### Introduction to Double Tagging 00:00-00:13
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Another method attackers use is called double tagging.](undefined)[This VLAN hopping technique is used when the attacker's](undefined)[computer is on one VLAN switch and the target computer](undefined)[is on a VLAN attached to a separate switch.](undefined)
            - ### Role of Native VLANs 00:13-00:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In addition, the switches must be configured](undefined)[to use what's called native VLANs.](undefined)[When a packet is sent to a switch, it](undefined)[includes what's known as a VLAN tag.](undefined)[This tag's purpose is to indicate](undefined)[which switch should process the frame.](undefined)
            - ### VLAN Tagging Process 00:26-00:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [For example, you'll notice that both users are on VLAN 10.](undefined)[If user A wants to send data to user B, the](undefined)[frame would include the VLAN tag indicating](undefined)[that the frame was intended for VLAN 10.](undefined)[When the data reaches the switch, the VLAN tag is removed](undefined)[and the packet is sent to its rightful destination.](undefined)
            - ### Double Tagging Attack 00:45-01:18
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Double tagging is accomplished by manipulating](undefined)[the frame being sent to include two VLAN tags, so](undefined)[one for VLAN 10 and one for switch two or VLAN 20.](undefined)[When this type of frame is received by VLAN 10](undefined)[switch, the first VLAN tag is removed, but seeing that](undefined)[the second VLAN tag is referencing VLAN 20, the frame](undefined)[is forwarded to that switch then as it normally would.](undefined)[This switch strips off the second VLAN tag](undefined)[and sends the frame to the victim's VLAN,](undefined)[successfully hopping from one VLAN to another.](undefined)
            - ### Preventing Double Tagging 01:18-01:34
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To prevent double tagging, it's important to make sure](undefined)[that your switches aren't configured to use native VLANs.](undefined)[This is because trunk ports configured with a](undefined)[native VLAN won't apply their own VLAN tag when](undefined)[sending these frames, which lets the attacker's](undefined)[tag frames to continue as I just described.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - Another common switch attack is known as a Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) manipulation attack. STP is normally configured on a network with several switches. The primary purpose is to prevent switching loops. To make STP work, a single switch is designated as the root bridge. If an attacker can become the root bridge, they are then able to see a variety of frames that they normally wouldn't see. To perpetrate this attack, the attacker inserts their switch into the tree and manipulates it to appoint their switch as the root bridge. By doing this, they can use a sniffer to collect data traversing the network.
        - **9.4 Rogue System Attacks** 
            - ## 9.4.1 Rogue Devices and Services
                - When a device or service on your network isn't under the administrative control of the network staff, it's called a rogue. Rogue devices and services are often completely malicious. They exist for the sole purpose of stealing sensitive information such as credit card numbers and passwords. Most legitimate network hardware and services can be exploited through rogues. Rogue devices and services could include wireless access point, DHCP servers, DNS servers, and so on. Ordinary PCs and laptops can be maliciously configured to run any type of service, or they could use packet crafting software to perpetrate spoofing attacks.
                - Threats and vulnerabilities can also arise from unintentional sources. In the context of rogue devices and services, one example of an unintentional insider threat is the concept of **shadow IT**, where users purchase or introduce computer hardware or software to the workplace without the sanction of the IT department and without going through a procurement and security analysis process. The problem of shadow IT is exacerbated by the proliferation of cloud services and mobile devices, which are easy for users to obtain. Shadow IT creates a new unmonitored attack surface for malicious adversaries to exploit.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 9.4.2 Rogue DHCP
                - The Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) provides IP addressing autoconfiguration to hosts. If a Windows client fails to obtain a DHCP lease, it defaults to using an address in the Automatic Private IP Addressing (APIPA) range of 169.254.0.0/16. It will be limited to communication with other APIPA hosts on the same network segment (broadcast domain). Linux hosts will use the 169.254.0.0/16 range if they have Zeroconf support, leave the IP address set to 0.0.0.0, or disable IPv4 on the interface.
                - APIPA is Microsoft terminology. Standards documentation refers to this address range as IPv4 link local (IPV4LL). Zeroconf is a standards-based approach to technologies that allows hosts to obtain a usable network configuration and discover services automatically and without the use of DHCP or DNS server infrastructure.
                - Clients have no means of preferring a DHCP server. If two DHCP servers are running on the same subnet, clients could end up with an incorrect IP configuration because they have obtained a lease from a rogue server. A rogue DHCP server may be deployed accidentally (forgetting to disable a DHCP server in an access point or router, for instance) or may be used by a malicious threat actor to subvert the network. A threat actor would normally use a rogue server to change the default gateway and/or DNS resolver addresses for the subnet and route communications via their machine. This is a means of using DHCP to facilitate an on-path attack.
                - A DHCP starvation attack uses bogus requests to use up leases in a legitimate DHCP server's address pool. An exhausted DHCP scope means legitimate hosts cannot obtain a lease. A DHCP starvation attack might be a denial of service (DoS) mechanism or be used to force legitimate hosts to obtain a lease from a rogue DHCP server.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_rogue_dhcp_n09/d_rogue_dhcp_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_rogue_dhcp_n09/d_rogue_dhcp_n09.jpg)

            - ### Detect a Rogue Device 00:00-00:31
                - [Size]();-[H3]()

            - ### Rogue DHCP 00:31-00:53
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [As clients connect to the network, both the](undefined)[rogue and real DHCP server offer them IP](undefined)[addresses as well as the other network settings.](undefined)[If the information provided by the rogue DHCP differs](undefined)[from the information provided by the real one, clients](undefined)[accepting IP addresses from it may experience network](undefined)[access problems or have their network traffic sniffed](undefined)[as part of a man-in-the-middle attack.](undefined)
            - ### Use Wireshark to Examine the DHCP Traffic 00:53-01:13
                - [Size]();-[H3]()

            - ### View IP Information from a Windows 10 Client 01:13-01:56
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Okay.](undefined)[I'm on the client machine that belongs to the](undefined)[employee that can't connect to the internet.](undefined)[I have a command prompt here, and I'm](undefined)[going to do an ipconfig on the machine.](undefined)[Now, as I look up, I have an IP address of 10.10.10.11.](undefined)[The subnet mask is correct, but there's no default gateway.](undefined)[Also, my domain isn't CorpNet.xyz.](undefined)[This one, here, says BadDomain.com,](undefined)[so I seem to be having some problems.](undefined)[Just to double-check, I'm going to do an ipconfig /release.](undefined)[Now, let's do an ipconfig /renew.](undefined)[I'm getting the exact same information,](undefined)[so I'm going to investigate.](undefined)[Now I'm going to jump over to another workstation and see](undefined)[what IP addresses they're getting from the DHCP server.](undefined)
            - ### View IP Information from a Second Windows 10 Client 01:56-03:49

            - [So, I'm going to go turn off my real DHCP for a few](undefined)[minutes, come back here, and renew the IP address to](undefined)[see if I pick up something from the rogue DHCP that](undefined)[appears to be running on this network.](undefined)
            - [Okay.](undefined)[I went in and turned off the DHCP server.](undefined)[I'm going to do an ipconfig release.](undefined)[I get a message saying I need elevated privileges, too.](undefined)[Let's close this and reopen the command](undefined)[prompt with elevated privileges.](undefined)[I'll say yes to the User Account Control.](undefined)[Let's try that again.](undefined)[That's 'ipconfig /release', and](undefined)[then I'll do 'ipconfig /renew'.](undefined)[Now I'll just do an 'ipconfig'.](undefined)[And when I do, we get BadDomain.com.](undefined)[This IP certainly isn't coming from my DHCP server.](undefined)[It's not even in the IP range that I've set up.](undefined)[That tells me I have a rogue DHCP server.](undefined)[I'm going to turn on my real DHCP](undefined)[server again, so I don't forget.](undefined)
            - [All right.](undefined)[Let's turn the DHCP back on.](undefined)[I'm going to try to obtain a new IP address again.](undefined)[I'll do 'ipconfig /release' and then 'ipconfig /renew'.](undefined)[Now it looks like my real DHCP](undefined)[server beat the rogue DHCP server.](undefined)[This gateway and IP info is from the correct DHCP server.](undefined)[But I still need to find out more](undefined)[about this rogue DHCP server.](undefined)[At this point, we're going to go back to Wireshark on](undefined)[the Kali Linux system and see what's going on there.](undefined)
            - ### View Wireshark Data 03:49-04:51
                - [Size]();-[H3]()

            - [If I scroll down there a bit, I can see 10.10.10.197 getting](undefined)[a request for an IP from the real DHCP server, 10.10.10.1.](undefined)

            - ### Prevent Rogue DHCP Server 04:51-05:31
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now, come down here, and I can see](undefined)[the MAC address for that device.](undefined)[It says it's the source MAC address.](undefined)[To fix this on a temporary basis, I can kick that](undefined)[device off the network based on the MAC address.](undefined)[Long-term, I need to prevent this](undefined)[from happening in the first place.](undefined)
            - [Rogue DHCP servers can be stopped by an IDS with](undefined)[appropriate signatures, as well as by certain](undefined)[multilayer switches configured to drop the packets.](undefined)[One method for dealing with rogue](undefined)[DHCP servers is DHCP snooping.](undefined)[DHCP snooping drops DHCP messages that](undefined)[aren't trusted from a DHCP server.](undefined)[You can configure DHCP snooping on LAN switches](undefined)[to prevent rogue DHCP servers from operating.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 05:31-06:04
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we investigated a rogue device on the network.](undefined)[First, we identified an IP address](undefined)[that was assigned by the rogue device.](undefined)[Then we verified that there was a](undefined)[rogue device from a second system.](undefined)[After that, we viewed our filtered Wireshark traffic and](undefined)[identified the rogue DHCP server's IP and MAC addresses.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 9.4.4 DNS Attacks
                - The Domain Name System (DNS) resolves requests for named host and services to IP addresses. Name resolution is a critical addressing method on the Internet and on private networks. There are many potential attacks against DNS. On the public Internet, attacks might use typosquatting techniques to cause victims to confuse malicious sites with legitimate ones. DNS can be exploited in a DRDoS attack. Threat actors can also directly target public DNS services as a means of performing DoS against a website or cloud resource. Finally, a threat actor might be able to hijack a public DNS server and insert poisoned records, directing victims to rogue websites.
                - On a private network, a DNS attack is likely to mean some sort of DNS spoofing or **DNS poisoning**. These DNS attacks compromise the process by which clients query name servers to locate the IP address for a domain name.
                - As with ARP spoofing/poisoning, DNS spoofing and poisoning attacks are often taken to mean the same thing, but technically spoofing is using false DNS requests or replies or running a rogue DNS service, while poisoning is manipulating cached records.
                - ## DNS-Based On-Path Attacks
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - If the threat actor has access to the same local network as the victim, the attacker can use ARP poisoning to respond to DNS queries from the victim with spoofed replies. This might be combined with a denial of service attack on the victim's legitimate DNS server. A rogue DHCP could be used to configure clients with the address of a DNS resolver controlled by the threat actor.
                - ## DNS Client Cache Poisoning
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Before DNS was developed in the 1980s, name resolution took place using a text file named HOSTS. Each name:IP address mapping was recorded in this file, and systems administrators had to download the latest copy and install it on each Internet client or server manually. Even though most name resolution now functions through DNS, the HOSTS file is still present and most operating systems check the file before using DNS or load it into a cache of known name:IP mappings, and the client only contacts a DNS server if the name is not cached. Therefore, if an attacker is able to place a false name:IP address mapping in the HOSTS file and effectively poison the DNS cache, they will be able to redirect traffic. The HOSTS file requires administrator access to modify. In UNIX and Linux systems it is stored as `/etc/hosts`, while in Windows it is placed in `%SystemRoot%\System32\Drivers\etc\hosts`. The presence of suspect entries in the HOSTS file is an indictator that the machine has been compromised.
                - ## DNS Server Cache Poisoning
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - DNS server cache poisoning aims to corrupt the records held by the DNS server itself. One attack method involves getting the victim name server to respond to a recursive query from the attacking host. A recursive query compels the DNS server to query the authoritative server for the answer on behalf of the client. The attacker's DNS, masquerading as the authoritative name server, responds with the answer to the query, but also includes a lot of false domain:IP mappings for other domains that the victim DNS accepts as genuine. The `nslookup` or `dig` tool can be used to query the name records and cached records held by a server to discover whether any false records have been inserted.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/2kWQt_ZQeeOFnqeCvKuD_p3J2kzFYYGJDsaP7u4mr5dSN0I8ZW3TJz4z1GjtWbUdklEWrqDSieczaLuiwXm-e0QV86XDWiPXaVcPbW9_vmxfRyeU4msJUJoVCjne75HK.png)
                -  *Attempting to poison a DNS server cache—This attack has failed.* 
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_dns_attack_n09/d_dns_attack_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_dns_attack_n09/d_dns_attack_n09.jpg)

            - ### Examining DNS Attacks  00:00-00:17
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we're going to look at a few ways an](undefined)[attacker may use the DNS system to get information](undefined)[about the computers in an organization, and also to](undefined)[compromise a machine and get access to information.](undefined)
            - ### Reconnaissance Using DNS Servers  00:17-01:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's first look at doing reconnaissance using DNS servers.](undefined)[One way this can be done is with the nslookup command.](undefined)
            - [You see that I have a PowerShell command](undefined)[prompt window open here, and if you just type](undefined)[nslookup, that will put you into this utility.](undefined)[nslookup is used to troubleshoot DNS server settings.](undefined)
            - [You can see here that the DNS server](undefined)[that I'll be using is this one.](undefined)[It's the default one that I have](undefined)[configured for my machine here.](undefined)[When a computer wants to resolve a name, the first thing](undefined)[it does is look into a special file called a host file.](undefined)
            - [We'll talk more about that later, but](undefined)[essentially the host file can contain mappings](undefined)[between domain names and specific IP addresses.](undefined)[If it's not in the host file, then an](undefined)[operating system checks its DNS cache.](undefined)
            - [If the mapping is in there, then it](undefined)[uses the IP address that it finds.](undefined)[If not, it goes out and does the name resolution](undefined)[with the DNS server that's configured.](undefined)
            - [In this case, nslookup simply allows us](undefined)[to do those queries by passing the cache](undefined)[that might be on a local operating system.](undefined)
            - [So, if we want to, here, we can simply type in](undefined)[the name that we want to resolve like](undefined)[google.com, and it will go out and resolve that.](undefined)[My DNS server here goes out and says, I don't](undefined)[know what IP address that is associated with.](undefined)
            - [And then it issues the query to](undefined)[the resolver that it's pointing to.](undefined)[And you see here the IP address of www.google.com.](undefined)[Of course, we can try other ones like testout.com and others](undefined)[in you'll see that the resolution occurs appropriately.](undefined)
            - [For this demo, I've set up a domain that we](undefined)[can use to see how a few vulnerabilities work.](undefined)
            - ### Domain Information  01:45-02:21
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [One vulnerability that exists with DNS servers](undefined)[is the ability to find out all the domain](undefined)[information associated with a particular name.](undefined)[One way we can do that is using the list command.](undefined)
            - [To do that, we type IS semi colon. We specify the domain that we](undefined)[want using the -d flag; and then we can say ajptech.net.](undefined)[This will tell us all of the computers that](undefined)[are associated with the ajptech domain.](undefined)
            - [If you do that, you see that we have](undefined)[a full list of all the machines.](undefined)
            - [Now, this is just a test environment.](undefined)[I've just listed a few servers, etc.](undefined)
            - ### Zone Transfer  02:21-04:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [You can imagine in an organization that has](undefined)[hundreds of computers, this provides very nice](undefined)[information to know which IP addresses we should](undefined)[spend time attacking, based on these records.](undefined)
            - [This is referred to as a zone transfer, and we need](undefined)[to make sure that our DNS servers in our organization](undefined)[don't allow zone transfers to just anyone.](undefined)
            - [They're useful because you don't want just a](undefined)[single server, and so you want to share those](undefined)[records with other secondary zones, but you need to](undefined)[restrict who is allowed to do those zone transfers.](undefined)
            - [Let's take a look at that, inside of Windows Server 2019.](undefined)[You see here I have launched the DNS manager on the server.](undefined)
            - [If you haven't installed that role before,](undefined)[it's super simple to do, and you do so](undefined)[before configuring these different zones.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and look at some of the](undefined)[properties that you might want to set.](undefined)
            - [The one that's of most interest to us](undefined)[right now is this Zone Transfers tab.](undefined)[This basically specifies who is allowed to](undefined)[gather all those records at once, and you](undefined)[can see here that we have 'To any server'.](undefined)
            - [We could come in and just say,](undefined)['No zone transfers are allowed.'](undefined)[But as I explained a minute ago, that's useful,](undefined)[because if this is the primary zone for that domain](undefined)[name, then you want to have a secondary zone.](undefined)
            - [You don't want a single point of failure here, and](undefined)[so you want to be able to allow zone transfers.](undefined)
            - [You can only allow servers in the Name Servers tab,](undefined)[which is nice because usually you set those up specific](undefined)[to a domain, and that would be anything that's listed](undefined)[here, or you can restrict it to a specific IP address.](undefined)
            - [Let's go ahead and apply those settings.](undefined)[Let's say that we're going to only allow](undefined)[the following servers, and I'll just put](undefined)[in a fake IP address here right now.](undefined)[It won't resolve right now, but that's okay.](undefined)[But it will restrict those zone transfers.](undefined)
            - [Let's go ahead and switch back over to our](undefined)[PowerShell window, and let's try that same command.](undefined)[You can see now that it says it has refused transferring](undefined)[all that information from the zone--perfect.](undefined)
            - [Now when people are doing reconnaissance, they won't](undefined)[be able to see everything that we have in that zone.](undefined)
            - [However, the domain name server continues to](undefined)[work, so I can say server1, and you can see](undefined)[that the name resolution continues to work.](undefined)[So, make sure you secure your zone transfers.](undefined)
            - [Today, restricting zone transfers is commonplace;](undefined)[in fact, it's the default of many operating systems,](undefined)[and it's the best practice everybody should have.](undefined)[If your DNS server isn't configured that way,](undefined)[you want to make sure that you take care of that.](undefined)[Now let's move on to a couple of other concepts.](undefined)
            - ### Distributor Reflection  04:45-07:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [There are many different DNS attacks, such as](undefined)[distributor reflection, denial of service attacks,](undefined)[cache poisoning, TCP send floods, and others.](undefined)
            - [Let me just briefly explain one](undefined)[of these to give you a flavor.](undefined)
            - [A distributor reflection denial of service attack](undefined)[occurs when several DNS queries are sent to a DNS](undefined)[server that has an open resolver, and that DNS server](undefined)[responds to the spoofed address with a large payload.](undefined)
            - [The key to the attack is that the attacker only has to send](undefined)[a small packet of information to the DNS server, and then](undefined)[the DNS server sends a large response, but that response](undefined)[doesn't go back to the attacker; it sends it to the target](undefined)[that the attacker listed, using a spoofed source IP address.](undefined)
            - [In short, the attacker uses the DNS to create a](undefined)[denial of service attack against the third party.](undefined)[In this case, you need to make sure](undefined)[that you don't have any open resolvers.](undefined)
            - [An open resolver is essentially a DNS server](undefined)[that's willing to resolve names for third parties.](undefined)
            - [Usually you protect this in a couple of different](undefined)[ways--either restricting the DNS requests that come into](undefined)[your DNS server, that is, you don't allow outside](undefined)[requests; or simply not having any forwarding](undefined)[capabilities associated with your DNS server, and making](undefined)[sure that recursion is disabled on your DNS server.](undefined)
            - [Let me show you that setting quickly here.](undefined)[If you come to your server, you'll see](undefined)[the properties of the server itself.](undefined)[We are in the zone from before.](undefined)[We're going to go over here to the Advanced tab.](undefined)
            - [You want to make sure that this option is](undefined)[set, so that you disable the recursion.](undefined)[That, of course, disables the forwarders as indicated there.](undefined)[I'll say OK there.](undefined)
            - [Then I'll come over to the Forwarders tab here.](undefined)[You can see that I'm set up, but](undefined)[it's not using those anymore.](undefined)[The downside of this is we need to use a different](undefined)[DNS server in order to do our name resolution.](undefined)
            - [This server now can only do name resolution for](undefined)[the domains that are on it, but that's okay.](undefined)[That's best practice.](undefined)[You definitely don't want to be susceptible to](undefined)[those amplification attacks that I just explained.](undefined)
            - [Let's come back here to our PowerShell](undefined)[prompt and get out of the nslookup command.](undefined)
            - [Let me show you a couple of other things.](undefined)[I'll go ahead and clear the screen, as well.](undefined)
            - [A minute ago, I explained that when name resolution](undefined)[needs to occur on an operating system, it first checks](undefined)[the set host file, and then it goes to cache, sees](undefined)[what's in cache and uses that if there is something](undefined)[that's associated with that name, and then it goes and](undefined)[does the name resolution using the DNS system.](undefined)
            - [Let's go ahead and take a look at that cache.](undefined)
            - [But before we do so, we need to make](undefined)[sure that we have something in cache.](undefined)[I'll simply go ahead and try to ping google.com.](undefined)[You can see that I get a response.](undefined)
            - [Now, to see what's in cache, you](undefined)[simply say ipconfig /displaydns.](undefined)[You can see that I have several different entries in there.](undefined)
            - ### Clear Out the Cache  07:25-09:03
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I have some of those entries because I have a browser](undefined)[running on my system, and it's already resolved some names.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and try to clear that out.](undefined)[To clear out your cache, you type ipconfig /flushdns.](undefined)
            - [Let's ping google.com, and then let's go ahead](undefined)[and see if we can see just those name resolutions.](undefined)
            - [You can see here there is only one](undefined)[record now when we display the dns cache.](undefined)
            - [So, this cache constantly keeps track of](undefined)[what's been resolved previously so it doesn't](undefined)[have to invoke the DNS system for every single](undefined)[request if it already knows that information.](undefined)[It saves time.](undefined)
            - [Each of these records also has a time to live,](undefined)[as you can see there, and so they're constantly](undefined)[refreshing as the system is being used.](undefined)
            - [One mistake that some people come across](undefined)[when they're trying to resolve DNS issues](undefined)[is they forget to clear out their cache.](undefined)
            - [They hit a site that's in cache, they make some](undefined)[changes, and they don't realize that it's actually](undefined)[pulling it out of cache instead of going on.](undefined)[I want to make sure you're aware of how cache works.](undefined)
            - [Now what if we could poison the cache or insert a record in](undefined)[there that wasn't legitimate, that came from another source?](undefined)
            - [This is a little bit harder to architect, but if we](undefined)[could specify a different IP address for the name](undefined)[server, the DNS server, we could pull records from it.](undefined)[It would be saved in the cache or locally,](undefined)[and then subsequent resolutions would pull](undefined)[the IP address out of the cache.](undefined)
            - [Indeed, that's one attack that individuals](undefined)[use when exploiting the DNS system.](undefined)[We're not going to show that to you today, but](undefined)[I want to show you one other concept before we](undefined)[wrap up this demo, and that has to do with the](undefined)[host file that I mentioned a few minutes ago.](undefined)
            - ### The HOSTS File  09:03-11:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [This is the host file I mentioned a minute ago, and you can](undefined)[see that it's located in Windows System32\drivers\etc\hosts.](undefined)[In this file, you can include specific mappings](undefined)[between a domain name and an IP address.](undefined)
            - [Before there was this DNS system, this file was](undefined)[distributed to all the computers on the internet, and so](undefined)[there's just a huge file of these mappings so that you](undefined)[wouldn't have to remember the specific IP addresses.](undefined)
            - [Thankfully, the DNS system came along, making](undefined)[our lives a lot easier and being allowed to](undefined)[update, but this is still in place.](undefined)[And as I mentioned, it gets checked before](undefined)[the OS resolver cache, before going out to](undefined)[the DNS system to resolve domain names.](undefined)
            - [You can see an example of how you can use this.](undefined)[But before I put in an example, let me show you a](undefined)[legitimate site so that you can see how this works.](undefined)[Let's go to cat-bounce.com, just a random site.](undefined)
            - [You can see that there's something out there.](undefined)[If we want to redirect individuals, instead of them](undefined)[going to this site to a different site, we can go into](undefined)[the host file and add a mapping that we're interested in.](undefined)
            - [You can see that we put the IP address](undefined)[first, and then the domain name.](undefined)[So, I know that the duckduckgo server is at](undefined)[the following address, and then I can simply](undefined)[put www.cat-bounce.com, and I'll save that.](undefined)
            - [Recent browsers have incorporated a](undefined)[DNS cache inside of the browser itself.](undefined)
            - [They do this because it gives them a competitive](undefined)[advantage to go ahead and resolve those domain](undefined)[names ahead of time, the minute that they show up](undefined)[on a page and that you might potentially click on.](undefined)
            - [Lets open an incognito window to bypass the cache.](undefined)
            - [If we go to www.cat-bounce.com, you](undefined)[see that it forwards on duckduckgo.com.](undefined)[I hope you can see how dangerous this could be.](undefined)[This is considered host file manipulation.](undefined)
            - [However, an attacker could simply](undefined)[keep that in place and use.](undefined)[An innocent individual wouldn't have any](undefined)[idea that you weren't on the legitimate site.](undefined)
            - [Consider banking sites or corporate sites, anywhere](undefined)[where sensitive information might be shared.](undefined)[An attacker can get access to this host file,](undefined)[present what looks to be a legitimate website,](undefined)[and then siphon off data as you use it.](undefined)
            - [It's also good to know that you can use host file to block](undefined)[malicious information, if you are a systems administrator.](undefined)
            - [This is a common site.](undefined)[It looks a little dated, but the](undefined)[information is pretty good on it.](undefined)[You can come and download a specific host file](undefined)[to block out a bunch of bad or malicious sites.](undefined)
            - [The IP address you see is a null IP address.](undefined)[In other words, if we added this to our host file](undefined)[and you went there, it simply wouldn't go anywhere.](undefined)
            - [One thing to note, you'll need to have](undefined)[administrator rights to adjust the host file.](undefined)
            - ### Summary  11:29-12:00
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we've look at a few ways that an](undefined)[attacker can use the DNS system to get out information](undefined)[about computers in the organization and to compromise](undefined)[a machine and eventually grab sensitive information.](undefined)
            - [We've looked at the nslookup command.](undefined)[We've looked at zone transfers.](undefined)[We've looked at securing the open resolvers.](undefined)[And we've looked at the host file and clearing cache.](undefined)
            - [It's really important that you protect your DNS servers and](undefined)[make sure that you aren't taken advantage of in that way.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - 
        - **9.5 Social Engineering** 
            - ## 9.5.1 Social Engineering Attacks
                - Threat actors can use a diverse range of techniques to compromise a       security system. A prerequisite of many types of attacks is to obtain       information about the network and security system. **Social         engineering** (or hacking the human) refers to a collection of       techniques and tricks designed to gain some type of unauthorized access to       premises or data. Passive social engineering takes advantage of the       unintentional actions of others to gather information or gain access to a       secure facility. Active social engineering involves       direct interaction with users, asking them to disclose information or take       actions.
                - Impersonation (pretending to be someone else) is one of the basic social       engineering techniques. The classic impersonation attack is for the threat       actor to phone into a department pretending to be calling from IT support,       claim they have to adjust something on the user's system remotely, and get       the user to reveal their password. For this attack to succeed, the       approach must be persuasive and establish trust. Social engineering might       also use intimidation or hoaxes as a means of eliciting information.
                - ## Phishing Attacks
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - **Phishing**       is a combination of social engineering and spoofing. It persuades or       tricks the target into interacting with a malicious resource disguised as       a trusted one, traditionally using email as the vector. A phishing message       might try to convince the user to perform some action, such as installing       disguised malware or allowing a remote access connection by the attacker.
                - Other types of phishing campaigns use a spoof website set up to imitate a       bank or e‑commerce site or some other web resource that should be trusted       by the target. The attacker then emails users of the genuine website to       inform them that their account must be updated or with a hoax alert or       alarm. This message will contain aa disguised link that actually leads to       the spoofed site. When the user authenticates with the spoofed site, their       login credentials are captured.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/kQPsrHuhES5tj-xfKuG8unAOUMK352Oet5SiQyYETlz-hEJNNViPRXwCggviUhxUMeH8mnRp_TWjnyEoHe7DrZI3sxVnFNY8YCvI1tFBSQB93KUNl0-RU_KVJAgW4JHk.jpeg)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - Both the windows are titled, Your Acme Corp subscription invoice is           ready; dated Friday, 5 slash 2 slash 2021; 5:49 PM; from noreply at           acmecorp dot com. At the left, the ACMECorp logo is on the top left,           followed by the message. The text reading, to view your Acme Corp           subscription invoice, is followed by the link reading, click here. At           the right, the U R L of the ACMECorp logo is on the top left, followed           by the message. The text reading, to view your Acme Corp subscription           invoice click here, is followed by a U R L.
                    -  *Example phishing email—on the right, you can see the message in its           true form as the mail client has stripped out the formatting (shown on           the left) designed to disguise the nature of the links.* 
                - ## Shoulder Surfing Attack
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A threat actor can learn a password or PIN (or other secure information)       by watching the user type it. This is referred to as a **shoulder         surfing** attack. Despite the name, the attacker may not have to be       in close proximity to the target—they could use high-powered binoculars or       CCTV to directly observe the target remotely.
                - ## Tailgating and Piggybacking Attacks
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - **Tailgating**       is a means of entering a secure area without authorization by following       closely behind the person that has been allowed to open the door or       checkpoint. **Piggybacking**       is a similar situation but means that the attacker enters a secure area       with an employee's permission. For instance, an attacker might impersonate       a member of the cleaning crew and request that an employee hold the door       open while they bring in a cleaning cart or mop bucket. Another technique       is to persuade someone to hold a door open, using an excuse such as "I've       forgotten my badge (or key)." Alternatively, piggybacking may be a means       of an insider threat actor to allow access to someone without recording it       in the building's entry log.
                - ## Dumpster Diving Attack
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - **Dumpster         diving** refers to combing through an organization's (or       individual's) garbage to try to find useful documents (or even files       stored on discarded removable media).
                - A threat actor might stage attacks over a long       period. Initial attacks may only aim at compromising low-level information       and user accounts, but this low-level information can be used to attack       more sensitive and confidential data and better protected management and       administrative accounts.
                -

            - ### Investigating a Social Engineering Attack 00:00-00:19
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we'll talk about](undefined)[investigating a social engineering attack.](undefined)[We're going to focus on identifying the](undefined)[validity of emails, identifying the validity](undefined)[of websites, and identifying virus hoaxes.](undefined)
            - ### Email Hoaxes 00:19-02:06
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's take a look at email validity.](undefined)[You should always be wary of emails](undefined)[from unknown organizations or senders.](undefined)[Be careful opening them, and be even](undefined)[more cautious with their attachments.](undefined)[A lot of hoax emails are pretty easy to spot](undefined)[because of their bad spelling and terrible](undefined)[grammar, but some of them look very, very real.](undefined)[Let's take a look at a couple of examples.](undefined)
            - [Let's start with this first one, Final](undefined)[Notice, Postal Status Notification.](undefined)[The first clue is the return address.](undefined)[It's from a variation of a Walmart address.](undefined)[It could be legit, but I'm not so sure.](undefined)[Next is the logo.](undefined)[It has a logo with a question mark after the word Walmart.](undefined)[I've never seen a Walmart logo like this, so I really](undefined)[wouldn't have to go any further to know it's a fake.](undefined)[If I hover over the logo, a bunch of addresses pop up.](undefined)[This looks like a batch of addresses](undefined)[that were spammed from this email.](undefined)[A little lower, when I hover over the Update Your](undefined)[Address Here link, I see a bunch more addresses.](undefined)[No legitimate email is going to be formatted to do this.](undefined)[Finally, here, it tells me to hurry, or](undefined)[I'll lose 17% of the money owed to me.](undefined)
            - [Let's look at this other one, the Check Your Account email.](undefined)[The address looks a bit odd.](undefined)[That's always the first clue.](undefined)[They want to confirm my information.](undefined)[I've never heard of this company, and I don't](undefined)[gamble, so within a second, I know this is fake.](undefined)[These links down here are Bitly,](undefined)[which is a URL shortening service.](undefined)[I don't think most legitimate emails](undefined)[would use a shortening service like this.](undefined)
            - [Then we have another email that I took a screenshot of.](undefined)[It's this Attention email that says it's from usafed.gov.](undefined)[This is someone posing as somebody from the US Treasury.](undefined)[They're using a Gmail address for](undefined)[communication, which the government wouldn't do.](undefined)[At the bottom of the email, they've listed the](undefined)[legitimate website for the Department of the Treasury](undefined)[to make you think this is a legitimate email.](undefined)
            - ### Header Information 02:06-03:04
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Another way you can verify an email is by](undefined)[taking a look at the header information.](undefined)[I have my Walmart email opened up in Outlook.](undefined)[This is another way to verify an email and its content.](undefined)[The header information is helpful because it](undefined)[shows IP information for the path an email](undefined)[took from its origin to the destination.](undefined)[It also shows other information, like the mail](undefined)[client that was used, the actual email address of the](undefined)[sender, and the originating IP address of the email.](undefined)
            - [There are a few ways to look at header information.](undefined)[In Outlook, it's helpful, but not easy to read.](undefined)[If we go into File > Properties, you can](undefined)[see the header information right here.](undefined)[As you can tell, this can't be resized,](undefined)[and it's hard to read in this format.](undefined)[The mail client in Windows 10 doesn't have a](undefined)[way to view header information, but don't worry.](undefined)[You can view header information by opening the email](undefined)[in a text editor like I've done with Notepad++.](undefined)
            - ### View Header in Text Editor 03:04-03:22
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [By looking through the email in this format,](undefined)[you can pick out many things about its origin](undefined)[and determine whether it's legitimate.](undefined)[Remember, this email was supposed to be from Walmart.](undefined)[Here's the sender's originating IP.](undefined)[If we highlight this address and copy it, we](undefined)[can actually look up some information on that](undefined)[IP address to see where it's coming from.](undefined)
            - ### IP Lookup 03:22-03:49
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [All internet IP addresses for](undefined)[users come from some ISP somewhere.](undefined)[We can go to a website called arin.net, go](undefined)[to the search, and paste in our address.](undefined)[It'll find some information about this IP address.](undefined)[There are services that you can pay for that will give you](undefined)[a lot more information, but this one is free and simple.](undefined)[Looking at this information, I'm very skeptical](undefined)[that this email came from Walmart because the](undefined)[mailing address for this IP is from France.](undefined)
            - ### Website Validity 03:49-05:24
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Another thing we want to talk about is verifying websites.](undefined)[The first thing that you should always do is double-check](undefined)[the spelling of the website you're navigating to.](undefined)[If you're trying to go to PayPal and the address you end](undefined)[up at is paypals.com (with an s) or paypal.fakesight.com,](undefined)[then you're likely in the wrong place.](undefined)[You could accidentally give away your login information for](undefined)[PayPal (or a similar site) by entering it into a false site.](undefined)[You could also verify a site by viewing](undefined)[the SSL certification information.](undefined)[An SSL certificate (Secure Sockets Layer certificate)](undefined)[is a digital certificate that authenticates the](undefined)[identity of a website and encrypts the information](undefined)[sent to its server using SSL technology.](undefined)
            - [Let's look at two different browsers, Google](undefined)[Chrome and the Microsoft Edge browser.](undefined)[If we open both of these, there's a slight](undefined)[difference in how the SSL certificates are shown.](undefined)[In the Chrome browser, if you click on the lock in the](undefined)[address bar, it shows information about the certificate.](undefined)[I see that it's issued to google.com.](undefined)[I have a few tabs up here, Details and Certification Path.](undefined)[Under Certification Path, I see more information](undefined)[about the Certificate Authority and the certificate.](undefined)
            - [Now, if you go to Edge, open up Gmail, and click](undefined)[on the little lock, it'll show you the same](undefined)[information, but in a slightly different format.](undefined)[If there's no lock and no SSL certificate,](undefined)[you should be a little more cautious.](undefined)[Certificates aren't the focus of this lesson, but you](undefined)[want to make sure you know how to verify certificate](undefined)[information when you're not sure about a website.](undefined)
            - ### Virus Hoaxes 05:24-06:10
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Our last topic is identifying virus hoaxes.](undefined)[You've probably seen something like this before.](undefined)[Maybe one of your coworkers has come to](undefined)[you in a panic, worried about this type of](undefined)[error, and it's obviously very alarming.](undefined)[It's meant to be; this hoax is designed](undefined)[to scare or intimidate users into thinking](undefined)[they've contracted some type of virus.](undefined)[Normally, it says it's something](undefined)[like a Trojan, as you can see here.](undefined)[Often, there's a number you can call where](undefined)[they'll take payment over the phone for fixing](undefined)[the virus, but they never actually do anything.](undefined)
            - [There have been similar hoaxes that use the](undefined)[same tactics, so it's good to understand that](undefined)[viruses don't usually want to be discovered.](undefined)[Unless you're being notified by a trusted](undefined)[antivirus software that you or your administrator](undefined)[has installed, then it's probably just a hoax.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 06:10-06:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this lesson.](undefined)[We discussed verifying emails by looking](undefined)[at the HTML version and header information.](undefined)[We talked about verifying that a website is](undefined)[correct by checking the spelling of the address](undefined)[and the presence of a valid SSL certificate.](undefined)[We finished by discussing virus hoaxes.](undefined)

            - ### Web Page Cloning for Credential Capture 00:00-01:51
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we're going to talk about how](undefined)[to capture user credentials from a web page.](undefined)[While there are many different methods for gaining](undefined)[unauthorized access to a user's web account, we're going](undefined)[to do it by cloning a website sign-in page and directing](undefined)[the user to enter their information on a fake page.](undefined)[To fool the user into going to our cloned sign-in page,](undefined)[we would create a phishing email and send it to them.](undefined)[But, in this demo, we're just going to cover cloning.](undefined)[To do this, we will use one of the many exploits](undefined)[provided by the social engineering toolkit.](undefined)
            - [Because this is a demo, we're going to use a fake website.](undefined)[I'll enter the credentials myself](undefined)[to show how the tool works.](undefined)[First, we need to find a website that we would like to](undefined)[attack, but since we'll only be cloning one page of the site,](undefined)[we need to know what the full URL is for the login page.](undefined)[Let's go to www.fakewebsite.com.](undefined)[This is a WordPress site, which makes our life a little](undefined)[easier since the login page name is almost always the same.](undefined)[To make sure, though, we'll click the login](undefined)[button on the page and see where it takes us.](undefined)[I'll scroll down here and click log in.](undefined)[Now that we're on the login page, we can](undefined)[look in the address field and see the full](undefined)[URL of the exact page we want to clone.](undefined)[Since we'll need this URL later, we're going to copy it.](undefined)
            - [Now that we have the page we want to clone, let's open up](undefined)[the social engineering toolkit, sent to the command line app.](undefined)[We'll be working in the terminal.](undefined)[Once the terminal opens, I'll type](undefined)[in "SE toolkit" and press enter.](undefined)[On this screen, you can see that there are two attack](undefined)[options: social engineering attacks and penetration testing.](undefined)[If you add extra attacks made by others,](undefined)[they'll appear under third-party modules.](undefined)[The remaining options are for maintaining](undefined)[the social engineering toolkit.](undefined)
            - ### Setting Up the Attack 01:51-02:20
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The option that we're looking for can be](undefined)[found under social engineering attacks, so](undefined)[I'll select number one and press enter.](undefined)[We're interested in cloning a website, so we'll](undefined)[select option two, website attack vectors.](undefined)[To get to the clone option from here, we'll](undefined)[select #3, credential Harvester attack](undefined)[method, and then select #2, site cloner.](undefined)[Since we'll be using this machine to host the clone page and](undefined)[listen for credentials, we can leave the default options.](undefined)[I'll just press enter to continue.](undefined)
            - ### Cloning the Website 02:20-02:41

            - [Next, we'll use control-shift-B to paste the](undefined)[URL we copied earlier and press enter again.](undefined)[Now the social engineering toolkit is saying,](undefined)["Hey, do you understand what you're saying here?](undefined)[Yes, yes, I do."](undefined)[It also offers a couple of troubleshooting](undefined)[instructions in case the tool isn't working.](undefined)[Since we expect it to work, we'll](undefined)[just press enter to continue.](undefined)
