            - ### Securing a Switch 00:00-00:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going to](undefined)[secure access to our network switch.](undefined)[We're using a Cisco Small Business Switch, which](undefined)[has a web interface for access and configuration.](undefined)
            - [We access the switch by typing its IP](undefined)[address, 192.168.254.8, into a web browser.](undefined)[And here's the login page.](undefined)
            - [This is a new switch, so it still has the default](undefined)[user name and password configured of cisco and](undefined)[cisco, which we'll enter and then click Log In.](undefined)
            - ### Login as the Default User 00:32-00:46
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now, the default user name and password for this](undefined)[device can be found very easily, so you can tell](undefined)[that this switch is not very secure right now.](undefined)
            - [Normally, the first thing you should do with a new network](undefined)[device is change the default user name and password.](undefined)
            - ### Create a New User 00:46-01:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [On this device, we can use the Change Device](undefined)[Password link under Quick Access to do this.](undefined)
            - [This takes us directly to the User](undefined)[Accounts page under Administration.](undefined)[And here's the default user, cisco.](undefined)[Let's first create another user.](undefined)[Click Add.](undefined)[Enter ciscoAdmin for the user name.](undefined)
            - [Enter a strong, complex password.](undefined)[And we want Read/Write Management Access.](undefined)[And click Apply.](undefined)[And we can see the new ciscoAdmin user here.](undefined)
            - [We also want to change the password for the default user.](undefined)
            - [So we'll select the cisco user and click Edit.](undefined)[And again we want to use a complex password.](undefined)[And for this user, let's also change the user level.](undefined)[We'll select Read-Only CLI Access.](undefined)
            - ### Change the Default User's Password and User Level 01:45-02:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [This way, if someone is able to get into the switch using](undefined)[the default account, they won't be able to change anything.](undefined)[And we'll click Apply.](undefined)
            - [So, the first thing we've done to secure access](undefined)[to our switch is add another admin account with](undefined)[a custom user name and a complex password.](undefined)[We then changed the default user's password](undefined)[and set the user level to read-only.](undefined)
            - [Doing this makes it harder for people to get into](undefined)[our switch here and change the configuration.](undefined)[And this should be the first step](undefined)[you do with any new network device.](undefined)
            - ### Save Changes to the Startup Configuration Files 02:15-02:41
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now, there's one more important step to take here.](undefined)[And that's to save the changes we've to the startup config.](undefined)[If we don't, as soon as the switch is rebooted,](undefined)[the changes will be lost; we don't want this.](undefined)
            - [So we'll click Save to go to the](undefined)[Copy/Save Configuration page.](undefined)[And here we want to save the contents of the](undefined)[Running configuration to the Startup configuration.](undefined)[And click Apply.](undefined)[Now when the device reboots, it](undefined)[will load our custom configuration.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 02:41-02:59
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demo, we secured access to our network switch.](undefined)[First we added a new management user with full access.](undefined)[We changed the default user's password.](undefined)[And then we saved our changes to](undefined)[the startup configuration file.](undefined)

            - ### Intro  00:00-00:21
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [There may come a time where hardening](undefined)[a network switch will be required.](undefined)[One method of hardening is to disable unused switch ports.](undefined)[This prevents someone from randomly](undefined)[plugging in a network cord or having loops](undefined)[between two switch ports on the same switch.](undefined)
            - ### Disable Port 00:21-01:06
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [On our 28-port switch, we have some unused](undefined)[ports that are required to be disabled.](undefined)[To start this process of hardening our switch, we want](undefined)[to look at the port setting under 'Port Management'.](undefined)[As you can see, all 28 ports are listed here.](undefined)[This network won't require the use of all](undefined)[ports, so we'll be disabling ports 21-27.](undefined)[Let's click 'Edit' and look at port 21 first.](undefined)[The setting we're interested in is Administrative Status.](undefined)[If the Administrative Status is down on the](undefined)[switch, anything plugged into this port won't work.](undefined)[That sounds like what we need, so I'm](undefined)[going to click 'Down' and 'Apply'.](undefined)[To exit this port, we must click 'Close'.](undefined)
            - ### Copy Settings 01:06-01:55
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [One thing that will make life easy is if I](undefined)[could copy this setting to multiple ports.](undefined)[Well, that's what this button down here is.](undefined)[If I select 'port 21' and then 'Copy Settings', it'll](undefined)[bring up a menu to select the ports I'd like to copy to.](undefined)[Port 21 is already set to Down, so we must finish](undefined)[this up with ports 22-27 and click 'Apply'.](undefined)[Keep in mind any other settings that may have been set on](undefined)[port 21 will copy over to the rest, excluding VLAN settings.](undefined)[I'm going to click on one of the ports in](undefined)[question just to make sure my settings took.](undefined)[Great!](undefined)[Looks like I'm in good shape and ready to](undefined)[save the config to the startup configuration.](undefined)[On the flashing red Save button, we're going](undefined)[to select that and apply the configuration.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 01:55-02:09
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we showed how to disable a switch port](undefined)[and how you can leverage the copy settings button to](undefined)[make the same changes to other switch ports.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 10.4.5 Extensible Authentication Protocol and IEEE 802.1X
                - The **Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP)** provides a framework for deploying multiple types of authentication protocols and technologies when an endpoint device needs to be authenticated before it can join the network. This pre-authentication requirement arises in a number of scenarios:
                    - When the user is accessing a wireless network and needs to authenticate with the network directory server.
                    - When a device is connecting to a network via a switch and network policies require the user to be authenticated before the device is allowed to communicate.
                    - When the user is connecting to the network over a public network via a virtual private network (VPN).
                - EAP allows  many different authentication methods, but some of them use a digital certificate on the server and/or client machines. These certificates allow the machines to establish a trust relationship and create a secure tunnel to transmit the user credential, or to perform smart card authentication without a user password.
                - Where EAP implements a particular authentication factor and mechanism, the **IEEE 802.1X Port-Based Network Access Control (NAC) **standard provides the means of using an EAP method when a device connects to an Ethernet switch port, wireless access point, or VPN gateway. 802.1X uses authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA) architecture. If the AAA protocol is RADIUS, the switch is configured as a RADIUS client by specifying the IP address or host name of the RADIUS server and setting a shared secret. The RADIUS server is positioned in a secure zone within the private network. The RADIUS server stores (or can obtain) account details and can validate authentication credentials. The switch does not have to store any authentication credentials. The switch forwards authentication data between the RADIUS server and the supplicant device. The RADIUS server uses the shared secret to validate RADIUS clients.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/nM8sit51AmHW6Hgs0xbKTy_rxma9rWpLww6NvwnZV2uhZ3OA_NePYr6Oy2EqYNvGOwfkI72Hjb6Yw7i6xh85JqBHS4HEuXwAsyqlYzkMyewFOQd11qbkXpd_FayM_JqZ.png)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The steps are as follows.
            - 1.The RADIUS server and Client are pre-configured with the same shared with the same shared secret.
            - 2.The supplicant connects to the network.
            - 3.The N A P enables E A P o L and instructs the supplicant to authenticate.
            - 4.The supplicant transmits A E P data.
            - 5.The N A P encrypts the E A P data using the shared secret and forwards it to the RADIUS server.
            - 6.The RADIUS server decrypts the packet using the shared secret and validates the credential.
            - 7.The RADIUS server issues an Access-Accept.
            - The N A P decrypts the Access-Accept and opens the network channel for regular traffic.
            -  *RADIUS authentication with EAP overview. (Images © 123RF.com.)* 
            - Basic NAC solutions can authenticate a client on the basis of machine certificates and/or user passwords. More sophisticated solutions can enforce a health policy. A health policy means that the client must submit an attestation report. This secure report proves that the client is running an authorized OS and has up-to-date patches and security scanner configurations.
            -

            - ### Configuring Network Access Control on a Cisco Switch 00:00-01:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's look at configuring network](undefined)[access control on a Cisco Switch.](undefined)[First, we'll log into the switch](undefined)[and enter global configuration mode.](undefined)[Once we're in global configuration mode, we'll](undefined)[need to configure each port individually.](undefined)[So, I'll go into interface FastEthernet 0/1,](undefined)[This is the first interface on my switch.](undefined)[Next, I'll run the command switchport mode](undefined)[access and then switchport port-security.](undefined)[Once I run the port security command, I'll set](undefined)[the maximum of one, allowing only one device to](undefined)[connect to this switch through this port.](undefined)[And then I'll run violation restrict, which](undefined)[will then restrict access from any other device](undefined)[that tries to communicate through this port.](undefined)[The last command we'll run is](undefined)[port-security mac-address sticky.](undefined)[This will stick one MAC address that's](undefined)[allowed to communicate through this port.](undefined)[In order to configure that MAC address,](undefined)[we'll need to configure a MAC address table,](undefined)[which we'll take a look at in a moment.](undefined)[The last command we'll run is dot1x pae authenticator.](undefined)[This will enable port security on the interface, this will](undefined)[allow only one MAC address to access the port and use](undefined)[802.1X port-based authentication on the interface, which](undefined)[then can communicate with any type of domain integration.](undefined)
            - ### Configuring the MAC Address List 01:39-03:37
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now we need to configure the MAC address list that will](undefined)[tie the MAC address to that MAC address sticky command.](undefined)[Let's type the command mac access-list extended](undefined)[and the name or MAC address list ALLOWED_MACS.](undefined)[This will get us out of the configuration of](undefined)[the interface and into configuring a MAC](undefined)[address list to allow a device into this port.](undefined)[We'll have to add that host in there and their MAC address.](undefined)[So, we'll type in permit host the MAC address](undefined)[of the allowed host and the command any.](undefined)[This will tie the MAC address to this list, and](undefined)[now we're going to apply the list to the interface.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and exit the MAC address list](undefined)[configuration and go back into the FastEthernet.](undefined)[Next, we'll run the command mac access-group ALLOWED_ MACS to](undefined)[use that MAC address list we just created and the command in.](undefined)[Now, the MAC address that we put inside](undefined)[that MAC address list is the only device](undefined)[allowed to communicate through this port.](undefined)[In order to be extremely secure, you can sometimes](undefined)[create multiple lists that will then apply to each](undefined)[individual port and only allow those devices then.](undefined)[Only allowing certain MAC addresses is very useful](undefined)[when using a switch within an MDF environment where](undefined)[servers are going to be communicating, and they](undefined)[will not typically move to different switch ports.](undefined)[Once we've configured everything, we can](undefined)[exit global configuration and copy our](undefined)[running configuration to our startup.](undefined)[Once that's complete, we can then exit](undefined)[global configuration mode, and we're done.](undefined)

            - ### Configuring a RADIUS Client  00:00-01:06

            - [After this, you need to go to the RADIUS client itself--the](undefined)[switch, the other router, the other server, whatever's the](undefined)[RADIUS client--and tell it to talk to NPS on this server.](undefined)[How you do that depends on what's using this NPS](undefined)[for authentication, authorization, and accounting.](undefined)[It's always two steps: defining the RADIUS client](undefined)[on NPS and then, at the RADIUS client, defining](undefined)[RADIUS and pointing it to the NPS server.](undefined)

            - ## 10.4.6 Port Guards
                - There are various switch port security features to mitigate attacks on network infrastructure:
                    - A malicious host may use a spoofed MAC address to try to perform ARP cache poisoning against other hosts on the network and perpetrate an on-path attack. A switch port security feature such as dynamic ARP inspection (DAI) prevents a host attached to an untrusted port from flooding the segment with gratuitous ARP replies. ARP inspection maintains a trusted database of IP:ARP mappings. It also ensures that ARP packets are validly constructed and use valid IP addresses.
                    - Configuring **DHCP snooping** causes the switch to inspect DHCP traffic arriving on access ports to ensure that a host is not trying to spoof its MAC address. It can also be used to prevent rogue DHCP servers from operating on the network. With DHCP snooping, only DHCP offers from ports configured as trusted are allowed.
                    - Neighbor Discovery (ND) Inspection and **Router Advertisement (RA) Guard** perform similar functions to DAI and DHCP snooping for IPv6 networks. Most hosts have IPv6 enabled by default and disabling it can often cause unexpected problems. Consequently, these switch protections should be enabled to mitigate spoofing and on-path attacks over IPv6.
                    - When configuring VLANs, ensure that the default VLAN uses a different ID than any other user accessible VLAN. This mitigates against double tagging attacks.
                    - Ensure that ports allowed to be used as trunks are predetermined in the switch configuration and that access ports are not allowed to auto-configure as trunk ports. This mitigates against VLAN hopping attacks.
                    - To mitigate risks from attacks on spanning tree and root bridge selection, make sure that attackers can't easily guess which bridge ID number is being used by the legitimate root bridge. Set up Bridge Protocol Data Units Guard, or BPDU Guard, to allow an interface to put itself into blocking state when it receives a BPDU packet meant to change the root bridge switch. Enable root guard on the ports not being used as trunk lines. This keeps ports in their assigned roles. If one of these ports receives a BPDU frame, an error is logged and that port is blocked, thwarting the attacker's attempt to change the root bridge.
                -

            - ### Hardening a Switch 00:00-00:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration we'll look](undefined)[at various methods to harden a managed switch.](undefined)[In this case we're using a Cisco Small](undefined)[Business Managed Switch and are logged into](undefined)[the management interface using Internet Explorer.](undefined)[We're authenticated as the Admin](undefined)[User so we can make changes.](undefined)[In this demonstration we'll look at](undefined)[port security, management access,](undefined)[and access control using access control lists, ACLs.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Port Settings 00:29-02:10
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To begin, let's look at port security.](undefined)[When hardening a switch, one of the first](undefined)[things we want to do is shut down any unused ports.](undefined)[Click on Configure Port Settings,](undefined)[which will take us directly to the Port](undefined)[Settings page under Port Management.](undefined)
            - [On this page we can select one of the unused ports.](undefined)[You'll notice we have several ports here](undefined)[that are either down and unused, or up and used.](undefined)[The ones that are unused are currently down.](undefined)[We want to make sure they're administratively down,](undefined)[meaning they have to be turned on before they can be used.](undefined)[Let's take a look at Port Two.](undefined)[It's down.](undefined)
            - [Let's scroll to the bottom of the page and click Edit.](undefined)[On this page you'll notice that the Administrative](undefined)[Status of this port is actually up,](undefined)[even though it's operationally down.](undefined)[This means there's nothing plugged into it.](undefined)[To make sure that this port remains down](undefined)[and is not used without permission,](undefined)[we'll set the Administrative Status as Down.](undefined)[Click Apply, Close.](undefined)
            - [To set all other unused ports as Administratively Down,](undefined)[we'll select Port Two then click Copy Settings.](undefined)[In the Copy Settings window we can](undefined)[list all the ports that we want to be down.](undefined)[In this case, we'll type Three, Six,](undefined)[and Eight through 28.](undefined)[Go ahead and click Apply.](undefined)[All of those ports are now administratively down.](undefined)
            - [Let's look at Port Three for confirmation.](undefined)[Select Port Three and click Edit.](undefined)[You'll notice on this page that](undefined)[the status Administratively Down.](undefined)[So, the status that we copied from Port Two](undefined)[went to the rest of the ports.](undefined)[Click Close.](undefined)
            - [By setting the ports that we're not using](undefined)[to Administratively Down, those ports will](undefined)[come up at a down state each time](undefined)[the switch starts up.](undefined)[At the end we'll make sure that](undefined)[we save our Configuration File to the Startup Configuration.](undefined)
            - ### Enable Port Security 02:10-04:15
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, we also want to enable port security.](undefined)[To do that, we'll open the Security Tab](undefined)[and click on Port Security.](undefined)[Under this tab we can configure locking](undefined)[mechanism for each of these ports.](undefined)[We'll click on Port One and select Edit.](undefined)
            - [On this page you'll notice that we can lock the port,](undefined)[meaning we're going to only allow certain MAC addresses](undefined)[to pass traffic through this port.](undefined)[A Classic Lock means that the first](undefined)[device you connect to that port](undefined)[will be remembered and allowed to](undefined)[pass traffic through this port.](undefined)[You can also select Limited Dynamic Lock,](undefined)[which gives you a variable number from one to 256.](undefined)[Let's change this to four.](undefined)
            - [Now, if you connect a hub to a specific port](undefined)[on the switch and connect four devices to that,](undefined)[you can allow all four of those devices](undefined)[to talk through that port.](undefined)[No additional devices would be allowed to pass traffic.](undefined)
            - [We'll go ahead and select the Classic Lock.](undefined)[That will give us one device,](undefined)[and the device will be locked in.](undefined)[If you try to connect a device that](undefined)[is not supposed to be on that port,](undefined)[the Action on Violation determines what happens.](undefined)[The default is to discard the packet.](undefined)[If you connect any other device to the port,](undefined)[the traffic will be ignored.](undefined)
            - [You can also forward the traffic,](undefined)[or you can shut down the port.](undefined)[That's a little drastic for our purposes.](undefined)[However, if you're extremely cautious](undefined)[about your security and you don't want](undefined)[anybody connecting a device you don't know about,](undefined)[being able to shut down the port is](undefined)[one of the most secure things that you can do.](undefined)
            - [We'll go ahead and select Discard](undefined)[if it's a device that we aren't sure about.](undefined)[If somebody plugs in a device that's](undefined)[not locked in you can also trap the MAC addresses](undefined)[of the offending device.](undefined)[We've enabled locking for Port One,](undefined)[as well as the classic lock.](undefined)[We're going to discard the frames](undefined)[of any other devices that come onto that port.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and click Apply, Close.](undefined)
            - [Once again, we can take the settings that we've created](undefined)[for Port One and apply them to the rest of the ports.](undefined)[Scroll down to the bottom and select Copy Settings.](undefined)[In this case, we'll select all of the ports,](undefined)[or Two through 28, and click Apply.](undefined)[You'll see that the settings that we've](undefined)[created for port security now apply to all 28 ports.](undefined)
            - ### Management Access 04:15-06:19
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The next piece that we would like](undefined)[to cover is management access.](undefined)[We'll go to Management Access Method](undefined)[and click Access Profiles.](undefined)[In this case, the only profile that's](undefined)[on the machine is the Console Profile,](undefined)[which is the default.](undefined)
            - [We're going to add a new access profile,](undefined)[so click Add.](undefined)[We'll call this access profile Management.](undefined)[In this Management Profile the very first rule,](undefined)[or Rule One that we want to have](undefined)[will deny all management.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and select Deny](undefined)[and specify all different management methods,](undefined)[and apply it to all interfaces.](undefined)[That will be our first and main rule.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and click Apply, Close.](undefined)
            - [Now that we've created our Management Profile,](undefined)[we can select Profile Rules.](undefined)[It can seem a little confusing that](undefined)[you create a Management Profile then add a rule to it.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and select our Management Profile,](undefined)[and add another rule.](undefined)[It looks very similar to the page we just used.](undefined)
            - [For Rule Number Two, we want to allow](undefined)[HTTP access.](undefined)[We want to select all interfaces,](undefined)[but we also only want to allow traffic](undefined)[from a specific workstation or IP address.](undefined)[We'll specify that 192.168.1.200](undefined)[will be the only workstation allowed to access this port.](undefined)[We'll also input the network mask](undefined)[by typing 255.255.255.0.](undefined)[Alternatively, we can use the prefix length](undefined)[and use the prefix instead.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and click Apply, Close.](undefined)
            - [We now have a management access rule](undefined)[that denies all traffic, and one access rule](undefined)[which allows us to access the HTTP interface](undefined)[from one specific workstation.](undefined)[But, we're not done yet.](undefined)
            - [We need to go back to Access Profiles](undefined)[and set the Active Profile.](undefined)[Right now, we don't have an active management profile.](undefined)[Go ahead and select Management.](undefined)[This denies all types of management interfaces](undefined)[with this switch, except for the HTTP access](undefined)[from the one workstation that we have selected.](undefined)
            - [We're not going to click Apply this time.](undefined)[If we do, we'll probably be locked out of the switch,](undefined)[and we want to make sure that we have access](undefined)[to complete this demo.](undefined)
            - ### Firmware Updates 06:19-07:04
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's go to the next section,](undefined)[and look at firmware updates.](undefined)[Under Administration, File Management](undefined)[we can select Upgrade, Backup Firmware.](undefined)[Part of hardening the device or a computer of any kind](undefined)[is to make sure you have the latest software and patches.](undefined)[You can use browse to select the latest](undefined)[firmware and download it.](undefined)
            - [In this particular switch, you have to](undefined)[select the Active Image once you've loaded the firmware.](undefined)[You can select Image One or the new image,](undefined)[Image Two, and apply it.](undefined)[Next you'll need to reboot the switch.](undefined)[Once you do that the switch will come](undefined)[back with the latest and greatest software](undefined)[and the best enhancements.](undefined)[This allows you to have the best](undefined)[possible security for this switch.](undefined)[Upgrading firmware for the switch](undefined)[as well as applying patches to any computer](undefined)[is a key element to hardening the switch.](undefined)
            - ### Access Control Using ACLs 07:04-10:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The last aspect of hardening the switch](undefined)[is access control using the ACLs.](undefined)[Let's select Access Control.](undefined)[On this switch, we have various methods](undefined)[of access control.](undefined)[We can use a MAC based ACL.](undefined)[We can use an IPV4 based ACL.](undefined)[And we can also use an IPV6 based ACL.](undefined)[We'll set up a MAC based ACL.](undefined)
            - [In this case, we don't have an access control list,](undefined)[so we'll go ahead and create one.](undefined)[We want to make sure that nobody](undefined)[can attach game consoles or connect any game to our switch.](undefined)[Type Games as the name of our ACL.](undefined)[Click Apply, Close.](undefined)
            - [We have the ACL, but to put access control entries,](undefined)[ACE, into this access control list,](undefined)[we need to click the MAC Based ACE Table.](undefined)[It has a slightly different interface,](undefined)[but let's click Add to add an entry to this page.](undefined)
            - [For our first access control entry, Entry One,](undefined)[we're going to select Deny.](undefined)[We'll select Any for the Destination MAC address.](undefined)[Under Source MAC Address, we're going to select](undefined)[User Defined and put in the address,](undefined)[one of the game consoles, or the prefix of the MAC address,](undefined)[which is used for all devices of the same manufacturer.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and enter 00041F](undefined)[followed by six ones.](undefined)[Under Source MAC Wildcard Mask](undefined)[we'll enter six zeroes followed by six ones.](undefined)
            - [This says that we only care about](undefined)[the first six digits of the MAC Address.](undefined)[This is preventing any device](undefined)[from the same manufacturer from being connected.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and click Apply.](undefined)
            - [That creates our first entry into](undefined)[the Access Control List Entry Table.](undefined)[We'll add one more just for demonstration purposes.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and create Priority Two.](undefined)
            - [Once again, we're going to select Deny,](undefined)[and specify the Destination MAC Address as Any.](undefined)[We're going to specify the Source MAC Address](undefined)[value as 005042 followed by six ones.](undefined)[We'll do the same Source MAC Wildcard Mask,](undefined)[or six zeroes followed by six ones.](undefined)[Click Apply, Close to create our access control entries.](undefined)
            - [We could add more for any of the game](undefined)[console types that are out there,](undefined)[or any other device that we want](undefined)[to prohibit from using our network.](undefined)
            - [We can also add anything we want to allow on the network.](undefined)[Now that we've created the MAC Based ACL Table,](undefined)[and have created the entries in that table,](undefined)[we need to make sure that the ports](undefined)[have a binding to that table.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and click on ACL Binding.](undefined)[Select Port One, scroll down, and click Edit.](undefined)[We want to apply this MAC based ACL,](undefined)[so we'll select that and then select Games,](undefined)[which we just created.](undefined)[If there were additional ones,](undefined)[we could choose from them as well.](undefined)[We'll go ahead and click Apply, Close.](undefined)
            - [Just as we've done in the previous portions](undefined)[of this demonstration, we'll select Port One.](undefined)[The GE One interface, and click on Copy Settings.](undefined)[We'll enter two through 28 and click Apply.](undefined)[That gave us a successful binding](undefined)[of the Games ACL to all of the ports in our interface.](undefined)
            - [One final thing that you will always](undefined)[want to do when you've made configuration](undefined)[changes to your switch is to save](undefined)[the Running Configuration to the Startup Configuration.](undefined)[On this switch, simply click Save at the top.](undefined)[It will blink when there have been changes.](undefined)[Select Running Configuration as the Source File Name.](undefined)[And set the Destination File Name](undefined)[as Startup Configuration, then click Apply.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 10:26-10:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration we've examined](undefined)[the idea of port security.](undefined)[We've looked at management access to our switch.](undefined)[And we've also looked at restricting](undefined)[certain traffic to the switch using ACLs.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 10.4.8 Port Mirroring
                - If it is operating normally, a switch forwards unicast       traffic only to the specific port connected to the intended destination       interface. This prevents sniffing of unicast traffic by hosts attached to       the same switch. There are circumstances in which capturing and analyzing       network traffic is a legitimate activity, however, and **port         mirroring** provides the facility to do this. Port mirroring copies       all packets sent to one or more source ports to a mirror (or destination)       port. On a Cisco switch, this is referred to as a switched port analyzer       (SPAN).
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/jZxx22f2pQGptSL3OC_3qTgwqdlSoQU3q7jnarH7fehem5YwFQkC5wpAq_eQRVYKrFazrODfw0LqJTGHULcXSPBip0r4WeiVEgX-_cnZQgFG3mtNXo8UhDZJtnOrCs2R.png)
                -  *Configuring port mirroring on a Cumulus VX switch. (Screenshot           courtesy of Nvidia.)* 
                    - The mirror port would be used by management or monitoring software, such       as a packet sniffer, network analyzer, or intrusion detection system (IDS)       sensor. Either ingress or egress traffic, or both, can be captured.       Optionally, in order to avoid overloading the monitoring system, packets       may be filtered based on criteria such as protocol ID or TCP/UDP port       number.
                    - Port mirroring demands a lot of processing and can       lead to the switch hardware becoming overloaded and consequently crashing.       If possible, test any security solution that requires port mirroring under       typical loads before deploying it on a production network.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
        - **10.5 Network Security Rules** 
            - ## 10.5.1 Security Rules and ACL Configuration
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
            -

            - 00:001. ACL Overview
            - CC
            - 1.0x

            - ### ACL Overview 00:00-00:22
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [ACLs are a set of rules or conditions that tell the](undefined)[firewall whether to allow, restrict or block a packet.](undefined)[These rules are stored in a text file and](undefined)[are read by the firewall when it starts up.](undefined)[The firewall does exactly what the rules tell](undefined)[it to do, basically operating in an if, then](undefined)[format if the condition of the rule is met.](undefined)[Then the action occurs.](undefined)
            - ### ACL Composition 00:22-00:35
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [ACL rules are made-up of one or](undefined)[more access control entries or ACEs.](undefined)[Each of these ACEs are one line of text that contain](undefined)[rules or instructions for various conditions.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - Logical network segments can be established using either a physical or virtual LAN (VLAN), switching topology mapped to IP subnets. Traffic between these segments must be routed. A firewall can be deployed to filter traffic entering or leaving a segment.
            - We're focusing on firewalls that protect routed network traffic here. Do be aware that firewalls can also be deployed in different ways. These include layer 2 inline or "bump in the wire" appliances and host or personal firewalls. A firewall can also be deployed as a virtual appliance, typically in cloud environments.
            - The security rules for what traffic is permitted and what should be blocked are configured as access control lists (ACLs). Design of firewall ACLs is guided by the principle of least access. This is similar to the principle of least privilege; only allow the minimum amount of traffic required for the operation of network services that should be permitted on the network segment and no more. The rules in a firewall's ACL are processed from top to bottom. If traffic matches a rule that allows the packet, then it is allowed to pass. Conversely, if a match is made to a block rule, the traffic is dropped. Once the firewall matches traffic to a rule, it stops processing subsequent rules. Consequently, the most specific rules are placed at the top of the ACL. If traffic does not match any rule, a firewall can be configured to block the traffic by default. This is called an **implicit deny**. If the firewall is not configured for default implicit deny, an **explicit deny** all rule can be added manually to the end of the ACL.
            - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/_DXZU9jnK-Nl9GCvIQwm1hmxAtxFCAvbi_3Wi3T9w1muUvU37wm7sDp6Yl13Uk60F9_LwYjEQJZfihABt-fFEWwVcc6gOq4iRXoIYyLHlK6F4d7QSRzSp-RHav0X1d05.png)
            - Description
            - The left pane shows W A N under Rulers selected. The right pane titled, Firewall: Rules: W A N shows a table. The table provides data on protocol, source, port, destination, port, gateway, schedule, and description.
            -  *Sample firewall ruleset configured on OPNsense. This ruleset allows any ICMP traffic, HTTP/HTTPS traffic being forwarded to a local server (172.16.0.201), and SMTP traffic sent to the firewall (it is operating an SMTP mail gateway). (Screenshot used with permission from OPNsense.)* 
            - Each rule can specify whether to block or allow traffic based on parameters, often referred to as a tuple. For example, in the screenshot, the firewall imposes a 5-tuple rule, with matches against Protocol, Source address, Source port, Destination address, and Destination port.
                - As well as allowing and blocking, rules can be configured to log matches. Log-only rules are often used as a means of testing a new rule.
                - Most firewalls apply stateful rules. For example, a stateful firewall can differentiate between new and established connections. If a rule allows a new connection to be made by hosts on the public network to a server on the private network, a stateful firewall could automatically allow server replies via the established connection, but not allow the server to create new connections. To apply stateful rules, the firewall must be capable of maintaining a state table of established connections. If the firewall is stateless, an explicit rule would have to be configured for replies.
            - ## 10.5.1 Security Rules and ACL Configuration
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            -

            - Click one of the buttons to take you to that part of the video.
            - ### ACL Overview 00:00-00:22
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [ACLs are a set of rules or conditions that tell the](undefined)[firewall whether to allow, restrict or block a packet.](undefined)[These rules are stored in a text file and](undefined)[are read by the firewall when it starts up.](undefined)[The firewall does exactly what the rules tell](undefined)[it to do, basically operating in an if, then](undefined)[format if the condition of the rule is met.](undefined)[Then the action occurs.](undefined)
            - ### ACL Composition 00:22-00:35
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [ACL rules are made-up of one or](undefined)[more access control entries or ACEs.](undefined)[Each of these ACEs are one line of text that contain](undefined)[rules or instructions for various conditions.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - Logical network segments can be established using either a physical or virtual LAN (VLAN), switching topology mapped to IP subnets. Traffic between these segments must be routed. A firewall can be deployed to filter traffic entering or leaving a segment.
            - We're focusing on firewalls that protect routed network traffic here. Do be aware that firewalls can also be deployed in different ways. These include layer 2 inline or "bump in the wire" appliances and host or personal firewalls. A firewall can also be deployed as a virtual appliance, typically in cloud environments.
            - The security rules for what traffic is permitted and what should be blocked are configured as access control lists (ACLs). Design of firewall ACLs is guided by the principle of least access. This is similar to the principle of least privilege; only allow the minimum amount of traffic required for the operation of network services that should be permitted on the network segment and no more. The rules in a firewall's ACL are processed from top to bottom. If traffic matches a rule that allows the packet, then it is allowed to pass. Conversely, if a match is made to a block rule, the traffic is dropped. Once the firewall matches traffic to a rule, it stops processing subsequent rules. Consequently, the most specific rules are placed at the top of the ACL. If traffic does not match any rule, a firewall can be configured to block the traffic by default. This is called an **implicit deny**. If the firewall is not configured for default implicit deny, an **explicit deny** all rule can be added manually to the end of the ACL.
            - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/7fMA0EMWiG7v0Lmwxclu2rGzc9xFBHakC1Y9C0QrOq1DQ_i3yiLoyoZ3I79I-FHh-_vlFkyTOJxBWR47vG9irxyL9OKAeuRcQGi7Dnn42JhUJ7hNQHwWdVWnf9gdqmZW.png)
            - Description
            - The left pane shows W A N under Rulers selected. The right pane titled, Firewall: Rules: W A N shows a table. The table provides data on protocol, source, port, destination, port, gateway, schedule, and description.
            -  *Sample firewall ruleset configured on OPNsense. This ruleset allows any ICMP traffic, HTTP/HTTPS traffic being forwarded to a local server (172.16.0.201), and SMTP traffic sent to the firewall (it is operating an SMTP mail gateway). (Screenshot used with permission from OPNsense.)* 
            - Each rule can specify whether to block or allow traffic based on parameters, often referred to as a tuple. For example, in the screenshot, the firewall imposes a 5-tuple rule, with matches against Protocol, Source address, Source port, Destination address, and Destination port.
                - As well as allowing and blocking, rules can be configured to log matches. Log-only rules are often used as a means of testing a new rule.
                - Most firewalls apply stateful rules. For example, a stateful firewall can differentiate between new and established connections. If a rule allows a new connection to be made by hosts on the public network to a server on the private network, a stateful firewall could automatically allow server replies via the established connection, but not allow the server to create new connections. To apply stateful rules, the firewall must be capable of maintaining a state table of established connections. If the firewall is stateless, an explicit rule would have to be configured for replies.
            - ## 10.5.2 Proxy Servers
                - A typical network firewall filters traffic that is routed through its       interfaces. By contrast, a **proxy         server** forwards requests and responses on behalf of its clients.       A proxy implies more of a break in the communications flow. Rather than       inspecting traffic as it passes through, the proxy deconstructs each       packet, performs analysis, and then rebuilds the packet and forwards it       on, providing it conforms to the rules. The proxy could also perform       address translation to convert between private and public addressing       schemes.
                - Note that you should consider proxying as a       function, rather than a class of device. It is possible to configure proxy       server software on general-purpose computer hardware, but equally most       firewall appliances will be capable of working as a proxy. Similarly, a       proxy can be configured as a virtual appliance.
                - ## Forward Proxies
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A forwarding proxy server provides for protocol-specific outbound       traffic. For example, you might deploy a web proxy that enables hosts on a       private network to connect to websites and secure websites on the       Internet. A proxy server must understand the application it is servicing.       A web proxy must be able to parse and modify HTTP and HTTPS requests and       replies (and potentially HTML too). Some proxy servers are       application-specific; others are multipurpose. A multipurpose proxy is one       configured with filters for multiple protocol types, such as HTTP, FTP,       and SMTP.
                - The main benefit of a proxy server is that clients connect to a specified       point within the perimeter network for web access. This provides for a       degree of traffic management and security. In addition, most web proxy       servers provide caching engines, whereby frequently requested webpages and       image assets are retained on the proxy, negating the need to refetch those       files for subsequent requests.
                - Proxy servers can generally be classed as nontransparent or transparent.       A nontransparent proxy means that the client must be configured with the       proxy server address and port number to use it. The port on which the       proxy server accepts client connections is often configured as port 8080.       A transparent (or "forced" or "intercepting") proxy intercepts client       traffic without the client having to be reconfigured.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/R5YlYZtkLk3b3s8L8GCt8e4IMLLwMBGWzGcAce6d0Inxzg2Tfm-oHRiUrjUZrDL6qh6QGt9T7FKWCu6qf2MDWEM2fXvmJgKIyGpJR0DXUnKLVgGDfgAGY-oos7RRSdKH.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The left pane shows Administration under Web Proxy selected. The           right pane shows tabs from which Forward Proxy tab is selected. It           provides fields for proxy interfaces, proxy port, enable transparent H           T T P proxy, enable S S L inspection, log S N I information only, S S           L proxy port, and C A to use.
                    -  *Configuring transparent proxy settings for the proxy server running           on the OPNsense security appliance. (Screenshot used with permission           from OPNsense.)* 
                - ## Reverse Proxies
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A reverse proxy server provides for protocol-specific inbound traffic.       Security and performance factors might make it unwise to allow hosts on a       public network to connect directly to application servers. Instead, you       can deploy a reverse proxy and configure it to listen for client requests       from a public network (the Internet) and create the appropriate request to       the application server. The proxy is said to publish the application.       Typical applications for reverse proxy servers include publishing a web       server, publishing messaging or conferencing applications, and enabling       POP/IMAP mail retrieval.
                - A reverse proxy might handle the encryption/decryption and authentication       on behalf of the application servers, reducing the overhead on those       servers. It can also perform caching to improve performance. Reverse       proxies could also be configured to perform load balancing across an       application server pool.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - ### Use a Caching Proxy Server with pfSense 00:00-01:01
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Employees at a given company often access](undefined)[the same web resources and websites.](undefined)[When dozens, hundreds, or even thousands of](undefined)[employees do this at the same time, they end](undefined)[up downloading the same content repeatedly.](undefined)[This can end up being a very inefficient use of the](undefined)[internet connection and can produce latency and timeouts.](undefined)[A common way to resolve this problem](undefined)[is to use a caching proxy server.](undefined)[These proxies are placed between users and the internet](undefined)[to save, or cache, much of the content being accessed.](undefined)[Then when the content is requested again, the proxy](undefined)[has a copy of the contents available for users instead](undefined)[of them having to go out to the internet for it.](undefined)
            - [In this demonstration, I'm going to set](undefined)[up a proxy server on my pfSense appliance.](undefined)[The appliance has already been configured](undefined)[as the router for all workstations on the](undefined)[network and also provides DHCP services.](undefined)
            - ### Install a Squid Proxy on pfSense 01:01-01:39
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [To start, I need to log into the](undefined)[pfSense configuration web page.](undefined)[The proxy functions for pfSense are](undefined)[installed through additional packages.](undefined)[We browse to 'System' > 'Package Manager' and type](undefined)['squid' in the Search field; then press Enter.](undefined)[Squid is a popular, open-source caching](undefined)[proxy that works nicely for our purposes.](undefined)[I install it by clicking on the 'Install'](undefined)[button to the right and clicking 'Confirm'.](undefined)[The package is then downloaded and installed.](undefined)[It'll display the word "Success" when it's finished.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Squid on pfSense 01:39-04:45
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now we need to configure the proxy.](undefined)[In the 'Services' menu, select 'Squid Proxy Server'.](undefined)[As you can see, there are many settings](undefined)[to manage with a Squid proxy server.](undefined)[I'll just demonstrate a few of them.](undefined)[Before I can check off the 'Enable Squid Proxy'](undefined)[box, I have to configure the Local Cache.](undefined)[The 'Local Cache' tab allows us to change](undefined)[how much and which kind of data to cache.](undefined)[I'll leave the settings on the defaults, but we](undefined)[need to click the 'Save' button before we move on.](undefined)[Back on the 'General' tab, I'm going to leave](undefined)[many of these Squid General Settings unchanged.](undefined)[For example, only IP version 4 is used on this](undefined)[network, so I won't change it to handle IPv6 traffic.](undefined)[I want this to work as a regular proxy not a reverse](undefined)[proxy because I want the service to bind to the LAN](undefined)[interface, making its services available to network hosts.](undefined)[I also want outgoing traffic to be routed through the](undefined)[WAN interface, which will direct traffic to the internet.](undefined)[The default port of 3128 is fine.](undefined)
            - [As I mentioned already, there are quite a few settings.](undefined)[I'm going to accept the defaults for the rest of](undefined)[this tab, but there are more things to consider.](undefined)[We can use multiple proxy servers together to spread the](undefined)[network load and maximize resource request efficiency.](undefined)[They can, essentially, share each](undefined)[other's cached data across a local link.](undefined)[We add these shared data pools in the 'Remote Cache' tab.](undefined)[The rest of the tabs can configure things](undefined)[like user authentication, allowed hosts, and](undefined)[they can even scan incoming data for viruses.](undefined)
            - [On the 'General Squid Settings' tab, I'll click 'Save'.](undefined)[Now let's check the status of our proxy on the 'Status' tab.](undefined)[I can see that the service is started, and there are](undefined)[a number of captured statistics for me to review.](undefined)[But since I haven't used any clients to connect](undefined)[to the internet yet, there isn't too much here.](undefined)
            - [The machine I've been using to configure](undefined)[the pfSense appliance will also be used](undefined)[to verify that the proxy is working.](undefined)[Know that most Windows 10 systems are configured](undefined)[to detect proxy settings automatically.](undefined)[I'm going to change that here to be more specific.](undefined)[If I click on 'Start' and type 'proxy',](undefined)[I find the 'Proxy' settings option.](undefined)[If I scroll down the Proxy settings window to 'Manual proxy](undefined)[setup', I can enter in the information for my proxy server.](undefined)[I just put the address and port in here and click 'Save'.](undefined)
            - [Let's test to ensure that the proxy settings are](undefined)[working and that this system can access the internet.](undefined)[I'll just browse to a website and make sure it loads.](undefined)[It does.](undefined)
            - [There are many other things we can do with a proxy server.](undefined)[Remember that proxy servers are key components not only](undefined)[of an efficient network but of a secure one as well.](undefined)[A proxy server can even be used to restrict access](undefined)[to unauthorized or potentially harmful websites.](undefined)[It can also balance inbound traffic between servers.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 04:45-05:01
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demo, I've shown you how to install and configure](undefined)[a proxy server on a pfSense appliance and explained a few](undefined)[of the available features you have to choose from.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 10.5.3 Content Filtering
                - An ACL-type security rule applies basic Network or Transport layer       filtering. By contrast, **content           filtering** is capable of applying Application layer       filters based on HTTP data. It could also apply more general business       rules, such as time-of-day restrictions. Most firewalls and proxies now       support some level of content filtering.
                - Content filtering plays a pivotal role in safeguarding an organization's       network by blocking users from accessing malicious or inappropriate       websites. A content filtering firewall or proxy can restrict access based       on various criteria:
                    - **Uniform Resource Locator (URL) filtering**—A         URL contains protocol (http or https, for instance), domain name, server         path, and optionally query parameter components. A URL filter can scan         the URL embedded in an HTTP request and allow or block it. The filter         might use simple domain filtering to block access to specific URLs that         are known to host malicious content, are inappropriate, or violate the         company's Internet usage policy. It could use regular expression pattern         matching to filter by keywords or path and query parameters.
                    - **Content and reputation-based filtering**—This         filtering leverages continually updated databases that score websites         based on their observed behavior and history. Sites known for hosting         malware, engaging in phishing attacks, or distributing spam, for         instance, would have a poor reputation score and could be automatically         blocked. These databases can also sort websites into categories, such as         social networking, gambling, adult content, webmail, or file sharing.         Allowing or denying each category provides a simpler way to enforce web         usage policies than allowing or denying individual URLs.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/kahlmS8Ylj40RD0DUQqX4Esxi4FGQLm0n9hxoG2Ge6SuW7RROSehhC9ULoE7BFGvBxrX42ENNm5XSwVfldnYwcDL7nV4LwhOBTN2eMMZj4VTZElpSXKVCC1F9V-q878W.png)
                    - Description
                    - The tabs at the top are system, status, network, services, firewall,           IPFire, and logs. The screen lists the block categories under the head           URL filter settings. The categories are as follows: ads, arjel, bank,           chat, cryptojacking, dialer, drugs, filehostings, games, liste           blanche, manga, phishing, publicite, remote-control, shortener,           trichun, warez, adult, associations religieuses, bitcoin, child,           dangerous material, doh, educational games, financial, hacking, liste           bu, marketingware, porn, radio, sect, social networks, strict           redirector, update, webmail, aggressive, astrology, blog, cleaning,           dating, download, examen pix, forums, jobsearch, mail, press,           reaffected, sexual education, special, strong redirector, violence,           agressif, audio-video, celebrity, cooking, ddos, drogue, exceptions           liste bu, gambling, lingerie, malware, mobile-phone, proxy,           redirector, shopping, sports, translation, and vpn. A checkbox is           present against each category.
                    -  *Web filter content categories using the IPFire open source firewall.           (Screenshot used with permission from IPFire.)* 
                - Transport Layer Security (TLS) poses major issues       for proxies and content filters. A proxy cannot inspect or modify       application data in encrypted traffic, but it cannot decrypt the traffic       without breaking the TLS handshake between the client and the website. To       perform TLS inspection, the proxy has to generate an enterprise       certificate for each domain. The client trusts this certificate as it is       issued by an enterprise CA but still matches the domain it has requested.       The proxy establishes its own TLS tunnel with the website, forwarding the       client's requests (if they conform to policy).
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 10.5.4 Misconfigured Firewall and ACL Issues
                - A misconfigured firewall or access control list (ACL) can cause different kinds of network access problems.
                - ## Access Denied Issues
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - One type of firewall, ACL, or content filter misconfiguration causes blocked services, ports, or addresses that should be allowed through. This will cause an application or protocol to fail to function correctly. For example, the firewall might be blocking TCP or UDP ports that are supposed to be open, or it might be allowing the ports but denying access to an IP network or host address that is supposed to be able to connect. Also, consider that advanced firewalls can apply additional filtering criteria, such as evaluating process/service executable names/locations or authorizations based on user accounts or group memberships.
                - A deny type of error will usually be easy to identify, as users will report incidents connected with the failure of the data traffic. With such incidents, firewall configuration will always be a likely cause, so will be high on the list to investigate. Diagnosis can be confirmed by trying to establish the connection from both inside and outside the firewall. If it connects from outside the firewall but not from inside, this will confirm the firewall to be the cause of the issue.
                - ## Multiple Firewall Issues
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Another potential issue is where there are both network-based and host-based firewall settings to navigate in the communication path. There could be a host firewall running on the client, on the server, or on both. To diagnose an issue with a host firewall, attempt the connection with the host firewall disabled. If the connection attempt succeeds, then the network firewall ACL is allowing the packets, but the host firewall is configured to block them. If the connection attempt fails, investigate the network firewall ACL first. You can also inspect the firewall's log files to discover what rules have been applied to block traffic at a particular time.
                - ## Security Violation Issues
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The other possible outcome of a badly configured firewall is that packets may be allowed through that should be blocked. This is a more serious outcome, because the result is to open the system to security vulnerabilities. It is also not necessarily so easily detected, as it does not typically cause anything to stop functioning. As no incidents usually arise from this outcome (except in the case that a vulnerability is exploited), it is not a scenario that is subject to troubleshooting. Rather, it underlines the need for regular firewall audits and thorough change control processes to deal with firewall change requests.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - ### Configuring Firewall Rules 00:00-00:32
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going to configure](undefined)[firewall rules on a pfSense security appliance.](undefined)[First, we're going to configure a firewall rule to](undefined)[allow both HTTP and HTTPS traffic from internet, or WAN,](undefined)[through the firewall and to our web server on our DMZ.](undefined)[The second thing we're going to do is configure](undefined)[a rule to allow all traffic coming from our LAN](undefined)[to get to our DMZ through the firewall.](undefined)
            - ### Allow HTTP and HTTPS 00:32-04:42
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I want to verify that I have my DMZ ready to go.](undefined)[I'll scroll down, and you can see I have my three](undefined)[interfaces here, and they have assigned IPs.](undefined)
            - [Let's create our rules.](undefined)[I'll go to Firewall > Rules, and the first rules](undefined)[I'll configure are the ones from our WAN to our](undefined)[DMZ to allow HTTP and HTTPS to our web server.](undefined)[I want to go to our DMZ tab here,](undefined)[and down here, I have a few buttons.](undefined)[You'll notice there are two buttons that say Add.](undefined)[This one has the arrow pointing up, and if I pick](undefined)[that one, it'll add the rule to the top of my list.](undefined)[If I click on the one with the arrow pointing down, it'll](undefined)[add the rule to the bottom, so I'll click on that one.](undefined)
            - [For our rule, we want to choose to](undefined)[pass the traffic through the firewall.](undefined)[My other choices are to block it or reject it.](undefined)[If we choose Reject, the packets are returned to the](undefined)[sender, and the sender can see they were blocked.](undefined)[Block will just drop the packets as if they never arrived.](undefined)[For security reasons, sometimes you don't want](undefined)[senders to know that your device is even there,](undefined)[and it's better to just reject the packets.](undefined)[But if you're troubleshooting](undefined)[issues, blocking can be more helpful.](undefined)[Either way, with block or reject, the](undefined)[packets won't reach the destination.](undefined)
            - [The interface that I want to configure is the DMZ.](undefined)[You can see that I have my LAN and WAN here as well.](undefined)[For Address Family, I'll leave it as IPv4.](undefined)[The other choices are IPv6 or both.](undefined)[For Protocol, we're allowing HTTP, which runs on TCP.](undefined)[If I click the dropdown list, you can see all](undefined)[the other protocols that we could choose if we](undefined)[were going to allow some other type of traffic.](undefined)
            - [Our source is going to be from our WAN network.](undefined)[I need to see more options, so I'll](undefined)[click on the Display Advanced button.](undefined)[The Source Port Range is going to be HTTP.](undefined)[I could put in a single port or a](undefined)[range, but we just want HTTP port 80.](undefined)[Since this is going to be my web server,](undefined)[I need to put in the IP address here.](undefined)[First, I'll pick Single host or alias,](undefined)[and now let's pop our diagram back up.](undefined)[Our web server's IP is right here,](undefined)[so let's go back and enter that in.](undefined)[Destination Address will be '172.16.1.5'.](undefined)[We're using HTTP, and the rest of this looks good.](undefined)
            - [Down here, we could log packets handled by this rule.](undefined)[Since this is a web server, we probably don't want](undefined)[to do that since our log would be overwhelmed.](undefined)[If it was FTP, SSH, or other traffic like that, we probably](undefined)[would want to log traffic, but not for normal web requests.](undefined)[We do want to put a description in here because as you](undefined)[create more and more rules, you'll forget what they all do.](undefined)[For this, I'll type in 'HTTP to](undefined)[DMZ from WAN' and click Save.](undefined)[On our next page here, we have to click on Apply](undefined)[Changes for the changes to my firewall to take effect.](undefined)
            - [So, here are my rules so far.](undefined)[I have one that allows my DMZ out, and](undefined)[this second one is our HTTP rule from the](undefined)[WAN on port 80 the web server on our DMZ.](undefined)
            - [Now, you might be saying to yourself, "Doesn't most](undefined)[web traffic use HTTPS these days?" Well, that answer](undefined)[is certainly yes, so now we need an HTTPS rule.](undefined)
            - [Here's some good news: to save a bit of time and](undefined)[keep from making any errors, we can just copy this](undefined)[rule and change the HTTP port 80 to HTTPS port 443.](undefined)[I'll click here, which is the Copy icon.](undefined)[What this does creates a new rule with the exact settings.](undefined)[I'll scroll down and change my source](undefined)[from HTTP port 80 to HTTPS port 443.](undefined)[For the destination, I'll do the same.](undefined)[Scroll down change the description](undefined)[by just adding the letter 'S'.](undefined)[Click Save.](undefined)[On this page, Click Apply Changes.](undefined)[Down here, you now see our rules for both HTTP and HTTPS.](undefined)
            - ### Allow LAN Traffic to WAN 04:42-06:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now let's look at our diagram again.](undefined)[This time, I want to create a rule that allows](undefined)[any traffic from my LAN to get to the DMZ.](undefined)[Let's go back to our firewall and configure that now.](undefined)[These rules are read from the top down.](undefined)[So, for example, if the first rule says block](undefined)[everything, none of the other rules would ever](undefined)[be seen because that's the very first rule.](undefined)[However, if we made a rule right now that said to block](undefined)[everything and put it at the bottom, our first three rules](undefined)[would still be fine, but everything else would be blocked.](undefined)[By default, everything is blocked with pfSense](undefined)[anyway unless you open it, so we wouldn't really need](undefined)[that sort of rule, but it doesn't hurt anything.](undefined)[So far, our rules don't affect one another, so](undefined)[it really doesn't matter where we put them, but](undefined)[keep all of this in mind when creating rules.](undefined)[We'll just click on Add and use the one](undefined)[that puts it at the bottom of the list.](undefined)
            - [Our Action will be to pass traffic.](undefined)[We'll leave the interface and address family alone.](undefined)[But for protocol, we're going](undefined)[to change this from TCP to Any.](undefined)[This might not be the best practice, but we're allowing](undefined)[any and all traffic from our LAN to reach our DMZ.](undefined)[Under Source, we'll choose our LAN network from the list.](undefined)[Our destination will be the DMZ from this list.](undefined)[We don't want to forget a description for this rule.](undefined)[I'll type 'LAN to DMZ Any'.](undefined)[Click Save.](undefined)[As always, Apply Changes.](undefined)
            - [Here's my latest rule.](undefined)[My source is from the LAN on any port to my DMZ on any port.](undefined)[By the way, the asterisk is wildcard, which means any.](undefined)[Over here, the green check mark means it's enabled.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 06:29-06:53
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we created firewall rules on our firewall.](undefined)[First, we configured HTTP and HTTPS traffic from the WAN](undefined)[to a web server on our DMZ, and then we created a rule to](undefined)[allow any traffic from our LAN to get to our DMZ.](undefined)

    - ### 11.0 Supporting Network Security Design
        - **11.1 Zone-based Security** 
            - ## 11.1.1 Network Security Zones
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
            -

            - CC
