            - ### Configuring a Captive Portal 00:07-01:06
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going](undefined)[to configure a captive portal.](undefined)[Captive portals force a user to view and interact with](undefined)[them before they can access a network or the internet.](undefined)
            - [After you connect your device to the wireless or](undefined)[guest network, but before you can access the internet,](undefined)[you are redirected to a captive portal page.](undefined)
            - [You might be prompted to agree to the terms](undefined)[and conditions of using the network, and you](undefined)[might be asked to pay a fee to you gain access.](undefined)[Some even allow users to log in to use the network.](undefined)[They are used a great deal, and you've most likely](undefined)[used one before, especially at hotels or airports.](undefined)
            - [Today, we'll be using a captive](undefined)[portal that's part of pfSense.](undefined)[pfSense sits between the internal network and](undefined)[the internet, as you can see in this diagram.](undefined)
            - [We want to configure the captive portal so that any device](undefined)[that connects to our guest Wi-Fi will have to go through](undefined)[the captive portal in order to get internet access.](undefined)
            - ### Add Captive Portal Zone 01:06-01:49
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I have a network adaptor installed in pfSense](undefined)[that I'm going to use for my guest Wi-Fi.](undefined)[If I scroll down, you can see it listed right here.](undefined)[So, let's get started.](undefined)[I'll go to Services > Captive](undefined)[portal and click on the Add button.](undefined)[Under Add Captive Portal Zone, I want](undefined)[to enter in our zone name, 'Guest_WiFi'.](undefined)[For Zone description, I'll put in Guest Wi-Fi.](undefined)[By the way, for the zone name, you can only](undefined)[use letters, digits, and underscores, but you](undefined)[can use any character for the description name.](undefined)[Click the Save & Continue button.](undefined)
            - ### Captive Portal Configuration 01:49-05:42
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now, under Captive Portal Configuration, let's](undefined)[check the Enable Captive Portal check box.](undefined)[When we do, we get a bunch of options.](undefined)[Under Interfaces, you'll see my Guest Wi-Fi interface](undefined)[that we looked at in the beginning of the demo.](undefined)[Depending on your network, you could have](undefined)[a VLAN configured for your guest Wi-Fi.](undefined)[My demo environment isn't set up with VLAN,](undefined)[but that would work really well for this.](undefined)[This is the one we want to pick.](undefined)
            - [Under Maximum concurrent connections, we can](undefined)[put in a number of concurrent connections.](undefined)[I'll put five in here.](undefined)[This is not the number of users, but rather how many](undefined)[connections a single IP can establish to the portal.](undefined)[For Idle timeout, we'll put in 60 minutes.](undefined)[If a client is inactive for that amount](undefined)[of time, they'll be disconnected.](undefined)[Hard timeout limits a connection no matter what.](undefined)[We might not want users on our guest network](undefined)[forever, so you can limit the time they spend.](undefined)[Let's put in 120 minutes.](undefined)[We can also give guest users a traffic quota.](undefined)[We might not want them streaming video for two hours, so](undefined)[we can put something in here to restrict that if we like.](undefined)[I'll just leave it blank for now.](undefined)
            - [I'm going to leave the next several set to the defaults and](undefined)[scroll down a bit until I see Per-user bandwidth restriction.](undefined)[Let's go ahead and check the box to enable it.](undefined)[This limits users' download and upload speeds.](undefined)[For Default download, I'll put in 10,000 kilobits](undefined)[per second, which is about 10 megabits per second.](undefined)[For the Default upload, I'll put in 3,000 kilobits](undefined)[per second, which is about 3 megabits per second.](undefined)
            - [We could use a custom captive portal page.](undefined)[Let's check the box.](undefined)[When I do, you can see that I](undefined)[would need a custom page to upload.](undefined)[I would also need an error page and logout page.](undefined)[I'm just going to use the default page that](undefined)[comes with pfSense for my captive portal page.](undefined)[But if I were setting this up for real](undefined)[use, I'd certainly want a custom page.](undefined)[I'll uncheck that box.](undefined)
            - [I could also upload a custom logo](undefined)[or a custom background for my page.](undefined)[For the Terms and Conditions, I can put in](undefined)[something like, 'Don't be bad on the internet.'](undefined)
            - [For Authentication Method, we're](undefined)[not going to use any authentication.](undefined)[We could use RADIUS if we had that set up, or](undefined)[we could use some other type of authentication.](undefined)[But for this demo, I'll select None, don't](undefined)[authenticate users from the dropdown.](undefined)
            - [We could Enable HTTPS login, but if I did, I'd have](undefined)[to configure the server to have a certificate, and I](undefined)[haven't done that, so I'll leave that unchecked.](undefined)
            - [Here, at the bottom, it reminds us to set](undefined)[up DHCP on our captive portal interface.](undefined)[I've already done that, so we're good to go.](undefined)[That is all we're going to set up](undefined)[here, so click on Save to continue.](undefined)[And here, you can see that our](undefined)[captive portal has been created.](undefined)
            - [Now, before we continue, let's briefly look at a few](undefined)[options that we skipped and aren't going to configure.](undefined)
            - [Under the MAC's tab, we can get a little more granular with](undefined)[bandwidth control as far as limiting the upload and download.](undefined)[Under Allowed IP Addresses, we can do the exact](undefined)[same thing, but by IP instead of MAC addresses.](undefined)[Allowed Hostnames is the same, but by hostname.](undefined)
            - [Here, we have Vouchers.](undefined)[Vouchers are single-use codes used to gain](undefined)[internet access through a captive portal.](undefined)[Vouchers are commonly implemented in places](undefined)[where authentication is required, but where](undefined)[there is also a time limit on internet access.](undefined)
            - [And lastly, the File Manager tab is used to](undefined)[upload files that are used inside a captive portal](undefined)[page, such as style sheets and image files.](undefined)
            - [Now let's test out our captive portal](undefined)[over on our Guest Wi-Fi network.](undefined)
            - ### Test the Captive Portal in a Guest Wi-Fi Network 05:42-06:07
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I've switched over to our Guest Wi-Fi network.](undefined)[What should happen when we go to surf the web is that](undefined)[we should get redirected to our captive portal page.](undefined)[I'll open up our web browser, and we're](undefined)[directed to our pfSense captive portal page.](undefined)[Remember, I didn't configure authentication,](undefined)[so all I have to do is check the box to agree](undefined)[with the terms and conditions to proceed.](undefined)[I'll click Login, and after a](undefined)[second, I'm forwarded to my homepage.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 06:07-06:22
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demo.](undefined)[In this demo, we configured a captive portal on](undefined)[pfSence to force users to agree to terms and conditions](undefined)[before being allowed access to the internet.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 12.3.5 Bring Your Own Device Issues
                - **Bring Your Own Device (BYOD)** is a smartphone/tablet provisioning model that allows users to select a personal device to use to interact with corporate network services and cloud apps. Allowing user selection of devices introduces numerous compatibility, support, and security challenges:
                    - **Compatibility/support**—The wide range of devices, mobile OS versions, and vendor support for patches make the job of ensuring that each device can connect to corporate network apps and data resources complex.
                    - **Security**—This device variety also causes security issues, especially in terms of unpatched devices. Another issue is that the device is not fully under the administrative control of the IT department. An insider threat actor could install apps that might pose a risk to corporate data or misuse the device to exfiltrate data.
                - Some of the impact of these issues can be mitigated through the use of enterprise mobility management (EMM) suites and corporate workspaces. EMM (or mobile device management) is a type of network access control solution that registers devices as they connect to the network. It can then enforce security policies while the device is connected. These might restrict use of device functions or personal apps. A corporate workspace is an app that is segmented from the rest of the device and allows more centralized control over corporate data. Users must also agree to acceptable use policies, which might prohibit installing non-store apps and rooting/jailbreaking a device and require keeping the device up to date with patches. Users might have to permit some level of inspection of the device to protect corporate data.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - ### Introduction  00:00-00:14
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going to look](undefined)[at creating a Bring Your Own Device (BYOD)](undefined)[guest wireless network for employees.](undefined)
            - ### Configuring the Network 00:14-01:03
                - [Size]();-[H3]()

            - ### Configuring VLAN and IP Settings 01:03-02:19

            - [Next, we're going to specify that](undefined)[this network will be VLAN fifty.](undefined)[This tags the traffic so that our other](undefined)[network equipment knows how to handle it.](undefined)[Now, we need to specify the IP for the BYOD network.](undefined)[I'm going to give it an IP of](undefined)[192.168.50.1 with the /24 subnet.](undefined)[This can be anything you want, but I like](undefined)[to name things to correlate with the VLAN.](undefined)[So, in this case, I made the third](undefined)[octet fifty to match the VLAN.](undefined)[You don't have to; it's just something I do](undefined)[to help me remember that when I see addresses](undefined)[with fifty dot something, it's VLAN fifty.](undefined)[This is a class C private IP range, and it](undefined)[will give us 254 IP addresses that we can use.](undefined)[When I hit enter, it populated some other settings](undefined)[automatically for me: I have the gateway, the broadcast](undefined)[IP, total IP count, the range, and my subnet mask.](undefined)[I'll scroll down a bit and then come over](undefined)[here and click on "Update DHCP Range."](undefined)[This populates a suggested range of addresses that](undefined)[I can use for my DHCP and hand out to the guests.](undefined)[It put in 50.6 to 50.254, which is fine with me.](undefined)[By the way, I do like to leave a few addresses](undefined)[at the beginning of my networks for potential](undefined)[static assignments, so this worked out well.](undefined)

                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [I'll scroll down a bit more, and now](undefined)[let's take a look at our DHCP lease time.](undefined)[By default, it's set to 86,400 seconds.](undefined)[For those of you who don't like math, that's 24 hours.](undefined)[Now, I might want to shorten that](undefined)[if this is an office setting.](undefined)[Perhaps ten hours is plenty.](undefined)[Just do the math and change the number of](undefined)[seconds to satisfy your lease time reference.](undefined)[My DHCP gateway is set to automatic.](undefined)[If my gateway were something different, I](undefined)[could change it, but I'm using the here.](undefined)[I also have some various advanced DHCP options,](undefined)[but there's nothing here that we need to configure.](undefined)[Just be aware that you do have some additional](undefined)[options if your organization happens to need them.](undefined)[I'll click on "Save" here and go to the next step.](undefined)
            - ### Creating the Wireless Network 03:04-04:27

            - ### Introduction to Enterprise Security Enhancement 00:00-00:12
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going to look](undefined)[at several things you can do to increase](undefined)[the overall security of your enterprises.](undefined)
            - ### Default Wireless Network Security 00:12-00:20
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Wireless network defaults may be easy to set,](undefined)[but they don't always provide the proper](undefined)[security needed from unwanted guests or hackers.](undefined)
            - ### Changing Default Admin Credentials 00:20-01:33
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's take a look at our first task, which is to change](undefined)[the admin username and password when you open up a piece](undefined)[of wireless equipment and take it out-of-the-box.](undefined)[It's usually pre-configured with](undefined)[a default username and password.](undefined)[The problem is a lot of administrators continue to use that](undefined)[default username and password without ever changing them.](undefined)[These usernames and passwords are widely](undefined)[documented and available on the Internet.](undefined)[If you don't change them, you're leaving](undefined)[yourself wide open to exploitation.](undefined)[On a Cisco wireless LAN controller, they typically make](undefined)[you set a username and password when you first set up](undefined)[the device, but that isn't the case with all vendors.](undefined)[If we want to change or add a username and password, we](undefined)[can go to management and then local management users.](undefined)[Here you can see we have a user](undefined)[named wireless admin already set.](undefined)[Let's add a new user by clicking on](undefined)[new in the top right-hand corner.](undefined)[This will ask you to put in a username, a](undefined)[password and then have you confirm the password.](undefined)[The access mode will determine which](undefined)[level of access you want to give it.](undefined)[In our case, we want to read and write, but](undefined)[it's possible to give someone read only access.](undefined)[Click apply.](undefined)[Now we have a test out user and it's possible to log in](undefined)[as that user and delete the previous one if you want.](undefined)
            - ### Reviewing Wireless LAN Security Settings 01:33-02:38
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The second thing we want to look at is](undefined)[reviewing our wireless LAN security settings.](undefined)[When I come over here to WLANS and select a wireless SSID,](undefined)[you can see settings regarding the wireless network.](undefined)[The security tab defines which type of](undefined)[security is applied to this test out WLAN.](undefined)[Currently this is using WPA2 with AES](undefined)[encryption and PSK which is a pre shared key.](undefined)[These settings are typical even on home](undefined)[wireless networks or small businesses.](undefined)[If a radius server were defined, it would](undefined)[be set in the security tab under radius and](undefined)[then applied here to the AAA servers tab.](undefined)[It can be Active Directory authenticated](undefined)[if it's set up with a network policy server.](undefined)
            - [Now we have other options that can add](undefined)[complexity to our wireless network.](undefined)[Not only can we either authenticate with a pre-shared key](undefined)[or radius server, we can add MAC filtering on top of that.](undefined)[Since we don't have a radius server, we're going](undefined)[to configure a pre-shared key with MAC filtering.](undefined)[This checkbox right here enables](undefined)[MAC filtering for the test out SSID.](undefined)[Go ahead and click apply after that checkbox is selected.](undefined)
            - ### Configuring MAC Filtering 02:38-03:19
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next, we need to go to security and then MAC filtering.](undefined)[This will be a new addition to the list, so let's click new.](undefined)[Enter the MAC address of the wireless device you](undefined)[want to connect, change the profile name to testout.](undefined)[Since we only want this MAC address to](undefined)[connect to testout, it's possible to leave](undefined)[it on any WLAN if that's what you want.](undefined)[Descriptions can be useful for knowing which device this is.](undefined)[Interface is the network you plan on connecting to.](undefined)[Now click apply.](undefined)[OK, our wireless device is ready](undefined)[to connect to the test out SSID.](undefined)[Be mindful, other vendors may use a MAC block list](undefined)[or access control list to filter MAC addresses.](undefined)
            - ### Wireless Protection Policies 03:19-03:54
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The last thing we're going to take a](undefined)[look at is wireless protection policies.](undefined)[These settings help aid in protecting](undefined)[and monitoring security threats.](undefined)[Some manufacturers include wireless intrusion](undefined)[prevention, but with Cisco, this is a separate product](undefined)[that is not included in the wireless LAN controller.](undefined)[Since we're already in security, we just](undefined)[need to go to wireless protection policies.](undefined)[AP Authentication offers a couple of protection types](undefined)[such as AP Authentication and management frame protection.](undefined)[We're going to leave it at management](undefined)[frame protection, which helps prevent](undefined)[frames against forgery and replay attacks.](undefined)
            - ### Client Exclusion Policies and Signatures 03:54-04:19
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [If we check out client exclusion policies,](undefined)[you can see everything is already enabled](undefined)[by default, such as IP theft or IP reuse.](undefined)[That means clients are excluded if the](undefined)[IP is already assigned to another device.](undefined)[Lastly, if you check out standard signatures,](undefined)[you can see the different types of signatures](undefined)[that will be reported in the logs.](undefined)[If an SNMP trap was set up with a monitoring](undefined)[server, this could alert you on these signatures.](undefined)
            - ### Enforcing Management Frame Protection 04:19-05:01
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [We want to make sure the management frame protection](undefined)[isn't optional for the testout SSID, so let's go to](undefined)[WLANs, select the testout SSID, and go to advanced.](undefined)[You can see the MFP is optional](undefined)[which we want to be required.](undefined)[Cisco makes notes for certain areas such](undefined)[as this one where you see the number four.](undefined)[If you look in the footnotes, it tells us that](undefined)[client MFP is not active unless WPA2 is configured.](undefined)[In our case, we are using WPA2, so this does apply.](undefined)[When we click the apply button it gives us the warning](undefined)[about the radio resetting and clients being disconnected.](undefined)[Click OK.](undefined)
            - ### Saving Configuration Changes 05:01-05:13
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Always when you're done configuring Cisco devices,](undefined)[you want to save your configuration to the Flash.](undefined)[Otherwise, on reboot, the changes will be gone.](undefined)[Click save configuration and click OK.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 05:13-05:42
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demonstration, we talked about some](undefined)[things you can do to increase the overall](undefined)[security of your enterprise's wireless network.](undefined)[We first looked at changing the admin username and password.](undefined)[We talked about making sure that your wireless](undefined)[LAN security settings are set properly.](undefined)[We looked at enabling MAC address filtering and we talked](undefined)[about wireless protection policies and how they can be](undefined)[useful in preventing and monitoring security events.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 12.3.6 Wireless Network Attacks
                - Wireless networks can open several avenues for a threat actor to gain unauthorized network access.
                - ## Rogue Access Points
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A **rogue access point** is one that has been installed on the network without authorization, whether with malicious intent or not. A malicious user can set up such an AP with something as basic as a smartphone with tethering capabilities, and a non-malicious user could enable such an AP by accident. If connected to a LAN without security, an unauthorized AP creates a backdoor through which to attack the network.
                - ## Evil Twins
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A rogue AP masquerading as a legitimate one is called an **evil twin**. An evil twin might advertise a similar network name (SSID) to the legitimate one. For example, an evil twin might be configured with the network name "compeny" where the legitimate network name is "company." Alternatively, the evil twin might spoof the SSID and BSSID (MAC address) of an authorized access point, and then the attacker might use some DoS technique to overcome the legitimate AP. After a successful DoS attack, the users will be forced to disconnect from the network and then manually attempt to reconnect. At that point, with many users busy and trying to get back to work, some or all may associate with the evil twin AP and submit the network passphrase or their credentials for authentication.
                - However it is configured, when a user connects to an evil twin, it might be able to harvest authentication information and, if it is able to provide wider network or Internet access, execute an on-path attack to snoop on connections established with servers or websites.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Q_iabTWyw0pVyRHa6W0PXYvul2fUCgsQEC7BJl6lSnKNF7LMwNAYSPHbyOR7KCIpDE2O3hX2goUTLaiQHXd6foyK-Rep7menesEnMEsMaBu4uHyzeV3woOvEynXEHwMW.jpeg)
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - Description
            - The window shows Layout tab selected to list radar, network, history, history and networks, and show all. The screen has four sections labeled connection, radar, networks, and signal history. Connection lists wireless detail and addresses. The third section, networks lists data in a table format: S S I D, signal level, Wi-Fi mode, security, vendor, U S S I D, channel, frequency, network type, and graph.
            -  *Surveying Wi-Fi networks using Xirrus Wi-Fi Inspector (xirrus.com)—Note the presence of print devices configured with open authentication (no security) and a smart TV appliance (requiring authentication). (Screenshot used with permission from Xirrus.)* 
            - One solution to the risk of rogue access points is to use EAP-TLS security so that the authentication server and clients perform mutual authentication. There are also various scanners and monitoring systems that can detect rogue APs, referred to as a wireless intrusion detection system (WIDS) or wireless intrusion prevention system (WIPS).
                - ## Deauthentication Attacks
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The use of an evil twin may be coupled with a **deauthentication attack**. This sends a stream of spoofed management frames to cause a client to deauthenticate from an AP. This might allow the attacker to interpose the evil twin, sniff information about the authentication process, or perform a denial of service (DoS) attack against the wireless infrastructure. The attacks can be mitigated if the wireless infrastructure supports Management Frame Protection (MFP/802.11w). Both the AP and clients must be configured to support MFP.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/kKVceG7vXSxZbG9ZanCOQl7GW0LiME8BXKLcjMTYgtk2dWi4cNOxQ534rB0iZs-xuNaAKTw_FJQYQgeMCw0NdZzLS2t1owqdgqFzRs8pR_MbEPmiGB8hwfETLZc_Awrh.png)
                -  *Aireplay sniffs ARP packets to harvest IVs while Airodump saves them to a capture, which Aircrack can analyze to identify the correct encryption key.* 
            - 
        - **12.4 Wireless Troubleshooting** 
            - ## 12.4.1 Wireless Performance Assessment
                - Wireless troubleshooting can be broadly divided into Physical       layer issues with signal strength or interference and configuration       issues. This lesson focuses on issues that affect signal strength and       performance, but always check that the security and authentication       parameters are correctly configured before assuming you have a Physical       layer connectivity problem.
                - As with cabled networks, you should distinguish between bit rate and       throughput when measuring and assessing wireless performance against the       specifications and limitations of a particular Wi-Fi standard:
                    - Bit rate is the amount of total amount of data transferred per second         established at the Physical and Data Link layers. The nominal link bit         rate is determined by standards support (Wi-Fi 5 or Wi-Fi 6, for         instance), use of bonded channels, and optimizations, such as MU-MIMO.         If the sender and receiver are far apart or subject to interference, a         lower rate will be negotiated to make the link more reliable.
                    - Throughput is the amount of data that can be transferred at the         Network layer, discarding overhead from layers 1 and 2. Often the term         "goodput" is used to describe data transfer achieved at the Application         layer (accounting for overhead from header fields and packet         loss/retransmissions).
                - **Radio         frequency (RF) attenuation** or free space path loss is the       degradation of a signal as the distance between a radio transmitter and       receiver increases. The strength of the signal decreases per the       inverse-square rule. For example, doubling the distance decreases the       signal strength by a factor of four. Meanwhile, interference sources       collectively overlay a competing background signal, referred to as noise.       These factors impose distance limitations on how far a client can be from       an access point. Loss of power/signal strength is measured in dB units.       For example, if transmit power is 14 dBm (~25 mW), antenna gain is 3 dBi,       and free space loss over 30 meters is 70 dB, the received signal strength       is approximately 0.000005 mW:
                - `(14 dBm + 3 dBi) – 70 dB = -53 dBm = ~0.000005         mW`
                - If noise is -80 dBm, the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) would be 27 dB. Most       sites would aim for a margin of 20+ dB, so there is enough margin for       signal loss through typical indoor environmental obstacles. For example,       you might budget for 5 dB loss per internal wall.
                - Signal strength and noise can be measured by using a **Wi-Fi         analyzer**. This type of software can be installed to a laptop or       smartphone. It will record statistics for the AP that the client is       currently associated with and detect any other access points in the       vicinity. There are also dedicated Wi-Fi tester hardware devices.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/jnXMGGz54s8Ihs86i4fBu2bLQjqxo0w2zIGZ1VNdaGEEpkxHjUn8mcnT5cHnR5-N_p_ats8lKdm0f6qSino89k7I3IVxacyiDckKfq2N9lHLu-sZd9j0UppcnOIQ8Lol.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The window shows two sections. The first section lists data in a           table format whose headers are S S I D, signal, radios, clients,           channels, security, mode, max rate, and so on. The second section           shows graphs under the titles, 2.4 Gigahertz and 5 Gigahertz.
                    -  *Surveying Wi-Fi networks using inSSIDer. The chart shows which           channels are active and the signal strength of different networks in           each channel. (Screenshot used with permission from MetaGeek.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 12.4.2 Insufficient Wireless Coverage Issues
                - Insufficient wireless coverage refers to spots within a building with no or weak Wi-Fi signal. If a sufficient signal strength cannot be obtained and sources of interference cannot be mitigated, the only solution is to install an additional device to cover the gap. If you cannot extend the distribution system (cabled network) to support an additional access point, you will need to configure a wireless bridge or use a range extender.
                - ## Antenna Placement
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Incorrect antenna placement could cause or exacerbate attenuation and interference problems. Use a site survey and heat map to determine the optimum position for APs and (if available) the direction in which to point adjustable antennae. Also, using an incorrect antenna type may adversely affect the signal strength at any given point. A unidirectional antenna is only suitable for point to point connections, not for general client access. The internal antennae built into APs may also be optimized to transmit and receive in some directions more than others. For example, an AP designed for ceiling mounting may produce a stronger signal in a cone directed downward from its central axis, whereas the signal from a desktop AP is likely to radiate in a doughnut-like pattern. Consult the documentation for your specific model of AP or use site survey software to produce a heat map.
                - Remember that some client devices might support a standard such as 802.11n, but only have a single band 2.4 GHz radio. They will not be able to join a 5 GHz network.
                - ## Antenna Cable Attenuation
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Another source of attenuation is where the antenna is connected at some distance from the access point via coax cabling. Signal loss along this cable is referred to as **antenna cable attenuation**. LMR/HDF/CFD 200 cable has attenuation of about 0.6 dB/m (decibels per meter), while 400 cable improves that to about 0.22 dB/m. Connector loss is usually calculated as 0.15 dB.
                - If a device has removable antennae, check that these are screwed in firmly. A loose or disconnected antenna may reduce the range of the device or prevent connectivity altogether.
                - ## Effective Isotropic Radiated Power/Power Settings
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Wireless devices have a configurable transmit power. **Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP)** is calculated as the sum of transmit power, antenna cable/connector loss, and antenna gain. For example, if you are configuring a point to point link with a directional antenna, you might derive the following value for EIRP:
                - `15 dBm (Transmit Power) – 1 dB (Cable Loss) + 6 dBi (Gain) = 20 dBm (100 mW)`
                - The EIRP for each radio is reported through the access point or controller management software. EIRP must not exceed regulatory limits. Power limits are different for the 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands and for point to multipoint versus point to point operation modes.
                - Increasing transmit power is not usually an effective solution for improving wireless coverage. While an AP might have an EIRP of around 23 dBm, smartphone devices are more likely to be around 10 to 14 dBm. If the client detects a strong signal, it will set a high data rate. However, because the EIRP of the client radio is much lower, it fails to transmit a strong signal back to the AP. Because it is trying to use a high data rate, this results in excessive packet errors.
                - As a general rule of thumb, AP power should be two-thirds of the weakest client power. For example, if the weakest client can output 14 dBm, the AP should transmit at 9 to 10 dBm.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 12.4.3 Channel Overlap Issues
                - **Channel overlap** refers to interference       issues resulting from multiple access points that are all in range of one       another and are configured to use similar wavelengths. There are two main       types of channel interference:
                    - **Co-channel           interference (CCI)**—Each Wi-Fi channel is a collision domain.         Where multiple devices use the same channel, there is contention. The         wireless devices must use CSMA/CA to find opportunities to transmit. CCI         can be measured as a channel utilization percentage. Channel utilization         can be measured from the access point or by using a Wi-Fi         analyzer. As a design goal, a channel should exhibit no more than 50%         utilization.
                    - **Adjacent           channel interference (ACI)**—This occurs when access points are         configured to use different but overlapping channels, such as 1 and 3 in         the 2.4 GHz band. ACI slows down the CSMA/CA process and raises noise         levels.
                - One of the design goals for a multi-AP site is to create clean basic       service areas so that clients can select an AP with the strongest signal       easily and the WLAN operates with a minimum of co-channel interference. At       least 25 MHz spacing should be allowed to avoid channel overlap. In       practice, therefore, no more than three nearby APs using the 2.4 GHz band       can have non-overlapping channels. This could be implemented, for example,       by selecting channel 1 for AP1, channel 6 for AP2, and channel 11 for AP3.       When you are using the 5 GHz band for 802.11a or Wi-Fi 4/5/6, more       non-overlapping channels are available.
                - In a complex environment, it may be necessary to adjust the power level       used by an AP on a given channel. Using the maximum available power on an       AP can result in it interfering with other "cells" and to situations where       a client can "hear" the AP but cannot "talk" to it because it lacks       sufficient power.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/Bzqxw2o-_P0CwAuCvpk33gy5ZgdYEhS4GywuzpfgkTvlTMd0_zhE7-mtqpA2uhDVpxFPOPabRl8xho9z3ztnctIYRP3AH9qNG9i1QlEQoGGHUEgIlUYPfetKj2AgyO9t.png)
                    - Description
                    - 
                    - The left pane titled, Advanced Statistics shows General selected. The           right pane shows two sections: connection status and general.           Connection status: connected. General section provides data on A P M A           C address, received beacons, percent missed beacons, percent transmit           errors, C C X version, current T x power, and supported power levels.           Reset stats and close buttons are at the bottom.
                    -  *Checking power levels on a wireless station using Intel's PROSet           Wi-Fi configuration utility. (Screenshot courtesy of Intel Corp.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 12.4.4 Interference Issues
                - If a device is within the supported range but the signal is weak or you       cannot get a connection, there is likely to be interference. Apart from       CCI and ACI, there are several other sources of interference to consider:
                    - **Reflection/bounce (multipath interference)**—Mirrors         or shiny surfaces cause signals to reflect, meaning that a variable         delay is introduced. This causes packets to be lost and consequently the         data rate to drop.
                - The Wi-Fi 4/5/6 standards actually use bounce       (multipath) as a means of optimizing throughput and range via MIMO.
                    - **Refraction**—Glass or water can cause radio waves to         bend and take a different path to the receiver. This can also cause the         data rate to drop.
                    - **Absorption**—This refers to the degree to which         walls, windows, and people will reduce signal strength (some of the         radio wave's energy is lost as heat when passing through construction         materials or human bodies). An internal wall might "cost" 3 to 15 dB,         depending on the material used (concrete being the most effective         absorber). The 2.4 GHz frequency has better penetration than the 5 GHz         one, given the same power output. To minimize absorption from office         furniture (and people), use ceiling-mounted APs.
                    - **Electromagnetic interference (EMI)**—Interference         from a powerful radio or electromagnetic source working in the same         frequency band, such as a Bluetooth device, cordless phone, or microwave         oven.
                - EMI can be detected by using a **spectrum         analyzer**. Unlike a Wi-Fi analyzer, a spectrum analyzer must use a       special radio receiver—Wi-Fi adapters filter out anything that isn't a       Wi-Fi signal. They are usually supplied as handheld units with a       directional antenna, so that the exact location of the interference can be       pinpointed. A 6 dB change in the level of a particular source represents a       halving or doubling of the distance between the analyzer and the RF source.
                - Also consider that signal problems could be a       result of someone trying to attack the network by jamming the legitimate       AP and making clients connect to a rogue AP.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_trouble_wireless_comm_n09/d_trouble_wireless_comm_n09.mp4)Play Video![](https://cdn.testout.com/network-plus-n10-009-en-us/en-us/resources/demos/d_trouble_wireless_comm_n09/d_trouble_wireless_comm_n09.jpg)

            - ### Troubleshooting Wireless Connection 00:00-00:29
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we're going to look at](undefined)[troubleshooting some common wireless problems.](undefined)[We're not going to talk about troubleshooting](undefined)[wireless controllers, the back haul, and the access points.](undefined)[Instead, we're going to look at end user problems](undefined)[you might have to troubleshoot to get](undefined)[users' systems connected to the wireless network.](undefined)[The assumption here is that we've confirmed](undefined)[that the wireless network is working,](undefined)[and the problem is with the client.](undefined)
            - ### Problems with the Network Connection 00:29-01:21
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The first one we're going to look at is one where](undefined)[the end user has accidentally disabled the wireless adapter.](undefined)[And believe me, this happens all the time,](undefined)[especially on notebook systems.](undefined)[You get a help desk call, you go to troubleshoot](undefined)[the problem, the user says, "Can't connect](undefined)[to the wireless network," and you look and you see that](undefined)[they've accidentally turned the wireless adapter off.](undefined)[That's what's happened with this particular Windows system.](undefined)
            - [Notice, down here, that we don't have a network connection.](undefined)[The reason we don't have any network connections](undefined)[is because the wireless network adapter is disabled.](undefined)[So I'm going to turn the wireless adapter on.](undefined)[On this particular model, I can do that by pressing](undefined)[the Fn key plus the F12 key. Or I can simply](undefined)[click down here and see that they somehow turned it off.](undefined)[They may have also put it into Airplane mode,](undefined)[and that is, essentially, the same sort of problem.](undefined)[If the problem is with a button on the machine,](undefined)[you have to look around and find out](undefined)[where it is on your device.](undefined)
            - ### Connecting to a Wireless Network 01:21-02:43
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Okay. So, I've re-enabled the wireless adapter.](undefined)[But let's look to make sure we're connected](undefined)[to the right wireless network.](undefined)
            - [If I click on this icon, I can see the list of](undefined)[all the wireless networks that are currently in range.](undefined)[You can see that I have several.](undefined)[One is named NotYourWireless.](undefined)[Here's another called WACC.](undefined)[If I want to connect to one of these networks,](undefined)[I simply click on it here, and then I have](undefined)[the option to connect to that wireless network.](undefined)
            - [Because I've never connected this system to this](undefined)[wireless network before, I'm prompted here as to whether](undefined)[or not I want to connect automatically](undefined)[every time this wireless network is in range.](undefined)
            - [If I leave this option turned on,](undefined)[then a wireless profile will be automatically created for me](undefined)[in the networking sharing center that contains all the](undefined)[necessary parameters to connect to this wireless network.](undefined)[We don't have to do that if we don't want to.](undefined)[I can leave that turned off,](undefined)[and then I can connect just this one time.](undefined)[The parameters that I specify won't be saved.](undefined)[I'm going to leave it on and go ahead and click Connect.](undefined)[Now I need to provide the security key.](undefined)
            - [I happen to know that this network is using WPA2.](undefined)[Notice, over here, that there's an option to show characters](undefined)[if you need to confirm that you typed the key in correctly.](undefined)[I'll click Next, and we're prompted to decide if we want our](undefined)[PC to be discoverable on the network. I'll just select No.](undefined)
            - [We're connected to the WACC network.](undefined)[We have no internet access, but we resolved the issue](undefined)[of the disabled wireless button.](undefined)[So now, let's look at another issue.](undefined)
            - ### Spelling Issues Entering Passphrase 02:43-04:09
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Another problem you're going to run into](undefined)[is one where the end user has put in the incorrect](undefined)[pre-shared key on a wireless network](undefined)[that uses a pre-shared key, such as WPA2 PSK.](undefined)[This is also a very common problem](undefined)[because with WPA and WPA2, the security key](undefined)[that you put in is case sensitive.](undefined)[WEP, which is an older wireless standard,](undefined)[uses hexadecimal characters.](undefined)[So you can use uppercase or lowercase with it,](undefined)[but we don't recommend using WEP any longer.](undefined)
            - [Let's suppose that my wireless key was working](undefined)[just fine yesterday for the NotYourWireless network,](undefined)[but the system administrator has gone in](undefined)[and changed the pre-shared key on the access point.](undefined)[Therefore, the pre-shared key that I currently have](undefined)[saved on this system in the wireless profile](undefined)[isn't going to work anymore.](undefined)[What I might want to do in this case](undefined)[is delete this network and then reconnect.](undefined)
            - [If I go to Start and then Settings,](undefined)[select Network and Internet, go over to Wi-Fi,](undefined)[and finally to Manage known networks, you can see that](undefined)[I have a profile defined for the NotYourWireless network.](undefined)[I'm going to click on it and select Forget.](undefined)
            - [Now, let's go back and reconnect to it and enter the](undefined)[new pass phrase that I received from the network admin.](undefined)[I'm going to click on my wireless icon,](undefined)[and I'm going to connect to NotYourWireless.](undefined)[I'll enter in my credentials and select No here.](undefined)[Now we're connected again with full internet access.](undefined)
            - ### Modern Wireless Access Points 04:09-05:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Understand that most modern wireless access points](undefined)[can be configured to run in the 5-gigahertz mode, 2.4-gigahertz mode,](undefined)[or mixed mode, where the point supports both standards-](undefined)[-one radio runs 5-gigahertz, and one radio runs 2.4-gigahertz.](undefined)[That's really handy because it supports](undefined)[a wide range of devices.](undefined)[The problem is that you can't use that](undefined)[2.4-gigahertz radio for your 5-gigahertz communications.](undefined)[If you turn 2.4-gigahertz off, you can use that extra radio](undefined)[and antenna for your 5-gigahertz clients](undefined)[and actually increase throughput using MIMO,](undefined)[or multiple-input multiple-output technology.](undefined)[However, this can be a problem](undefined)[if your users have older wireless network cards](undefined)[in their systems that only support 802.11G.](undefined)[If that's the case and you turn off the 2.4 gigahertz radio,](undefined)[then they won't be able to connect](undefined)[to the wireless network.](undefined)
            - [Let's go in and see how this works.](undefined)[I'm logged in to this dual band wireless router.](undefined)[I haven't configured it yet,](undefined)[so I'm just going to click the Login button here.](undefined)[I'm going to go up to Wireless and then the Basic button.](undefined)[Over here to the right, I have the option](undefined)[to uncheck the 2.4 gigahertz wireless.](undefined)[If you have clients that can't connect,](undefined)[it could be that they have a 2.4-gigahertz radio](undefined)[trying to connect to your 5-gigahertz wireless network.](undefined)
            - ### Other Options 05:25-06:00
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Before we end this demo,](undefined)[I'll mention just a few more options you might try.](undefined)
            - [You can check and make sure that no one](undefined)[has modified the IP settings on the client.](undefined)
            - [You can check and see if Windows recently did](undefined)[any updates to the wireless drivers.](undefined)[I can do that by going to the Start button, Settings,](undefined)[Update and security, and clicking on Update history.](undefined)[I can then look at my updates](undefined)[and see if there's anything new here.](undefined)[It's always recommended that you obtain](undefined)[drivers from the manufacturer's website.](undefined)
            - [Finally, you can go down to the network icon,](undefined)[right-click, and select Troubleshoot problems.](undefined)
            - ### Summary 06:00-06:25
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)[In this demo, we talked about how to](undefined)[troubleshoot common wireless problems.](undefined)[First, we looked at issues where we have a](undefined)[disabled wireless adapter on a network host.](undefined)[We looked at problems that happen](undefined)[when we used the wrong pre-shared key.](undefined)[Then we talked about how a client might have a 2.4-gigahertz](undefined)[host trying to connect to a 5-gigahertz access point.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 12.4.5 Roaming and Client Disassociation Issues
                - Roaming means that wireless clients can remain connected to the same network ESSID while moving around within an extended service area (ESA). An ESA is created by connecting APs via a wired network and configuring them with the same ESSID and security parameters. The access points are configured with different channels so that where the basic service area of each access point overlaps, there is no interference.
                - ## Roaming Misconfiguration Issues
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - In order to enable seamless roaming, the cells served by each AP need to overlap to some extent. This is one of the trickiest elements of site design to get right, as client behaviors and capabilities for roaming can vary widely. The main issues with roaming are the following:
                    - Sticky clients that do not identify signal issues and do not reassociate with a different AP that could provide a better connection.
                    - Flapping clients that switch repeatedly between access points.
                    - Clients that do not support roaming standards (802.11k, 802.11r, and 802.11v) and so experience service interruptions due to having to reauthenticate or associate too slowly with the new AP. 802.11r assists with reauthentication, support for 802.11k can mitigate sticky and flapping client issues, as it transmits information about the wireless topology to the client, and 802.11v can "push" a client toward a less congested access point.
                    - Inconsistent service areas for 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz. 2.4 GHz supports longer ranges than 5 GHz, and this can cause it to "attract" more clients. Typically, a 2.4 GHz BSS is configured with a lower transmit power than the equivalent 5 GHz BSS.
                - Issues with roaming can be identified by analyzing access point association times for client devices. A WLAN controller will be able to track client mobility, showing each access point and the time that the client associated with it.
                - ## Client Disassociation Issues
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - In the normal course of operations, an access point and client use management frames to control connections. The access point broadcasts a beacon frame to advertise service capabilities. Clients can choose to first authenticate and then associate to an access point when they move into range of the beacon. The client or access point can use **disassociation** and/or deauthentication frames to notify the other party that it has ended a connection. A legitimate client might disassociate but not deauthenticate because it is roaming between access points in an extended service area. A client might "flap" between two access points, causing numerous disassociations and reassociations. Investigate the access point or controller event log to identify the cause of disassociations.
                - If clients are disassociated unexpectedly and there is no roaming, interference, or standards support issue, you should consider the possibility of a malicious attack. A disassociation attack exploits the lack of encryption in management frame traffic to send spoofed frames. One type of disassociation attack injects management frames that spoof the MAC address of a single victim station in a disassociation notification, causing it to be disconnected from the network. Another variant of the attack broadcasts spoofed frames to disconnect all stations. Frames can be spoofed to send either disassociation or deauthentication notifications.
                - Disassociation/deauthentication attacks may be used to perform a denial of service attack against the wireless infrastructure or to exploit disconnected stations to try to force reconnection to a rogue or evil twin access point. Disassociation/deauthentication attacks might also be used in conjunction with a replay attack aimed at recovering the network key.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - ### Optimize Wireless Networks 00:00-00:11
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demonstration, we'll look at things you](undefined)[can do to optimize an enterprise wireless network.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Wireless Network Settings  00:11-01:59
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The first task is to manage access point power levels.](undefined)[Access point power levels can help](undefined)[spread or weaken the wireless coverage.](undefined)[More power provided to an access point](undefined)[can increase both coverage and speed.](undefined)[However, you don't want access points to be overpowered,](undefined)[which would cause too much overlap in coverage.](undefined)
            - [Also, distortion and Interference can](undefined)[be caused by overpowered access points.](undefined)[Originally, some wireless controllers didn't have the](undefined)[capability to auto determine transmit power, called TX power.](undefined)[But, the Cisco Wireless Lan Controller (WLC)](undefined)[we use in this demo can auto detect TX power.](undefined)
            - [Let's go to Wireless and then to 802.11a.](undefined)[You can see a drop down for RRM.](undefined)[This stands for Radio Resource Management.](undefined)[This Cisco built-in tool automatically](undefined)[detects and configures access points.](undefined)[It also gives statistics.](undefined)[802.11a is the 5ghz while 802.11b is the 2.4ghz.](undefined)[Both radio frequencies have an RRM configuration.](undefined)[Let's check it out.](undefined)
            - [If you have multiple WLCs, one must be the leader.](undefined)[The RF Grouping determines the WLC that is the leader.](undefined)[The TPC algorithm controls the TX power.](undefined)[There is an option up here to](undefined)[select coverage or interference.](undefined)
            - [If you are more concerned about coverage,](undefined)[you leave Coverage optimal mode selected.](undefined)[Although you can set TX power to fixed,](undefined)[it's usually best to set it as automatic.](undefined)[The dynamic channel assignment (DCA) feature can](undefined)[automatically assess how much TX power is needed.](undefined)
            - [If two access points are near each](undefined)[other, use the same channel for both.](undefined)[It's possible that the access points](undefined)[could interfere with each other.](undefined)[This setting used to be manually set.](undefined)[The Coverage setting, gives you information to see if more](undefined)[access points are needed or if they need to be relocated.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Access Point Settings 01:59-02:46
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Let's look at settings directly on an access point.](undefined)[If we go to Wireless and then to All APs,](undefined)[you can see the two connected access points.](undefined)[If you click one, it provides more](undefined)[settings related to the access point.](undefined)
            - [We could rename this AP to lobby, for example, to](undefined)[indicate that this access point is in the lobby.](undefined)[In the Advanced area, we have more information and](undefined)[can change the AP group name and other settings.](undefined)
            - [The power over Ethernet (POE) settings](undefined)[say the AP is running at 15.4 watts.](undefined)[However, the access point is capable of running](undefined)[POE+ which can be up to 30 watts per port.](undefined)[Because the switch the access point is plugged into](undefined)[doesn't have POE+, the access point defaults to 15.4 watts.](undefined)[Click Apply to save the settings.](undefined)
            - ### Configure Load Balancing  02:46-04:18
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [The last topic we'll look at in this](undefined)[demonstration is load-balancing.](undefined)[Load-balancing is an important network technology.](undefined)[It balances the load between two or more](undefined)[access points to provide optimum performance.](undefined)[It redistributes the load between access points to](undefined)[eliminate overloaded and underloaded conditions.](undefined)
            - [Go to Wireless tab > Advanced > Load Balancing.](undefined)[There is an option to configure.](undefined)[Cisco calls this aggressive load balancing](undefined)[if you search for information on this.](undefined)[The client window size is used with the Cisco](undefined)[algorithm to determine a busy access point.](undefined)[This value can be adjusted from 1 to 20.](undefined)[The default is 5.](undefined)
            - [The Maximum denial count sets the max number of times client](undefined)[associations can be rejected by a certain access point.](undefined)[Since we currently don't have a heavy load on](undefined)[the access points, we'll leave the default.](undefined)[These are global settings, but if you see](undefined)[this note below, you must enable it per WLAN.](undefined)[Otherwise, settings will not be in effect.](undefined)
            - [Let's go to the WLANS tab and then to the Testout network.](undefined)[In the Advanced tab, there is an](undefined)[option for client load balance.](undefined)[Let's check that and click Apply.](undefined)
            - [There are some warnings, such as the first one,](undefined)[that says it could impact applications like voice.](undefined)[After we click OK, another warning says wireless](undefined)[connectivity could be affected as the radio resets.](undefined)[Click OK again.](undefined)[Remember to click Save configuration](undefined)[in the top right-hand corner.](undefined)[This is always required.](undefined)[Otherwise, the changes will not](undefined)[be there if the WLC is rebooted.](undefined)
            - [That's it for this demonstration.](undefined)
            - ### Summary  04:18-04:40
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [In this demo, we talked about some things you can](undefined)[do to optimize an enterprise wireless network.](undefined)[We first showed you how to manage access point power levels.](undefined)[Then we covered a wireless network setting for RRM.](undefined)[We ended this demonstration by showing you](undefined)[how to load-balancing between access points.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 12.4.6 Overcapacity Issues
                - Overcapacity (or device saturation) occurs when too many client devices connect to the same AP. The maximum number of clients that an AP can support varies, depending on the Wi-Fi standard used and the type of network traffic generated. For example, web browsing will typically place a lighter load on the network than local client-server traffic or is likely at least to move any bottleneck further upstream to the WAN, rather than the wireless network. While individual circumstances must be considered, a maximum of 30 clients per AP is generally accepted as a rule of thumb. In designing the network, enough APs should be provided in appropriate locations to support the expected client density at this ratio. APs can usually be configured to enforce a maximum number of connections, so that additional clients will connect to the next nearest AP.
                - Even with a relatively low number of clients, the wireless network can suffer from bandwidth saturation. Since wireless is a broadcast medium, the available bandwidth is shared between all clients. Thus, if one client is a bandwidth hog, others may find it difficult to maintain a reliable connection. In an enterprise Wi-Fi solution, a controller will normally provide reporting tools to diagnose bandwidth issues and to report on which clients are consuming the most bandwidth. It could also report on wireless channel utilization and configure APs and clients to reassign channels dynamically to reduce overutilization. If a traffic shaper is deployed, it may work automatically to throttle bandwidth to overactive nodes.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
        - [Size]();-[H3]()
    - ### 13.0 Comparing Remote Access Methods
        - **13.1 WAN and Internet Connectivity** 
            - ## 13.1.1 Wide Area Networks and the OSI Model
                - Wide area network (WAN) technologies support data communications over greater distances than LANs. The term "enterprise WAN" is used to describe a WAN that is used and controlled by a single organization. However, even though an enterprise may control its WAN, it rarely owns all the infrastructure that supports it. Long-distance communications usually involve the use of public networks. Public networks are owned by telecommunications (telco) companies and provide WAN services to businesses and households. Organizations often choose to use public networks, as the cost is far less than implementing a private solution. Service providers often have rights of access to locations that are not available to other organizations, such as under roads.
                - As with a LAN, the WAN Physical layer describes the media type and interface specifications. Where the provider link is a copper cable, some type of modem is usually used, rather than a switch. A modem performs modulation of outgoing signals and demodulation of incoming data, working only at the Physical layer of the OSI model. Modulation means transforming an electromagnetic wave to represent information, such as using the amplitude (height) of the wave to represent bits. Legacy modems perform digital to analog modulation for transmission over voice lines. An analog (or dial-up) modem only supports low bandwidths (up to 56 Kbps). Digital modems perform a different type of modulation to transform digital signals received as Ethernet frames for transmission over the WAN media. Digital modem types include data service units (DSUs) for leased lines, digital subscriber line (DSL) modems, cable modems, and satellite modems.
                - At the Data Link layer, WANs often use simpler protocols than Ethernet LANs as the links are more likely to be point to point and do not need much complexity. That said, Ethernet is increasingly being deployed for end-to-end connectivity over WANs.
                - At the Network layer, the customer and provider site are addressed using the Internet Protocol (IP). A customer edge (CE) router connects to a provider edge (PE) via the underlying Link layer interface. The provider allocates public IPv4/IPv6 addresses or address ranges to the customer.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 13.1.2 Internet Access Types
                - Establishing a WAN provider link means terminating the access provider's       cabling at some point in your premises, and then attaching modem and       routing equipment to that line. The service-related entry point at which       the access provider's network terminates is called the demarcation         point (or demarc for short) or minimum point of entry (MPOE). The       demarc point represents the end of the telco's responsibility for       maintaining that part of the cabling. Any cable problems arising from the       other side of the demarc point are the responsibility of the customer.
                - Modems and routers or other access equipment that are provided or leased       by the customer and installed at their site are referred to as customer       premises equipment (CPE). Some providers may take on responsibility for       faults that arise in CPE, depending on the contract and installation       circumstances.
                - The demarc and CPE should be installed to a secure location within the       premises, with access controls to restrict the area to authorized staff.       This location is referred to as entrance facilities in TIA/EIA structured       cabling standards.
                - ## T-Carrier
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The T-carrier       system enabled voice traffic to be digitized for transport around the core       of the telecommunications network. It also enabled other types of digital       data to be transported and could be provisioned directly to subscribers as       a leased line. T-carrier is based on time division       multiplexing (TDM). The protocol assigns each circuit (or       channel) a time slot. Each 64 Kbps channel provides enough bandwidth for a       digitized voice call.
                - A single 64 Kbps channel is known as a DS0 or narrowband link. For leased       line data services, however, the foundation level of T-carrier is the DS1       or T1 digital signal circuit. This service comprises 24 channels       multiplexed into a single 1.544 Mbps full-duplex digital connection that       can be used for voice and data. The T1 lines themselves can be multiplexed       to provide even more bandwidth.
                - At the Data Link layer, T1 leased lines typically use either High-Level       Data Link Control (HDLC) or Point-to-Point Protocol (PPP).
                - ## Digital Subscriber Line
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Digital         subscriber         line (DSL) is a technology for transferring data over voice-grade       telephone lines, often referred to as the local loop. A DSL modem is       installed as CPE, typically as a multifunction “wireless router,” where       the RJ11 WAN port connects to the provider’s phone jack. DSL modems can       also be supplied as separate appliances or plug-in cards for routers. A       filter (splitter) must be installed on each phone point to prevent noise       from affecting either voice calls or the DSL link.
                - There are various "flavors" of DSL, notably asymmetrical and symmetrical       types:
                    - Asymmetrical DSL (ADSL) provides a fast downlink but a slow uplink.         There are various iterations of ADSL, with the latest (ADSL2+) offering         downlink rates up to about 24 Mbps and uplink rates of 1.25 Mbps or 2.5         Mbps.
                    - Symmetric versions of DSL offer the same uplink and downlink speeds.         These are of more use to businesses and for branch office links, where         more data is transferred upstream than with normal Internet use.
                - ## Cable Internet
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A cable Internet connection is usually available along with Cable Access       TV (CATV). These networks are sometimes described as hybrid fiber coax       (HFC) because they combine a fiber optic core network with coax links to       CPE, but are more simply just described as cable broadband.
                - Installation of a cable         modem follows the same general principles as for a DSL modem. An       Ethernet cable connects the cable modem to the customer's router, and a       short segment of coax connects the WAN port to the provider network. More       coax then links all the premises in a street with a Cable Modem       Termination System (CMTS), which routes data traffic via the fiber       backbone to the ISP's point of presence (PoP) and from there to the       Internet. Cable based on the Data Over Cable Service Interface       Specification (DOCSIS) supports downlink speeds of up to 38 Mbps (North       America) or 50 Mbps (Europe) and uplinks of up to 27 Mbps. DOCSIS version       3 allows the use of multiplexed channels to achieve higher bandwidth.
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - ## 13.1.3 Fiber to the Curb and Fiber to the Premises
                - The major obstacle to providing WAN access that can approach LAN performance is bandwidth in the last mile, where the copper wiring infrastructure is generally not good. The projects to update this wiring to use fiber optic links are referred to by the umbrella term Fiber to the X (FTTx).
                - ## Fiber to the Curb and VDSL
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A fiber to the curb (FTTC) solution retains some sort of copper wiring to the customer premises while extending the fiber link from the point of presence to a communications cabinet servicing multiple subscribers. The service providers with their roots in telephone networks use very high-speed DSL (VDSL) to support FTTC. VDSL achieves higher bit rates than other DSL types at the expense of range. It allows for both symmetric and asymmetric modes. Over 300 m (1,000 feet), an asymmetric link supports 52 Mbps downstream and 6 Mbps upstream, while a symmetric link supports 26 Mbps in both directions. VDSL2 specifies a very short range (100 m/300 feet) rate of 100 Mbps (bidirectional).
                - The modem type must match the service. An ADSL-only modem cannot be used to access a VDSL service, for instance.
                - ## Fiber to the Premises and Optical Network Terminals
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - A fiber to the premises (FTTP) Internet connection means that the service provider’s fiber optic cable is run all the way to the customer’s building. This full fiber connection type is implemented as a passive optical network (PON). In a PON, a single fiber cable is run from the point of presence to an optical line terminal (OLT) located in a street cabinet. From the OLT, splitters direct each subscriber’s traffic over a shorter length of fiber to an optical network terminal (ONT) installed at the customer’s premises. The ONT converts the optical signal to an electrical one. The ONT is connected to the customer’s router using an RJ45 Ethernet patch cord.
                - ![](https://remnote-user-data.s3.amazonaws.com/1u71zaUDC5M_U6vW9Xwj7KNcqzpPP6jLtLUA9i7BRfLSrHlDjG6YXjlom3goZ5FnE7yaKnaIUlD9xYnSRdclhFLncO2Cyde-pzX_bariwabV6GuubCOEwMXOaoPi9CU1.png)
                -  *Optical network terminal—the PON port terminates the external fiber cable, and the LAN ports connect to local routers or computers over RJ45 patch cords. (Image by artush © 123RF.com.)* 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()
            - 
        - **13.2 Virtual Private Networks** 
            - ## 13.2.1 Remote Access Considerations
                - Remote network access means that the user's device does not make a direct cabled or wireless connection to the network. The connection occurs over or through an intermediate network, usually a public WAN. Historically, remote network access might have used analog modems connecting over the telephone system. These days, most remote network access is implemented as a virtual private network (VPN), running over the Internet.
                - Given that, administering remote access involves essentially the same tasks as administering the local network. Only authorized users who have successfully authenticated should be allowed access to local network resources and communication channels. Additional complexity comes about because it can be more difficult to ensure the security of remote workstations and servers, and there is greater opportunity for remote logins to be exploited.
                - The creation of a remote access server (RAS) should be accompanied by documentation describing the uses of the service, security risks and countermeasures, and authorized users of the service. There should also be authorization to run the service from the network manager. The remote access policy should then implement the measures identified through compiling the documentation. Typical policy restrictions would be:
                    - Restricting access to defined users or groups.
                    - Restricting access to defined times of day or particular days of the week.
                    - Restricting privileges on the local network (ideally, remote users would only be permitted access to a clearly defined part of the network).
                    - Logging and auditing access logons and attempted logons.
                - In addition to this, a management plan should ensure that RASs and other hardware are kept up to date with the latest software or firmware updates. Administrative access to the devices should also be secured, using strong authentication.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

                - Video Previous Segment
                - Video Control Resume Play
                - Video Control Pause
                - Video Next Segment
            -

            - ### VPN Protocol and Encryption 00:00-00:26
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [A VPN uses a protocol that tunnels or encapsulates](undefined)[each packet into a new encrypted packet.](undefined)[Information in these encrypted packets, headers routes](undefined)[itself through the Internet on the destination device, the](undefined)[outer wrapping is removed and the packet is decrypted,](undefined)[meaning that the data is back in its original format.](undefined)[The encrypted packet provides a secure](undefined)[connection because only the device on the other](undefined)[end has the decryption key to read the contents.](undefined)
            - ### Host-to-Host VPN 00:26-00:50
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now there are multiple ways you can](undefined)[configure a VPN through the Internet.](undefined)[The first is with a host to host VPN.](undefined)[This type of VPN allows an individual host on the](undefined)[Internet to establish a VPN connection with another host.](undefined)[With this connection type, both devices need the](undefined)[ability to understand the protocol being used.](undefined)[Both of them need to have the right software](undefined)[for encrypting and encapsulating the packets.](undefined)
            - ### Site-to-Site VPN 00:50-01:28
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Another way is to use a site to site VPN.](undefined)[With this type you have a local](undefined)[network of hosts at each location.](undefined)[Each host is able to communicate securely](undefined)[with any other host at any location.](undefined)[Rather than requiring a VPN configuration for each one,](undefined)[you install a single device to act as a VPN gateway.](undefined)[A VPN gateway is simply a dedicated device that](undefined)[handles VPN connections and packet encryption.](undefined)[It also allows all traffic between the two](undefined)[sites to be encrypted 100% of the time.](undefined)
            - ### Remote Access VPNs 01:28-02:06
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Next we have remote access VPNs.](undefined)[In this case individual hosts establish](undefined)[a VPN connection to a remote site.](undefined)[The client computer has to be able to establish the](undefined)[connection with a device called a VPN concentrator](undefined)[that sits on the edge of the private network.](undefined)[VPN concentrators are hardware devices that are](undefined)[dedicated to establishing connections to hosts.](undefined)[Each host is configured with software, often](undefined)[called a VPN client, that allows it to do this.](undefined)[The VPN concentrator is configured to](undefined)[allow or reject connections from users.](undefined)[It also removes the encryption before](undefined)[forwarding the information onto the private.](undefined)
            - ### Clientless VPN 02:06-02:37
                - [Size]();-[H3]()
            - [Now the last type of VPN we're](undefined)[going to cover is a client list VPN.](undefined)[With this, an individual host connects](undefined)[to resources on a remote site.](undefined)[A client list VPN, as the name implies, doesn't](undefined)[require a host to install VPN client software.](undefined)[Instead, a host connects to the](undefined)[remote resource using a web browser.](undefined)[Access to the site is typically controlled](undefined)[with a simple username and password.](undefined)[This type of VPN is also known as an SSL VPN because](undefined)[it uses the secure sockets layer to encrypt the data.](undefined)
            - Resume Auto-Scroll
            - ## 13.2.2 Tunneling Protocols
                - Most modern remote network solutions use Internet access infrastructure to implement a **virtual private network (VPN)**. This requires a protocol that can create a secure tunnel for private communications through the Internet. Tunneling is where the hosts are on the same logical network but connected via different physical networks. The tunnel encapsulates the packet for the local network within a public network packet. Typically, the local network packet is encrypted. When the packet is delivered, the remote access server strips the public packet headers, extracts and decrypts the local packet, and forwards it over the local network.
                - ## Point-to-Point Protocol
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - The **Point-to-Point Protocol (PPP)** is an encapsulation protocol that works at the Data Link layer (layer 2). PPP is used to encapsulate IP packets for transmission over serial digital lines. PPP has no security mechanisms, so must be used with other protocols to provision a secure tunnel.
                - ## Generic Routing Encapsulation
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Where PPP works at layer 2, **Generic Routing Encapsulation (GRE)** works at layer 3. A GRE packet can itself encapsulate an IP packet (or most other Network layer protocol types) as its payload. The "outer" GRE packet is assigned protocol number 47 and has its own IP source and header address fields. The GRE packet is then itself encapsulated in a layer 2 frame for transmission to the next hop router. Each intermediate router inspects only the outer GRE header to determine the forwarding destination. At the final destination, the receiving router de-encapsulates the GRE packet to extract the inner IP payload and forwards that inner packet to its destination. GRE does not have any mechanisms for authenticating users or devices and so is often used with other protocols in a VPN solution.
                - ## IP Security
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Internet Protocol Security (IPSec) also operates at the Network layer of the OSI model to authenticate hosts and encrypt packets. IPSec is used with other protocols to provide connection security, and it is increasingly used as a standalone VPN protocol.
                - ## Transport Layer Security
                    - [Size]();-[H2]()
                - Transport Layer Security (TLS) over TCP or datagram TLS (DTLS) over UDP can be used to encapsulate frames or IP packets. The main drawback is that as TLS already operates at the Session layer, the headers from the inner and outer packets add up to a significant overhead.
                - 
                - 
                - [Size]();-[H2]()

            - [Our VPN uses the default port of 1194.](undefined)[We'll enter a description for our](undefined)[VPN and just call it 'TestoutVPN'.](undefined)
            - [We'll leave all the cryptographic settings set to](undefined)[their defaults and skip down to Tunnel Settings.](undefined)

            - [Scroll down and click Next.](undefined)
